=== STACK DETECTION (manifest files) ===

--- Makefile ---
PROJECT_HOME := .
VULTRON_DIR := $(PROJECT_HOME)/vultron
TEST_DIR := $(PROJECT_HOME)/test

# Load environment variables from .env file if it exists
ifneq (,$(wildcard .env))
  include .env
  export
endif

PROJECT_NAME ?= vultron

# Display help information about available targets
.PHONY: help
help:  ## Show this help message
	@grep -E '^[a-zA-Z_-]+:.*?## .*$$' $(filter-out .env,$(MAKEFILE_LIST)) | sort | awk 'BEGIN {FS = ":.*?## "}; {printf "  %-20s %s\n", $$1, $$2}'

# set up dev environment
.PHONY: setup_dev
setup_dev:  ## Set up development environment
	uv sync --dev

# upgrade dev dependencies
.PHONY: upgrade_dev
upgrade_dev:  ## Upgrade dev dependencies
	uv sync --upgrade --dev

# upgrade dependencies
.PHONY: upgrade_deps
upgrade_deps:  ## Upgrade all dependencies
	uv sync --upgrade

# run tests locally
.PHONY: test
test:  ## Run tests locally
	uv run pytest test

# black code formatting
.PHONY: black
black:  ## Format code with black
	uv run black .

# markdownlint-cli2 code formatting
.PHONY: mdlint
mdlint:  ## Lint markdown files
	./mdlint.sh

# flake8
.PHONY: flake8
flake8:  ## Check code with flake8
	# edit $(PROJECT_HOME)/.flake8 to configure flake8 options
	uv run flake8 ${VULTRON_DIR} ${TEST_DIR}

# flake8 code linting
.PHONY: flake8-lint
flake8-lint:  ## Lint code with flake8
	# edit $(PROJECT_HOME)/.flake8 to configure flake8 options
	uv run flake8 --exit-zero ${VULTRON_DIR} ${TEST_DIR}

# mypy type checking
.PHONY: mypy
mypy:  ## Run mypy for type checking
	# edit $(PROJECT_HOME)/.mypy.ini to configure mypy options
	uv run mypy

# pyright static type checking
.PHONY: pyright
pyright:  ## Run pyright for static type checking
	uv run pyright

# run all linters
.PHONY: lint
lint: black mdlint flake8-lint mypy ## Run all linters (black, markdownlint)

# serve docs locally
.PHONY: docs
docs:  ## Serve documentation locally
	uv run mkdocs serve

# build docker images

site/search/search_index.json:1: {"config":{"lang":["en"],"separator":"[\\s\\-]+","pipeline":["stopWordFilter"],"fields":{"title":{"boost":1000.0},"text":{"boost":1.0},"tags":{"boost":1000000.0}}},"docs":[{"location":"","title":"The Vultron Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure Protocol","text":"<p>Work in progress</p> <p>We are currently working on the documentation of the Vultron CVD Protocol. This documentation is a work in progress and is not yet complete. Our focus so far is on</p> <ul> <li>Understanding Vultron, which describes the protocol in detail</li> <li>Implementing Vultron, which provides guidance for potential implementations of Vultron</li> <li>Reference, which provides the formal protocol specification</li> </ul> <p>The Vultron Protocol is a research project to explore the creation of a federated, decentralized, and open source protocol for coordinated vulnerability disclosure (CVD). It has grown out of the CERT/CC's decades of experience in coordinating global response to software vulnerabilities. Our goal is to create a protocol that can be used by any organization to coordinate the disclosure of vulnerabilities in information processing systems (software, hardware, services, etc.), and to build a community of interoperability across independent organizations, processes, and policies that can work together to coordinate appropriate responses to vulnerabilities.</p> <p>The Vultron Protocol is a collection of ideas, models, code, and work in progress, and is not yet ready for production use.</p> <p>Current Version</p> <p>The current version of the Vultron Protocol is</p> <p>2024.4.4.dev1598+g2cfa08f9.d20260505</p>"},{"location":"#how-this-documentation-is-organized","title":"How this documentation is organized","text":"<p>We are in the process of documenting the Vultron CVD Protocol as we work towards a prototype implementation. We are using the Di\u00e1taxis Framework to organize our documentation into four main categories, oriented around the different ways that people might need to learn about and use the Vultron Protocol.</p> <p>Our current focus is on the Understanding Vultron section, which describes the protocol in detail.</p> <ul> <li> <p> Learning About Vultron</p> <p>The Learning Vultron section is intended to eventually include tutorials and other information about the Vultron Protocol that is oriented towards novice users and getting started with the protocol. However, because we are still in the early stages of the project, this section is just a placeholder for now.</p> <p> Learning Vultron</p> </li> <li> <p> Understanding Vultron</p> <p>The Understanding Vultron section includes background information about Vultron, including the motivation for the project, the problem space that we are trying to address, and the design principles that we are using to guide our work. It also includes a detailed description of the Vultron Protocol, including the state machines and behavior logic that we use to model the behavior of the protocol.</p> <p>Focus on your content and generate a responsive and searchable static site</p> <p> Understanding Vultron</p> </li> <li> <p> Implementing Vultron</p> <p>The Implementing Vultron section includes guidance for potential implementations of Vultron. In the future, we plan to include how-to guides to help you use Vultron, but for now it is focused on guidance for potential implementers of Vultron.</p> <p>Change the colors, fonts, language, icons, logo and more with a few lines</p> <p> Implementing Vultron</p> </li> <li> <p> Reference</p> <p>The Reference section includes the formal Vultron Protocol specification, crosswalks the protocol with other related standards and protocols, etc. In the future, we plan to include other reference information about Vultron, including code documentation.</p> <p> Reference</p> </li> </ul>"},{"location":"#background","title":"Background","text":"<p>The Vultron Protocol is a continuation of the CERT/CC's work on improving the coordination of vulnerability disclosure and response. Our previous work in this area includes:</p> <ul> <li>The CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure</li> <li>Prioritizing Vulnerability Response: A Stakeholder-Specific Vulnerability Categorization (SSVC) (Version 1.0, Version 2.0, github)</li> <li>The Vulnerability Information and Coordination Environment   (VINCE)   (blog post,   github)</li> </ul> <p>along with a variety of related research, including</p> <ul> <li>Cybersecurity Information Sharing: Analysing an Email Corpus of Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (WEIS 2021)</li> <li>Historical Analysis of Exploit Availability Timelines (CSET 2020)</li> </ul> <p>More recently, the CERT/CC has been working towards formalizing this knowledge into a protocol for CVD. Our recent work in this area includes:</p> <ul> <li>A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (MPCVD), which also appeared in an abridged form as Are We Skillful or Just Lucky? Interpreting the Possible Histories of Vulnerability Disclosures in the ACM Journal Digital Threats: Research and Practice</li> <li>A collection of Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure User Stories derived from both our process modeling work and from the experience of building VINCE.   These user stories are collected in the User Stories section of this documentation.</li> <li>Designing Vultron: A Protocol for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (MPCVD),   which serves as the basis for the work contained here.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"_acronyms/","title":"Index","text":""},{"location":"about/acknowledgements/","title":"Acknowledgements","text":"<p>This work builds upon the following prior works:</p> Work Authors URL The CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure Allen D. We're not there yet.</p> <p>In the meantime, there are a number of other things that can help. We're looking for help with:</p>"},{"location":"about/faq/#how-do-we-apply-encryption-to-activitypub-messages-to-enable-end-to-end-encryption","title":"How do we apply encryption to ActivityPub messages to enable end-to-end encryption?","text":"<p>We're of the opinion that encrypted messaging is a feature that should be available to all users of ActivityPub, not just those using the Vultron Protocol to coordinate vulnerability cases. Therefore, our position is that end-to-end encryption would be better implemented as a layer on top of the ActivityPub protocol, rather than as a specific feature of the Vultron Protocol. From what we can tell, the topic has come up before in the ActivityPub community, but there is no standardized solution yet. We're currently focused on developing the Vultron Protocol, but we realize that encryption may be a requirement for widespread adoption, so we're keeping an eye on the topic.</p> <p>Some relevant links include:</p> <ul> <li>A November 2022 blog   post Towards End-to-End Encryption for Direct Messages in the Fediverse</li> <li>Issue #225 on the W3C ActivityPub Github repo talks about the   need for encrypted content, but it seems to have been closed without a solution in 2017.</li> <li>However, a much more       recent April 2023 comment       mentions:       &gt; If we restrict to direct message (with a single recipient) one may just encrypt the message (Note) payload using       the       public key of the recipient. So, if the recipient holds her own private key, she can decrypt the message payload.       The       main obstacle I see is the common practice to use HTTP signature, so that private kays must resides on server and       not       on clients.</li> <li>There have been numerous Discussions on the   ActivityPub SocialHub that mention end-to-end encryption.</li> </ul> <p>We are interested in any developments in this space, please let us know if you are aware of more recent developments. We'd be interested to know how we could help with efforts to bring encrypted messaging to ActivityPub.</p>"},{"location":"about/faq/#what-are-the-requirements-for-contributing","title":"What are the requirements for contributing?","text":"<p>See CONTRIBUTING</p>"},{"location":"about/license/","title":"Vultron Multiparty Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure Protocol Prototype","text":""},{"location":"about/license/#mit-sei","title":"MIT (SEI)","text":"<p>Copyright \u00a9 2025 Carnegie Mellon University and Contributors.</p> <ul> <li>See Contributors.txt for a full list of Contributors;</li> <li>See ContributionInstructions.txt for information on how you can Contribute to this project.</li> </ul> <p>All Rights Reserved.</p> <p>Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy of this software and associated documentation files (the \"Software\"), to deal in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:</p> <p>The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions of the Software.</p>"},{"location":"about/license/#acknowledgements-and-disclaimers","title":"ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS AND DISCLAIMERS","text":"<p>This program may include and/or can make use of certain third party source code, object code, documentation and other files (\"Third Party Software\"). We're using the expanded concept of an Any Decision Record (ADR) to capture any decision that is important to the project, not just architectural decisions. We use Markdown Any Decision Records (MADR) to document our architectural decisions.</p>"},{"location":"adr/#when-to-write-an-adr","title":"When to write an ADR","text":"<p>The primary signal for an ADR is evaluated alternatives: if you considered more than one option and rejected at least one, document the decision. The record preserves context for future maintainers who might otherwise re-open a settled question.</p> <p>Concretely, write an ADR when:</p> <ul> <li>You adopted a structural or architectural approach over one or more   alternatives (e.g., hexagonal architecture over layered, SQLModel over   TinyDB).</li> <li>You made a one-time process or tooling decision with lasting project-wide   impact (e.g., CalVer over SemVer, pinning CI action SHAs).</li> <li>A decision will be hard or costly to reverse, so the rationale should be   preserved explicitly.</li> </ul> <p>ADR vs. spec: an ADR records why a choice was made; a spec entry records what the system must do going forward. When a significant decision also generates recurring testable requirements, create both \u2014 see <code>notes/specs-vs-adrs.md</code> for the full delineation guidelines and worked examples.</p> <p>You do not need an ADR for:</p> <ul> <li>Uncontested conventions with no real alternatives (write a spec entry   instead).</li> <li>Small tactical choices where the rationale is obvious from the code.</li> </ul> <p>If you're unsure, err on the side of writing one \u2014 a brief ADR is better than losing context.</p>"},{"location":"adr/#how-to-write-an-adr","title":"How to write an ADR","text":"<p>For new ADRs, please use adr-template.md as basis. More information on MADR is available at https://adr.github.io/madr/. General information about architectural decision records is available at https://adr.github.io/.</p>"},{"location":"adr/#accepted-adrs","title":"Accepted ADRs","text":"<ul> <li>ADR-0000 Record Which format and structure should these records follow?</p>"},{"location":"adr/0001-use-markdown-any-decision-records/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>MADR 3.0.0 \u2013 The Markdown Any Decision Records</li> <li>Michael Nygard's template \u2013 The first incarnation of the term \"ADR\"</li> <li>Sustainable Architectural Decisions \u2013 The Y-Statements</li> <li>Other templates listed at https://github.com/joelparkerhenderson/architecture_decision_record</li> <li>Formless \u2013 No conventions for file format and structure</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0001-use-markdown-any-decision-records/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"MADR 3.0.0\", because</p> <ul> <li>Implicit assumptions should be made explicit.   Design documentation is important to enable people understanding the decisions later on.   See also A rational design process: How and why to fake it.</li> <li>MADR allows for structured capturing of any decision.</li> <li>The MADR format is lean and fits our development style.</li> <li>The MADR structure is comprehensible and facilitates usage &amp; maintenance.</li> <li>The MADR project is vivid.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/","title":"Model Processes with Behavior Trees","text":""},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The Vultron protocol is defined as a set of three interacting state machines for each agent:</p> <ul> <li>Report Management</li> <li>Embargo Management</li> <li>Case State Management</li> </ul> <p>We have a need to simulate the behavior of these state machines in order to test the protocol. However, the interactions between them can be complex, so we need a way to model those interactions.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Need to simulate complex interactions between state machines</li> <li>Need to explore and exercise state space of the protocol even if some states are rarely reached in practice</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Object-oriented Agent implementation</li> <li>Behavior Tree Agent implementation</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"Behavior Tree Agent implementation\", because behavior trees allow us to model complex interactions between state machines. By building in stochastic behaviors, they also allow us to explore and exercise the state space of the protocol even if some states are rarely reached in practice.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Behavior trees are a new technology for the team, so there will be a learning curve.</li> <li>Simulating changes to process logic should be easier with behavior trees than with object-oriented code.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#object-oriented-implementation","title":"Object-oriented implementation","text":"<p>Good, because:</p> <ul> <li>Standard OO python approach understood by the team</li> <li>State management could be implemented as a set of classes for each state machine</li> </ul> <p>Bad, because:</p> <ul> <li>Complex interactions between state machines would be difficult to model and maintain</li> <li>Reactive behaviors (e.g., based on state changes in outside world) would be difficult to model</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0002-model-processes-with-behavior-trees/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>Michele Colledanchise, Petter \u00d6gren: Behavior Trees in Robotics and AI book @ Amazon pdf @ arXiv</li> <li>Petter \u00d6gren's YouTube channel   has a number of good videos on Behavior Trees</li> <li>Wikipedia Behavior Tree (artificial intelligence, robotics and control)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/","title":"Build our own Behavior Tree engine in Python","text":""},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>We need a Behavior Tree engine to support our Behavior Tree Agent implementation.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Need to support stochastic behaviors</li> <li>Team is most familiar with Python</li> <li>Need to support quick prototyping and experimentation</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>BehaviorTree.CPP</li> <li>py_trees</li> <li>Build our own</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"Build our own\", because</p> <ul> <li>It's a good opportunity to learn about Behavior Trees</li> <li>py_trees is closely tied to robot operating system (ROS), so it might not be a good fit for our use case</li> <li>BehaviorTree.CPP is written in C++, so it would be difficult to integrate with the rest of our Python codebase</li> </ul> <p>This decision can/should be revisited if</p> <ul> <li>the complexity of building our own Behavior Tree engine becomes too high</li> <li>we discover that existing Behavior Tree engines already support features that would be difficult for us to build</li> </ul> <p>In order to mitigate the risk of building our own Behavior Tree engine, we will maintain a modular design that allows us to swap out our engine for an existing engine if necessary.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<p>Good, because:</p> <ul> <li>Provides an opportunity to learn more about Behavior Trees</li> <li>We can build it in Python, so it should be easy to integrate with the rest of our codebase</li> <li>We can build it in a way that supports stochastic behaviors</li> <li>We can customize it to our needs</li> </ul> <p>Neutral, because:</p> <ul> <li>We might discover that we need features that are already supported by existing Behavior Tree engines</li> <li>We might find that other Behavior Tree engines are better suited to our needs</li> </ul> <p>Bad, because:</p> <ul> <li>We have to build and maintain our own Behavior Tree engine</li> <li>It's another dependency we have to own and maintain</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#behaviortreecpp","title":"BehaviorTree.CPP","text":"<p>Good, because:</p> <ul> <li>It's a mature, well-tested Behavior Tree engine</li> </ul> <p>Bad, because:</p> <ul> <li>It's written in C++, so it would be difficult to integrate with the rest of our Python codebase</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#py_trees","title":"py_trees","text":"<p>Good, because:</p> <ul> <li>It's written in Python, so it should be easy to integrate with the rest of our codebase</li> <li>It seems to be a mature, well-tested Behavior Tree engine</li> </ul> <p>Neutral-to-bad, because:</p> <ul> <li>It's closely tied to robot operating system (ROS), so it might not be a good fit for our use case</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0003-build-custom-python-bt-engine/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>BehaviorTree.CPP GitHub</li> <li>py_trees GitHub</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0004-use-factory-methods-for-common-bt-node-types/","title":"Use factory methods for common BT node node types that are used in multiple trees.  We want to be able to create these nodes in a consistent way, and we want to be able to easily change the implementation of these nodes without having to change the code that creates them.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0004-use-factory-methods-for-common-bt-node-types/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>We want to be able to create these nodes in a consistent them.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0004-use-factory-methods-for-common-bt-node-types/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<p>Good because:</p> <ul> <li>retains flexibility to change the underlying place to maintain the factory methods</li> </ul> <p>Bad because:</p> <ul> <li>less pythonic than just subclassing the BT node types</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0004-use-factory-methods-for-common-bt-node-types/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the <p>Bad because:</p> <ul> <li>Harder to enforce consistency in how the nodes are created</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0004-use-factory-methods-for-common-bt-node-types/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<p>This decision was inspired in part by the <code>py_trees</code> documentation (in the context of composite nodes):</p> <p>Don\u2019t subclass merely to auto-populate it, build a create__subtree() library instead <p>Which got us thinking about using factory methods to help maintain a clean separation between the <code>vultron.bt.base</code> module and the things that live above it.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/","title":"Use ActivityStreams Vocabulary as the basis for Vultron Message Formats","text":""},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The Vultron protocol specifies a number of required message types. These message types are used to communicate state changes and other information between Vultron agents. The message types are defined in the formal Vultron protocol specification.</p> <p>However, the protocol specification does not define the message format. The message format is the structure of the message itself.</p> <p>What format should be used for Vultron messages?</p>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>We need to prototype the Vultron protocol as runnable code.</li> <li>We need for Vultron prototype instances to be able to communicate with each   other.</li> <li>We would prefer to use an existing message format rather than invent our own.</li> <li>We would prefer to use a message format that is</li> <li>extensible</li> <li>naturally adaptable to the needs of the Vultron protocol</li> <li>well understood</li> <li>well supported</li> <li>widely used</li> <li>well documented</li> <li>easy to implement</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>JSON</li> <li>ActivityStreams Vocabulary</li> <li>JSON-LD</li> <li>Protocol Buffers</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"ActivityStreams Vocabulary\", because it most closely matches the semantics of the Vultron protocol, is well supported, and is easy to implement.</p> <p>ActivityStreams Vocabulary is a vocabulary for describing social interactions. It builds semantics on top of JSON-LD, which builds semantics on top of JSON.</p> <p>ActivityStreams Vocabulary is foundational to the ActivityPub protocol, which is the basis for the Fediverse. In turn, ActivityPub and its underlying ActivityStreams Vocabulary, is widely used in the Fediverse, which is a collection of social networking systems that use the same underlying protocol (e.g., Mastodon and others). We anticipate that, given widespread adoption of ActivityStreams Vocabulary as part of the Fediverse, it will be easier to find libraries and tool implementations that support it.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>For our prototype, we will use   the ActivityStreams Vocabulary   as the basis for our message format.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#pros-and-cons","title":"Pros and Cons","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because it is well supported and easy to implement</li> <li>Good, because it provides rich semantics that are well suited to the Vultron   protocol</li> <li>Good, because it is widely used and well documented</li> <li>Neutral, because its broader success is tied to the success of the Fediverse</li> <li>Bad, because it is not as widely used as JSON, JSON-LD, or Protocol Buffers</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#json","title":"JSON","text":"<p>JSON is a reasonable serialization format for web-based APIs. However, from our perspective, it merely provides the serialization syntax but is not expected to provide any semantics. This means that any semantics that we want to use in the Vultron protocol would have to be defined in software that implements the protocol, and that would make Vultron more bespoke and potentially difficult to integrate with other systems.</p> <ul> <li>Good, because it is well supported and easy to implement</li> <li>Bad, because it does not provide any semantics</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#json-ld","title":"JSON-LD","text":"<p>JSON-LD adds data linking semantics to JSON. This is a step in the right direction, but does not go as far as we would like.</p> <ul> <li>Good, because it is well supported and easy to implement</li> <li>Bad, because it does not provide the rich semantics that we would like</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#protocol-buffers","title":"Protocol Buffers","text":"<p>Protocol Buffers is a binary serialization format that is well suited to high-performance applications. However, it is not well suited to the Vultron protocol, which has no specific performance requirements. In addition, like pure JSON, Protocol Buffers does not provide any semantics, so we would have to define our own semantics on top of it.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0005-activitystreams-vocabulary-as-vultron-message-format/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>ActivityStreams Vocabulary</li> <li>ActivityPub</li> <li>Fediverse</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/","title":"Vultron Project Versioning","text":""},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>We need to delineate a versioning strategy for the Vultron project to ensure that we can manage changes to the protocol and its implementations effectively.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Release of new documentation</li> <li>Changes to the protocol</li> <li>Changes to the prototype implementation</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Semantic Versioning with Major.Minor.Patch</li> <li>Calendar Versioning with YYYY.M.Patch</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"Calendar Versioning\", because we are still in the early stages of the project and are not yet ready to commit to a stable API. Most of the project consists of documentation and prototypes, so we can use the date to indicate the version.</p> <p>We will use the format YYYY.MM.Patch, where:</p> <ul> <li>YYYY is the year of the most recent non-patch release (four digits)</li> <li>MM is the month of the most recent non-patch release (no zero padding, e.g., 1, 2, 3, ..., 12)</li> <li>Patch is the patch number for that release (default to 0 for the first release in a month, omit for non-patch releases)</li> </ul> <p>Version increments will be as follows:</p> <ul> <li>Significant releases will use the current year and month, with the patch number starting at 0 (normally omitted)</li> <li>Small changes will increment the patch number from the most recent release, even if it is a later month or year</li> </ul> <p>Examples:</p> <ul> <li>The first significant release in April 2024 will be 2024.4.0 (shortened 2024.4)</li> <li>The third small update to v2024.4 will be 2024.4.3, even if it is released in May 2024 or later.</li> <li>A subsequent significant release in September 2024 would be 2024.9.0 (shortened 2024.9)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because we can easily track the progress of the project and the changes to the protocol and its implementations.</li> <li>Good, because we can still communicate the relative importance of versions</li> <li>Bad, because we may need to change the versioning strategy if we decide to commit to a stable API in the future</li> <li>Neutral, because the versioning strategy is not as widely recognized as semantic versioning, but it is still   a common practice for projects that are more complex than a simple library or tool.'</li> <li>Neutral, because we are not committing to either a release schedule or a stable API at this time.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#semantic-versioning","title":"Semantic Versioning","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because it is widely recognized and understood</li> <li>Good, because it can communicate the relative importance of versions and the impact of changes</li> <li>Bad, because it may not be appropriate for projects like Vultron that are still in the early stages of implementation</li> <li>Neutral, because pre-1.0 versions lose granularity in the version number, having only a minor and patch number</li> <li>Neutral, because it is not as well-suited for projects that are primarily documentation and prototypes</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0006-use-calver-for-project-versioning/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>Semantic Versioning</li> <li>Calendar Versioning</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/","title":"Introduce a Behavior Dispatcher Between Inbox Handling and Behavior Execution","text":""},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>Vultron actors are implemented as ActivityPub actors with inboxes and outboxes. Inbound messages are ActivityStreams objects, typically Activities that include domain-specific objects (e.g., Vulnerability Reports).</p> <p>Early prototypes implemented inbound message handling using a layered routing model:</p> <ul> <li>Inbox handler</li> <li>Activity-type handler</li> <li>Object-type handler</li> <li>Downstream \u201cwork\u201d execution</li> </ul> <p>This approach introduced several problems:</p> <ul> <li>Loss of semantic cohesion: separating activity handling from object handling discarded context required to determine correct behavior; downstream workers had to reconstruct state already known at receipt time.</li> <li>Poor alignment with existing behavior logic: Vultron already defines message types as (Activity Type, Object Type) tuples, with corresponding behavior trees defined in the protocol simulator. The routing model obscured this mapping.</li> <li>Tight coupling of protocol handling and execution: the inbox handler effectively controlled execution flow, making asynchronous or distributed execution difficult.</li> <li>Deployment inflexibility: the design did not cleanly support both local development (\u201cgit clone + docker-compose up\u201d) and integration into environments with job queues or workers.</li> </ul> <p>These issues indicated a missing architectural boundary between protocol handling and behavior execution.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Preserve semantic context when invoking behavior</li> <li>Align runtime execution with existing message-type semantics</li> <li>Support both simple local deployments and scalable asynchronous architectures</li> <li>Improve testability and separation of concerns</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Keep layered routing inside the inbox handler (current approach)</li> <li>Introduce a Behavior Dispatcher abstraction between inbox and behavior execution</li> <li>Mandate a specific queuing/pubsub system for dispatching (rejected as non-goal)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"Introduce a Behavior Dispatcher abstraction between inbox handling and behavior execution.\"</p> <p>Justification:</p> <ul> <li>Restores a clear execution boundary that preserves semantics determined at receipt time.</li> <li>Keeps behavior trees message-type-specific and implementation-agnostic with respect to invocation mechanics.</li> <li>Enables multiple implementations (in-process direct invocation or queued/asynchronous invocation), supporting both local development and distributed deployments.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good:</li> <li>Removes redundant activity/object routing layers</li> <li>Preserves semantic context at behavior invocation time</li> <li>Aligns runtime execution with existing Vultron message-type semantics</li> <li>Enables both synchronous and asynchronous execution models</li> <li>Supports both local development and scalable deployments</li> <li>Improves testability by isolating behavior trees</li> <li>Bad / Tradeoffs:</li> <li>Introduces an additional abstraction layer</li> <li>Requires explicit dispatcher interface definition and implementations</li> <li>Asynchronous implementations require explicit failure and retry semantics</li> <li>Persistence ordering (write-on-receipt vs behavior-owned writes) must be chosen deliberately</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li>Code and API reviews of dispatcher interface and implementations</li> <li>Unit and integration tests exercising both direct and queued dispatcher implementations</li> <li>End-to-end tests in local and queued deployment scenarios to validate preservation of message-type semantics and observable behavior</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#keep-layered-routing-inside-inbox-handler","title":"Keep layered routing inside inbox handler","text":"<ul> <li>Good: minimal initial change; behavior executes immediately</li> <li>Bad: loses semantic context, couples protocol handling and execution, reduces deployment flexibility and testability</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#introduce-behavior-dispatcher-chosen","title":"Introduce Behavior Dispatcher (chosen)","text":"<ul> <li>Good: separates concerns, preserves context, supports multiple execution models, improves testability</li> <li>Bad: adds an interface and implementation work; requires decisions on persistence semantics for some flows</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#mandate-a-specific-queuepubsub-system","title":"Mandate a specific queue/pubsub system","text":"<ul> <li>Good: can simplify an asynchronous reference implementation</li> <li>Bad: violates non-goals by specifying infrastructure; reduces portability and increases coupling</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#non-goals","title":"Non-Goals","text":"<ul> <li>This ADR does not specify a particular queue, pub/sub system, or infrastructure provider.</li> <li>This ADR does not define federation or delivery mechanics between actors.</li> <li>This ADR does not mandate a specific persistence strategy.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0007-use-behavior-dispatcher/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>This ADR reframes the original design impasse as a missing execution boundary rather than a protocol or domain modeling issue. The Behavior Dispatcher serves as that boundary, allowing Vultron\u2019s protocol-facing components and behavior logic to evolve independently.</li> <li>Related ADRs: refer to docs/adr for other architectural records and the ADR template at docs/adr/_adr-template.md.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/","title":"Use py_trees for Behavior Tree Execution in Handler Integration","text":""},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>ADR-0002 establishes that Vultron models CVD processes as behavior trees (BTs), and ADR-0007 introduces a behavior dispatcher between inbox handling and behavior execution. The <code>vultron/bt/</code> module implements a custom BT engine tailored to Vultron's simulation needs. As the prototype API v2 handlers evolve, complex workflows (e.g., report validation) require structured orchestration beyond procedural code.</p> <p>The question is: which BT library should be used for handler-level BT execution in the prototype API?</p>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Need a mature BT library with standard semantics (Sequence, Fallback,   Condition, Action)</li> <li>Must support blackboard state management for multi-node data passing</li> <li>Visualization and debugging support is highly desirable</li> <li>The <code>vultron/bt/</code> custom engine is optimized for simulation; it should not   be modified or reused for API handler execution</li> <li>Must be compatible with Python 3.12+ and FastAPI's synchronous handler model</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Use the existing <code>vultron/bt/</code> custom engine for handler BT execution</li> <li>Introduce <code>py_trees</code> (v2.2.0+) as the handler-level BT library</li> <li>Implement ad-hoc procedural logic in handlers without a BT library</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: \"Introduce <code>py_trees</code> as the handler-level BT library.\"</p> <p>Justification:</p> <ul> <li><code>py_trees</code> provides standard BT semantics, blackboard state management,   and tree visualization \u2014 all needed for handler-level execution.</li> <li>Reusing <code>vultron/bt/</code> would couple simulation internals to the API runtime   and risk breaking existing simulation code.</li> <li>Procedural logic in handlers is workable for simple cases but grows   unwieldy for complex, multi-step workflows; BTs provide structured   composition.</li> <li><code>py_trees</code> is a mature, well-tested library used in robotics and AI; it   aligns with the BT literature referenced in ADR-0002.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good:</li> <li>Standard BT semantics (SUCCESS/FAILURE/RUNNING) align with spec     requirements</li> <li>Built-in blackboard management simplifies inter-node state passing</li> <li><code>py_trees.display</code> utilities provide tree visualization for debugging</li> <li>Clean separation: <code>vultron/bt/</code> remains the simulation engine;     <code>vultron/behaviors/</code> uses <code>py_trees</code> for API handler execution</li> <li>Proof-of-concept validated in Phase BT-1: P99 execution time &lt; 1ms</li> <li>Bad / Tradeoffs:</li> <li>Adds an external dependency (<code>py_trees ^2.2.0</code>)</li> <li>Introduces a bridge layer (<code>vultron/behaviors/bridge.py</code>) to adapt     <code>py_trees</code> execution context to Vultron's DataLayer and handler protocol</li> <li>Blackboard key naming constraints (no slashes; use simplified keys)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li>Phase BT-1 proof-of-concept: <code>validate_report</code> handler refactored to use   <code>py_trees</code> BT execution via <code>BTBridge</code></li> <li>78 BT tests passing in <code>test/behaviors/</code></li> <li>Performance baseline: P50=0.44ms, P95=0.69ms, P99=0.84ms (well within   100ms target)</li> <li>All 456 existing tests continue to pass (no regressions)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0008-use-py-trees-for-handler-bt-integration/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li><code>vultron/behaviors/bridge.py</code>: <code>BTBridge</code> adapter class</li> <li><code>vultron/behaviors/helpers.py</code>: DataLayer-aware base node classes</li> <li><code>vultron/behaviors/report/</code>: Report validation BT implementation</li> <li><code>specs/behavior-tree-integration.yaml</code>: BT integration requirements</li> <li>Related ADRs: ADR-0002 (BT rationale), ADR-0003 (custom engine retained   for simulation), ADR-0007 (dispatcher architecture)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/","title":"Adopt Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters) for Vultron","text":""},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The Vultron codebase grew incrementally from a prototype, causing concerns from different layers to accumulate in the same modules. ActivityStreams 2.0 (AS2) structural types leaked into domain logic; semantic extraction was scattered across multiple files; handler functions instantiated the persistence layer directly; and the root <code>vultron/</code> package mixed domain enums with wire-format enums. An architectural review (<code>archived_notes/architecture-review.md</code>) catalogued twelve violations of the intended separation of concerns, ranging in severity from minor to critical. A clear architecture decision was needed to guide remediation and prevent the same violations from re-accumulating.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Wire format replaceability: AS2 is the current wire format because its   vocabulary matches Vultron's domain vocabulary. That match must not collapse   the boundary between wire concerns and domain concerns.  If AS2 is replaced   or extended, the change must be absorbable in the wire layer alone.</li> <li>Testability: Core domain logic should be testable with plain domain   objects, without constructing AS2 activities or making HTTP requests.</li> <li>Single extraction seam: The mapping from AS2 vocabulary to domain   semantics must live in exactly one place so that future wire format changes   have a single point of entry.</li> <li>Dependency injection over direct instantiation: Driven adapters   (persistence, delivery) must be injectable to support testing and alternate   backends.</li> <li>Alignment with existing clean points: Several existing patterns are   already architecturally sound (the <code>DataLayer</code> Protocol, <code>DispatchEvent</code>   wrapper, <code>MessageSemantics</code> enum vocabulary, FastAPI 202 + BackgroundTasks)   and should be preserved.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Continue organic layering \u2014 Impose light conventions without a formal   architecture pattern. Enforce import rules via linting. Let the structure   evolve.</li> <li>Adopt hexagonal architecture (Ports and Adapters) \u2014 Define explicit   layers (<code>core/</code>, <code>wire/</code>, <code>adapters/</code>), ports (abstract interfaces), and   adapters (thin translation layers). Use a separate wire layer for AS2   parsing and semantic extraction.</li> <li>Clean layered architecture (no hexagon) \u2014 Impose strict top-down   layering (controller \u2192 service \u2192 repository) without the explicit   driving/driven adapter distinction.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Adopt hexagonal architecture (Ports and Adapters), because it directly addresses the identified violations, provides the single extraction seam the project needs, and cleanly separates the wire format from the domain \u2014 a property that is especially important given that AS2 and the Vultron domain vocabulary are semantically isomorphic and will otherwise naturally collapse.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#layer-model","title":"Layer Model","text":"<p>Four layers are defined:</p> <p><code>text wire (AS2 JSON)     \u2193 AS2 Parser          (vultron/wire/as2/parser.py)     \u2193 Semantic Extractor  (vultron/wire/as2/extractor.py)     \u2193 Handler Dispatch    (vultron/behavior_dispatcher.py)     \u2193 Domain Logic        (vultron/core/ \u2014 no AS2 awareness)</code></p> <p>The <code>wire/</code> package is structurally separate from both <code>core/</code> and <code>adapters/</code> to reflect its status as a distinct inbound pipeline stage, not an adapter of a particular external system.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#key-rules","title":"Key Rules","text":"<ol> <li><code>core/</code> MUST NOT import from <code>wire/</code>, <code>adapters/</code>, or any framework.</li> <li><code>core/</code> functions MUST accept and return domain types, not AS2 types.</li> <li><code>MessageSemantics</code> (<code>SemanticIntent</code>) is a domain type, defined in    <code>core/</code>.</li> <li>The semantic extractor is the only place AS2 vocabulary is mapped to    domain concepts.</li> <li>Driven adapters are injected via port interfaces, never instantiated    directly.</li> <li>The <code>wire/</code> layer is replaceable as a unit without touching <code>core/</code>.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because domain logic is testable with plain Pydantic objects and no   AS2 or HTTP dependencies.</li> <li>Good, because a future wire format (binary encoding, revised federation   standard) can be introduced by adding <code>wire/new_protocol/</code> without touching   <code>core/</code> or <code>adapters/</code>.</li> <li>Good, because the single extraction seam (<code>wire/as2/extractor.py</code>) means   new activity types require exactly one file change to add a new pattern.</li> <li>Good, because dependency injection via port interfaces enables alternative   backends (in-memory, TinyDB, PostgreSQL) to be swapped without modifying   handler code.</li> <li>Bad, because the transition from the organic prototype structure to the   hexagonal layout requires incremental refactoring across multiple modules.</li> <li>Bad, because the semantic match between AS2 vocabulary and the Vultron   domain vocabulary means some violations are subtle and may re-accumulate   without active review.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li>Violations V-01 through V-12 (see <code>archived_notes/architecture-review.md</code>)   serve as the acceptance criteria: each is tracked and remediated   incrementally.</li> <li>The review checklist in <code>notes/architecture-ports-and-adapters.md</code>   (Core, Wire, Adapters, Connectors, Tests sections) is applied during code   review.</li> <li>Import boundary rules are enforced by code review and, where practical,   by <code>pylint</code>/<code>flake8</code> import order checks.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0009-hexagonal-architecture/#violation-inventory-and-remediation-status","title":"Violation Inventory and Remediation Status","text":"<p>The architectural review identified incrementally.</li> <li>Bad: violations re-accumulate naturally because AS2 and domain vocabulary   look identical; without a formal pattern, there is no shared vocabulary for   \"this is a <code>DispatchEvent</code> wrapper and <code>ActivityDispatcher</code> Protocol that this     architecture refines.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/","title":"Standardize Object IDs to URI Form","text":""},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The Vultron ActivityStreams 2.0 (AS2) object model requires every object to have an <code>id</code> field. The <code>generate_new_id()</code> utility in <code>vultron/wire/as2/vocab/base/utils.py</code> currently returns a bare UUID-4 string (e.g., <code>2196cbb2-fb6f-407c-b473-1ed8ae806578</code>) as the default <code>as_id</code> for new objects. The AS2 specification and the Vultron object-IDs spec (<code>specs/object-ids.yaml</code> OID-01-001) require that <code>id</code> values be full URI identifiers. Bare UUIDs are not valid AS2 identifiers and will cause interoperability problems once federation is implemented.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>AS2 compliance: ActivityStreams 2.0 requires <code>id</code> to be an absolute   IRI. Bare UUIDs are not IRIs.</li> <li>Federation readiness: Federated delivery requires globally unique,   resolvable identifiers. Bare UUIDs cannot be resolved across systems.</li> <li>Blackboard key safety: py_trees blackboard keys derived from full-URI   IDs must not use the raw URI (which contains slashes). Using the last path   segment or UUID suffix as the key avoids hierarchical key-parsing issues   (OID-03-001).</li> <li>DataLayer compatibility: The DataLayer uses <code>as_id</code> as the primary   lookup key. Changing the default ID format is a forward-looking migration;   any existing bare-UUID records are a prototype artifact.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ol> <li><code>urn:uuid:</code> prefix \u2014 <code>urn:uuid:{uuid4}</code> (e.g.,    <code>urn:uuid:2196cbb2-fb6f-407c-b473-1ed8ae806578</code>)</li> <li>Configurable HTTPS base URL \u2014 <code>https://{base}/{type}/{uuid4}</code> driven    by a <code>VULTRON_BASE_URL</code> environment variable</li> <li>Continue using bare UUIDs \u2014 defer the change</li> </ol>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Configurable HTTPS base URL with <code>urn:uuid:</code> fallback, because:</p> <ul> <li>HTTPS URLs are the canonical AS2 <code>id</code> form and are directly resolvable.</li> <li>A configurable base URL (via <code>VULTRON_BASE_URL</code> env var) allows deployment   flexibility without hard-coding a hostname.</li> <li>When <code>VULTRON_BASE_URL</code> is not set, the helper defaults to <code>urn:uuid:</code>   form, which is a valid absolute IRI and avoids any need for a running HTTP   server during development and testing.</li> <li>The <code>make_id()</code> helper in <code>vultron/api/v2/data/utils.py</code> (which already   uses HTTPS base-URL form) becomes the shared canonical ID factory for all   layers, satisfying OID-01-004.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good: all newly created objects carry globally unique, AS2-compliant <code>id</code>   values.</li> <li>Good: <code>VULTRON_BASE_URL</code> enables environment-specific ID namespaces   (dev / staging / production) without code changes.</li> <li>Good: <code>urn:uuid:</code> default keeps development and test setups self-contained.</li> <li>Neutral: existing bare-UUID records in prototype data stores remain usable   during the prototype phase; a migration script is not required until   production deployment (OID-04-002, PROTO-01-001).</li> <li>Bad: <code>generate_new_id()</code> now produces IDs that contain colons, which must   not be used directly as py_trees blackboard keys (use the UUID suffix   portion only \u2014 see OID-03-001).</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li>Unit tests in <code>test/wire/as2/vocab/base/test_utils.py</code> assert that   <code>generate_new_id()</code> returns a value starting with <code>urn:uuid:</code> or a   configured HTTPS prefix.</li> <li>Unit tests in <code>test/api/v2/data/test_utils.py</code> confirm that <code>make_id()</code>   produces HTTPS-prefixed IDs and that <code>BASE_URL</code> is read from   <code>VULTRON_BASE_URL</code> when set.</li> <li>Code review: no new code should assign a bare UUID directly to <code>as_id</code>   without wrapping it in a URI prefix.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#urnuuid-prefix","title":"<code>urn:uuid:</code> prefix","text":"<ul> <li>Good: simple \u2014 no environment configuration required.</li> <li>Good: valid absolute IRI; accepted by AS2 parsers.</li> <li>Good: no slashes in the ID, so the full ID can be used as a path segment   without percent-encoding.</li> <li>Neutral: not directly HTTP-resolvable.</li> <li>Bad: does not support the future federation model where IDs are resolvable   URLs on actor servers.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#configurable-https-base-url","title":"Configurable HTTPS base URL","text":"<ul> <li>Good: IDs are HTTP-resolvable, matching the ActivityPub federation model.</li> <li>Good: per-environment namespacing via <code>VULTRON_BASE_URL</code>.</li> <li>Bad: IDs contain slashes; API routes must URL-encode or use query   parameters for ID-based lookups.</li> <li>Bad: requires a configured hostname to produce meaningful IDs.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#continue-using-bare-uuids","title":"Continue using bare UUIDs","text":"<ul> <li>Good: no code changes required.</li> <li>Bad: violates AS2 spec; blocks federation.</li> <li>Bad: violates <code>specs/object-ids.yaml</code> OID-01-001.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0010-standardize-object-ids/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li><code>specs/object-ids.yaml</code> \u2014 normative requirements OID-01 through OID-04.</li> <li><code>notes/codebase-structure.md</code> \u2014 \"Technical Debt: Object IDs Should Be   URL-Like, Not Bare UUIDs\" section.</li> <li><code>plan/IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md</code> \u2014 domain logic.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/","title":"Remove API v1 and consolidate vocabulary examples into API v2","text":""},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The project has two API versions mounted side-by-side: <code>api/v1</code> (at <code>/api/v1</code>) and <code>api/v2</code> (at <code>/api/v2</code>). The <code>api/v1</code> package consists entirely of stub endpoints that return <code>vocab_examples</code> objects and have no business logic. All real protocol behaviour lives in <code>api/v2</code>. The v1 stubs create confusion about which API to use and add dead code to the project.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Reduce dead code and confusion about which API version is authoritative.</li> <li>Keep the vocabulary showcase / validation capability that v1 provided.</li> <li>Align with the hexagonal architecture where <code>api/v2</code> is the single driving   adapter for HTTP.</li> <li>Simplify <code>vultron/api/main.py</code> by removing the v1 sub-application mount.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Option A: Keep <code>api/v1</code> as-is with a clear \"vocabulary showcase\" label.</li> <li>Option B: Migrate the <code>/examples/</code> endpoints from v1 into v2 as a   <code>/examples/</code> subrouter, then remove v1.</li> <li>Option C: Deprecate and remove v1 with no migration of its endpoints.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Option B \u2014 migrate the vocabulary-showcase endpoints into the existing <code>api/v2/routers/examples.py</code> router and delete the <code>api/v1</code> package.</p> <p>The v2 examples router already covers <code>actors</code> and <code>notes</code>. It is extended to include <code>reports</code>, <code>cases</code>, <code>cases/statuses</code>, <code>cases/participants</code>, <code>cases/participants/statuses</code>, and <code>cases/embargoes</code> from v1. All other v1 stub routes (CRUD-style operations on actors, reports, cases, etc.) are removed without replacement because they are fully superseded by the inbox-based protocol implementation in v2.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because the codebase no longer contains ~1 000 lines of stubs.</li> <li>Good, because <code>/api/v2/examples/</code> provides a single place to explore and   validate vocabulary objects.</li> <li>Good, because <code>vultron/api/main.py</code> no longer imports or mounts the v1 app.</li> <li>Bad, because any client that relied on <code>/api/v1/*</code> endpoints will need to   update to <code>/api/v2/examples/*</code> (for the examples) or to the real v2   protocol endpoints.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0011-remove-api-v1/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li><code>uv run pytest --tb=short</code> passes with no regressions.</li> <li><code>GET /api/v2/examples/reports</code>, <code>/api/v2/examples/cases</code>, etc. return   valid example objects.</li> <li><code>POST</code> to each examples endpoint validates an object through Pydantic.</li> <li>The <code>vultron/api/v1/</code> directory no longer exists.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/","title":"Per-Actor DataLayer Isolation","text":""},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>All actors currently share a singleton <code>TinyDbDataLayer</code> backed by a single <code>plan/mydb.json</code> file. This violates <code>specs/case-management.yaml</code> CM-01-001 (actor isolation): Actor A can read and mutate Actor B's records.</p> <p>PRIORITY 100 requires each actor to have an isolated data store so that actors can only interact through the Vultron Protocol (ActivityStreams messages), not through shared state.</p> <p>Three related design decisions must be made before implementation can begin:</p> <ol> <li>DataLayer isolation strategy \u2014 how to partition data per actor.</li> <li><code>get_datalayer</code> FastAPI DI strategy \u2014 how to inject a per-actor    DataLayer into route handlers given the current zero-argument singleton    factory pattern.</li> <li><code>actor_io.py</code> inbox/outbox ownership \u2014 whether the in-memory    inbox/outbox store in <code>vultron/api/v2/data/actor_io.py</code> should migrate    into the per-actor DataLayer or remain as a separate adapter.</li> <li>OUTBOX-1 scope boundary \u2014 whether outbound delivery is in scope for    PRIORITY 100 or deferred.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Actors must not be able to read or mutate each other's state (CM-01-001).</li> <li>The <code>DataLayer</code> port/adapter separation must be preserved; core must not   depend on the backing store.</li> <li>The DI pattern must be consistent across all 20+ route files and trigger   endpoints; the chosen pattern must be simple to apply uniformly.</li> <li><code>actor_io.py</code> predates the DataLayer abstraction and is not per-actor   isolated; its role must be resolved to avoid creating a parallel state   management path.</li> <li>The prototype must continue to work with the existing Docker Compose   setup; a one-step migration to a robust production backing store is   desirable.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#datalayer-isolation-strategy","title":"DataLayer isolation strategy","text":"<ul> <li>Option A \u2014 One TinyDB file per actor (e.g., <code>{actor_id}.json</code>)</li> <li>Option B \u2014 Namespace prefix per actor in one TinyDB file (one table   per actor)</li> <li>Option C \u2014 In-memory DataLayer per actor (no persistence)</li> <li>Option D \u2014 MongoDB Community Edition per actor in Docker</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#get_datalayer-fastapi-di-strategy","title":"<code>get_datalayer</code> FastAPI DI strategy","text":"<ul> <li>DI-1 \u2014 Closure lambda: <code>Depends(lambda actor_id: get_datalayer(actor_id))</code></li> <li>DI-2 \u2014 Custom dependency class (<code>class GetDataLayer: def __call__(...)</code>)</li> <li>DI-3 \u2014 Explicit parameter threading (pass <code>actor_id</code> everywhere)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#actor_iopy-inboxoutbox-ownership","title":"<code>actor_io.py</code> inbox/outbox ownership","text":"<ul> <li>IO-A \u2014 Migrate inbox/outbox into per-actor DataLayer (TinyDB   collections <code>{actor_id}_inbox</code> / <code>{actor_id}_outbox</code>)</li> <li>IO-B \u2014 Formalize <code>actor_io.py</code> as a named in-process queue adapter   wired to the <code>ActivityEmitter</code> port, kept separate from DataLayer</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#outbox-1-scope-boundary","title":"OUTBOX-1 scope boundary","text":"<ul> <li>OX-A \u2014 Include local outbound delivery (OX-1.1\u2013OX-1.4) as part of   PRIORITY 100</li> <li>OX-B \u2014 Defer OUTBOX-1 until per-actor DataLayer isolation is proved   out; merge as a follow-on task after ACT-3</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>DataLayer isolation: Option B \u2014 TinyDB namespace prefix, with a concurrent MongoDB Community Edition implementation as the production-grade backing store.</p> <p>Option B mirrors the natural collection-per-actor pattern of MongoDB and requires only a modified <code>_table()</code> helper in <code>TinyDbDataLayer</code>. It keeps a single backing file, supports the multi-tenant and vendor scenarios described in <code>notes/domain-model-separation.md</code>, and makes the eventual TinyDB \u2192 MongoDB migration straightforward because both use the same logical namespace model.</p> <p>Option A creates many files and complicates the DataLayer reset endpoint used by demo scripts. Option C has no persistence and cannot be used for Docker demos. A pure MongoDB-first approach (Option D alone) is the right production target, but implementing it as the sole P100 step adds Docker infrastructure risk on the critical path.</p> <p><code>get_datalayer</code> DI strategy: DI-1 \u2014 closure lambda.</p> <p><code>Depends(lambda actor_id=Path(...): get_datalayer(actor_id))</code> is the simplest pattern, requires no new classes, and is consistent with FastAPI conventions. It supports future dynamic actor instantiation without architectural change. DI-2 adds unnecessary ceremony; DI-3 is too invasive across 20+ route files.</p> <p><code>actor_io.py</code> inbox/outbox ownership: IO-A \u2014 migrate into per-actor DataLayer.</p> <p>Inbox and outbox activities are persistent state. Storing them as TinyDB collections (<code>{actor_id}_inbox</code>, <code>{actor_id}_outbox</code>) alongside other per-actor records is architecturally consistent and avoids a parallel, in-memory-only state management path. Once the per-actor DataLayer is in place, <code>actor_io.py</code> can be removed (VCR-014).</p> <p><code>actor_io.py</code> is in <code>vultron/api/v2/data/</code>, predates the DataLayer abstraction, and does not persist to disk \u2014 all three are violations of the hexagonal architecture. IO-B formalizes the violation; IO-A removes it.</p> <p>OUTBOX-1 scope: OX-B \u2014 defer outbound delivery until ACT-3 is complete.</p> <p>The <code>ActivityEmitter</code> port stub (OX-1.0) and <code>DeliveryQueueAdapter</code> stub are already in place. Local delivery (OX-1.1\u2013OX-1.4) requires the per-actor DataLayer inbox collection to exist (IO-A decision above), so OX-1.1 MUST follow ACT-2. Deferring OUTBOX-1 until after ACT-3 keeps each task's scope clear and avoids implementing delivery against a still-changing DataLayer.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because per-actor TinyDB tables enforce data isolation with minimal   code change \u2014 only <code>TinyDbDataLayer.__init__</code> and <code>_table()</code> need updating.</li> <li>Good, because the closure lambda DI pattern is consistent, easy to audit,   and can be applied uniformly across all route files in one pass (ACT-3).</li> <li>Good, because migrating inbox/outbox into DataLayer eliminates <code>actor_io.py</code>   and the ACTOR_IO_STORE global (VCR-014 becomes unblocked after ACT-2).</li> <li>Good, because deferring OX-1.1 avoids delivery-against-a-moving-target risk.</li> <li>Neutral, because Option B (namespace prefix) is a prototype-grade solution;   the MongoDB backend is the production target and should be implemented as   part of the PRIORITY 100 / PRIORITY 300 transition.</li> <li>Bad, because every route file must be updated to pass <code>actor_id</code> to   <code>get_datalayer</code> (ACT-3 scope); this is a large but mechanical change.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li><code>uv run pytest --tb=short</code> passes after each of ACT-2 and ACT-3.</li> <li>Actor A's <code>dl.list()</code> does not return records belonging to Actor B.</li> <li><code>actor_io.py</code> is removed after ACT-2 (VCR-014 acceptance criterion met).</li> <li>All inbox/outbox entries written via <code>actor_io</code> are now retrievable via   the DataLayer inbox/outbox collections.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#option-a-one-tinydb-file-per-actor","title":"Option A \u2014 One TinyDB file per actor","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because data isolation is trivially complete (separate files).</li> <li>Bad, because it creates many files and complicates DataLayer reset.</li> <li>Bad, because it diverges from the MongoDB collection model, making   migration harder.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#option-b-namespace-prefix-per-actor-one-tinydb-file","title":"Option B \u2014 Namespace prefix per actor (one TinyDB file)","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because it mirrors MongoDB's collection-per-actor model.</li> <li>Good, because a single backing file is easier to manage in Docker demos.</li> <li>Good, because only <code>_table()</code> in <code>TinyDbDataLayer</code> needs updating.</li> <li>Neutral, because it is a prototype-grade isolation (not cryptographic   separation), but sufficient for the research prototype purpose.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#option-c-in-memory-datalayer-per-actor","title":"Option C \u2014 In-memory DataLayer per it.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#option-d-mongodb-community-edition-only","title":"Option D \u2014 MongoDB Community Edition only","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because it is the production-grade solution.</li> <li>Good, because each actor container can have its own MongoDB instance.</li> <li>Bad, because it adds Docker infrastructure complexity on the critical path.</li> <li>Neutral, because it is the intended production target; implementing   Option B first provides a bridge with low risk.</li> <li>Bad, because it adds boilerplate for a trivial transformation.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#di-3-explicit-parameter-threading","title":"DI-3 \u2014 Explicit parameter threading","text":"<ul> <li>Bad, because it requires invasive changes across all 20+ route files and   every function in the call chain.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#io-a-migrate-inboxoutbox-into-datalayer","title":"IO-A \u2014 Migrate inbox/outbox into DataLayer","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because it <li>Bad, because it creates two state management systems for actor data.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0012-per-actor-datalayer-isolation/#ox-a-outbox-1-in-scope-for-priority-100","title":"OX-A \u2014
