</ul>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/","title":"Unify RM State Tracking into Persisted VultronParticipantStatus Records","text":""},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The Report Management (RM) state machine spans two lifecycle phases with a pivot at <code>RM.VALID</code>:</p> <ul> <li>Report phase (START \u2192 RECEIVED \u2192 INVALID/VALID): Currently tracked in a   transient in-memory <code>STATUS</code> dict via <code>ReportStatus</code> objects in   <code>vultron.core.models.status</code>. This data is process-local and is lost on   restart.</li> <li>Case phase (VALID \u2192 ACCEPTED/DEFERRED \u2192 CLOSED): Currently tracked via   persisted <code>VultronParticipantStatus</code> / <code>ParticipantStatus</code> records appended   to <code>CaseParticipant.participant_statuses</code>.</li> </ul> <p>The two tracking mechanisms are disjoint. The pivot point \u2014 when a report transitions to <code>RM.VALID</code> and a case is created \u2014 is implemented ad hoc with no formal transition from the report-phase record to the case-phase record. New participant status records are seeded with <code>rm_state=RM.START</code> rather than carrying forward the current <code>RM.VALID</code> state.</p> <p>This creates a situation where:</p> <ul> <li>RM history for the pre-case phase is not queryable or auditable.</li> <li>RM transition guards on case-engagement cannot verify that a report was truly   valid before <code>ACCEPTED</code>/<code>DEFERRED</code> was appended.</li> <li>The RM machine's <code>RECEIVE</code> trigger (START \u2192 RECEIVED) is never explicitly   executed anywhere in the <code>vultron.core</code> pipeline.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Protocol correctness: RM state should be a coherent, auditable history per   (actor \u00d7 report/case) pair, matching the <code>create_rm_machine()</code> definition.</li> <li>Persistence: state should survive process restart.</li> <li>Simplicity: a single mechanism for RM state is easier to guard and query than   two unconnected mechanisms.</li> <li>Minimal disruption: changes should be incremental and testable.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Option A \u2014 Persist the full RM lifecycle in <code>VultronParticipantStatus</code>   records (recommended)</li> <li>Option B \u2014 Persist the <code>ReportStatus</code> STATUS layer in the DataLayer as a   new record type</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Option A, because it reuses the existing <code>VultronParticipantStatus</code> data model and makes the complete RM lifecycle visible in one place without introducing a new record type.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#implementation-steps","title":"Implementation steps","text":"<ol> <li> <p>When a report is first received (<code>CreateReportReceivedUseCase</code>,    <code>SubmitReportReceivedUseCase</code>), create and persist an initial    <code>VultronParticipantStatus</code> with <code>rm_state=RM.RECEIVED</code> for the receiving    actor. This is the <code>START \u2192 RECEIVED</code> (RECEIVE trigger) transition.</p> </li> <li> <p>When a report is validated (<code>ValidateReportReceivedUseCase</code> /    <code>SvcValidateReportUseCase</code>), append <code>rm_state=RM.VALID</code> to the participant    status history. When invalidated, append <code>rm_state=RM.INVALID</code>.</p> </li> <li> <p>When a case is created from a valid report, the actor's new    <code>VultronParticipantStatus</code> for the case should be initialised with    <code>rm_state=RM.VALID</code> rather than <code>RM.START</code>, carrying the current RM state    forward into the case-engagement phase.</p> </li> <li> <p>Deprecate and then remove the transient in-memory <code>STATUS</code> dict    (<code>vultron.core.models.status.STATUS</code>) once the above steps are in place and    tested. The <code>ReportStatus</code> model and <code>set_status()</code>/<code>get_status_layer()</code>    helpers can be removed at that point.</p> </li> <li> <p>All RM transition guard logic should read from <code>VultronParticipantStatus</code>    (via the DataLayer) rather than the in-memory STATUS dict.</p> </li> </ol>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because RM history is complete, persistent, and queryable.</li> <li>Good, because the <code>create_rm_machine()</code> definition becomes the single   authoritative source of valid RM transitions for both lifecycle phases.</li> <li>Good, because RM guards (e.g., \"is the report currently RECEIVED or INVALID?\")   work consistently whether the query is pre- or post-case.</li> <li>Bad, because existing tests that read from the in-memory STATUS dict will need   updating.</li> <li>Bad, because <code>CaseParticipant.append_rm_state()</code> (wire layer) may need to be   mirrored on the core domain counterpart; see   <code>notes/domain-model-separation.md</code> on wire-layer mutation helpers that belong   in core.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li>All pre-existing tests pass after the migration.</li> <li>New integration tests verify that a complete RM history   (RECEIVED \u2192 VALID \u2192 ACCEPTED \u2192 CLOSED) is stored in   <code>CaseParticipant.participant_statuses</code> and survives a DataLayer round-trip.</li> <li>The in-memory <code>STATUS</code> dict is empty (or removed) after the migration.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0013-unify-rm-state-tracking/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>See <code>archived_notes/state-machine-findings.md</code> OPP-09, P-07 for the analysis that   led to this decision.</li> <li>Related: <code>vultron/core/states/rm.py</code> <code>create_rm_machine()</code> \u2014 authoritative   RM state machine definition.</li> <li>Related fix: <code>notes/domain-model-separation.md</code> \u2014 wire-layer mutation   helpers that should move to core domain objects.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/","title":"Pin GitHub Actions to Full Commit SHAs with Version Comments","text":""},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>GitHub Actions workflow files reference third-party actions using <code>uses:</code> directives. When these references use mutable identifiers \u2014 version tags (e.g., <code>@v4</code>) or branch names (e.g., <code>@main</code>) \u2014 a compromised or modified upstream action can silently inject malicious code into CI without any change to the repository. This is a known software supply-chain attack vector.</p> <p>The Vultron project uses GitHub Actions for CI (tests, linting, deployment, link-checking) and needs a sustainable, low-maintenance policy that prevents this class of attack while keeping pins up-to-date.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Prevent supply-chain compromise via tag or branch mutation in upstream   actions.</li> <li>Keep the maintenance burden low so that pins do not become stale.</li> <li>Maintain human-readable context for each pin so that reviewers can   understand which version is pinned without decoding a SHA.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ol> <li>SHA-pin every <code>uses:</code> line; use Dependabot for automatic pin updates</li> <li>SHA-pin only third-party (non-<code>actions/</code>) actions</li> <li>Rely on version tags only (no SHA pinning)</li> </ol>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Option 1 \u2014 SHA-pin every <code>uses:</code> line with Dependabot, because it provides complete protection against tag/branch mutation for all actions (including first-party GitHub actions that are also susceptible), while Dependabot automates the routine maintenance of keeping SHAs current.</p> <p>All six workflow files under <code>.github/workflows/</code> have been updated to pin every <code>uses:</code> reference to a full 40-character commit SHA. Each pin carries an inline comment with the human-readable version tag (e.g., <code># v4.1.0</code>). Dependabot is configured (<code>.github/dependabot.yml</code>) to monitor <code>github-actions</code> on a weekly schedule, providing automatic pull requests whenever a new version is released.</p> <p>An automated pytest test (<code>test/ci/test_workflow_sha_pinning.py</code>) verifies at every CI run that no unpinned <code>uses:</code> line is accidentally introduced.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good: Every <code>uses:</code> line is immutable at the referenced commit,   eliminating supply-chain risk from tag or branch mutation.</li> <li>Good: Dependabot opens PRs automatically when new versions are   released, so pins do not become stale without manual tracking.</li> <li>Good: Inline version comments preserve human-readable context for   code review.</li> <li>Good: The automated test (<code>CISEC-01-003</code>) catches any regression   before it reaches <code>main</code>.</li> <li>Neutral: SHA-only references are harder to read without the version   comment, mitigated by the mandatory comment requirement   (<code>CISEC-01-002</code>).</li> <li>Bad: Dependabot may open PRs for patch and minor releases that have   not been security-reviewed; reviewer discretion is required when merging   pin-bump PRs.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<p>Compliance is verified continuously by <code>test/ci/test_workflow_sha_pinning.py</code>, which:</p> <ol> <li>Discovers all <code>.github/workflows/*.yml</code> files.</li> <li>For each <code>uses:</code> line, asserts that the referenced version is a full    40-character hexadecimal SHA (CISEC-01-001).</li> <li>For each SHA line, asserts that an inline <code># &lt;version&gt;</code> comment is    present (CISEC-01-002).</li> </ol> <p>This test runs as part of the standard <code>pytest</code> suite in every CI pipeline execution.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0014-sha-pin-github-actions/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li><code>specs/ci-security.yaml</code> \u2014 full requirement set (CISEC-01-001 through   CISEC-04-002)</li> <li><code>.github/dependabot.yml</code> \u2014 Dependabot configuration</li> <li><code>test/ci/test_workflow_sha_pinning.py</code> \u2014 automated enforcement test</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/","title":"Create VulnerabilityCase at Report Receipt (RM.RECEIVED)","text":""},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>When a vendor actor receives a <code>Offer(Report)</code> activity (i.e., a reporter submits a vulnerability report), the current implementation defers <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> creation until the report passes validation \u2014 that is, until the <code>ValidateReport</code> behavior tree transitions the receiver's RM state to <code>RM.VALID</code>.</p> <p>This was originally a natural mapping from the CVD process model: a \"case\" traditionally implies the vendor has committed to working the issue. However, in the Vultron prototype this deferral creates several problems:</p> <ol> <li>Awkward pre-case tracking: Before a case exists, the system needs    separate mechanisms to track the receiver's RM state and the reporter's    participation. These \"proto-case\" structures mimic a case without being    one, adding complexity with no benefit.</li> <li>Late finder notification: The reporter does not learn that a case    has been opened until after validation. Under MPCVD, this creates    unnecessary gaps in coordination.</li> <li>Overcrowded <code>ValidateReport</code> BT: The validate-report behavior tree    currently performs both report validation and case/participant setup.    These are distinct concerns. Mixing them makes the tree harder to    reason about and test.</li> <li>Blocking FINDER-PART-1: The need to track participants before a    case exists drove the FINDER-PART-1 task, which required a complex    retroactive re-linking mechanism. This complexity dissolves if the    case is created earlier.</li> </ol> <p>The question is: when should the <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object be created?</p>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Simplicity: minimize distinct tracking mechanisms</li> <li>Protocol fidelity: the CVD process begins at receipt, not at validation</li> <li>Separation of concerns: case setup vs. report validation are distinct   workflows</li> <li>Finder visibility: reporters should know a case was opened as soon as   possible</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ol> <li>Keep case creation at RM.VALID (status quo) \u2014 create the case only    after the report is validated</li> <li>Create case at RM.VALID, but emit earlier notification \u2014 keep    creation deferred but send provisional notification sooner</li> <li>Create case at AckReport (RM.RECEIVED, post-ack) \u2014 wait for the    receiver to acknowledge before creating the case</li> <li>Create case at <code>Offer(Report)</code> receipt (RM.RECEIVED) \u2014 create the    case immediately in <code>SubmitReportReceivedUseCase</code></li> </ol>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Option 4 \u2014 Create case at <code>Offer(Report)</code> receipt (RM.RECEIVED).</p> <p>When <code>SubmitReportReceivedUseCase</code> processes an inbound <code>Offer(Report)</code>, it MUST invoke a new behavior tree (<code>receive_report_case_tree</code>) that creates a <code>VulnerabilityCase</code>, two initial <code>VultronParticipant</code> records (reporter at RM.ACCEPTED, receiver at RM.RECEIVED), a default embargo, and queues a <code>Create(Case)</code> notification to the reporter.</p> <p>The <code>ValidateReport</code> BT is slimmed to validation-only logic.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good: No separate \"pre-case\" object or participant tracking mechanism   is needed. The case object exists from the moment the report is received.</li> <li>Good: <code>ValidateReport</code> BT focuses on what it is named for: evaluating   report credibility and validity. Case/participant creation moves to its   own BT.</li> <li>Good: Reporter receives immediate <code>Create(Case)</code> notification,   improving MPCVD coordination latency.</li> <li>Good: <code>InvalidateReportReceivedUseCase</code>, <code>CloseReportReceivedUseCase</code>,   and <code>ValidateReportReceivedUseCase</code> can all dereference <code>report_id \u2192   case_id</code> and delegate to corresponding case-level use cases. This   makes report-centric flows consistent.</li> <li>Good: FINDER-PART-1 (the complex retroactive re-linking task) is   superseded and can be removed from the backlog.</li> <li>Neutral: The term \"proto-case\" is preserved but redefined. A   proto-case is now a case object in the RM.RECEIVED or RM.INVALID stage   (i.e., the case exists but has not yet been validated). The   caterpillar/butterfly analogy still holds: caterpillar = case in   RM.RECEIVED/INVALID stages; butterfly = case in RM.VALID/ACCEPTED/   DEFERRED stages.</li> <li>Neutral: Cases for reports that are later invalidated or closed   without validation do exist as persisted objects in a terminal   proto-case state (RM.INVALID or RM.CLOSED). This is acceptable and   consistent with the protocol model.</li> <li>Bad: <code>SubmitReportReceivedUseCase</code> becomes more complex (requires   BT invocation). This is offset by <code>ValidateReport</code> BT becoming simpler.</li> <li>Bad: The <code>InitializeDefaultEmbargoNode</code> and participant-creation   nodes move from the validate-report BT to the new case-from-report BT.   The BT tests for these nodes must be updated accordingly.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<p>This decision is validated by:</p> <ul> <li><code>test/core/use_cases/received/test_submit_report.py</code>: verifies that   <code>SubmitReportReceivedUseCase</code> creates a case, two participants, and   queues <code>Create(Case)</code> to the outbox</li> <li><code>test/core/behaviors/case/test_receive_report_case_tree.py</code>: verifies   the new BT creates case and participants with correct initial RM states</li> <li><code>test/core/behaviors/report/test_validate_tree.py</code>: verifies the   slimmed validate-report BT does NOT create case or participants</li> <li>All existing tests for <code>ValidateReportReceivedUseCase</code> continue to   pass (the report\u2192case dereference is transparent to callers)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#option-1-keep-case-creation-at-rmvalid-status-quo","title":"Option 1 \u2014 Keep case creation at RM.VALID (status quo)","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because it preserves the traditional CVD meaning of \"case\" (an   accepted commitment to work the issue)</li> <li>Bad, because it requires separate pre-case tracking for RM state and   participants</li> <li>Bad, because <code>ValidateReport</code> BT mixes two concerns</li> <li>Bad, because finder notification is delayed until after validation</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#option-2-emit-earlier-notification-keep-deferred-creation","title":"Option 2 \u2014 Emit earlier notification, keep deferred creation","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because \"case\" retains its committed-work meaning</li> <li>Bad, because adds another activity type to the protocol with no   corresponding state change</li> <li>Bad, because the pre-case tracking problem remains</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#option-3-create-case-at-ackreport","title":"Option 3 \u2014 Create case at AckReport","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because case creation is still tied to an explicit vendor action</li> <li>Bad, because the <code>AckReport</code> step is optional and may not occur in all   demo or protocol paths</li> <li>Bad, because complicates the path for implementations that skip   explicit acknowledgment</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#option-4-create-case-at-offerreport-receipt-chosen","title":"Option 4 \u2014 Create case at <code>Offer(Report)</code> receipt (chosen)","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because aligns case object lifecycle with the start of CVD work</li> <li>Good, because eliminates pre-case tracking complexity</li> <li>Good, because separates validation from case setup</li> <li>Good, because sends immediate notification to reporter</li> <li>Neutral, because \"proto-case\" term requires redefinition (not removal)</li> <li>Bad, because cases for subsequently-invalidated reports persist in   storage (acceptable; they are in a terminal state)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0015-create-case-at-report-receipt/#more-information","title":"More Information","text":"<ul> <li>Supersedes: FINDER-PART-1 task in <code>plan/IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md</code></li> <li>Refines: <code>notes/case-state-model.md</code> (proto-case / caterpillar\u2013butterfly   metaphor)</li> <li>Updates: <code>specs/case-management.yaml</code> (new CM-12 requirements)</li> <li>Updates: <code>specs/duration.yaml</code> (DUR-07-002 timing, new DUR-07-004)</li> <li>Updates: <code>notes/protocol-event-cascades.md</code> (cascade list)</li> <li>Source idea: IDEA-260408-01 in <code>plan/IDEAS.md</code></li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/","title":"Replace TinyDB with SQLModel/SQLite DataLayer Adapter","text":""},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#context-and-problem-statement","title":"Context and Problem Statement","text":"<p>The current <code>TinyDbDataLayer</code> adapter (<code>vultron/adapters/driven/datalayer_tinydb.py</code>) uses TinyDB, which rewrites the entire backing JSON file on every read and write operation. The I/O cost is therefore O(n) relative to total database size, not query size. As the test suite grew, this caused the full test run to slow from ~13 seconds to 15+ minutes (BUG-2026041001).</p> <p>The fix required patching <code>TinyDbDataLayer.__init__</code> in <code>pytest_configure</code> to force <code>MemoryStorage</code> globally \u2014 infrastructure complexity paid entirely to work around the backend, not to test application behaviour.</p> <p>Two architectural options were evaluated: a SQL-native backend (SQLite via SQLModel) and a document database (MongoDB). A third option \u2014 doing nothing and simply patching around the performance problem \u2014 is the status quo.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#decision-drivers","title":"Decision Drivers","text":"<ul> <li>Test-suite speed: full run must stay under ~1 minute without a <code>pytest_configure</code>   patch (BUG-2026041001).</li> <li>Hex-arch compliance: the chosen adapter must live entirely in   <code>vultron/adapters/driven/</code>; core and wire layers must have zero new imports.</li> <li>Test isolation: unit tests that construct a DataLayer directly must get an isolated   store with no patching or fixtures required.</li> <li>Prototype scope: no data migration from existing <code>mydb.json</code> files is required   (IDEA-26040903 \u2014 prototype phase, no backward compat).</li> <li>Future-proof schema: the storage model should map naturally to a MongoDB document   collection for a future production upgrade.</li> <li>Minimal new infrastructure: avoid adding Docker services or complex tooling on   the critical path to D5-7-HUMAN.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#considered-options","title":"Considered Options","text":"<ul> <li>Option A \u2014 SQLModel/SQLite single-table polymorphic storage (chosen)</li> <li>Option B \u2014 SQLModel/SQLite per-type-table storage</li> <li>Option C \u2014 MongoDB Community Edition in Docker</li> <li>Option D \u2014 Keep TinyDB with MemoryStorage patch (status quo)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#decision-outcome","title":"Decision Outcome","text":"<p>Chosen option: Option A \u2014 SQLModel/SQLite single-table polymorphic storage, because it delivers row-level I/O, test isolation via <code>sqlite:///:memory:</code>, and a document-oriented schema that maps naturally to MongoDB \u2014 all without adding Docker infrastructure or changing core/wire layer imports.</p>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#consequences","title":"Consequences","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because <code>sqlite:///:memory:</code> gives automatic per-engine isolation in tests   with no monkey-patching of application code.</li> <li>Good, because row-level SQLite I/O keeps the full test suite under ~1 minute   regardless of how many records are stored.</li> <li>Good, because the single-table <code>(id_, type_, actor_id, data JSON)</code> schema mirrors   a MongoDB collection, making a future backend swap a new adapter + one env-var   change.</li> <li>Good, because <code>db_record.py</code>'s <code>object_to_record</code> / <code>record_to_object</code> helpers   (vocabulary-registry-aware) are reused unchanged \u2014 no new serialization logic.</li> <li>Good, because <code>TECHDEBT-32c</code> (the <code>datalayer_tinydb</code> fallback import in   <code>vultron/wire/as2/rehydration.py</code> that violated CS-05-001) is removed as part   of the same task (DL-SQLITE-2).</li> <li>Neutral, because SQLite is still a single-writer file; concurrent multi-process   writes are possible but uncommon in the prototype scenario. The file lock is   acceptable for prototype use.</li> <li>Bad, because all existing <code>mydb.json</code> data is abandoned (acceptable per   IDEA-26040903 \u2014 prototype phase).</li> <li>Bad, because TinyDB's per-table namespace model (actor prefix) is replaced by an   <code>actor_id</code> column; any code that constructs table names directly must be updated.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#validation","title":"Validation","text":"<ul> <li><code>uv run pytest --tb=short</code> passes after each of DL-SQLITE-1 through DL-SQLITE-4,   with no <code>pytest_configure</code> TinyDB monkey-patch in <code>test/conftest.py</code>.</li> <li>The full test suite completes in under 2 minutes.</li> <li>Actor A's <code>dl.list()</code> does not return records belonging to Actor B.</li> <li><code>uv run mypy &amp;&amp; uv run pyright</code> pass with zero errors after DL-SQLITE-4.</li> <li><code>uv run flake8 vultron/ test/</code> passes with zero errors after DL-SQLITE-4.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#pros-and-cons-of-the-options","title":"Pros and Cons of the Options","text":""},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#option-a-sqlmodelsqlite-single-table-polymorphic","title":"Option A \u2014 SQLModel/SQLite single-table polymorphic","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because <code>sqlite:///:memory:</code> provides automatic isolation per engine in   tests; no patches needed.</li> <li>Good, because row-level I/O; query cost is O(matched rows), not O(total DB size).</li> <li>Good, because single table mirrors a MongoDB collection \u2014 future migration is   straightforward.</li> <li>Good, because <code>db_record.py</code> helpers are reused unchanged.</li> <li>Neutral, because adding SQLModel/SQLAlchemy adds ~2 MB of dependency weight.</li> <li>Bad, because <code>type_</code> queries require a <code>WHERE type_ = ?</code> clause instead of a   per-type table, which is slightly less explicit but well-supported with an index.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"adr/0016-sqlmodel-sqlite-datalayer/#option-b-sqlmodelsqlite-per-type-table-storage","title":"Option B \u2014 SQLModel/SQLite per-type-table storage","text":"<ul> <li>Good, because table names match the existing TinyDB table-per-type pattern.</li> <li>Bad, because schema migrations require adding new tables for each new domain type.</li> <li>Bad, because it does not map to MongoDB's collection model.</li> <li>Bad, because IDEA-26040901 (the per-type table consolidation question) identified   this as the problem to solve; Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Here we collect some guidance for potential implementations of Vultron.</p> <p>While a complete protocol implementation specification remains a work in progress, we do have a few additional suggestions for potential implementers.</p> <p> </p> <p>Prerequisites</p> <p>The Implementing Vultron section assumes that you have:</p> <ul> <li>an interest in implementing the Vultron Protocol</li> <li>basic familiarity with the Vultron Protocol</li> <li>familiarity with the CVD process in general</li> </ul> <p>If you are unfamiliar with the Vultron Protocol, we recommend that you start with Understanding Vultron. For technical reference, see Reference. If you're just trying to understand the CVD process, we recommend that you start with the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</p> <p>In this section, you will find:</p> <ul> <li> an abstract case object for use in tracking MPCVD cases</li> <li> Notes on the core Vultron Protocol subprocesses (RM, EM, and CS), including how the CS model might integrate with other processes</li> <li> An in-depth exploration of applying the ActivityPub protocol as an underlying foundation to   the Vultron Protocol.</li> <li> A few thoughts on the Embargo Management Process and how it might be implemented using the <code>iCalendar</code> protocol.</li> <li> General notes on future implementations.</li> </ul> <p>Over time, we plan to expand this section of the documentation to include:</p> <ul> <li>Basic data model examples</li> <li>Behavior logic implementation examples</li> <li>Simulation examples</li> <li>Communication protocol implementation examples</li> <li>Other implementation notes as needed</li> </ul> <p>The Vultron Protocol is an interoperability protocol</p> <p>The protocol and data structures outlined in this documentation are intended to facilitate interoperability among individual  organizations' workflow management systems. As such, they are focused on the exchange of information and data necessary for the MPCVD process to function and will  not likely be sufficient to fully address any individual organization's vulnerability response process.</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/","title":"A Vultron Protocol Case Object","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Here we describe a notional MPCVD <code>Case</code> object that incorporates the process models and formalisms that define the Vultron Protocol. The object model we describe is intended to provide the necessary core information for an implementation of the formal protocol. The diagram below depicts a UML Class Diagram of the <code>Case</code> model. It is not the minimal possible model required by the Vultron Protocol; for example, strictly speaking, a Participant does not need to attempt to track the state of every other Participant, but it might help to do so. Rather, this model is intended to be compact yet sufficient for an implementation to effectively track the coordination effort of an MPCVD case.</p> <p>The remainder of this section provides details about the diagram below.</p> <pre><code>classDiagram\nclass Case {\n    em_state EMStateEnum = None\n    pxa_state PXAStateEnum = pxa\n    +receive_message(Message)\n}\n\nclass Report\n\nclass Message {\n    sender Participant\n    recipient Case or Participant\n    msg_type MessageTypeEnum\n}\n\nclass LogEvent\n\nclass Participant {\n    case_role CaseRoleFlags\n    rm_state RMStateEnum = Start\n    case_engagement Boolean = True\n    embargo_adherence Boolean = True\n    + receive_message(Message)\n    - emit_message(Message)\n}\n\nclass VendorParticipant {\n    vfd_state VFDStateEnum = vfd\n}\n\nclass DeployerParticipant {\n    vfd_state VFDStateEnum = ..d\n}\n\nclass Contact\n\nCase *--&gt; \"1..*\" Report: reports\nCase *--&gt; \"0..*\" Message : messages\nCase *--&gt; \"0..*\" LogEvent : log_events\nCase *--&gt; \"1..*\" Participant : participants\nParticipant *--&gt; \"1\" Contact : contact\nParticipant &lt;|-- VendorParticipant\nParticipant &lt;|-- DeployerParticipant</code></pre> <p>With the following supporting enumerations and flags:</p> <pre><code>classDiagram\n\nclass CaseRoleFlags {\n    &lt;&lt;flags&gt;&gt;\n    Finder\n    Reporter\n    Vendor\n    Coordinator\n    Deployer\n    Other\n}\n\nclass EMStateEnum {\n    &lt;&lt;enumeration&gt;&gt;\n    None\n    Proposed\n    Accepted\n    Revise\n    Exited\n}\n\nclass PXAStateEnum {\n    &lt;&lt;enumeration&gt;&gt;\n    pxa\n    pxA\n    pXa\n    pXA\n    Pxa\n    PxA\n    PXa\n    PXA\n}\n\nclass RMStateEnum {\n    &lt;&lt;enumeration&gt;&gt;\n    Start\n    Received\n    Invalid\n    Valid\n    Deferred\n    Accepted\n    Closed\n}\n\nclass VFDStateEnum {\n    &lt;&lt;enumeration&gt;&gt;\n    vfd\n    Vfd\n    VFd\n    VFD\n    ..d\n    ..D\n}\n\nclass MessageTypeEnum {\n    &lt;&lt;flags&gt;&gt;\n    RS, RI, RV, RD, RA, RC, RK, RE,\n    EP, ER, EA, EV, EJ, EC, ET, EK, EE,\n    CV, CF, CD, CP, CX, CA, CK, CE,\n    GI, GK, GE\n}\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-case-class","title":"The <code>Case</code> Class","text":"<p>The <code>Case</code> class has attributes to track the Embargo Management (EM) state and the participant-agnostic portion of the Case State (CS) model (i.e., the <code>pxa</code> substates), as outlined in model interactions. The <code>Case</code> class aggregates one or more  <code>Reports</code> and <code>Participants</code>, and 0 or more <code>Messages</code> and <code>LogEvents</code>. We include a <code>receive_message()</code> method to allow <code>Participants</code> to send messages to the <code>Case</code>. See below for more details.</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-report-class","title":"The <code>Report</code> Class","text":"<p>The <code>Report</code> class represents the vulnerability report that serves as the impetus for the case. Since it is possible for multiple reports to arrive that describe the same vulnerability, the cardinality of the composition relationship allows for a <code>Case</code> to have many <code>Reports</code>. In most <code>Cases</code>, however, there will be only a single associated <code>Report</code>.</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-message-class","title":"The <code>Message</code> Class","text":"<p>The <code>Message</code> class represents a protocol message as outlined in Message Types. We expect that any implementation of this model will expand this data type to include numerous message-related attributes. Here, we highlight the minimum requirements that the protocol demands:</p> <ul> <li>Each <code>Message</code> has an identified sender (who is a <code>Participant</code> in the case) and one or more message types as enumerated in Message Types.</li> <li>Message types are represented as flags since a single actual message might represent multiple message types.</li> <li>For example, a report submission that includes an embargo proposal might have both the RS and EP message type flags set.</li> </ul> <p>Conceptually, one might think of the <code>Case</code> as a shared object among engaged <code>Participants</code> and that <code>Messages</code> are sent to the <code>Case</code> for all <code>Participants</code> to see. In other words, the <code>Case</code> acts as a broadcast domain, a topic queue, or a blackboard pattern (depending on your preferences for networking or software engineering terminology). Because of this shared-channel assumption, we could have omitted the <code>recipient</code> attribute from the <code>Message</code> class, as the <code>Case</code> itself can serve as the recipient of each message emitted by any <code>Participant</code>. Implementations of this model could, of course, choose a more traditional messaging model with specified recipients, so we leave that choice to implementers.</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-logevent-class","title":"The <code>LogEvent</code> Class","text":"<p>The <code>LogEvent</code> class is a placeholder to represent an event log or history for the <code>Case</code>. Although not required for the protocol to operate, it is a good idea for <code>Case</code> tracking to include a timestamped list of events (e.g., state changes or messages sent or received) so that new <code>Participants</code> can be brought up to speed and so that cases can be analyzed for process improvement in the future.</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-participant-class","title":"The <code>Participant</code> Class","text":"<p>The <code>Participant</code> class represents an individual or organization's involvement in the case. The attributes of the <code>Participant</code> class are as follows:</p> <p>Tip</p> <p>Flags are used instead of an enumeration to convey that a <code>Participant</code> may have multiple roles in a single <code>Case</code>. Roles may differ for the same actor across different cases.</p> <code>case_role</code> <p>A set of flags indicating the Role(s) this <code>Participant</code> plays in the <code>Case</code></p> <p>For example, an organization might be the Vendor in one case and the Coordinator in another.</p> <code>rm_state</code> <p>An enumeration attribute that captures the RM state for this <code>Participant</code> consistent with Report Management.</p> <p>Tip</p> <p>The <code>case_engagement</code> attribute is provided to allow other <code>Participants</code> to recognize the status of other <code>Participants</code>.</p> <code>case_engagement</code> <p>A Boolean attribute that indicates whether the <code>Participant</code> should be included in future communications about the <code>Case</code></p> <p>For example, a Reporter who bows out of a case shortly after reporting it to a Coordinator might be listed as a <code>Participant</code> with <code>case_engagement=False</code> and could, therefore, be left out of further communication about the case.</p> <p>Tip</p> <p>As discussed in Embargo Principles,  it is possible for a <code>Participant</code> to exit a case while still agreeing to abide by the terms of the extant embargo.</p> <code>embargo_adherence</code> <p>A Boolean attribute that indicates the expectation that a <code>Participant</code> is adhering to any existing embargo</p> <p>Continuing our example of a Reporter leaving a case early, they might still be cooperative and indicate their <code>embargo_adherence=True</code>. A more hostile <code>Participant</code> exit could warrant setting <code>embargo_adherence=False</code>, likely triggering an embargo teardown procedure as a consequence.</p> <p><code>Participants</code> can also emit (send) and receive messages.</p> <p>Tip</p> <p>The <code>+</code> on <code>receive_message</code> indicates that this capability is accessible to others (i.e., you can send a <code>Participant</code> a message). On the contrary the <code>-</code> on <code>emit_message</code> conveys that this capability is only accessible to the <code>Participant</code> class  itself (i.e., each <code>Participant</code> gets to decide if, when, and what messages to send).</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#vendor-and-deployer-participant-classes","title":"<code>Vendor</code> and <code>Deployer</code> <code>Participant</code> Classes","text":"<p>The presence of the <code>VendorParticipant</code> and <code>DeployerParticipant</code> classes---depicted as implementations of the <code>Participant</code> class---is necessitated by the discussion in States, where we describe how Vendors and Deployers have unique parts to play in the creation, delivery, and deployment of fixes within the CVD process. These two classes add the <code>vfd_state</code> attribute with different possible values. Vendors can take on one of four possible values (<code>vfd</code>, <code>Vfd</code>, <code>VFd</code>, and <code>VFD</code>), whereas Deployers only have two possible values (<code>..d</code> and <code>..D</code>). Other than that, Vendors and Deployers have the same attributes as other <code>Participants</code>.</p>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-contact-class","title":"The <code>Contact</code> Class","text":"<p>Since a <code>Participant</code> is a specific relationship between an individual or organization and the <code>Case</code> itself, we can safely assume that those individuals or organizations exist and persist independently of the <code>Cases</code> they participate in. Hence, each <code>Participant</code> class in a <code>Case</code> is associated with a long-lived <code>Contact</code> record that represents an individual or organization. Defining the <code>Contact</code> class is outside the scope of this document, so we will simply say that there is nothing particularly special about it. One might reasonably expect <code>Contacts</code> to have names, email addresses, phone numbers, etc.</p> <p>Directory Services Are (Currently) Out of Scope</p> <p>A separate contact management process and accompanying directory service is a likely candidate for future integration work. For now, we observe that similar directories already exist, although there is room for improvement:</p> <ul> <li> <p>FIRST maintains a directory of member teams for incident response purposes</p> </li> <li> <p>Disclose.io offers a searchable list of bug bounty and vulnerability disclosure programs. Contributions are solicited as pull requests on GitHub.</p> </li> <li> <p>Many vulnerability disclosure platform service providers host directories of the programs hosted on their platforms.</p> </li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/case_object/#the-enumeration-classes","title":"The Enumeration Classes","text":"<p>The remainder of the class diagram above consists of classes representing the Role and Message Type flags and various enumerations.</p> <ul> <li>The <code>Role</code> flags are consistent with the roles we defined in Terms and Definitions, as taken from the CVD Guide.</li> <li><code>Message Type</code> flags are consistent with Message Types.</li> <li>The other enumeration classes are consistent with the Report Management, Embargo Management, and Case State process models and their respective state machines. Note that we split the CS model states into two separate enumerations to reflect the participant-agnostic and participant-specific portions of the model described in Model Interactions.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/em_icalendar/","title":"Embargo Management and the iCalendar Protocol","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>We are including this page because the ideas outlined here were instrumental to the development of the more general Embargo Management process in the main protocol and may remain of use in future implementations.</p> <p>The embargo negotiation process\u2014in terms of the proposal, acceptance, rejection, etc.\u2014is strikingly parallel to the process of scheduling a meeting in a calendaring system. To that end, we note the potential application of the <code>iCalendar</code> protocol specified in RFC 5545 to the EM process with the semantics described in this section. While we anticipate that future CVD APIs could adopt an <code>iCalendar</code>-compatible syntax like <code>jCal</code> (RFC 7265), for this conceptual mapping, we use the basic <code>iCalendar</code> syntax from RFC 5545.</p> <p>A CVD Case might have an associated <code>iCalendar</code> object. Embargo schedules can be represented as a single <code>VEVENT</code> object.</p> <p>A mapping of EM concepts to <code>iCalendar</code> field mappings is provided in the table below.</p> Embargo Concept iCalendar Mapping EM Msg Type Embargo object <code>VEVENT</code> - Embargo ID <code>SUMMARY:&lt;case id&gt; embargo expiration</code> - Embargo End Time and Date <code>DSTART = DTEND</code> (0 duration event) - Proposer <code>ORGANIZER</code> - Participant (proposed) <code>ATTENDEE;</code><code>ROLE=OPT-PARTICIPANT;</code><code>PARTSTAT=NEEDS-ACTION</code> EP, EV Participant (acknowledge without acceptance) <code>ATTENDEE;</code><code>ROLE=OPT-PARTICIPANT;</code><code>PARTSTAT=TENTATIVE</code> EK Participant (accept) <code>ATTENDEE;</code><code>ROLE=OPT-PARTICIPANT;</code><code>PARTSTAT=ACCEPTED</code> EA, EC Participant (reject) <code>ATTENDEE;</code><code>ROLE=OPT-PARTICIPANT;</code><code>PARTSTAT=DECLINED</code> ER, EJ Details (link to case trackers, etc.) <code>DESCRIPTION</code> - Embargo Status \\(q^{em} \\in P\\) <code>STATUS:TENTATIVE</code> EP Embargo Status \\(q^{em} \\in A\\) <code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code> EA, EC Embargo Status \\(q^{em} \\in X\\) due to early termination <code>STATUS:CANCELLED</code> ET Embargo Status \\(q^{em} \\in N\\) due to lack of acceptance quorum <code>STATUS:CANCELLED</code> ER Other <code>CATEGORIES:EMBARGO</code><code>RSVP: TRUE</code> - <p>Reflecting the non-binding nature of embargoes, each <code>ATTENDEE</code> SHOULD be marked as <code>ROLE=OPT-PARTICIPANT</code> in the invitation.</p> <p>Vulnerability details MUST NOT appear in the iCalendar data.</p> <p>A case or vulnerability identifier SHOULD appear in the <code>VEVENT</code> <code>SUMMARY</code> along with the words \"embargo expiration.\"</p> <p>Case or vulnerability identifiers SHOULD NOT carry any information that reveals potentially sensitive details about the vulnerability.</p> <p>An embargo proposal SHALL set <code>RSVP:True</code> for all attendees.</p> <p>A Participant response with <code>ATTENDEE:partstat=TENTATIVE</code> serves as a basic acknowledgment that the embargo proposal has been received, but it does not represent agreement to the proposal.</p> <p>The <code>iCalendar</code> <code>ATTENDEE:partstat=DELEGATED</code> value has no semantic equivalent in the EM process.</p>"},{"location":"howto/em_icalendar/#proposing-an-embargo","title":"Proposing an Embargo","text":"<p>Following the model of inviting a group of attendees to a meeting, a proposed embargo can be achieved as follows:</p> <ol> <li> <p>An <code>ORGANIZER</code> sends an embargo invitation, represented by a     <code>VEVENT</code> with <code>STATUS:TENTATIVE</code> listing Participants as <code>ATTENDEE</code>s     (\\(q^{em} \\in N \\xrightarrow{p} P\\)).</p> </li> <li> <p>Each <code>ATTENDEE</code> has <code>partstat=NEEDS-ACTION</code> set on the invitation,     indicating that they have not yet accepted it.</p> </li> <li> <p>Individual <code>ATTENDEE</code>s acknowledge (<code>partstat=TENTATIVE</code>), accept     (<code>partstat=ACCEPTED</code>), or decline (<code>partstat=DECLINED</code>). Their     response is sent to the <code>ORGANIZER</code>.</p> </li> <li> <p>If the <code>ORGANIZER</code> determines that there is a quorum of accepts,     they mark the <code>VEVENT</code> as <code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code>     (\\(q^{em} \\in P \\xrightarrow{a} A\\)).</p> </li> <li> <p>If the <code>ORGANIZER</code> determines that there is no sufficient quorum of     accepts, they mark the new <code>VEVENT</code> as <code>STATUS:CANCELLED</code>     (\\(q^{em} \\in P \\xrightarrow{r} N\\)).</p> </li> </ol>"},{"location":"howto/em_icalendar/#embargo-counterproposals","title":"Embargo Counterproposals","text":"<p>Counterproposals can be achieved in two ways:</p> <ol> <li> <p>by declining an initial invitation and then proposing a new one     (\\(q^{em} \\in P \\xrightarrow{r} N \\xrightarrow{p} P\\))</p> </li> <li> <p>by proposing a new embargo without declining the first one     (\\(q^{em} \\in P \\xrightarrow{p} P\\))</p> </li> </ol> <p>Either way, this is analogous to requesting a proposed meeting to be shifted to a different time or date prior to accepting the original proposed meeting time. However, following the argument from Default Embargoes, we suggest that Participants start by (1) accepting the shortest proposed embargo and (2) proposing a revision to the new embargo instead, which we cover next.</p>"},{"location":"howto/em_icalendar/#proposing-a-change-to-an-existing-embargo","title":"Proposing a Change to an Existing Embargo","text":"<p>Assumption</p> <p>This process assumes that an existing embargo is represented by a <code>VEVENT</code> with<code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code>.</p> <p>Similar to rescheduling an existing meeting, a proposed change to an existing embargo can be achieved as follows.</p> <ol> <li> <p>A new proposal is made as a <code>VEVENT</code> with <code>STATUS:TENTATIVE</code> and the     same <code>ATTENDEE</code> list as the existing one     (\\(q^{em} \\in A \\xrightarrow{p} R\\)).</p> </li> <li> <p>Each <code>ATTENDEE</code> on the new invitation has <code>partstat=NEEDS-ACTION</code>     set, indicating that they have not yet accepted the new invitation.</p> </li> <li> <p>Individual <code>ATTENDEE</code>s acknowledge (<code>partstat=TENTATIVE</code>), accept     (<code>partstat=ACCEPTED</code>), or decline (<code>partstat=DECLINED</code>). Their     response is sent to the <code>ORGANIZER</code>.</p> </li> <li> <p>If the <code>ORGANIZER</code> determines that there is a quorum of accepts     (\\(q^{em} \\in R \\xrightarrow{a} A\\)), they</p> <ol> <li> <p>mark the new <code>VEVENT</code> as <code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code></p> </li> <li> <p>mark the old <code>VEVENT</code> as <code>STATUS:CANCELLED</code></p> </li> </ol> </li> <li> <p>If the <code>ORGANIZER</code> determines that there is no sufficient quorum of     accepts (\\(q^{em} \\in R \\xrightarrow{r} A\\)), they</p> <ol> <li> <p>mark the new <code>VEVENT</code> as <code>STATUS:CANCELLED</code></p> </li> <li> <p>retain the old <code>VEVENT</code> as <code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code></p> </li> </ol> </li> </ol>"},{"location":"howto/em_icalendar/#terminating-an-existing-embargo","title":"Terminating an Existing Embargo","text":"<p>Terminating an existing embargo (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{A,R\\} \\xrightarrow{t} X\\)) can be triggered in one of two ways:</p> <ul> <li> <p>A normal exit occurs when the planned embargo end time has     expired.</p> </li> <li> <p>An abnormal exit occurs when some external event causes the     embargo to fail, such as when the vulnerability or its exploit has     been made public, attacks have been observed, etc., as outlined in     Early Termination.</p> </li> </ul> <p>Translating this into <code>iCalendar</code> semantics, we have the following, which assumes an existing embargo is represented by a <code>VEVENT</code> with <code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code>.</p> <ol> <li> <p>Normal termination: The <code>VEVENT</code> retains its <code>STATUS:CONFIRMED</code>     and passes quietly from the future through the present into the     past.</p> </li> <li> <p>Abnormal termination: The <code>ORGANIZER</code> sets the <code>VEVENT</code> to     <code>STATUS:CANCELLED</code> and sends it out to the <code>ATTENDEE</code> list.</p> </li> </ol> <p>The above is consistent with our premise in Early Termination: Embargo Termination (\\(ET\\)) messages are intended to have immediate effect.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/","title":"General Implementation Notes","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Here we provide a few general implementation notes.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#message-formats","title":"Message Formats","text":"<p>We defined a number of message types in the formal protocol, and showed how they fit into a case object, but we did not specify any format for these messages. Message formats can be thought of as two related problems:</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#structure-and-semantic-content-of-each-message-type","title":"Structure and Semantic Content of Each Message Type","text":"<p>In addition to the commentary throughout this section, messages will likely need to have some sort of consistent header information and some content specifically designed to address the semantic needs of each message type. Such a format must include fields, datatypes, and an underlying formatting structure.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#container-syntax-for-messaging-and-data-exchange","title":"Container Syntax for Messaging and Data Exchange","text":"<p>While we have a predilection for JSON Schema-defined formats, other format specifications (e.g., XSD or protobuf) could serve implementers' needs just as well. In fact, to the degree possible, it seems preferable for the container syntax to remain a late-binding decision in implementation. As long as the structure and semantics are well defined, most standard data formats should be adaptable to the task.</p> <p>ActivityPub and Vultron</p> <p>While an complete Vultron implementation remains a work in progress, we have made some progress on translating the Vultron Protocol into an ActivityPub-based semantics.  Our initial efforts so far indicate that ActivityPub could be a good fit for the Vultron Protocol. The message formats discussion above and transport protocl below are addressed by ActivityPub directly, and the last two (identity management and  encryption) might be better addressed in the context of ActivityPub rather than reinventing the wheel. See Vultron ActivityPub for more information. </p> <p>Vultron Protocol Messages SHOULD use well-defined format specifications (e.g., JSON Schema, protobuf, XSD).</p> <p>Related formats and ontologies</p> <p>We anticipate that emerging formats like the OASIS CSAF and ontologies like the NIST Vulnerability Data Ontology (Vulntology) will play a part.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#transport-protocol","title":"Transport Protocol","text":"<p>We have not specified how Vultron Protocol implementations connect to each other. Presumably, technologies such as REST APIs or WebSockets would be leading candidates to resolve this gap. However, other system architectures could be adapted as well. For example, an XMPP message-routing system might be desired, or even blockchain-related technologies might be adaptable to portions of this protocol as well.</p> <p>Vultron Protocol Implementations SHOULD use common API patterns (e.g., REST, WebSockets).</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#identity-management","title":"Identity Management","text":"<p>We have not addressed Participant authentication as part of the protocol, but obviously implementers will need to determine how Participants know who they are talking to. Individual user accounts with multi-factor authentication are the de facto standard for modern CVD tools, but in an interoperable MPCVD world, the assumption of centralized identity management may not be practical.</p> <p>Related Identity Standards</p> <p>Federated identity protocols such as OAuth, SAML, and/or OpenID Connect may be useful.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#encryption","title":"Encryption","text":"<p>The protocol does not specify any encryption requirements, but it is likely that some form of encryption will be necessary for Vultron Protocol implementations.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#protecting-data-in-transit","title":"Protecting Data in Transit","text":"<p>It may be sufficient for implementations to rely on transport-layer encryption (e.g., TLS), but end-to-end encryption may be desirable in some cases. For now at least, we leave this decision to implementers.</p> <p>Vultron Protocol Implementations SHOULD use transport-layer encryption to protect sensitive data in transit.</p> <p>Vultron Protocol Implementations MAY use end-to-end encryption to protect sensitive data in transit.</p>"},{"location":"howto/general_implementation/#protecting-data-at-rest","title":"Protecting Data at Rest","text":"<p>Encryption at rest is likely to be a requirement for many implementations. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Integrating the Vultron Protocol into everyday MPCVD operations requires each Participant to consider how their business processes interact with the individual RM, EM, and CS, process models, respectively. Here we offer some thoughts on where such integration might begin.</p>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#rm-implementation-notes","title":"RM Implementation Notes","text":"<p>Roughly speaking, the RM process is very close to a normal IT Service Management (ITSM) incident or service request workflow. As such, the RM process could be implemented as a JIRA ticket workflow, as part of a Kanban process, etc. The main modifications needed to adapt an existing workflow are to intercept the key milestones and emit the appropriate RM messages:</p> <ul> <li> <p>when the reports are received (RK)</p> </li> <li> <p>when the report validation process completes (RI, RV)</p> </li> <li> <p>when the report prioritization process completes (RA, RD)</p> </li> <li> <p>when the report is closed (RC)</p> </li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#vulnerability-draft-pre-publication-review","title":"Vulnerability Draft Pre-Publication Review","text":"<p>Pre-Publication Drafts in Related Standards</p> <p>ISO/IEC 29148:2018 includes a pre-publication review step in its process.</p> <p>MPCVD case Participants often share pre-publication drafts of their advisories during the embargo period. Our protocol proposal is mute on this subject because it is not strictly necessary for the MPCVD process to complete successfully. However, as we observe in the ISO Crosswalk, the GI and GK message types appear to provide sufficient mechanics for this process to be fleshed out as necessary. This draft-sharing process could be built into the prepare publication process, where appropriate.</p>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#em-implementation-notes","title":"EM Implementation Notes","text":""},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#embargo-management-and-calendaring","title":"Embargo Management and Calendaring","text":"<p>In terms of the proposal, acceptance, rejection, etc., the EM process is strikingly parallel to the process of scheduling a meeting in a calendaring system. In EM and iCalendar, we suggest a potential mapping of many of the concepts from the EM process onto <code>iCalendar</code> protocol semantics.</p>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#embargo-management-does-not-deliver-synchronized-publication","title":"Embargo Management Does Not Deliver Synchronized Publication","text":"<p>In our protocol design, we were careful to focus the EM process on establishing when publication restrictions are lifted. That is not the same as actually scheduling publications following the embargo termination. Our experience at the CERT/CC shows that this distinction is rarely a significant problem since many case Participants simply publish at their own pace shortly after the embargo ends. However, at times, case Participants may find it necessary to coordinate even more closely on publication scheduling.</p> <p>TLP and Embargoes</p> <p>The Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) is a useful tool for managing the dissemination of sensitive information. TLP can be used to indicate how widely information can be shared and what restrictions apply during an embargo. For example, an embargoed case might be marked TLP:AMBER to indicate that the information is sensitive and should be shared only with those who need to know. Thus, an embargo declaration might take the form of \"This case is TLP:AMBER until 2024-03-31 23:59:59 UTC, at which time it becomes TLP:CLEAR.\"  We have more to say about the use of TLP in CVD in the CERT Guide to CVD.</p>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#cs-implementation-notes","title":"CS Implementation Notes","text":"<p>Because part of the CS model is Participant specific and the other is global to the case, we address each part below.</p>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#the-vfd-process","title":"The vfd Process","text":"<p>Similar to the RM process, which is specific to each Participant, the vfd process is individualized to each Vendor (or Deployer, for the simpler \\(d \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{D}} D\\) state transition). Modifications to the Vendor's development process to implement the Vultron Protocol are expected to be minimal and are limited to the following:</p> <ul> <li> <p>acknowledging the Vendor's role on report receipt with a CV message</p> </li> <li> <p>emitting a CF message when a fix becomes ready (and possibly terminating any active embargo to open the door to publication)</p> </li> <li> <p>(if relevant) issuing a CD message when the fix has been deployed</p> </li> </ul> <p>Non-Vendor Deployers are rarely involved in MPCVD cases, but when they are, their main integration point is to emit a CD message when deployment is complete.</p>"},{"location":"howto/process_implementation/#the-pxa-process","title":"The pxa Process","text":"<p>On the other hand, the pxa process hinges on monitoring public and private sources for evidence of information leaks, research publications, and adversarial activity. In other words, the pxa process is well positioned to be wired into Participants' threat intelligence and threat analysis capabilities. The goal would be to emit CP, CX, and CA messages as appropriate when such evidence is detected. Some portions of this process can be automated:</p> <ul> <li> <p>Human analysts and/or automated search agents can look for evidence of early publication of vulnerability information.</p> </li> <li> <p>IDS and IPS signatures might be deployed prior to fix availability to act as an early warning of adversary activity.</p> </li> <li> <p>Well-known code publication and malware analysis platforms can be monitored for evidence of exploit publication or use.</p> </li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/","title":"Vultron and ActivityPub","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>The Vultron protocol can be mapped onto the ActivityPub protocol. This mapping is described in the Vultron ActivityStreams Ontology.</p> <p>In this section, we'll provide some examples of how the Vultron protocol message types can be represented as ActivityPub messages using the ActivityStreams vocabulary.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/#activitystreams-vocabulary","title":"ActivityStreams Vocabulary","text":"<p>The ActivityStreams vocabulary can be thought of as defining a grammar for describing activities that are performed by actors. The generic structure of a sentence that can be described using the ActivityStreams vocabulary is:</p> <pre><code>graph LR\n    Actor --&gt;|performs| Activity\n    Activity --&gt;|on| Object\n    Activity --&gt;|from| Origin\n    Activity --&gt;|to| Target\n    Activity --&gt;|using| Instrument\n    Activity --&gt;|with| Result\n    Activity --&gt;|in| Context</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/#vultron-activitystreams","title":"Vultron ActivityStreams","text":"<p>From this perspective, the ActivityStreams vocabulary can be used to compose activities like the following:</p> <ul> <li>A Reporter Submits a Report to a Vendor</li> <li>A Vendor Validates a Report</li> <li>A Vendor Creates a Case from a Report</li> <li>A Reporter Proposes an Embargo on a Case</li> <li>A Vendor Accepts an Embargo on a Case</li> </ul> <p>Vultron ActivityStreams (Vultron AS) is an extension of the ActivityStreams vocabulary to describe the mapping of Vultron to ActivityStreams. Vultron AS defines a number of new objects and activities that are used in the Vultron protocol. These fall into the following categories:</p> <ul> <li>Actors (see below)</li> <li>Activities (see the Activities section for more information)</li> <li>Other (non-Activity) Object Types (see below)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/objects/#actors","title":"Actors","text":"<p>Actor vs CaseParticipant</p> <p>As we will see below, we will introduce a new object type called <code>CaseParticipant</code> to represent the participants in a <code>VulnerabilityCase</code>. This is because the <code>as:Actor</code> types are intended to represent persistent identities within a larger  ActivityPub network, while a <code>CaseParticipant</code> is a contextual identity that associates an actor to a  specific <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object. We need to make this distinction so that the Vultron protocol can represent a single actor participating in  multiple cases, with discrete roles and statuses within the context of each case.</p> <p>The standard ActivityStreams actor types can be used in Vultron. These include:</p> <ul> <li><code>as:Person</code></li> <li><code>as:Organization</code></li> <li><code>as:Group</code></li> <li><code>as:Service</code></li> <li><code>as:Application</code></li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/objects/#other-non-activity-object-types","title":"Other (non-Activity) Object Types","text":"<p>ActivityStreams also includes a number of native object types, including:</p> <ul> <li><code>as:Article</code></li> <li><code>as:Collection</code></li> <li><code>as:CollectionPage</code>, including <code>as:OrderedCollection</code> and <code>as:OrderedCollectionPage</code></li> <li><code>as:Document</code>, including <code>as:Audio</code>, <code>as:Image</code>, and <code>as:Video</code></li> <li><code>as:Event</code></li> <li><code>as:Note</code></li> <li><code>as:Page</code></li> <li><code>as:Place</code></li> <li><code>as:Profile</code></li> <li><code>as:Relationship</code></li> <li><code>as:Tombstone</code></li> </ul> <p>Of these, we mainly use <code>as:Note</code> to represent comments on a <code>VulnerabilityCase</code>. The <code>EmbargoEvent</code> object a specialization of the <code>as:Event</code> object. There is nothing stopping systems that implement the Vultron protocol from using other ActivityStreams object types as needed, but we do not define any special semantics for these objects in the context of the Vultron protocol.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/objects/#vultron-specific-objects","title":"Vultron-specific objects","text":"<p>See also</p> <p>As a reminder, we already described these objects in the Case Object section. This section is intended to describe how these objects are represented as ActivityStreams objects.</p> <p>We define the following objects for use in the Vultron AS vocabulary:</p> <ul> <li><code>VulnerabilityReport</code></li> <li><code>VulnerabilityCase</code></li> <li><code>CaseStatus</code></li> <li><code>CaseParticipant</code></li> <li><code>ParticipantStatus</code></li> <li><code>EmbargoEvent</code></li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/objects/#vulnerabilityreport","title":"VulnerabilityReport","text":"<p>A <code>VulnerabilityReport</code> object is used to represent a vulnerability report as an ActivityStreams object. We are not attempting to fully define a vulnerability report data object in this protocol. Instead, we are defining a minimal set of properties that are necessary to support the protocol.</p> <p>In the example below, we show a <code>VulnerabilityReport</code> object that contains a simple text description of a vulnerability in the <code>content</code> property. However, in a real implementation, the <code>content</code> property might contain any sort of vulnerability report format that could be embedded as part of the JSON object.</p> <p>Examples of what might go into the <code>content</code> property of a <code>VulnerabilityReport</code> object include:</p> <ul> <li>Community-developed Structured formats like CSAF or   CVE JSON</li> <li>Quasi-structured text formats based on templates like the   OWASP Vulnerability Template</li> <li>Proprietary formats used by vendors, coordinators, or finders, such as might be found behind a web form like   CERT/CC's Vulnerability Reporting Form</li> <li>A plain text description of the vulnerability, for example a markdown-formatted text description with section   headings and links to external resources</li> </ul> <p>We are deliberately leaving the format of the <code>content</code> property open-ended in order to allow for flexibility in the types of vulnerability reports that can be represented. However, we do recommend that the <code>content</code> property be used to represent a widely-used structured format whenever possible, since this will allow for more automation in the processing of vulnerability reports.</p> <p>While we recommend that the <code>content</code> property be used to contain text-based vulnerability report formats, we also recognize the potential need for some vulnerability reports to take the form of binary attachments, such as PDF documents, images, audio files, or video files. In these cases, the <code>content</code> property could contain a link to the binary file or an embedded encoding of the binary file. However, we recommend that binary attachments be avoided whenever possible, since they can be more difficult to process automatically.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"VulnerabilityReport\",   \"id\": \"https://demo.vultron.local/VulnerabilityReport/32443f5d-996d-4d8f-b433-bc085ad97309\",   \"name\": \"FDR-8675309\",   \"content\": \"I found a vulnerability!\",   \"attributedTo\": [     \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\"   ] }</code></p> <p>Articles and Documents</p> <p>Obviously a VulnerabilityReport or advisory draft could also be an  <code>as:Article</code> or <code>as:Document</code>, but at the moment we don't use those types  explicitly.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/objects/#vulnerabilitycase","title":"VulnerabilityCase","text":"<p>A <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object is used to represent a vulnerability case as an ActivityStreams object. As with <code>VulnerabilityReport</code>, we are not attempting to fully define a vulnerability case data object in this protocol. Instead, we are defining a minimal set of properties that are necessary to support the protocol. The <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object is intended to be consistent with the Case Object defined elsewhere.</p> <p>ActivityStreams Objects are for Interoperability</p> <p>The objects we define here are intended to be used to promote interoperability between systems that communicate using ActivityPub. They are not intended to be used as a data model for a single system. For example, one vendor might use Github issues to track vulnerability cases, while another might use Jira. Both vendors could use the same <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object to represent their cases in ActivityPub, but they would not necessarily use the same data model internally.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"VulnerabilityCase\",   \"id\": \"urn:uuid:e8b9498a-8ede-4347-96ee-cadb3e6d677d\",   \"name\": \"VendorCo Case #70146755\",   \"caseParticipants\": [     {       \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",       \"type\": \"Add\",       \"id\": \"urn:uuid:0d37a5ac-c31e-4776-8975-4f5bec6682ea\",       \"name\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco Add VendorCo to urn:uuid:e8b9498a-8ede-4347-96ee-cadb3e6d677d\",       \"published\": \"2026-05-05T18:54:58+00:00\",       \"updated\": \"2026-05-05T18:54:58+00:00\",       \"content\": \"We're adding ourselves as a participant to this case.\",       \"actor\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco\",       \"target\": status of a <code>CaseParticipant</code> within the context of a <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object. As noted above, see the Global vs Local section for more information about the distinction between participant-agnostic and participant-specific status.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"ParticipantStatus\",   \"id\": \"https://vultron.example/cases/1/participants/vendor/status/1\",   \"name\": \"RECEIVED Vfd NONE pxa\",   \"context\": \"https://vultron.example/cases/1/participants/vendor\",   \"attributedTo\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendor\",   \"rmState\": \"RECEIVED\",   \"vfdState\": \"Vfd\",   \"caseEngagement\": true,   \"embargoAdherence\": true,   \"caseStatus\": {     \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",     \"type\": \"CaseStatus\",     \"id\": \"https://vultron.example/cases/1/status/1\",     \"name\": \"NONE pxa\",     \"context\": \"https://vultron.example/cases/1\",     \"published\": \"2026-05-05T18:54:58+00:00\",     \"updated\": \"2026-05-05T18:54:58+00:00\",     \"emState\": \"NONE\",     \"pxaState\": \"pxa\"   } }</code></p> <p>Why is there a CaseStatus inside the ParticipantStatus?</p> <p>The <code>ParticipantStatus</code> object allows for a <code>CaseParticipant</code> to include a <code>CaseStatus</code> object that is specific to that participant. This allows for a participant to indicate that they believe the case as a whole is in a different state than the case owner believes it to be in. For example, a vendor might believe that a an exploit has been released for a vulnerability, while the coordinating case owner believes that the vulnerability is still unexploited. In this case, the vendor could include a <code>CaseStatus</code> object in their <code>ParticipantStatus</code> that indicates that the case has reached Exploit Public. Upon confirmation of this status by the case owner, the case owner would update the <code>CaseStatus</code> object in the <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object to reflect the new status by changing the <code>pxaState</code> to  include X.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/objects/#embargoevent","title":"EmbargoEvent","text":"<p>An <code>EmbargoEvent</code> object is used to represent the end date and time of an embargo event as an ActivityStreams object. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Activities are the core of the ActivityPub protocol. They are used to represent actions that are performed by actors. The ActivityStreams vocabulary defines a number of activities, and we extend these with specific activities that are used in the Vultron AS vocabulary. So far, we have found the ActivityStreams vocabulary to be sufficient for our needs, so our extensions are limited to specifying the types of activities, actors, and objects that are used in the Vultron protocol.</p> <p>A full mapping of Vultron to ActivityStreams is available in the Vultron ActivityStreams Ontology.</p> <p>Design Goals</p> <p>Our goal in each of these activity definitions is to</p> <ul> <li>Avoid creating new activity types when an existing activity type can be used.</li> <li>Avoid creating defined activity types with the same objects and targets to avoid confusion. Each activity type / object / target combination should have a single meaning within the protocol.</li> </ul> <p>For documentation purposes, we have divided the Vultron AS activities by user flow. Each of these user flows shows the activities that are used to perform a specific task. The user flows are:</p> <ul> <li> Reporting a Vulnerability</li> <li> Acknowledging a Report</li> <li> Initializing a Case</li> <li> Managing a Case</li> <li> Suggest Actor for Case</li> <li> Inviting an Actor to a Case</li> <li> Initializing a Participant</li> <li> Managing Participants</li> <li> Establishing an Embargo</li> <li> Managing an Embargo</li> <li> Status Updates</li> <li> Transferring Case Ownership</li> <li> Error Handling</li> </ul>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/_accept_embargo/","title":"Accept Embargo","text":"<p>An actor accepts an embargo proposal.</p> We use the <code>as:Add</code> activity to represent the addition of an existing object to another object, such as adding a note to  a case. However, it is likely acceptable within an ActivityPub implementation to use the <code>as:Create</code> activity, since the <code>as:Create</code> activity includes a <code>target</code> property that can be used to specify the object to which the newly created object  is being attached.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/_add_participant_to_case/","title":"Add Participant to Case","text":"<p>Here we provide two examples of adding a participant to a case.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/_add_participant_to_case/#vendor-adds-self-to-case","title":"Vendor adds self to case","text":"<p>In the first example, the vendor actor adds itself to the case in the vendor role.
