These include:</p> <ul> <li><code>as:Read</code></li> <li><code>as:View</code></li> <li><code>as:Listen</code></li> </ul> <p>Since most CVD cases are text-centric, we expect that the <code>as:Read</code> activity will be the most commonly used. However, we also expect that the <code>as:View</code> and <code>as:Listen</code> activities could be used in some cases, such as when a case participant views a video or listens to an audio recording.</p> <p>We specifically defined <code>RmReadReport</code> as a subclass of <code>as:Read</code> to indicate that a report has been read. This allows the receiver of a report to acknowledge receipt without indicating anything more than that the report has been read. That leaves <code>RmValidateReport</code> and <code>RmInvalidateReport</code> to indicate a more specific action (accept, reject) on the part of the receiver.</p> <pre><code>flowchart LR\n    subgraph RM:Received\n        a{Accept?}\n        subgraph as:Read\n            RmReadReport\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:Start\n        subgraph as:Offer\n            RmSubmitReport\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:Accepted\n        subgraph as:Accept\n            RmValidateReport\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:Invalid\n        subgraph as:Reject\n            RmInvalidateReport\n        end\n    end\n    a --&gt;|y| RmValidateReport\n    a --&gt;|undecided| RmReadReport\n    a --&gt;|n| RmInvalidateReport\n    RmSubmitReport --&gt; a</code></pre> <p>More Acknowledgements in the Ontology</p> <p>The Vultron AS ontology defines a number of ActivityStreams activities that can serve as the various acknowledgements that are used in the Vultron protocol. These include messages that are specifically <code>as:inReplyTo</code> a message defined as one of the core protocol message types.</p> <p>For example, it is not necessary to send a separate <code>RmReadReport</code> message if the <code>RmValidateReport</code> message is sent as a reply to the <code>RmSubmitReport</code> message. The <code>RmValidateReport</code> message logically indicates that the report has been read in order to have been validated.</p> <p>Like, Dislike and Flag</p> <p>The ActivityStreams vocabulary also includes actions that indicate an opinion  about a message or object, such as <code>as:Like</code>, <code>as:Dislike</code>, and <code>as:Flag</code>. While these may be relevant to implementations of the Vultron protocol, we do not have specific use cases for them at this time.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/acknowledge/#demo","title":"Demo","text":"<p>Try it: <code>vultron-demo acknowledge</code></p> <p>Run this workflow end-to-end with the unified demo CLI:</p> <p><code>bash vultron-demo acknowledge</code></p> <p>Or with Docker Compose:</p> <p><code>bash DEMO=acknowledge docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml run --rm demo</code></p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/error/","title":"Error Handling","text":"<p>While the ActivityStreams vocabulary doesn't include any specific error messages, we can use the <code>as:Reject</code> activity to indicate that a message has been rejected for some reason. We specify a number of specific error messages that can be indicated based on what kind of message they are <code>as:inReplyTo</code>.</p> <pre><code>flowchart LR\n\n    vultron_proto:VultronMessageType --&gt; VultronError\n    vultron_proto:CsMessageType --&gt; CsError\n    vultron_proto:EmMessageType --&gt; EmError\n    vultron_proto:GmMessageType --&gt; GmError\n    vultron_proto:RmMessageType --&gt; RmError</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/establish_embargo/","title":"Establishing an Embargo","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>The process to establish an embargo for a case can start in one of two ways:</p> <ul> <li>Any participant can propose an embargo for a case. This is the most common   way to establish an embargo.</li> <li>The case owner can add an embargo to a case without proposing it first. In some circumstances, this might be an   appropriate way to establish an embargo.</li> </ul> <p></p> <p>Embargo Management in Vultron</p> <p>The Vultron protocol contains quite a bit of detail about Embargo Management and how participants are expected to interact with embargoes. Please refer to that section for more information.</p> <p>Why would you add an embargo without asking first?</p> <p>There are a few reasons why you might want to add an embargo to a case without asking first. For example, when the finder is also the case owner and hasn't invited any other participants to join the case yet, the finder can just add an embargo. Then when the finder invites others to join the case, the invited participants can decide whether to accept the embargo or not. This could be an appropriate way to address the concept of Default Embargoes.</p> <pre><code>flowchart TB\n    subgraph as:Invite\n        EmProposeEmbargo\n    end\n    subgraph as:Question\n        ChoosePreferredEmbargo\n    end\n    subgraph as:Accept\n        EmAcceptEmbargo\n    end\n    subgraph as:Reject\n        EmRejectEmbargo\n    end\n    subgraph as:Announce\n        AnnounceEmbargo\n    end\n    subgraph as:Add\n        ActivateEmbargo\n        AddEmbargoToCase\n    end\n    start([Start]) --&gt; f{Ask first?}\n    f --&gt;|n| AddEmbargoToCase\n    f --&gt;|y| EmProposeEmbargo\n    EmProposeEmbargo --&gt; a{Accept?}\n    a --&gt;|y| EmAcceptEmbargo\n    a --&gt;|n| EmRejectEmbargo\n    EmProposeEmbargo --&gt; ChoosePreferredEmbargo\n    ChoosePreferredEmbargo --&gt; a\n    EmAcceptEmbargo --&gt; ActivateEmbargo\n    AddEmbargoToCase --&gt; AnnounceEmbargo\n    ActivateEmbargo --&gt; AnnounceEmbargo</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/establish_embargo/#propose-embargo","title":"Propose Embargo","text":"<p>An actor proposes an embargo to a case.</p> Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>The process of initializing a case involves creating the case and then adding at least one report, at least one participant, and any notes to the case.</p> <pre><code>flowchart LR\n    subgraph as:Create\n        CreateCase\n    end\n    subgraph as:Add\n        AddReportToCase\n        AddParticipantToCase\n        AddNoteToCase\n    end\n    CreateCase --&gt; AddReportToCase\n    CreateCase --&gt; AddParticipantToCase\n    CreateCase --&gt; AddNoteToCase</code></pre> <p>Combining steps</p> <p>It is not always necessary for these steps to be performed individually. It would be reasonable to create a case with the report and appropriate participants and notes all in the initial case object. We use the <code>as:Add</code> activity to represent the addition of an existing object to another object, such as adding a note to  a case. However, it is likely acceptable within an ActivityPub implementation to use the <code>as:Create</code> activity, since the <code>as:Create</code> activity includes a <code>target</code> property that can be used to specify the object to which the newly created object  is being attached.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/initialize_case/#demo","title":"Demo","text":"<p>Try it: <code>vultron-demo initialize-case</code></p> <p>Run this workflow end-to-end with the unified demo CLI:</p> <p><code>bash vultron-demo initialize-case</code></p> <p>Or with Docker Compose:</p> <p><code>bash DEMO=initialize-case docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml run --rm demo</code></p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/initialize_participant/","title":"Initializing a CaseParticipant","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>It may not always be necessary to generate a new <code>CreateParticipant</code> activity when creating a new <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object. It is possible to create a new <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object and add a new <code>CaseParticipant</code> object to it in a single <code>Create</code> activity.</p> <p>However, there times when all the case participants are not known at the time the case is created. For example, a finder might report a vulnerability to a coordinator, who then creates a new case. The relevant vendors might not be known at the time the case is created, but they might be added later. In this scenario, the coordinator would create the case with just the coordinator and the finder/reporter to start, and then add the vendors as participants in separate steps as they are identified.</p> <p>What's the difference between a Case Participant and an Actor?</p> <p>ActivityStreams actor objects are used to represent long-lived identities of people, organizations, groups, or  software agents. The <code>CaseParticipant</code> object is a wrapper around an <code>as:Actor</code> object that associates the actor with a specific <code>VulnerabilityCase</code> object.  This is done so that an actor can be associated with multiple cases, each with  different roles and statuses contextual to the individual cases.</p> <pre><code>flowchart LR\n    subgraph as:Create\n        CreateParticipant\n    end\n    subgraph as:Add\n        AddParticipantToCase\n    end\n    CreateParticipant --&gt; AddParticipantToCase</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/initialize_participant/#create-participant","title":"Create Participant","text":"<p>In the following example, the vendor actor creates a coordinator participant for a case.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"Create\",   \"id\": \"urn:uuid:e4a09b9f-5ebd-41f8-995c-38cd53485365\",   \"name\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco Create Coordinator LLC\",   \"context\": \"urn:uuid:e8b9498a-8ede-4347-96ee-cadb3e6d677d\",   \"content\": \"We're adding Coordinator LLC to the case.\",   \"actor\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco\",   \"object\": {     \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",     \"type\": \"CaseParticipant\",     \"id\": \"urn:uuid:e8b9498a-8ede-4347-96ee-cadb3e6d677d/participants/https://vultron.example/organizations/coordinator\",     \"name\": \"Coordinator LLC\",     \"context\": \"urn:uuid:e8b9498a-8ede-4347-96ee-cadb3e6d677d\",     \"published\": \"2026-05-05T18:54:59+00:00\",     \"updated\": \"2026-05-05T18:54:59+00:00\",     \"attributedTo\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/coordinator\"   } }</code></p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/initialize_participant/#add-participant-to-case","title":"Add Participant to Case","text":"<p>Here we provide two examples of adding a participant to a case.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/initialize_participant/#vendor-adds-self-to-case","title":"Vendor adds self to case","text":"<p>In the first example, the vendor actor adds itself to the case in the vendor role. an Actor to a Case","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Inviting an actor to a case is a way to add them as a participant in the case. The invitation is sent to the actor as an <code>as:Invite</code> activity. Depending on the actor's response, they may become a participant in the case.</p> <p> </p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Invite Actor to Case\n---\nsequenceDiagram\n    actor O as Case Owner\n    actor A as Actor\n    participant C as Case\n    activate O\n    O -&gt;&gt;+ A: Invite(actor=CaseOwner, object=Actor, target=Case)\n    note over A: Consider invitation\n    alt Accept Invitation\n        A --&gt;&gt; O: Accept(object=Invite)\n        alt Create CaseParticipant\n        O -&gt;&gt; C: Create(object=CaseParticipant(actor=Actor), target=Case)\n        else Add CaseParticipant\n        O -&gt;&gt; O: Create(object=CaseParticipant(actor=Actor))\n        O -&gt;&gt; C: Add(object=CaseParticipant(actor=Actor), target=Case)\n        end\n        note over C: Actor becomes participant in case\n    else Reject Invitation\n        A --&gt;&gt; O: Reject(object=Invite)\n        note over C: Actor is not participant in case\n    end\n    deactivate A\n    deactivate O</code></pre> <p>Invite vs Add?</p> <p>When a case is first created, the Case Owner and any known participants (e.g., the Reporter) should be automatically added to the case. It's not even necessary for these to be emitted as separate <code>as:Add</code> activities. The <code>as:Create</code> activity for the case can include the Case Owner and any known participants as <code>CaseParticipant</code> objects. See Initializing a Case for more.</p> <p>However, over the lifespan of a case, there may be other actors that were not already involved at the time the case was created, but who should be invited to participate in the case. This is where the <code>as:Invite</code> activity comes in.</p> <p>Avoid bogging down in details</p> <p>Adding a participant to a case involves creating the participant object and a participant status object. As we discuss elsewhere, it's probably overkill to emit separate <code>as:Create</code> and <code>as:Add</code> events for each of these events.</p> <pre><code>flowchart LR\n\na[create participant] --&gt; b[create participant status]\nb --&gt; c[add participant status to participant]\nc --&gt; d[add participant to case]</code></pre> <p>Instead, we could emit a single <code>as:Create</code> event for the participant, already containing a status object, and have the <code>target</code> of the <code>as:Create</code> event be the case object.</p> <pre><code>flowchart LR\na[create particpant with status] --&gt;|target| b[case]</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/invite_actor/#invite-to-case","title":"Invite to Case","text":"<p>A vendor invites a coordinator to a case.</p> page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Case management activities reflect the Report Management process model.</p> <pre><code>flowchart TB\n    subgraph RM:RECEIVED\n        subgraph as:Offer\n            RmSubmitReport\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:VALIDATED \n        subgraph as:Accept\n            RmValidateReport\n        end\n        subgraph as:Create\n            CreateCase\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:INVALID\n        subgraph as:Reject\n            RmInvalidateReport\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:ACCEPTED\n        subgraph as:Join\n            RmEngageCase\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:DEFERRED\n        subgraph as:Ignore\n            RmDeferCase\n        end\n    end\n    subgraph RM:CLOSED\n        subgraph as:Leave\n            RmCloseCase\n            RmCloseReport\n        end\n    end\n\n    d{Done?}\n    c{Close?}\n    p{Priority?}\n    v{Valid?}\n    start([Start])\n    start --&gt; RmSubmitReport\n    RmSubmitReport --&gt; v\n    v --&gt;|y| RmValidateReport\n    v --&gt;|n| RmInvalidateReport\n    RmInvalidateReport --&gt; c\n    c --&gt;|y| RmCloseReport\n    RmValidateReport --&gt; CreateCase\n    CreateCase --&gt; p\n    p --&gt;|act| RmEngageCase\n    d --&gt;|n| p\n    p --&gt;|defer| RmDeferCase\n    RmEngageCase --&gt; d\n    RmDeferCase --&gt; d\n    c --&gt;|n| v\n    d --&gt;|y| RmCloseCase</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/manage_case/#submit-report","title":"Submit Report","text":"<p>The finder submits a vulnerability report to the vendor.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"Offer\",   \"id\": \"urn:uuid:ae07f2dd-5d79-4c3e-a9b3-8b233446ebe5\",   \"name\": \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul Offer FDR-8675309\",   \"to\": [     \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco\"   ],   \"actor\": \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\",   \"object\": {     \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",     \"type\": \"VulnerabilityReport\",     \"id\": \"https://demo.vultron.local/VulnerabilityReport/32443f5d-996d-4d8f-b433-bc085ad97309\",     \"name\": \"FDR-8675309\",     \"content\": \"I found a vulnerability!\",     \"attributedTo\": [       \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\"     ]   } }</code></p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/manage_case/#invalidate-report","title":"Invalidate Report","text":"<p>The vendor invalidates the vulnerability report, which implies that they will not be taking further action on the vulnerability. (<code>as:Join</code>) activity. Because the RM model permits reversible transitions between <code>ACCEPTED</code> and <code>DEFERRED</code>, re-engagement is simply an <code>accept</code> transition emitted from the <code>DEFERRED</code> state \u2014 there is no separate <code>RmReEngageCase</code> activity. Using <code>as:Undo</code> was considered but rejected: <code>Undo</code> implies retracting the effects of a prior action, whereas re-engagement is a forward state     \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\"       ]     }   } }</code></p> <p>Close Case vs Close Report</p> <p>Closing a report is only relevant when the report is not valid, because  valid reports should be converted to cases. Hence, we define the  <code>RmCloseReport</code> activity as a an option for when a report is invalidated before a case is created. Both <code>RmCloseReport</code> and <code>RmCloseCase</code> are defined as subclasses of <code>as:Leave</code> to indicate that they are both activities that indicate that the actor's participation in the case or report has ended.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/manage_case/#demo","title":"Demo","text":"<p>Try it: <code>vultron-demo manage-case</code></p> <p>Run this workflow end-to-end with the unified demo CLI:</p> <p><code>bash vultron-demo manage-case</code></p> <p>Or with Docker Compose:</p> <p><code>bash DEMO=manage-case docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml run --rm demo</code></p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/manage_embargo/","title":"Managing an Embargo","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>This diagram is similar to the one shown in Establishing an Embargo, but it also shows the decisions and activities that are used to manage an embargo once it has been established. Once established, an embargo can be modified via a propose/accept/reject cycle. It can also be terminated or removed from a case.</p> <p> </p> <p>Embargo Management in Vultron</p> <p>The Vultron protocol contains quite a bit of detail about Embargo Management and how participants are expected to interact with embargoes. Please refer to that section for more information.</p> <pre><code>flowchart TB\n    subgraph as:Invite\n        EmProposeEmbargo\n    end\n    subgraph as:Accept\n        EmAcceptEmbargo\n    end\n Participants","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Typically most cases involve multiple participants, having various roles within the case. While the most common activities are inviting and adding participants, we've also included activities for removing participants.</p> <pre><code>flowchart TB\n    subgraph as:Invite \n        RmInviteToCase\n    end\n    subgraph as:Accept\n        RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    end\n    subgraph as:Reject\n        RmRejectInviteToCase\n    end\n    subgraph as:Create\n        RmCreateParticipant\n        RmCreateParticipantStatus\n    end\n    subgraph as:Add\n        AddParticipantToCase\n        AddStatusToParticipant\n    end\n    subgraph as:Remove\n        RemoveParticipantFromCase\n    end\n    start([Start])\n    start --&gt; RmInviteToCase\n    RmInviteToCase --&gt; a{Accept?}\n    a --&gt;|y| RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    a --&gt;|n| RmRejectInviteToCase\n    RmAcceptInviteToCase --&gt; RmCreateParticipant\n\n    RmCreateParticipantStatus --&gt; AddStatusToParticipant\n    RmCreateParticipant --&gt; AddParticipantToCase\n    AddParticipantToCase --&gt; s{Status?}\n    s --&gt;|y| RmCreateParticipantStatus\n    s --&gt;|n| r{Remove?}\n    AddStatusToParticipant --&gt; r\n    r --&gt;|y| RemoveParticipantFromCase\n    r --&gt;|n| s</code></pre> <p>Create or Add?</p> <p>There appears to be some logical interchangeability of <code>as:Create</code>  with <code>as:Add</code> since both include a <code>target</code> property that can be used to specify the object to which the new object is being added. We chose to  represent them separately here to acknowledge the difference between creating a new object and adding an existing object to another object, but in an actual implementation it may be acceptable to use either activity for both cases. It seems likely that the general idea should be to use <code>as:Create</code> when creating a new object to  add to another object (as the <code>target</code> of the activity), and <code>as:Add</code> when adding an existing object to another object. </p> <p>That said, our intent is that Vultron be consistent with however ActivityPub does this. If what we're describing here is inconsistent with ActivityPub, please let us know in the form of an issue or  pull request.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/manage_participants/#invite-to-case","title":"Invite to Case","text":"<p>A vendor invites a coordinator to a case.</p> <p><code>json { part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>The following task flow diagram addresses the opening portion of the Report Management process in which a finder creates a vulnerability report and submits it to a vendor. The vendor then reads the report and either accepts or rejects it. If the vendor accepts the report, they create a case for it. If the vendor rejects the report, they eventually close it unless there's a reason to reconsider and validate it.</p> <pre><code>flowchart TB\n    subgraph as:Create\n        RmCreateReport\n        CreateCase\n    end\n    subgraph as:Read\n        RmReadReport\n    end\n    subgraph as:Offer\n        RmSubmitReport\n    end\n    subgraph as:Accept\n        RmValidateReport\n    end\n    subgraph as:Reject\n        RmInvalidateReport\n    end\n    subgraph as:Leave\n        RmCloseReport\n    end\n    start([Start])\n    start -.-&gt; RmCreateReport\n    start --&gt; RmSubmitReport\n    RmCreateReport --&gt; RmSubmitReport\n    RmSubmitReport -.-&gt; v{Valid?}\n    RmSubmitReport --&gt; r{Read?}\n    r --&gt;|y| RmReadReport\n    r --&gt;|n| r\n    v --&gt;|y| RmValidateReport\n    RmReadReport --&gt; v\n    v --&gt;|n| RmInvalidateReport\n    RmInvalidateReport --&gt; c{Close?}\n    c --&gt;|y| RmCloseReport\n    c --&gt;|n| v\n    RmValidateReport --&gt; CreateCase</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/report_vulnerability/#create-report","title":"Create Report","text":"<p>A finder creates a vulnerability report.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"Create\",   \"id\": \"urn:uuid:46f168d5-2a2f-42cb-a9a8-02d16d422baf\",   \"name\": \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul Create FDR-8675309\",   \"actor\": \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\",   \"object\": {     \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",     \"type\": \"VulnerabilityReport\",     \"id\": \"https://demo.vultron.local/VulnerabilityReport/32443f5d-996d-4d8f-b433-bc085ad97309\",     \"name\": \"FDR-8675309\",     \"content\": \"I found a vulnerability!\",     \"attributedTo\": [       \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\"     ]   } }</code></p> <p><code>Create(Report) + Offer(Report)</code> vs <code>Offer(Report)</code></p> <p>Reports are delivered to recipients using the <code>Offer</code> activity. An <code>Offer</code> can include a <code>Report</code> object that the sender created immediately before sending. While a <code>Create(Report)</code> technically precedes an <code>Offer(Report)</code>, in practice the receiver usually only observes the <code>Offer</code> because the report was created entirely on the sender's side.</p> <p>In other cases the <code>Report</code> may have been created earlier or by a different actor (for example, a platform or intermediary). When that happens the <code>Create(Report)</code> and <code>Offer(Report)</code> are distinct activities. We show both patterns here to cover both workflows.</p> <p>When would <code>Create(Report)</code> be separate from <code>Offer(Report)</code>?</p> <p>A security researcher files a report with a bug-bounty platform that implements Vultron. The <code>Create(Report)</code> activity happens between the researcher and the platform. Later, the platform submits the <code>Report</code> to a vendor by sending an <code>Offer(Report)</code>. The vendor receives the <code>Offer</code> (which contains the <code>Report</code> object) and may not be aware of the earlier <code>Create(Report)</code> activity.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/report_vulnerability/#submit-report","title":"Submit Report","text":"<p>The finder submits a vulnerability report to the vendor.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"Offer\",   \"id\": \"urn:uuid:fa22755c-afb0-4aae-8406-16708da51b87\",   \"name\": \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul Offer FDR-8675309\",   \"to\": [     \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco\"   ],   \"actor\": \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\",   \"object\": {     \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",     \"type\": \"VulnerabilityReport\",     \"id\": \"https://demo.vultron.local/VulnerabilityReport/32443f5d-996d-4d8f-b433-bc085ad97309\",     \"name\": \"FDR-8675309\",     \"content\": \"I found a vulnerability!\",     \"attributedTo\": [       \"https://vultron.example/users/finndervul\"     ]   } }</code></p> <p>Report submission might be followed by either a <code>Read</code> or <code>Accept</code> activity.</p> <p>The <code>Read</code> activity is optional and should be used when the vendor wants to acknowledge that they have received the report without accepting or rejecting it.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/report_vulnerability/#read-report","title":"Read Report","text":"<p>The vendor reads the vulnerability report, acknowledging that they have received it.</p> <p><code>json {   \"@context\": \"https://www.w3.org/ns/activitystreams\",   \"type\": \"Read\",   \"id\": \"urn:uuid:a4b1e06d-c5a3-4a93-834a-55feb908a2f2\",   \"name\": \"https://vultron.example/organizations/vendorco Read FDR-8675309\",   \"content\": \"We've read the report. page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Here we have a number of activities that are used to update the status of a case, or to add a comment to a case.</p> <pre><code>flowchart TB\n    subgraph as:Add\n        AddNoteToCase\n        AddStatusToCase\n        AddStatusToParticipant\n    end\n    subgraph as:Create\n        CreateNote\n        CreateParticipantStatus\n        CreateStatus\n    end\n    start([Start])\n    start -.-&gt; CreateStatus\n    start -.-&gt; CreateParticipantStatus\n    start -.-&gt; CreateNote\n    CreateNote --&gt; AddNoteToCase\n    CreateStatus --&gt; AddStatusToCase\n    CreateParticipantStatus --&gt; AddStatusToParticipant\n    AddStatusToParticipant -.-&gt; AddStatusToCase\n    AddNoteToCase -.-&gt; AddStatusToParticipant\n    AddNoteToCase -.-&gt; AddStatusToCase</code></pre> <p>See also</p> <p>Descriptions of the <code>CaseStatus</code> and <code>ParticipantStatus</code>, and <code>CaseParticipant</code> objects can be found in the Objects section.</p> <p>In addition to the Create/Add process for each of these, there are sometimes additional activities that are triggered in response to a status update or a comment:</p> <ul> <li>A status update to a participant can trigger a status update to the case.</li> <li>A note might trigger a status update to a case or a participant.</li> </ul> <p>A Note on <code>as:Notes</code></p> <p>The ActivityStreams vocabulary includes an <code>as:Note</code> object, which is used to represent a comment or short post. We use this object to represent  comments on a case.</p> <p>Create then Add vs Create with a Target</p> <p>In this documentation, we use the <code>as:Create</code> activity to represent the  creation of a new object, such as a new note or a new status. We use the  <code>as:Add</code> activity to represent the addition of an existing object to another  object, such as adding a status to a case.</p> <p>However, it is likely acceptable within an ActivityPub implementation to use the <code>as:Create</code> activity for both cases, since the <code>as:Create</code> activity includes a <code>target</code> property that can be used to specify the object to which the new object is being added.</p> <p>Sinilarly, it may also be acceptable to use the <code>as:Add</code> activity to represent the creation of a new object, since the <code>as:Add</code> activity includes a <code>object</code> property that can be used to specify the object that is being created along with the <code>target</code> property that can be used to specify the object to which the new object is being added.</p>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/status_updates/#create-status","title":"Create Status","text":"<p><code>json { part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>During the course of coordinating a case, an existing case participant might recognize that another actor should be invited to participate in the case. The following mechanisms provide a way for a case participant to suggest that another actor be invited to participate in the case.</p> <p> </p> <p>Why Suggest instead of just Invite?</p> <p>The process described here makes an assumption that there is a case owner who is responsible for coordinating the  case. Participants having the case owner role can in principle just directly invite other actors to participate in  the case, and they might not need the suggestion mechanism described here. However, we include this mechanism to account for the possibilities that:</p> <ul> <li>there could be multiple case owners, and they might not all agree on who should be invited to participate in the    case.</li> <li>a non-case-owner participant might want to suggest that another actor be invited to participate in the case</li> </ul> <p>Of these, the latter is the more likely scenario, but the mechanism described here can be used in either case.    </p> <p>Reasons to Invite other Actors</p> <p>There are many reasons why a case participant might want to suggest another actor to participate in a case. The following are some examples:</p> <ul> <li>A finder, having reported to one vendor, might further discover that the vulnerability is actually in a    third-party library, and suggest inviting the library vendor to participate in the case.</li> <li>A vendor might be a participant in (but not the owner of) a case, and wants to suggest that the case owner   invite a sector-specific coordinator to participate in the case to address critical infrastructure concerns.</li> <li>A reporter participant might suggest a technical expert (for example, a member of a protocol working group) to    include in the case.</li> <li>A coordinator might suggest to the case owner that a large deployer be invited to participat in a case to    address concerns about the impact of deploying a fix for a vulnerability on infrastructure and operations.</li> </ul> <p>Below is a sequence diagram showing the process of suggesting an actor for a case. We used a sequence diagram instead of a flow chart since the process is relatively simple and the sequence diagram is easier to read.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Suggesting an Actor for a Case\n---\nsequenceDiagram\n    actor A as Participant\n    participant C as Case\n    actor B as Case Owner\n    actor D as Actor \n    Note over A: Recognize that Actor should be invited\n    A -&gt;&gt; C: Offer(object=Actor, target=Case)\n    activate A\n    C --&gt;+ B: Observe suggestion\n    alt Accept Suggestion\n        B --&gt;&gt; C: Accept(object=Offer)\n        B -&gt;&gt;+ D: Invite(actor=CaseOwner, object=Actor, target=Case)\n    else Reject Suggestion\n        B --&gt;&gt; C: Reject(object=Offer)\n    end\n    deactivate B\n    C --&gt; A: Observe response\n    deactivate A\n    note over D: Respond to invitation (not shown)\n    deactivate D</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/suggest_actor/#recommend-actor","title":"Recommend Actor","text":"<p>An actor can recommend that another actor be invited to participate in a case by sending an <code>Offer</code> activity with the <code>object</code> property set to the actor that is being recommended. The <code>target</code> property of the <code>Offer</code> activity is set to the <code>case</code> object. part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>Transfer if case ownership was not part of the original Vultron protocol, but it seems like a reasonable extension that could be useful in some cases, such as transferring a case</p> <ul> <li>from a researcher to a vendor</li> <li>from a vendor to an upstream vendor</li> <li>from a vendor to a coordinator</li> <li>from a coordinator to a vendor</li> <li>between coordinators</li> </ul> <p>The presumption here is that the initial creator of a case is its owner. Subsequent to that, the existing owner can offer to transfer ownership to another participant. The new owner can then accept or reject the offer.</p> <p>We use a sequence diagram instead of a flow chart since the process is relatively simple and the sequence diagram is easier to read.</p> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    actor A as Current Case Owner\n    actor B as Potential Case Owner\n    participant C as Case\n    A -&gt;&gt; B: Offer(object=Case)\n    note over B: Consider offer\n    activate B\n    alt Accept Offer\n        B --&gt;&gt; A: Accept(object=Offer)\n        A -&gt;&gt; C: Update(object=Case)\n        note over C: Case has new owner\n    else Reject Offer\n        B --&gt;&gt; A: Reject(object=Offer)\n        note over C: Case ownership unchanged\n    end\n    deactivate B</code></pre>"},{"location":"howto/activitypub/activities/transfer_ownership/#offer-case-ownership-transfer","title":"Offer Case Ownership Transfer","text":"<p>The current owner of a case can offer If you are familiar enough with the Vultron Protocol that you're interested in implementing it, see Implementing Vultron. And finally, if you're just trying to understand the CVD process, we recommend that you start with the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</p> <p>The Vultron Protocol Reference includes the formal Vultron Protocol specification, and crosswalks the protocol with other related standards and protocols, including:</p> <ul> <li> A Formal Protocol specification for the Vultron Protocol</li> <li> An annotated listing of the Case States of the Vultron Protocol</li> <li> Documentation of Python code included in the Vultron Protocol prototype</li> <li> A set of OWL Ontologies for the Vultron Protocol</li> <li> A Crosswalk of the Vultron Protocol against ISO/IEC standards (including ISO/IEC 29147, 30111, and 5895)</li> <li> A Crosswalk of the Vultron Protocol against the Stakeholder-Specific Vulnerability Categorization (SSVC) methodology</li> <li> A Decision Log for the Vultron Protocol project</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/","title":"Ubiquitous Language \u2014 Vultron","text":"<p>Domain terminology for Vultron's Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD) protocol and hexagonal architecture. Extracted from codebase models, state machines, and design notes.</p>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#core-cvd-concepts","title":"Core CVD Concepts","text":"Term Definition Aliases to avoid Vulnerability A weakness in an information system that could be exploited to cause harm Bug, issue, flaw Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD) A collaborative process where affected parties work together to manage vulnerability remediation and public disclosure Responsible disclosure, coordinated release Multi-Party CVD (MPCVD) CVD process involving three or more independent organizations with different interests and roles Multiparty coordination Report A formal notification of a discovered vulnerability, including technical details and potential impact Submission, notice Case A federated container tracking all parties, state, communications, and embargo agreements related to a specific vulnerability across multiple organizations Issue, coordination event"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#cvd-roles-and-participants","title":"CVD Roles and Participants","text":"Term Definition Aliases to avoid Finder The person or organization that discovers a vulnerability Researcher, discoverer Reporter The person or organization that submits a vulnerability report to affected parties (often the same as Finder) Submitter, notifier Vendor The organization that maintains or supplies the affected product or service Developer, supplier, maintainer Deployer An organization that deploys the Vendor's product and is responsible for applying patches in their own environment (distinct from Vendor) Operator, customer Coordinator A neutral third party (often a national CERT) that facilitates communication and negotiates embargo terms between other parties Mediator, facilitator Participant Any Actor engaged in a Case, holding one or more CVD Roles Stakeholder, party Actor Any URI-identified federated peer (person or organization) in the protocol Agent, endpoint Case Owner The Actor who creates and administers a Case (typically the party seeking vulnerability coordination) Case creator, case manager"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#case-state-model-six-dimensions","title":"Case State Model (Six Dimensions)","text":"<p>The Case State (CS) tracks awareness and readiness across six binary dimensions:</p> Abbreviation Uppercase Lowercase Meaning V/v V v Vendor is aware / unaware F/f F f Fix is ready / not ready D/d D d Fix is deployed / not deployed P/p P p Public is aware / unaware X/x X x Exploit code is public / not public A/a A a Active attacks have been observed / not observed <p>Each transition from lowercase to uppercase represents an event; once uppercase, it cannot revert. This forms a 64-state lattice (2^6 combinations).</p>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#state-machines-and-status","title":"State Machines and Status","text":"Term Definition Aliases to avoid Report Management (RM) Per-participant state machine tracking a Report's lifecycle: Start \u2192 Received \u2192 {Invalid | Valid} \u2192 {Accepted | Deferred} \u2192 Closed; independent for each participant Report workflow, report state Embargo Management (EM) Global (per-case) state machine tracking embargo coordination: None \u2192 Proposed \u2194 Active \u2194 Revise \u2192 eXited; exactly one active embargo per case Embargo workflow, embargo state Case State (CS) Hybrid state model tracking both participant-specific vendor fix path (vfd\u2192Vfd\u2192VFd\u2192VFD) and participant-agnostic public state (pxa combinations); 40 total states Vulnerability state, case lattice Case Status A snapshot of RM, EM, and CS state at a specific moment, with a timestamp and attribution State record Participant Status A snapshot of a Participant's RM state and embargo consent, recording their role and commitment at a specific moment Status record State Transition A move from one valid state to another in the RM, EM, or CS machine; used to track embargo history Embargo record Embargo Consent A Participant's individual agreement to or rejection of a proposed Embargo (a personal commitment, distinct from the Embargo itself); tracked as a per-participant 5-state machine: NO_EMBARGO \u2192 INVITED \u2194 SIGNATORY \u2194 LAPSED \u2192 DECLINED Embargo acceptance, embargo stance Active Embargo The currently in-force Embargo for a Case; Use \"Case State\" for the 64-node lattice; \"Case Status\" for a snapshot; \"RM state\" or \"EM state\" for a specific state machine location.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Participant\" vs. \"Actor\":</p> </li> <li>An Actor is any URI-identified federated peer (a role in the protocol).</li> <li>A Participant is an Actor actively engaged in a specific Case.</li> <li>A Reporter is both an Actor (in general) and might be a Participant (in a specific Case).</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Use \"Actor\" when discussing federation; use \"Participant\" when discussing a specific Case.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Report\" vs. \"Case\":</p> </li> <li>A Report is a one-time vulnerability notification from a Reporter.</li> <li>A Case is the ongoing coordination container that may span multiple Reports (if consolidated) or multiple Cases (if split).</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: \"Report\" = inbound vulnerability notification; \"Case\" = coordination event with state machines and participants.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Embargo\" vs. \"Embargo Consent\":</p> </li> <li>An Embargo is a shared agreement (one per Case) that all parties will not disclose until a certain date.</li> <li>Embargo Consent is each Participant's individual acceptance or rejection of that embargo.</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: If discussing terms, say \"Embargo\"; if discussing one party's stance, say \"Embargo Consent\".</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Activity\" direction \u2014 The term Activity is used for both Inbound and Outbound. Both    follow ActivityStreams 2.0 format, but context determines meaning.</p> </li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Always qualify as \"inbound Activity\" or \"outbound Activity\" when direction matters.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Factory\" scope \u2014 The term Factory can mean:</p> </li> <li>The set of Factory Functions in the <code>vultron/wire/as2/factories/</code> package (public API)</li> <li>A single Factory Function (e.g., <code>create_report()</code>)</li> <li>Recommendation: Say Factory Function or factories package, never generic \"factory\".</li> </ol>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#work-tracking-tasks","title":"Work Tracking &amp; the formal foundation for RM, EM, and CS models State machine, FSM Process In protocol formalism, an independent entity (Participant) maintaining its own state and communicating with other processes via messages Actor, participant Global State The complete system state comprising all N participants' composite states plus all messages in flight between them System state, protocol state Message Queue (or Channel) A FIFO buffer from Participant i to Participant j containing ordered messages; denoted C_ij in formal notation Message buffer, transport channel Reachable State A state logically possible for a Participant given protocol constraints (not all 1,400 theoretical states are reachable) Valid state, achievable state Unreachable State A state impossible due to protocol constraints (e.g., RM Start/Closed states make EM and CS irrelevant) Invalid state, forbidden state Ordering Preference One of 12 formally-defined preferences for CVD outcomes (e.g., D \u227a P: Fix Deployed Before Public Awareness); guides protocol design Success metric, outcome goal Avoid Surprise Core CVD principle embedded in protocol: participants whose state changes SHOULD send messages to other participants to minimize surprise Transparency principle, communication imperative"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#rm-model-details","title":"RM Model Details","text":"Term Definition Aliases to avoid Report Submission (RS) The only RM message that directly triggers a state change in receiver (from S \u2192 R); all other RM messages announce sender's state Initial RM message Report Received (R) Initial RM state when a Report arrives; recipient must validate before transitioning to Invalid or Valid Received state, intake state Report Valid (V) RM state indicating validation passed; next decision is whether to Accept or Defer Validated state, prioritization state Report Accepted (A) RM state indicating work accepted; prerequisite for sending RS to other parties In-progress state, active state Report Deferred (D) RM state indicating work deferred (parking lot); can transition back to Accepted if priorities change Parked state, backlog state Report Closed (C) Final RM state; recipient may ignore all messages on closed reports (no further coordination) Terminal state, archive state"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#em-model-details","title":"EM Model Details","text":"Term Definition Aliases to avoid Embargo None (N) EM initial state; no embargo currently in effect or agreed to Initial state, no-embargo state Embargo Proposed (P) EM state indicating one or more embargo proposals under negotiation Pending state, negotiation state Embargo Active (A) EM state indicating embargo is in effect across all participants; only one per case Effective state, in-force state Embargo Revise (R) EM state indicating active embargo with revision proposal pending; active embargo remains in force until revision accepted Renegotiation state, revision-pending state Embargo eXited (X) EM terminal state after embargo terminates (by expiration, early termination, or public disclosure) Expired state, terminated state Embargo Proposal (EP) EM message type proposing embargo terms (e.g., expiration date) Embargo offer Embargo Termination (ET) EM message type terminating embargo immediately; has immediate effect regardless of other pending messages Embargo end, embargo expiration Embargo Grammar Regular expression <code>(p*r)*(pa(p*r)*(pa)?t)?</code> describing all valid EM state transition sequences DFA language, EM language"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#cs-model-details","title":"CS Model Details","text":"Term Definition Aliases to avoid Vendor Fix Path Participant-specific CS submodel tracking vendor awareness (v/V), fix readiness (f/F), fix deployment (d/D); one path per Vendor Vendor progression, fix progression Public State Participant-agnostic CS submodel tracking public awareness (p/P), exploit public (x/X), attacks observed (a/A); shared across all participants Global state, pxa state Vendor Awareness (V) CS event: transition from vendor unaware (v) to vendor aware (V); typically triggered by Report Submission V event, vendor notification Fix Readiness (F) CS event: transition from fix not ready (f) to fix ready (F); indicates vendor has completed fix development F event, fix development complete Fix Deployed (D) CS event: transition from fix not deployed (d) to fix deployed (D); indicates vendor/deployer has applied fix D event, fix application Public Awareness (P) CS event: transition from public unaware (p) to public aware (P); indicates vulnerability known outside immediate parties P event, disclosure Exploit Public (X) CS event: transition from exploit not public (x) to exploit public (X); indicates exploit code publicly available X event, exploit disclosure Attacks Observed (A) CS event: transition from attacks not observed (a) to attacks observed (A); indicates active exploitation in the wild A event, active attack vfd\u00b7\u00b7 notation Compact notation for CS states where lowercase/uppercase in each position represents specific substate (v=vendor unaware, V=aware, f=fix not ready, F=ready, etc.) State shorthand, state code pxa notation Public state substates abbreviated as p/P (public aware), x/X (exploit public), a/A (attacks observed) Public substate Ephemeral State CS constraint: exploit public without vulnerability public (\u00b7\u00b7\u00b7pX\u00b7) cannot exist; immediately resolves to \u00b7\u00b7\u00b7PX\u00b7 Transient state, immediate transition"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#relationships","title":"Relationships","text":"<p>Protocol Structure:</p> <ul> <li>A Communicating Hierarchical State Machine consists of N independent Processes (Participants) coordinating via message passing.</li> <li>Each Process maintains a Composite State = (Case State + RM State + EM State).</li> <li>The Global State includes all Composite States plus message queues (Channels) between processes.</li> </ul> <p>State Transitions:</p> <ul> <li>RM transitions are mostly independent; only Report Submission (RS) directly changes receiver RM state.</li> <li>EM transitions are global; exactly one EM State per case affects all participants.</li> <li>CS transitions are hybrid: Vendor Fix Path is per-Vendor; Public State is global.</li> </ul> <p>Message Protocol:</p> <ul> <li>State-Change Notification: each state transition SHOULD emit a Message Type to other participants.</li> <li>RM messages: RS is unique (triggers receiver state); others announce sender status (RI, RV, RD, RA, RC).</li> <li>EM messages: all trigger global EM state updates (EP, EA, ER, EV, EC, EJ, ET).</li> <li>CS messages: announce CS Events (CV, CF, CD, CP, CX, CA).</li> <li>All valid messages receive acknowledgments (RK, EK, CK, GK); errors generate (RE, EE, CE, GE).</li> </ul> <p>Constraints:</p> <ul> <li>Precondition: Participant must be in RM Accepted (A) to send RS.</li> <li>Single Embargo: exactly one Embargo Active (A) per case.</li> <li>Public Embargo Boundary: no EM negotiation when Public Awareness (P) or Exploit Public (X) or Attacks Observed (A) occur.</li> <li>Reachable States: many of 1,400 theoretical Composite States are Unreachable due to constraints.</li> </ul> <p>Success Metrics:</p> <ul> <li>12 Ordering Preferences define CVD success; Embargo Active is primary mechanism for achieving top preferences (D \u227a P, F \u227a P).</li> </ul> <p>Architecture Integration:</p> <ul> <li>A Port is implemented by one or more Adapters.</li> <li>Inbound Activities are matched by ActivityPatterns to extract MessageSemantics (formal protocol Message Type).</li> <li>Outbound Activities are constructed by Factory Functions (not by core calling <code>from_core()</code>).</li> <li>A TASK may be blocked by another TASK via a Blocker.</li> <li>A TASK contains one or more Subtasks with Acceptance Criteria.</li> <li>CasePersistence and CaseOutboxPersistence replace the broad DataLayer interface for core use cases.</li> <li>Rehydration converts raw persisted dicts (from DataLayer) into typed domain objects.</li> <li>A Participant has zero or more CVDRoles (represented as CVDRoles bitmask; will become <code>list[CVDRoles]</code> after TASK-BTND5.4).</li> <li>A BT Node reads from and writes to the Blackboard and may emit Outbound Activities.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#flagged-ambiguities","title":"Flagged Ambiguities","text":"<ol> <li>\"State\" (multiple meanings):</li> <li>The Case State (CS) \u2014 the 40-state lattice combining Vendor Fix Path (vfd, Vfd, VFd, VFD, \u2205) and Public State (pxa combinations).</li> <li>An EM State \u2014 a single Embargo Management state (None, Proposed, Active, Revise, eXited).</li> <li>An RM State \u2014 a single Report Management state (Start, Received, Invalid, Valid, Accepted, Deferred, Closed).</li> <li>A Composite State \u2014 a participant's complete state tuple (q^cs, q^rm, q^em).</li> <li>A Case Status \u2014 a snapshot of all three machine states at one moment.</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Always qualify: \"Case State lattice,\" \"EM State,\" \"RM State,\" \"Composite State,\" or \"Case Status.\"</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Message\" vs. \"Activity\" vs. \"Message Type\":</p> </li> <li>A Message Type is a formal protocol category (e.g., RS, EP, CV) from the 29-message set.</li> <li>An Activity is the ActivityStreams 2.0 JSON wire format carrying a Message Type.</li> <li>A State-Change Notification is the abstract principle that every transition should generate a message.</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Use \"Message Type\" for protocol formalism; \"Activity\" for wire transport; \"Semantic Type\" for the extracted intent.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Participant\" vs. \"Actor\" vs. \"Process\":</p> </li> <li>An Actor is any URI-identified federated peer (federation concept).</li> <li>A Participant is an Actor actively engaged in a specific Case.</li> <li>A Process is a formal state machine in the mathematical protocol specification.</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Use \"Actor\" for federation; \"Participant\" for case-specific engagement; \"Process\" for formal protocol math.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Reachable\" vs. \"Valid\":</p> </li> <li>Reachable = logically possible given protocol constraints (state might not occur in practice but conforms to rules).</li> <li>Valid = permissible per protocol rules (state satisfies all constraints).</li> <li>Unreachable = impossible due to hard constraints (violates protocol rules).</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Use \"Reachable State\" for logical possibility; \"Valid Transition\" for rule compliance.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"CS Event\" vs. \"Message Type\":</p> </li> <li>A CS Event (V, F, D, P, X, A) is a formal state transition in the Case State lattice.</li> <li>A Message Type (CV, CF, CD, CP, CX, CA) is the protocol message announcing that event.</li> <li>Not all CS Events are announced (e.g., a Vendor may internally transition Vfd\u2192VFd without sending CF).</li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Use \"CS Event\" for state transitions; \"Message Type\" for protocol announcements.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Activity\" direction \u2014 The term Activity is used for both Inbound and Outbound. Both follow the same ActivityStreams 2.0 format, but the context (inbox vs. outbox, received vs. constructed) determines meaning.</p> </li> <li> <p>Recommendation: Always qualify as \"inbound Activity\" or \"outbound Activity\" when direction matters.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Factory\" scope \u2014 The term Factory can mean:</p> </li> <li>The set of Factory Functions in the <code>vultron/wire/as2/factories/</code> package (the public API)</li> <li>A single Factory Function (e.g., <code>create_report()</code>)</li> <li> <p>Never use \"factory\" to mean \"the place where activities are made\" \u2014 always say Factory Function or factories package.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Port\" (hexagonal vs. TCP) \u2014 In this domain, Port always means a hexagonal architecture interface contract, never a TCP port. No TCP concepts are in scope.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Deprecated\" methods \u2014 <code>get()</code> and <code>by_type()</code> on DataLayer are called \"deprecated\" but still exist in the codebase. This means they are marked for removal and are being gradually replaced by Narrow Ports (CasePersistence, CaseOutboxPersistence) and Rehydration. \"Deprecated\" \u2260 \"removed yet.\"</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Narrow\" vs. \"broad\" ports \u2014 A Narrow Port is small, typed, domain-specific (e.g., <code>CasePersistence</code> with <code>read_by_id()</code>, <code>list_by_status()</code>). A broad port is generic and untyped (e.g., <code>DataLayer</code> with <code>get(table, id)</code>, <code>by_type(type)</code>). The goal is to replace broad ports with Narrow Ports to improve type safety and reduce coupling.</p> </li> <li> <p>\"Report\" vs. \"Case\":</p> <ul> <li>A Report is a one-time vulnerability notification from a Reporter.</li> <li>A Case is the ongoing coordination container with state machines (RM, EM, CS) and participants.</li> <li>Multiple Reports may consolidate into one Case; one Report may split into multiple Cases.</li> <li>Recommendation: \"Report\" = inbound notification; \"Case\" = coordination entity with state machines.</li> </ul> </li> <li> <p>\"Embargo\" vs. \"Embargo Consent\":</p> <ul> <li>An Embargo is a shared agreement (one per Case) that all parties will not disclose until a certain date.</li> <li>Embargo Consent is each Participant's individual acceptance or rejection of that embargo.</li> <li>Recommendation: If discussing terms/dates, say \"Embargo\"; if discussing one party's stance, say \"Embargo Consent\".</li> </ul> </li> </ol>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#example-dialogues","title":"Example Dialogues","text":""},{"location":"reference/glossary/#formal-protocol-structure-example","title":"Formal Protocol Structure Example","text":"<p>Protocol Designer: \"OK, so in the formal model, we have N processes. What exactly is a process?\"</p> <p>Formal Spec Expert: \"Each Participant in a Case runs one Process. A Process maintains a Composite State \u2014 that's a 3-tuple: (Case State, RM State, EM State).\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"So if a Vendor is in the case, they're one Process?\"</p> <p>Formal Spec Expert: \"Exactly. The Vendor runs their own RM state machine, tracks the shared EM state, and maintains their part of the Case State (Vendor Fix Path). Meanwhile, a Coordinator in the same Case is another Process with their own RM state and the same EM state.\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"And they coordinate through Message Types?\"</p> <p>Formal Spec Expert: \"Yes. When a Vendor transitions their RM from Valid to Accepted, they send an RA (Report Accepted) Message Type. That Activity carries the RA and is transmitted via the wire protocol. The Coordinator receives it and updates their model of the Vendor's RM State.\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"But that doesn't change the Coordinator's own RM state?\"</p> <p>Formal Spec Expert: \"Correct. The RA message is an announcement \u2014 'I accepted the report.' It doesn't command the receiver to do anything. Only Report Submission (RS) directly changes receiver RM state.\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"What about CS Events \u2014 if the Vendor completes a fix, do they have to send a message?\"</p> <p>Formal Spec Expert: \"Not technically. The Vendor internal transitions from Vfd to VFd is a CS Event (F). But per the protocol, they SHOULD announce it by sending a CF (Fix Readiness) message. However, the message and the state transition are distinct \u2014 one doesn't require the other formally.\"</p>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#architecture-example","title":"Architecture Example","text":"<p>Architect: \"OK, so our issue is that <code>vultron/core/use_cases/triggers/sync.py</code> calls <code>from_core()</code> \u2014 but <code>from_core()</code> is in the wire layer. That violates ARCH-03-001.\"</p> <p>Developer: \"Right, I see it: <code>CaseLogEntry.from_core(entry)</code>. Why can't the core just call that?\"</p> <p>Architect: \"Because core has no imports from wire. The solution is TASK-AF: we create Factory Functions in <code>vultron/wire/as2/factories/</code>. Then instead of core calling <code>from_core()</code>, we have a driven Port \u2014 an Activity Translator adapter \u2014 that core calls with a domain object, and the adapter uses the Factory Function to build the wire Activity.\"</p> <p>Developer: \"So the Factory Function is in wire, and core doesn't see it?\"</p> <p>Architect: \"Exactly. Core only knows about the Port interface. The adapter implements it, and the adapter has the wire imports. Core stays clean.\"</p> <p>Developer: \"Got it. And once TASK-AF lands, we can fix the hexagonal violations in sync.py?\"</p> <p>Architect: \"Yes \u2014 that's TASK-ARCHVIO. After Factory Functions exist, we introduce the Activity Translator Port and move <code>from_core()</code> calls from core to the adapter. Then ARCH-03-001 is satisfied.\"</p> <p>Developer: \"And what about TASK-CP-CLEANUP? That's about removing <code>get()</code> and <code>by_type()</code>, right?\"</p> <p>Architect: \"Yes. Currently core uses the broad DataLayer Port and calls <code>get(table, id)</code>. We want core to use Narrow Ports like CasePersistence with typed methods like <code>read_by_id()</code>. Once all 64 call sites migrate, we remove the old Compatibility Methods.\"</p>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#cvd-domain-example","title":"CVD Domain Example","text":"<p>Protocol Designer: \"When a Vendor receives a Report, do they immediately become a Participant in the Case?\"</p> <p>Domain Expert: \"Not automatically. A Report is created by a Reporter and assigned to a Case. The Vendor becomes a Participant only after the Case Owner sends an Offer Activity and the Vendor sends an Accept Activity. Until then, the Vendor is invited but not yet participating.\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"So if a Vendor doesn't respond to the Offer, they don't show up in the Case participant list?\"</p> <p>Domain Expert: \"Correct. The Case tracks only actual Participants. An unresponded Offer is a protocol event we record, but it doesn't create a Participant.\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"What happens to the RM state if a Vendor joins the Case late\u2014after we've already transitioned to PUBLISHED?\"</p> <p>Domain Expert: \"Their Participant Status records RM states from the point they joined onward. They won't have history for earlier states, but their RM state machine can still advance through valid transitions. The Case Status is separate\u2014it shows the collective state across all parties.\"</p> <p>Protocol Designer: \"And when we send an Activity about a State Transition to all participants, we're not telling them to do the transition\u2014we're informing them that we did it?\"</p> <p>Domain Expert: \"Exactly. An Activity is a state-change notification, not a command. If I send you an Activity saying 'Fix Deployed' (the D event), I'm informing you that my organization deployed the fix. You update your model of the Case State and potentially make your own decisions based on that new information.\"</p>"},{"location":"reference/glossary/#metadata","title":"Metadata","text":"<ul> <li>Source: Vultron codebase, CERT/CC CVD research publications, architecture audit, formal protocol specification</li> <li>Last Updated: 2026-04-30</li> <li>Domains: Formal MPCVD protocol, CVD process models (RM/EM/CS), communicating state machines, hexagonal architecture, activity pattern matching, persistence abstraction, behavior tree orchestration</li> <li>Related References:</li> <li>A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CMU/SEI-2021-SR-021)</li> <li>Designing Vultron: A Protocol for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure</li> <li>CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (v2.0)</li> <li><code>docs/topics/process_models/</code> \u2014 detailed RM, EM, CS models</li> <li><code>docs/reference/formal_protocol/</code> \u2014 formal protocol specification</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/","title":"Inbox Handler Design","text":"<p>Vultron is designed with the ActivityPub protocol in mind. Most activity in Vultron involves actors exchanging activity messages containing objects.</p> <p>Submitting a Vulnerability Report</p> <p>For example, an actor might create a <code>VulnerabilityReport</code> object and then offer it to another actor, who can then accept, tentatively reject, or  reject the offer.</p> <p>Actors in ActivityPub can be of various types, such as <code>Person</code>, <code>Organization</code>, <code>Group</code>, <code>Application</code>, <code>Service</code>, etc. Each actor has an inbox and an outbox. The inbox is where the actor receives activities, and the outbox is where the actor sends activities.</p> <p>Actor Inboxes and Outboxes</p> <p>For example, if Alice is a <code>Person</code> actor, she has an inbox at <code>https://example.com/users/alice/inbox</code> and an outbox at <code>https://example.com/users/alice/outbox</code>. If Bob is another <code>Person</code> actor, he has his own inbox and outbox.</p> <p>When Alice wants to send an activity to Bob, she creates the activity with the <code>to</code> field set to Bob's actor ID (e.g., <code>https://example.com/users/bob</code>) and places it in her outbox. The ActivityPub server then delivers the activity to Bob's inbox. Bob can then process the activity and respond by sending an activity to his outbox, with the <code>to</code> field set to Alice's actor ID. He can also set the <code>inReplyTo</code> field to reference the original activity from Alice to indicate that it is a response. The ActivityPub server handles the delivery of the activity to Alice's inbox,  and the cycle continues.</p> <p>Scope of Vultron's ActivityPub Implementation</p> <p>However, in our Vultron prototype, we are not implementing a full ActivityPub server. Instead, we're initially focusing on the core functionality of actors sending activities directly to each other's inboxes. This simplifies the implementation while still allowing us to demonstrate the core concepts of ActivityPub interactions.</p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#inbox-handler-architecture","title":"Inbox Handler Architecture","text":"<p>Our initial architecture is as follows: An actor has an inbox implemented as a FastAPI endpoint that accepts POST requests containing activity messages. When a message is received, the inbox handler processes the activity and routes it to an appropriate handler based on the activity's semantics.</p> <p>Activity Handling</p> <p>For example, if the activity is an <code>Offer</code> of a <code>VulnerabilityReport</code>, it would be routed to a <code>submit_report</code> handler function, which contains the logic for processing a vulnerability report submission.</p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#out-of-scope","title":"Out of scope","text":"<p>We are deferring authentication, authorization, and server-to-server federation for future implementation.</p> <p>This design also does not describe what happens inside each handler function; that is left to the specific handler implementations to come later. The focus of this design is on the inbox handler and dispatching logic that routes activities to the appropriate handler functions.</p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#inbox-handler-process","title":"Inbox Handler Process","text":"<p>The inbox handler process consists of the following steps:</p> <ol> <li>Receive Activity: The inbox endpoint receives a POST request containing an activity message in JSON format.</li> <li>Validate Activity: The inbox endpoint validates the activity message structure to ensure it conforms to the ActivityPub specification.</li> <li>Log Activity: The inbox endpoint logs the received activity message for debugging and auditing purposes.</li> <li>Queue Activity: (only if Validate Activity succeeds) The inbox endpoint places the activity message into an asynchronous processing call to the inbox handler function, allowing the endpoint to respond immediately while the activity is processed in the background.</li> <li>Acknowledge Receipt: The inbox endpoint responds to the POST request with a 200 OK status to acknowledge receipt of the activity message. If the validation failed, it can respond with an appropriate error status (e.g., 400 Bad Request) and log the error.</li> <li>Extract Routing Information: The inbox handler extracts key fields from the activity message (e.g., <code>type</code>, <code>object</code>, <code>to</code>, <code>inReplyTo</code>) to determine routing.    It creates a <code>DispatchEvent</code> header object containing the routing information and attaches the original activity message as the payload.</li> <li>Dispatch Activity: The inbox handler invokes a dispatch function that routes the <code>DispatchEvent</code> object to the appropriate handler function based on the activity's semantic type.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#activity-patterns-and-semantics","title":"Activity Patterns and Semantics","text":"<p>Routing semantics are identified by <code>ActivityPattern</code> objects, which define patterns for matching activity messages to specific enumerated semantic meanings. This decouples the raw activity message structure from the higher-level semantics of the Vultron protocol, allowing us to translate raw messages into meaningful semantics that drive application logic.</p> <p>Defining Activity Patterns</p> <p>We might define an activity pattern for submitting a vulnerability report as an <code>Offer</code> activity with an object of type <code>VulnerabilityReport</code>.  Responses to that offer follow their own patterns:</p> <p><code>python ReportSubmission = ActivityPattern(     activity_=TAtype.OFFER,     object_=VOtype.VULNERABILITY_REPORT, ) AckReport = ActivityPattern(activity_=TAtype.READ, object_=ReportSubmission) ValidateReport = ActivityPattern(     activity_=TAtype.ACCEPT, object_=ReportSubmission ) InvalidateReport = ActivityPattern(     activity_=TAtype.TENTATIVE_REJECT, object_=ReportSubmission ) CloseReport = ActivityPattern(     activity_=TAtype.REJECT, object_=ReportSubmission )</code></p> <p>The various <code>*type</code>s are string enumerations defined elsewhere.</p> <p>Mapping Activity Patterns to Semantics</p> <p>We define the mapping from raw activity message patterns to higher-level semantics:</p> <p><code>python SEMANTICS_ACTIVITY_PATTERNS: dict[MessageSemantics, ActivityPattern] = {     MessageSemantics.SUBMIT_REPORT: ap.ReportSubmission,     MessageSemantics.ACK_REPORT: ap.AckReport,     MessageSemantics.VALIDATE_REPORT: ap.ValidateReport,     MessageSemantics.INVALIDATE_REPORT: ap.InvalidateReport,     MessageSemantics.CLOSE_REPORT: ap.CloseReport,     # ...etc. }</code></p> <p>Activity semantics are determined by the <code>type</code> field of the activity message, along with fields such as <code>object</code>, <code>to</code>, and <code>inReplyTo</code>. The dispatch function uses this information to determine which handler function should process the activity.</p> <p>DispatchEvent Object</p> <p>A <code>DispatchEvent</code> object encapsulates the routing information needed by the dispatch function. At the time of writing, the <code>DispatchEvent</code> object is defined as:</p> <p><code>python @dataclass class DispatchEvent:     semantic_type: MessageSemantics     activity_id: str     payload: InboundPayload</code></p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#dispatch-function","title":"Dispatch Function","text":"<p>The dispatch function uses the <code>semantic_type</code> field of the <code>DispatchEvent</code> to look up the appropriate handler function from a mapping of <code>MessageSemantics</code> to handler functions. The handler function is then invoked with the <code>DispatchEvent</code> object as an argument.</p> <p>Example</p> <p>The following is an example of the <code>ActivityDispatcher</code> Protocol that defines   the interface for dispatching activities:</p> <p>```python   class ActivityDispatcher(Protocol):       \"\"\"       Protocol for dispatching activities to their corresponding handlers based on message semantics.       \"\"\"</p> <pre><code>  def dispatch(self, dispatchable: DispatchEvent) -&gt; None:\n      \"\"\"Dispatches an activity to the appropriate handler based on its semantic type.\"\"\"\n      ...\n</code></pre> <p>```</p> <p>On the Modularity of Dispatchers</p> <p>The dispatch function is designed as a Python <code>Protocol</code> to allow for different dispatcher implementations. This provides flexibility in connecting the inbox  handler to specific handler functions for each semantic type. A simple  implementation might use a dictionary mapping from semantic types to handler  functions, while a more complex implementation could involve message queues  or other routing mechanisms.</p> <p>This modularity allows us to easily extend dispatching logic by defining new  <code>ActivityPattern</code>s with corresponding <code>MessageSemantics</code>, then adding new  handler functions to the dispatch mapping. By decoupling routing logic from  handling logic, we maintain clean separation of concerns and facilitate  future protocol extensions.</p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#direct-dispatch-implementation","title":"Direct Dispatch Implementation","text":"<p>Our first dispatch function implementation uses a simple direct dispatch approach with a dictionary mapping from <code>MessageSemantics</code> to handler functions. The dispatch function looks up the <code>semantic_type</code> from the <code>DispatchEvent</code> object in the mapping and invokes the corresponding handler function with the <code>DispatchEvent</code> as an argument.</p> <p>This direct dispatch implementation is straightforward and allows us to quickly begin handling activities based on their semantics.</p> <p>Into the Unknown?</p> <p>What happens if an activity is received that does not match any known <code>ActivityPattern</code>?   In this case, the semantic match function can return a special   <code>MessageSemantics.UNKNOWN</code> value, which the dispatch function can handle by   logging an error or ignoring the activity. This allows us to gracefully handle   unexpected or malformed activities without crashing the system. In fact, if we   implement this early, we will be able to detect and log any activities that   don't match our defined patterns, which will be useful to identify any issues   with our pattern definitions, omitted patterns, or unexpected activity messages   during testing and development.</p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#handler-functions","title":"Handler Functions","text":"<p>Handler functions contain the logic for processing specific types of activities based on their semantics. Each handler function is responsible for implementing the application logic for a particular semantic type, such as processing a vulnerability report submission, acknowledging receipt of a report, validating a report, etc.</p> <p>Defining Handler Functions</p> <p>Following is an example of how handler functions can be defined for each  semantic type. Each handler function takes a <code>DispatchEvent</code> object as an argument and contains the logic for processing that specific type of activity.</p> <p>```python class BehaviorHandler(Protocol):     \"\"\"     Protocol for behavior handler functions.     \"\"\"</p> <pre><code>def __call__(self, dispatchable: DispatchEvent) -&gt; None: ...\n</code></pre> <p><code>`` So a potential handler function for the</code>SUBMIT_REPORT` semantic type might look like this:</p> <p><code>python def handle_submit_report(dispatchable: DispatchEvent) -&gt; None:     # logic for processing a vulnerability report submission goes here     ...</code></p>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#development-goals","title":"Development Goals","text":""},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#phase-1-core-infrastructure","title":"Phase 1: Core Infrastructure","text":"<ul> <li> Implement actor-specific inbox handler as a FastAPI POST endpoint (<code>/inbox</code>) that receives activity messages in JSON format (<code>vultron.api.v2.routers.actors.post_actor_inbox</code>)</li> <li> Create activity validation logic to ensure conformance with ActivityPub specification (<code>vultron.api.v2.routers.actors.parse_activity</code>)</li> <li> Define core <code>ActivityPattern</code> class and implement pattern matching logic for routing semantics (<code>vultron.activity_patterns.ActivityPattern</code>)</li> <li> Create <code>MessageSemantics</code> enumeration for Vultron protocol semantics (<code>vultron.behavior_dispatcher.DirectActivityDispatcher</code>)</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/inbox_handler/#phase-3-handler-stubs","title":"Phase 3: Handler Stubs","text":"<ul> <li> Create
