request through dispatch to handler invocation</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/","title":"Interactions Between the Vultron Protocol and SSVC","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>In the context of the Vultron Protocol, once a report has been validated (i.e., it is in the RM Valid state, \\(q^{rm} \\in V\\)), it must be prioritized to determine what further effort, if any, is necessary. While any prioritization scheme might be used, here we demonstrate an application of the SSVC model.</p> <p> TODO merge with SSVC section of Situation Awareness </p>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#ssvc-supplier-and-deployer-trees","title":"SSVC Supplier and Deployer Trees","text":"<p>The default outcomes for both the SSVC Supplier and Deployer Trees are Defer, Scheduled, Out of Cycle, and Immediate. The mapping from SSVC outcomes to RM states is straightforward, as shown below for the Supplier Tree and the Deployer Tree.</p> <p>SSVC Supplier Tree Mapping to RM States</p> \\[ q^{rm} \\in \\begin{cases}     \\xrightarrow{d} D &amp; \\text{when } SSVC(Supplier~Tree) = Defer \\\\     \\\\     \\xrightarrow{a} A &amp; \\text{when } SSVC(Supplier~Tree) \\in       \\begin{Bmatrix}         Scheduled \\\\         Out~of~Cycle \\\\         Immediate \\\\     \\end{Bmatrix} \\\\ \\end{cases}\\] <p>SSVC Deployer Tree Mapping to RM States</p> \\[ q^{rm} \\in \\begin{cases}     \\xrightarrow{d} D &amp; \\text{when } SSVC(Deployer~Tree) = Defer \\\\     \\\\     \\xrightarrow{a} A &amp; \\text{when } SSVC(Deployer~Tree) \\in       \\begin{Bmatrix}         Scheduled \\\\         Out~of~Cycle \\\\         Immediate \\\\     \\end{Bmatrix} \\\\ \\end{cases}\\] <p>The SSVC Defer output maps directly onto the RM Deferred state. Otherwise, the three outputs that imply further action is necessary\u2014Scheduled_, Out-of-Cycle, and Immediate\u2014all proceed to the RM Accepted state. The different categories imply different processes within the Accepted state. But because the RM model does not dictate internal organizational processes, further description of what those processes might look like is out of scope for this crosswalk.</p> <p>We remind readers of a key takeaway from the protocol requirements in the main part of this documentation:</p> <p>Vendors SHOULD communicate their prioritization choices when making either a defer (\\(\\{V,A\\} \\xrightarrow{d} D\\)) or accept (\\(\\{V,D\\} \\xrightarrow{a} A\\)) transition out of the Valid, Deferred, or Accepted states.</p>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#ssvc-coordinator-trees","title":"SSVC Coordinator Trees","text":"<p>SSVC version 2 offers two decision trees for Coordinators: A Coordinator Triage Tree and a Coordinator Publish Tree. The outputs for the Coordinator Triage Decision Tree are Decline, Track, and Coordinate. Similar to the Supplier Tree mapping above, the mapping here is simple, as shown below.</p> <p>SSVC Coordinator Triage Tree Mapped to RM States</p> \\[ q^{rm} \\in \\begin{cases}     \\xrightarrow{d} D &amp; \\text{when } SSVC(Coord.~Triage~Tree) = Decline \\\\     \\\\     \\xrightarrow{a} A &amp; \\text{when } SSVC(Coord.~Triage~Tree) \\in       \\begin{Bmatrix}         Track \\\\         Coordinate \\\\     \\end{Bmatrix} \\\\ \\end{cases}\\] <p>Again, whereas the Decline output maps directly to the RM Deferred state, the remaining two states (Track and Coordinate) imply the necessity for distinct processes within the Coordinator's RM Accepted state.</p> <p>On the other hand, the SSVC Coordinator Publish Tree falls entirely within the Coordinator's Accepted state, so its output does not directly induce any Coordinator RM state transitions. However, a number of its decision points do touch on the protocol models, which we cover next.</p>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#ssvc-decision-points-and-the-vultron-protocol","title":"SSVC Decision Points and the Vultron Protocol","text":"<p>Additional connections between the protocol and the SSVC decision trees are possible. We now examine how individual SSVC tree decision points can inform or be informed by Participant states in the Vultron Protocol.</p>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#exploitation","title":"Exploitation","text":"<p>The SSVC Exploitation decision point permits three possible values:</p> <ul> <li>None</li> <li>PoC</li> <li>Active</li> </ul> <p>These values map directly onto state subsets in the Case State (CS) model, as shown below.</p> <p>SSVC Exploitation Decision Point Mapped to CS States</p> \\[ SSVC(exploitation) = \\begin{cases}     None &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot xa \\\\     PoC &amp; \\iff  q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot Xa \\\\     Active &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot A \\\\ \\end{cases}\\] <p>A value of None implies that no exploits have been made public, and no attacks have been observed (i.e., \\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot xa\\)). The PoC value means that an exploit is public, but no attacks have been observed (i.e., \\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot Xa\\)). Finally, the Active value indicates attacks are occurring (i.e., \\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot A\\)). These case states and SSVC values are equivalent in both directions, hence our use of the \"if-and-only-if\" (\\(\\iff\\)) symbol.</p>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#report-public","title":"Report Public","text":"<p>The SSVC Report Public decision point also maps directly onto the CS model. A value of Yes means that the report is public, equivalent to \\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot P \\cdot\\cdot\\). On the other hand, a No value is the same as \\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot p \\cdot\\cdot\\). As above, \"\\(\\iff\\)\" indicates the bidirectional equivalence.</p> <p>SSVC Report Public Decision Point Mapped to CS States</p> \\[SSVC(report~public) =  \\begin{cases}     Yes &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot P \\cdot\\cdot \\\\     No &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot p \\cdot\\cdot \\\\ \\end{cases}\\]"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#supplier-contacted","title":"Supplier Contacted","text":"<p>If the Supplier (Vendor) has been notified (i.e., there is reason to believe they are at least in the RM Received state, equivalent to the \\(V\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\) CS state subset) the Supplier Contacted value should be Yes, otherwise it should be No.</p> <p>SSVC Supplier Contacted Decision Point Mapped to RM States</p> \\[ SSVC(supp.~contacted) =  \\begin{cases}     Yes &amp; \\iff q^{rm}_{Vendor} \\not \\in S \\text{ or } q^{cs}_{Vendor} \\in V\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot \\\\     \\\\     No &amp; \\iff q^{rm}_{Vendor} \\in S \\text{ or } q^{cs}_{Vendor} \\in vfd \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot \\\\ \\end{cases} \\]"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#report-credibility","title":"Report Credibility","text":"<p>Unlike most of the other SSVC decision points covered here that form a part of a Participant's report prioritization process after report validation, the Report Credibility decision point forms an important step in the Coordinator's validation process. In fact, it is often the only validation step possible when the Coordinator lacks the ability to reproduce a vulnerability whether due to constraints of resources, time, or skill. Thus, a value of Credible can be expected to lead to an RM transition to Valid (\\(q^{rm} \\in R \\xrightarrow{v} V\\)), assuming any additional validation checks also pass. On the contrary, Not-Credible always implies the RM transition to Invalid (\\(q^{rm} \\in R \\xrightarrow{i} I\\)) because \"Valid-but-not-Credible\" is a contradiction.</p> <p>SSVC Report Credibility Decision Point Mapped to RM States</p> \\[ SSVC(report~cred.) =  \\begin{cases}     Credible &amp; \\text{implies }q^{rm} \\xrightarrow{v} V \\textrm{ (if validation also passes)}\\\\     Not~Credible &amp; \\text{implies } q^{rm} \\xrightarrow{i} I \\\\ \\end{cases} \\]"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#public-value-added","title":"Public Value Added","text":"<p>The SSVC Public Value Added decision point can take on the values Precedence, Ampliative, or Limited. Precedence means that publication adds value by providing information that is not widely known. Ampliative means that publication might providing additional information or reach to information that may or may not already be public. Limited means that publication impact might be limited because the information is already widely known.</p> <p>SSVC Public Value Added Decision Point Mapped to CS States</p> \\[ SSVC(pva) =      \\begin{cases}         Precedence &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot p\\cdot\\cdot \\\\         Ampliative &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in VFdp\\cdot\\cdot \\textrm{ or } q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot dP\\cdot\\cdot \\\\         Limited &amp; \\iff q^{cs} \\in VF\\cdot P\\cdot \\\\     \\end{cases}\\]"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#supplier-engagement","title":"Supplier Engagement","text":"<p>The possible values for the Supplier (Vendor) Engagement decision point are Active or Unresponsive. From the Coordinator's perspective, if enough Suppliers in a CVD case have communicated their engagement in a case (i.e., enough Vendors are in the RM Accepted state already or are expected to make it there soon from either the Received or Valid states), then the SSVC value would be Active.</p> <p>Vendors in Invalid or Closed can be taken as disengaged, and it might be appropriate to select Unresponsive for the SSVC Engagement decision point.</p> <p>Vendors in either Received or Deferred might be either Active or Unresponsive, depending on the specific report history.</p> <p>This mapping is formalized below and in the figure that follows.</p> <p>SSVC Supplier Engagement Decision Point Mapped to RM States</p> \\[ SSVC(supp.~eng.) =  \\begin{cases}     Active &amp; \\text{if } q^{rm} \\in \\{A,V\\} \\\\     \\\\     \\begin{cases}         Active \\\\         Unresponsive     \\end{cases} &amp; \\text{if } q^{rm} \\in \\{R,D\\} \\\\     \\\\     Unresponsive &amp; \\text{if } q^{rm} \\in \\{I,C,S\\} \\\\ \\end{cases}\\] <pre><code>---\ntitle: \"RM States and SSVC Supplier Engagement\"\n---\ngraph LR\n    subgraph ssvc_se[SSVC Supplier Engagement]\n        Active\n        Unresponsive\n    end\n    subgraph rm_states[RM States]\n        S[Start]\n        R[Received]\n        I[Invalid]\n        V[Valid]\n        D[Deferred]\n        A[Accepted]\n        C[Closed]\n    end\n    A --&gt; Active\n    V --&gt; Active\n    R --&gt; Active\n    D --&gt; Active\n    R--&gt; Unresponsive\n    D --&gt; Unresponsive\n    I --&gt; Unresponsive\n    C --&gt; Unresponsive\n    S --&gt; Unresponsive</code></pre>"},{"location":"reference/ssvc_crosswalk/#supplier-involvement","title":"Supplier Involvement","text":"<p>The Supplier Involvement decision point can take on the values Fix-Ready, Cooperative, or Uncooperative/Unresponsive. We begin by noting the equivalence of the Fix-Ready value with the similarly named substate of the CS model.</p> <p>SSVC Supplier Involvement Decision Point Mapped to CS States</p> \\[\\begin{aligned}     SSVC(supp.~inv.) = Fix~Ready \\iff q^{cs} \\in VF \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot \\end{aligned}\\] <p>The Vendor RM states map onto these values as formalized below and shown in the figure below.</p> <p>SSVC Supplier Involvement Decision Point Mapped to Vendor RM States</p> \\[\\begin{aligned} SSVC(supp.~inv.) =  \\begin{cases}     \\begin{cases}         Fix~Ready \\\\         Cooperative     \\end{cases} &amp; \\text{if } q^{rm} \\in A \\\\     \\\\     Cooperative &amp; \\text{if } q^{rm} \\in V \\\\     \\\\     Uncoop./Unresp. &amp; \\text{if } q^{rm} \\in \\{R,I,D,C,S\\} \\\\ \\end{cases} \\end{aligned}\\] <pre><code>---\ntitle: \"RM States and SSVC Supplier Involvement\"\n---\ngraph LR\n    subgraph rm_states[RM States]\n        S[Start]\n        R[Received]\n        I[Invalid]\n        V[Valid]\n        D[Deferred]\n        A[Accepted]\n        C[Closed]\n    end\n\n    subgraph ssvc_si[SSVC Supplier Involvement]\n        fr[Fix-Ready]\n        co[Cooperative]\n        un[Uncooperative/Unresponsive]\n    end\n    A --&gt; fr\n    A --&gt; co\n    V --&gt; co\n    R --&gt; un\n    D --&gt; un\n    I --&gt; un\n    C --&gt; un\n    S --&gt; un</code></pre> <p>Engagement vs. Involvement: What's the Difference?</p> <p>Note the discrepancy between the mappings given for SSVC Supplier Engagement versus those for Supplier Involvement. This distinction is most prominent in the connections from the Received and Deferred RM states in the two figures above. These differences are the result of the relative timing of the two different decisions they support within a CVD case.</p> <p>The decision to Coordinate (i.e., whether the Coordinator should move from RM Valid to RM Accept  (\\(q^{rm} \\in V \\xrightarrow{a} A\\)) occurs early in the Coordinator's RM process. The SSVC Supplier Engagement decision point is an attempt to capture this information. This early in the process, allowances must be made for Vendors who may not have completed their own validation or  prioritization processes.  Hence, the mapping allows Vendors in any valid yet unclosed state (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{R,V,A,D\\}\\)) to be categorized as  Active for this decision point.</p> <p>On the other hand, the decision to Publish\u2014a choice that falls entirely within the Coordinator's RM Accepted  state\u2014occurs later, at which time, more is known about each Vendor's level of involvement in the case to that point.  By the time the publication decision is made, the Vendor(s) have had ample opportunity to engage in the CVD process. They might already have a Fix-Ready (\\(q^{cs} \\in VF \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\)), or they might be working toward it (i.e., SSVC Cooperative). However, if the Coordinator has reached the point where they are making a publication decision, and the Vendor has  yet to actively engage in the case for whatever reason\u2014as indicated by their failure to reach the RM Accepted state or demonstrate progress toward it by at least getting to RM Valid  (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{A,V\\}\\))\u2014then they can be categorized as Uncooperative/Unresponsive.</p> <p>SSVC Decision Points and Anticipated Transitions</p> <p>Other SSVC decision points may be informative about which transitions to expect in a case. Two examples apply here:</p> <ol> <li>Supplier Engagement acts to gauge the likelihood of the F transitions. Coordination becomes more necessary the lower that likelihood is.</li> <li>Utility (the usefulness of the exploit to the adversary) acts to gauge the likelihood of the A transition.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"reference/case_states/","title":"Index","text":""},{"location":"reference/case_states/#case-states","title":"Case States","text":"<p>This directory contains a description of each A"},{"location":"reference/code/","title":"Vultron Modules","text":""},{"location":"reference/code/#vultron","title":"<code>vultron</code>","text":"<p>The <code>vultron</code> package is a collection of modules that provide the functionality for the Vultron project.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/#vultron.adapters","title":"<code>vultron.adapters</code>","text":"<p>Adapters layer for the Vultron hexagonal architecture.</p> <p>This package contains thin translation layers that connect the core domain to external systems. No domain logic, no AS2 parsing, no semantic extraction should live here \u2014 only translation and dispatch.</p> <p>Sub-packages:</p> <ul> <li> <p><code>driving/</code> \u2014 Driving (left-side) adapters that trigger the core.   Examples: HTTP inbox, CLI, MCP server.</p> </li> <li> <p><code>driven/</code> \u2014 Driven (right-side) adapters that the core calls out to.   Examples: activity store, delivery queue, HTTP delivery.</p> </li> <li> <p><code>connectors/</code> \u2014 Bidirectional connector plugins (tracker integrations,   third-party systems). Each plugin translates between external events and   Vultron domain events.</p> </li> </ul> <p>See <code>notes/architecture-ports-and-adapters.md</code> for the full design.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/#vultron.core","title":"<code>vultron.core</code>","text":"<p>Core domain layer for the Vultron Protocol.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/#vultron.demo","title":"<code>vultron.demo</code>","text":"<p>Provides demo functionality for Vultron behaviors.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/#vultron.wire","title":"<code>vultron.wire</code>","text":"<p>Wire layer for the Vultron Protocol.</p> <p>Handles parsing and semantic extraction of inbound ActivityStreams activities. This layer sits between the driving adapters (HTTP, CLI, MCP) and the core domain layer.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/#vultron.errors","title":"<code>vultron.errors</code>","text":"<p>The published, and attacks have been observed.</li> <li><code>PxA</code> indicates the public is aware, no exploit has been published, and attacks have been observed.</li> <li><code>Pxa</code> indicates the public is aware, an exploit has been published, and no attacks have been observed.</li> <li><code>PXA</code> indicates the public is aware, an exploit has been published, and attacks have been observed.</li> </ul> <p>Note that pXa and pXA are not valid states because once an exploit is published, the public is function</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/case_states/#vultron.core.case_states.validations.ensure_valid_state","title":"<code>ensure_valid_state(func)</code>","text":"<p>Function Decorator to ensure a valid state is passed to a function</p> <p>Example</p> <p><code>python @ensure_valid_state def my_func(state, ...):     ...</code></p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>func</code> <code>F</code> <p>the function to decorate</p> required <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>F</code> <p>the decorated function</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/case_states/#vultron.core.case_states.validations.ensure_valid_state_method_wrapper","title":"<code>ensure_valid_state_method_wrapper(func)</code>","text":"<p>Method Decorator to ensure a valid state is passed to a method. Equivalent to ensure_valid_state, but for methods.</p> <p>Example</p> <p><code>python class MyClass:     @ensure_valid_state_method_wrapper     def my_method(self, state, ...):         ...</code></p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>func</code> <code>F</code> <p>the method to decorate</p> required <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>F</code> <p>the decorated method</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/case_states/#vultron.core.case_states.validations.is_valid_history","title":"<code>is_valid_history(h)</code>","text":"<p>Validate a history string. Checks that the history is exactly six characters long, is all uppercase, and contains one each of V, F, D, P, X, and A. Also checks that the causally-related events are in the correct order:</p> <ul> <li>V \\(\\prec\\) F \\(\\prec\\) D</li> <li>P \\(\\prec\\) X or XP</li> <li>V \\(\\prec\\) P or PV</li> </ul> <p>Example</p> <p><code>python is_valid_history(\"VFDPXA\")</code> succeeds, but <code>python is_valid_history(\"VFDPX\")</code> fails with a <code>HistoryValidationError</code>.</p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>h</code> <code>str</code> <p>the history string to validate</p> required <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>None</code> <p>None</p> <p>Raises:</p> Type Description <code>HistoryValidationError</code> <p>if the history is invalid</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/case_states/#vultron.core.case_states.validations.is_valid_pattern","title":"<code>is_valid_pattern(pat)</code>","text":"<p>Validate a pattern string. Patterns are expected to be regular expressions with the following constraints:</p> <ul> <li>The pattern is exactly six characters long.</li> <li>The pattern contains either upper or lower case v, f, d, p, x, a, in that order, or a dot.</li> </ul> <p>Examples:</p> <p><code>python is_valid_pattern(\"vfdpxa\") is_valid_pattern(\"Vfd.X.\")</code> will succeed but <code>python is_valid_pattern(\"xpdfva\") is_valid_pattern(\"vfDPx\")</code> fail with a <code>PatternValidationError</code>.</p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>pat</code> <code>str</code> <p>the pattern string to validate</p> required <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>None</code> <p>None</p> <p>Raises:</p> Type Description <code>PatternValidationError</code> <p>if the pattern is invalid</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/case_states/#vultron.core.case_states.validations.is_valid_state","title":"<code>is_valid_state(state)</code>","text":"<p>Validate a state string. Checks that the state is a valid state pattern. Also checks that the state is not impossible (e.g. <code>vF....</code>, <code>.fD...</code>) Finally, verifies that only allowed symbols (<code>vVfFdDpPxXaA.</code>) are used.</p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>state</code> <code>str</code> <p>the state string to validate</p> required <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>None</code> <p>None</p> <p>Raises:</p> Type Description <code>StateValidationError</code> <p>if the state is invalid</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/case_states/#vultron.core.case_states.validations.is_valid_transition","title":"<code>is_valid_transition(src, dst, allow_null=False)</code>","text":"<p>Validate a transition from src to dst</p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>src</code> <code>str</code> <p>the source state</p> required <code>dst</code> <code>str</code> <p>the destination state</p> required <code>allow_null</code> <code>bool</code> <p>if True, allow null transitions (src==dst), default=False</p> <code>False</code> <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>None</code> <p>None</p> <p>Raises:</p> Type Description <code>TransitionValidationError</code> <p>if the transition is invalid</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/hexagonal_architecture/","title":"Hexagonal Architecture","text":"<p>Our prototype implementation of the Vultron Protocol is structured according to the principles of a port and adapter architecture, also known as hexagonal architecture. This design allows us to separate the core domain logic from the infrastructure and external interfaces, making the system more modular, testable, and adaptable to different use cases and environments.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Hexagonal Architecture Overview\n---\nflowchart LR\n\nadapters1[Driving Adapters]\nadapters2[Driven Adapters]\nports1[Driving Ports]\nports2[Driven Ports]\ncore{{Core Domain Logic}}\nadapters1 --o ports1\nports1 --o core\ncore --o ports2\nports2 --o adapters2</code></pre> <p>In this architecture:</p> <ul> <li>The Driving Adapters implement the driving ports using specific   technologies (e.g., FastAPI endpoints, CLI commands, etc.) and translate   external inputs into calls to the core domain logic.</li> <li>The Driving Ports define the interfaces through which external actors   (e.g., finders, vendors, coordinators) can interact with the core domain logic. These are the entry points for commands and queries that drive the system's behavior.</li> <li>The Core Domain Logic contains the business rules, policies, and behavior trees that govern how the system operates. It is completely agnostic to how it is accessed or what technologies are used to implement the interfaces.</li> <li>The Driven Ports define the interfaces through which the core domain   logic can interact with external systems (e.g., databases, message queues,   external APIs). These are the exit points for the core to perform actions   that have side effects or require external resources.</li> <li>The Driven Adapters implement the driven ports using specific technologies   (e.g., MongoDB for database access, Celery for task queues, etc.)   and   translate calls from the core domain logic into interactions with external   systems.</li> </ul> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Hexagonal Architecture Details\n---\nflowchart LR\n\nsubgraph driving_adapters [Driving Adapters]\n    direction LR\n    api[API Endpoints]\n    cli[CLI Commands]\n    mcp[MCP Tools]\nend\nsubgraph driving_ports [Driving Ports]\n    direction LR\n    use_cases[Use Cases]\n    commands[Commands]\nend\nsubgraph core [Core Domain Logic]\n    direction LR\n    domain_models[Domain Models]\n    services[Domain Services]\n    behavior_trees[Behavior Trees]\nend\nsubgraph driven_ports [Driven Ports]\n    direction LR\n    database[Database Access]\n    message_queue[Message Queue]\n    external_apis[External APIs]\nend\nsubgraph driven_adapters [Driven Adapters]\n    direction LR\n    mongo[MongoDB Adapter]\n    celery[Celery Adapter]\n    vince[VINCE Adapter]\nend\n\ndriving_adapters --&gt; driving_ports\ndriving_ports --&gt; core\ncore --&gt; driven_ports\ndriven_ports --&gt; driven_adapters</code></pre>"},{"location":"reference/code/api/actor_inbox_handler/","title":"Actor Inbox Handler","text":"<p>The Actor Inbox Handler is the hub that processes incoming activities. It</p> <ul> <li>receives activities sent to an actor's inbox</li> <li>looks up the appropriate handler based on the activity type and object type</li> <li>invokes the handler to process the activity</li> <li>if necessary, delegates to sub-handlers for more specific processing</li> </ul> <p>Some <code>Activity</code>/<code>Object</code> handlers have more than one job</p> <p>For some <code>Activity</code>/<code>Object</code> pairs, there may be multiple jobs to select from. For example, handling an <code>Accept(Invite(VulnerabilityCase))</code> activity is different from handling an <code>Accept(Invite(Embargo))</code> activity. In such cases, the Actor Inbox Handler will invoke a handler for the Accept(Invite) activity, which will then delegate to the appropriate sub-handler based on the details of the offer being accepted.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Actor Inbox Handler\n---\nflowchart TB\n\nsource[client]\nsubgraph Actor\n  inbox\nend\nsubgraph handler\n  func[ActivityType,&lt;br/&gt;ObjectType]\nend\nsource --&gt;|1. POST| inbox\ninbox --&gt;|2. async| handler\ninbox --&gt;|3. response| source\nregistry(Handler&lt;br/&gt;Registry)\nhandler &lt;--&gt;|4. lookup| registry\nhdlrA[handler actor_id,activity]\nhandler --&gt;|5. call| hdlrA\nhdlrB[handler actor_id,activity,details]\nhdlrA --&gt;|6. delegate| hdlrB</code></pre>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/","title":"Vultron Activity Streams <code>as_Object</code></p> <p>https://www.w3.org/TR/activitystreams-vocabulary/#dfn-activity An Activity is a subtype of Object that describes some form of action that may happen, is currently happening, or has already happened. The Activity type itself serves as an abstract base type for all types of activities. It is important to note that an Activity is not a representation of a currently executing process, but rather a statement about that process. For example, a person walking down the street is not an Activity unless they are posting a picture of themselves walking down the street. The Activity in that case would be the posting of the picture, not the person walking down the street.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_activities/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects.activities.transitive","title":"<code>vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects.activities.transitive</code>","text":"<p>This module provides transitive activity classes</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_activities/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects.activities.transitive.as_Accept","title":"<code>as_Accept</code>","text":"<p>               Bases: <code>as_TransitiveActivity</code></p> <p>The actor accepts the object.       Bases: <code>as_Base</code></p> <p>A Link is an indirect, qualified reference to a resource identified by a URL. The fundamental model for links is established by [RFC5988]. Many of the properties defined by the Activity Vocabulary allow values that are either instances of Object or a Link. When a Link is used, it establishes a qualified relation connecting the subject to the resource identified by the href property. Properties of the Link are properties of the reference as opposed to properties of the resource. Links are disjoint from the Object type. See definition in ActivityStreams Vocabulary https://www.w3.org/TR/activitystreams-vocabulary/#dfn-link</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_links/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.links.as_Mention","title":"<code>as_Mention</code>","text":"<p>               Bases: <code>as_Link</code></p> <p>A Link that represents an @mention.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_objects/","title":"ActivityStreams Vocabulary Objects","text":""},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects","title":"<code>vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects</code>","text":""},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects.base","title":"<code>vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects.base</code>","text":"<p>This module provides activity streams object classes</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/as_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.base.objects.base.as_Object","title":"<code>as_Object</code>","text":"<p>               Bases: <code>as_Base</code></p> <p>Base class for all ActivityPub objects. \"VulnerabilityReport\":\n    ...\n</code></pre> <p><code>_field_map</code> contract: If a subclass wire type uses different field names than the corresponding core domain type, declare the mapping as::</p> <pre><code>_field_map: ClassVar[dict[str, str]] = {\"domain_field\": \"wire_field\"}\n</code></pre> <p>The default :meth:<code>from_core</code> implementation applies <code>_field_map</code> renames before calling :func:<code>~pydantic.BaseModel.model_validate</code>, so subclasses with no name differences can inherit the default without override.</p> <p><code>to_core</code> always raises :exc:<code>NotImplementedError</code> at this level; subclasses that have a meaningful reverse mapping SHOULD override it.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.base.VultronAS2Object.from_core","title":"<code>from_core(core_obj)</code>  <code>classmethod</code>","text":"<p>Create a wire object from a core domain object.</p> <p>The default implementation performs a JSON round-trip::</p> <pre><code>data = core_obj.model_dump(mode=\"json\")\n# apply _field_map renames (domain key \u2192 wire key)\ncls.model_validate(data)\n</code></pre> <p>Subclasses MUST narrow the <code>core_obj</code> parameter type.  Override this method when simple field-rename mapping via <code>_field_map</code> is insufficient.</p> <p>Parameters:</p> Name Type Description Default <code>core_obj</code> <code>Any</code> <p>A core domain model instance (Pydantic <code>BaseModel</code> subclass).</p> required <p>Returns:</p> Type Description <code>VultronAS2Object</code> <p>A new instance of this wire type populated from <code>core_obj</code>.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.base.VultronAS2Object.to_core","title":"<code>to_core()</code>","text":"<p>Convert this wire object to a core domain object.</p> <p>Subclasses that have a well-defined reverse mapping SHOULD override this method and return the appropriate core domain type.</p> <p>The <code>_field_map</code> contract is the inverse of :meth:<code>from_core</code>: wire field names map to domain field names.  Subclass overrides should apply the reverse mapping before calling <code>CoreType.model_validate(data)</code>.</p> <p>Raises:</p> Type Description <code>NotImplementedError</code> <p>Always \u2014 no generic reverse mapping exists at the wire base level.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.case_participant","title":"<code>vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.case_participant</code>","text":"<p>Provides various CaseParticipant objects for the Vultron ActivityStreams Vocabulary.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.case_participant.CaseParticipant","title":"<code>CaseParticipant</code>","text":"<p>               Bases: <code>VultronAS2Object</code></p> <p>A CaseParticipant is a wrapper around an Actor in a VulnerabilityCase. It is used to track the status of the participant within the context of a specific case, as well as the roles they play in the case.</p> <p>Several subclasses of CaseParticipant are provided for convenience, including:</p> <ul> <li>FinderParticipant</li> <li>ReporterParticipant</li> <li>FinderReporterParticipant</li> <li>VendorParticipant</li> <li>DeployerParticipant</li> <li>CoordinatorParticipant</li> <li>OtherParticipant</li> </ul> <p>But you can also create your own CaseParticipant objects if you need to.</p> <p>Examples:</p> <p>The following example creates a case participant and adds the VENDOR and DEPLOYER roles to it.</p> <p><code>python actor = as_Actor(name=\"Actor Name\") cp = CaseParticipant(attributed_to=actor, context=\"case_id_foo\") cp.add_role(CVDRole.VENDOR) cp.add_role(CVDRole.DEPLOYER)</code></p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.case_participant.CaseParticipant.participant_status","title":"<code>participant_status</code>  <code>property</code>","text":"<p>Return the most recent ParticipantStatus (read-only; see participant_statuses for history).</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.case_participant.CaseParticipant.roles","title":"<code>roles</code>  <code>property</code>","text":"<p>Return the participant's current CVD roles (read-only copy).</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/as_vocab/v_objects/#vultron.wire.as2.vocab.objects.case_participant.CaseParticipant.add_role","title":"<code>add_role(role, raise_when_present=False)</code>","text":"<p>Add a role to the participant.</p> <p>Idempotent when role already exists. It calls the root node's tick() method. Two callbacks are provided for subclasses to override: _pre_tick() and _post_tick().</p> <p>Returns:</p> Name Type Description <code>NodeStatus</code> <code>NodeStatus</code> <p>the status of the root node after the tick</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/bt/bt_base/#vultron.bt.base.blackboard","title":"<code>vultron.bt.base.blackboard</code>","text":"<p>This module defines Blackboard class for sharing data between nodes in the tree.</p> <p>The default Blackboard class is a dict, which is good enough if you just want to run a single tree in a single process.</p> <p>If you want multiple trees in a single process with shared state, just use the same Blackboard object instance for each tree.</p> <p>If you want to run multiple trees in a single process without shared state, you'll need to use a different Blackboard object instance for each tree. You can still do that with the default Blackboard class, but you'll need to create a new Blackboard object instance for each tree.</p> <p>If you want to run multiple trees in multiple processes with shared state, you'll need to use a Blackboard object that can communicate with some external data store, such as a key-value store on a server. The default Blackboard class is not designed for that use case, but you can subclass it to use any object that implements the <code>__getitem__</code> and <code>__setitem__</code> methods to provide a python dict-like interface. For example, mongodict and redis-dict provide such an interface for MongoDb and Redis, respectively.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/bt/bt_base/#vultron.bt.base.blackboard.Blackboard","title":"<code>Blackboard</code>","text":"<p>               Bases: <code>dict</code></p> <p>Provides a blackboard object for sharing data between nodes in the tree. The maximum failures is then calculated as <code>N - m</code>, where N is the number of children.</p> <ul> <li>When a child returns SUCCESS, the ParallelNode increments a success counter.</li> <li>When a child returns FAILURE, the ParallelNode increments a failure counter.</li> <li>If the success counter reaches the minimum number of successes, the ParallelNode returns SUCCESS.</li> <li>If the failure counter reaches the maximum number of failures, the ParallelNode returns FAILURE.</li> <li>If neither of the above conditions are met, the ParallelNode returns RUNNING.</li> </ul> <p>Not Fully Implemented</p> <p>In the current implementation, the ParallelNode does not actually tick its children in parallel. Instead, it ticks them in a random order and counts the results until either SUCCESS or FAILURE is indicated as above. This is good enough for demonstration purposes, but it should be replaced with a proper parallel implementation.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/bt/bt_base_nodes/#vultron.bt.base.composites.SequenceNode","title":"<code>SequenceNode</code>","text":"<p>               Bases: <code>BtNode</code></p> <p>SequenceNode is a composite node that ticks its children in order.</p> <ul> <li>If a child returns SUCCESS, the SequenceNode ticks the next child.</li> <li>If a child returns RUNNING, the SequenceNode returns RUNNING.</li> <li>If a child returns FAILURE, the SequenceNode returns FAILURE.</li> <li>If all children return SUCCESS, the SequenceNode returns SUCCESS.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/code/bt/bt_base_nodes/#vultron.bt.base.decorators","title":"<code>vultron.bt.base.decorators</code>","text":"<p>This module defines a number of Behavior bot.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/bt/vultrabot_demo/#vultron.demo.vultrabot.main","title":"<code>main(args)</code>","text":"<p>Instantiates a <code>CvdProtocolBt</code> object and runs it until either it closes or 1000 ticks have passed. This demo is basically simulating a CVD agent coordinating a CVD case with itself. The agent's role is set to <code>FINDER_REPORTER_VENDOR_DEPLOYER_COORDINATOR</code>, which means it will perform all the roles in the CVD case. Messages emitted in one tick might be received later in the same tick, or in a future tick.</p> <p>Tip</p> <p>This demo leverages the ability to use leaf nodes as stochastic process fuzzers.  Using this feature, we can simulate the behavior of a CVD agent without having to  implement any actual communication mechanisms or simulate any complex real-world processes.</p> <p>One interesting effect of this design is that the places where the demo uses a fuzzer node are often  indicative of places where an actual bot would either need to call out to either a data source or  a human to decide what to do next. This is a good example of how the Vultron behavior tree  can be used to model complex reactive processes in a way that is still easy to understand and reason about.</p> <p>Note</p> <p>There is no underlying communication mechanism in this demo, so messages are not actually  sent anywhere. Instead, they are just added to the blackboard's incoming message queue.  They also have no content, and are only represented as message types.</p> <p>Warning</p> <p>This demo is not intended to be a fully realistic simulation of a CVD case. It is only intended  to demonstrate the behavior of the Vultron behavior tree.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/","title":"Demo Package","text":"<p>The <code>vultron.demo</code> package contains sub-command maps to one demo script in <code>vultron/demo/</code>. The <code>all</code> sub-command runs every demo in sequence and prints a pass/fail summary.</p> <p>A <code>vultrabot</code> sub-group provides access to the three standalone behaviour-tree demos (pacman, robot, cvd).</p> <p>The <code>seed</code> sub-command bootstraps actor records in the DataLayer on container startup, supporting multi-actor demo scenarios (D5-1-G2).</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/cli/#vultron.demo.cli.main","title":"<code>main(ctx, debug, log_file)</code>","text":"<p>Vultron demo CLI \u2014 run individual demos or all demos in sequence.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/cli/#vultron.demo.cli.multi_vendor","title":"<code>multi_vendor(finder_url, vendor_url, coordinator_url, case_actor_url, vendor2_url, finder_id, vendor_id, coordinator_id, case_actor_id, vendor2_id, skip_health_check)</code>","text":"<p>Run the multi-vendor ownership-transfer demo (D5-4).</p> <p>Demonstrates case ownership transfer from Vendor to Coordinator, followed by Coordinator inviting a second Vendor (Vendor2) to join the case.</p> <p>Requires five containers to be running and reachable at the configured base URLs: finder, vendor, coordinator, case-actor, and vendor2.</p> <p>\b Workflow:   1. Seed all five containers (actor records + peer registration).   2. Finder submits a vulnerability report to Vendor's inbox.   3. Vendor validates the report.   4. Vendor creates the authoritative case on the CaseActor container.   5. Vendor invites Finder; both establish an embargo.   6. Vendor transfers case ownership to Coordinator.   7. Coordinator invites Vendor2; Vendor2 joins and accepts the embargo.   8. Verify final state: Coordinator owns the case, three participants,      and the embargo is ACTIVE.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/cli/#vultron.demo.cli.run_all","title":"<code>run_all(skip_health_check)</code>","text":"<p>Run every demo in sequence and print a pass/fail summary (DC-01-003, DC-01-004).</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/cli/#vultron.demo.cli.seed","title":"<code>seed(config_path, actor_name, actor_type, actor_id, api_url)</code>","text":"<p>Bootstrap actor records in the local DataLayer (D5-1-G2).</p> <p>Creates the local actor and any configured peer actors in the DataLayer via <code>POST /actors/</code>. A YAML config file (<code>--config</code> / <code>VULTRON_SEED_CONFIG</code>). 2. Requires both containers to be running and reachable at the configured base URLs.</p> <p>Use <code>--finder-url</code> / <code>--vendor-url</code> (or env vars <code>VULTRON_FINDER_BASE_URL</code> / <code>VULTRON_VENDOR_BASE_URL</code>) to point the demo at running containers.</p> <p>\b Workflow:   1. Seed both containers (actor records + peer registration).   2. Finder submits a vulnerability report to Vendor's inbox.   3. Vendor validates the report (trigger: validate-report).   4. Vendor engages the case (trigger: engage-case).   5. Vendor invites Finder to the case (Finder's inbox).   6. Finder accepts the invitation (Vendor's inbox).   7. Each script can be run standalone via <code>python -m vultron.demo.exchange.&lt;script&gt;</code> or through the unified CLI (<code>vultron-demo &lt;sub-command&gt;</code>).</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/demos/#receive-report","title":"receive-report","text":""},{"location":"reference/code/demo/demos/#vultron.demo.exchange.receive_report_demo","title":"<code>vultron.demo.exchange.receive_report_demo</code>","text":"<p>Demonstrates the workflow for receiving and processing vulnerability reports via the Vultron API.</p> <p>This demo script showcases three different outcomes when processing vulnerability reports: 1. Validate Report: <code>as_Accept</code> - creates a case 2. Invalidate Report: <code>as_TentativeReject</code> - holds for reconsideration 3. Invalidate and Close Report: <code>as_TentativeReject</code> + <code>as_Reject</code> - rejects and closes</p> <p>This demo uses direct inbox-to-inbox communication between actors, per the Vultron prototype design (see docs/reference/inbox_handler.md). Actors post activities directly to each other's inboxes rather than relying on outbox processing.</p> <p>When run as a script, this module will: 1. Check if the API server is available 2. Reset the data layer to a clean state 3. Discover actors (finder, vendor, coordinator) via the API 4. Run three separate demo workflows, each with a unique report:    - demo_validate_report: Submit \u2192 Validate \u2192 Create Case \u2192 Notify finder via inbox    - demo_invalidate_report: Submit \u2192 Invalidate \u2192 Notify finder via inbox    - demo_invalidate_and_close_report: Submit \u2192 Invalidate \u2192 Close \u2192 Notify finder via inbox 5. Verify side effects in the data layer and finder's inbox for each workflow</p> <p>Note on Behavior Tree Execution: The validate_report handler uses py_trees behavior tree execution to orchestrate the validation workflow. The receiver can subsequently validate or invalidate the report. Sending RmValidateReportActivity or RmInvalidateReportActivity already implies that the report was read, so a separate RmReadReportActivity is optional but demonstrates good protocol hygiene.</p> <p>When run as a script, this module will: 1. They are the outgoing counterpart to the inbound handler pipeline and are essential for demonstrating the full Vultron Protocol (see <code>specs/triggerable-behaviors.yaml</code> and <code>notes/triggerable-behaviors.md</code>).</p> <p>Two demo workflows are shown:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Validate and Engage: Finder submits a report via inbox (reactive setup);    vendor then proactively validates the report and engages the resulting case    using <code>POST /actors/{actor_id}/trigger/validate-report</code> and    <code>POST /actors/{actor_id}/trigger/engage-case</code>.</p> </li> <li> <p>Invalidate and Close: Finder submits a second report via inbox; vendor    proactively invalidates it via <code>POST .../trigger/invalidate-report</code> and    then closes it via <code>POST .../trigger/close-report</code>.</p> </li> </ol> <p>Each demo verifies that the resulting ActivityStreams activity is returned in the response body and that the vendor's outbox is updated accordingly.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/demos/#vultron.demo.exchange.trigger_demo.demo_invalidate_and_close","title":"<code>demo_invalidate_and_close(client, finder, vendor)</code>","text":"<p>Demonstrate proactive invalidation and report closure via trigger endpoints.</p> <p>Workflow: 1. This demo is basically simulating a CVD agent coordinating a CVD case with itself. The agent's role is set to <code>FINDER_REPORTER_VENDOR_DEPLOYER_COORDINATOR</code>, which means it will perform all the roles in the CVD case. Messages emitted in one tick might be received later in the same tick, or in a future tick.</p> <p>Tip</p> <p>This demo leverages the ability to use leaf nodes as stochastic process fuzzers.  Using this feature, we can simulate the behavior of a CVD agent without having to  implement any actual communication mechanisms or simulate any complex real-world processes.</p> <p>One interesting effect of this design is that the places where the demo uses a fuzzer node are often  indicative of places where an actual bot would either need to call out to either a data source or  a human to decide what to do next. This is a good example of how the Vultron behavior tree  can be used to model complex reactive processes in a way that is still easy to understand and reason about.</p> <p>Note</p> <p>There is no underlying communication mechanism in this demo, so messages are not actually  sent anywhere. Instead, they are just added to the blackboard's incoming message queue.  They also have no content, and are only represented as message types.</p> <p>Warning</p> <p>This demo is not intended to be a fully realistic simulation of a CVD case. It is only intended  to demonstrate the behavior of the Vultron behavior tree.</p>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/utils/","title":"Demo Utilities","text":"<p><code>vultron.demo.utils</code> Vultron DataLayer REST API</li> <li><code>demo_environment</code> \u2014 context manager that sets up and tears down a clean   DataLayer state around each demo run</li> <li><code>setup_clean_environment</code> \u2014 resets the DataLayer and returns the three   default demo actors (Finder, Vendor, Coordinator)</li> <li><code>check_server_availability</code> \u2014 polls the health endpoint until the API   server is ready</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/code/demo/utils/#module-reference","title":"Module (Required)","text":""},{"location":"reference/codebase/ARCHITECTURE/#1-architectural-style","title":"1) Architectural Style","text":"<ul> <li>Primary style: layered hexagonal architecture with an explicit wire-format   layer and event-driven background processing</li> <li>Why this classification: the repository guidance and sampled modules separate   <code>core</code>, <code>wire</code>, and <code>adapters</code>, with <code>DataLayer</code> and dispatcher protocols in   <code>core/ports/</code>, AS2 translation in <code>wire/</code>, and FastAPI/SQLite/HTTP delivery   code in adapters.</li> <li>Primary constraints:</li> <li>Core code must remain isolated from FastAPI and adapter types.</li> <li>AS2-to-domain mapping is centralized in the semantic extraction path.</li> <li>HTTP processing uses background tasks and outbox draining instead of doing      all work inline in the request handler.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/codebase/ARCHITECTURE/#2-system-flow","title":"2) System Flow","text":"<p><code>text HTTP request -&gt; FastAPI app/router -&gt; inbox handler + rehydration -&gt; semantic extraction -&gt; dispatcher/use case -&gt; DataLayer + outbox -&gt; outbox monitor/delivery queue -&gt; peer inbox HTTP POST</code></p> <p>Evidence-backed flow:</p> <ol> <li>Docker and local API startup target    <code>vultron.adapters.driving.fastapi.main:app</code>.</li> <li>The root app mounts <code>app_v2</code> at <code>/api/v2</code>.</li> <li>Actor inbox handling rehydrates AS2 activities and extracts a domain event.</li> <li>The dispatcher looks up the use-case class from the semantic registry map.</li> <li>Use cases persist/read state through the <code>DataLayer</code> port and adapter.</li> <li>Outbox processing is drained by a background monitor and delivered over HTTP.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"reference/codebase/ARCHITECTURE/#3-layermodule-responsibilities","title":"3) Layer/Module Responsibilities","text":"Layer or module Owns Must not own Evidence This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>The MPCVD process can be described as a Communicating Hierarchical State Machine. In this section, we begin by laying out the requirements for a formal protocol definition followed by a step-by-step walkthrough of each of those requirements as they relate to the RM, EM, and CS models described elsewhere.</p> <p>Do Not Start Here</p> <p>If this is your first time reading about the Vultron Protocol, we recommend that you start with Understanding Vultron. This section is heavy on formalism and is intended for readers who are already familiar with the Vultron Protocol concepts and design principles.</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/#communication-protocol-definitions","title":"Communication Protocol Definitions","text":"<p>A communication protocol allows independent processes, represented as finite state machines, to coordinate their state transitions through the passing of messages. Brand and Zafiropulo defined a protocol as follows.</p> <p>Protocol Formally Defined</p> <p>A protocol with \\(N\\) processes is a quadruple:</p> \\[ protocol =      \\Big \\langle          { \\langle S_i \\rangle }^N_{i=1},          { \\langle o_i \\rangle }^N_{i=1},         { \\langle M_{i,j} \\rangle}^N_{i,j=1},         { succ }     \\Big \\rangle\\] <p>Where</p> <ul> <li> <p>\\(N\\) is a positive integer representing the number of processes.</p> </li> <li> <p>\\(\\langle S_i \\rangle_{i=1}^N\\) are \\(N\\) disjoint finite sets (\\(S_i\\) represents the set of states of process \\(i\\)).</p> </li> <li> <p>Each \\(o_i\\) is an element of \\(S_i\\) representing the initial state of process \\(i\\).</p> </li> <li> <p>\\(\\langle M_{ij} \\rangle_{i,j=1}^N\\) are \\(N^2\\) disjoint finite sets with \\(M_{ii}\\) empty for all \\(i\\). \\(M_{ij}\\) represents the messages that can be sent from process \\(i\\) to process \\(j\\),</p> </li> <li> <p>\\(succ\\) is a partial function mapping for each \\(i\\) and \\(j\\), $\\(S_i \\times M_{ij} \\rightarrow S_i \\textrm{ and } S_i \\times M_{ji} \\rightarrow S_i\\)$ \\(succ(s,x)\\) is the state entered after a process transmits or receives message \\(x\\) in state \\(s\\). It is a transmission if \\(x\\) is from \\(M_{ij}\\) and a reception if \\(x\\) is from \\(M_{ji}\\).</p> </li> </ul> <p>Global State Formally Defined</p> <p>The global state of a protocol given by the above is a pair \\(\\langle S, C \\rangle\\), where</p> <ul> <li> <p>\\(S\\) is an \\(N\\)-tuple of states \\(\\langle s_1,\\dots,s_N \\rangle\\) with each \\(s_i\\) representing the current state of process \\(i\\).</p> </li> <li> <p>\\(C\\) is an \\(N^2\\)-tuple \\(\\langle c_{1,1},\\dots, c_{1,N}, c_{2,1}, \\dots \\dots, c_{N,N} \\rangle\\), where each \\(c_{i,j}\\) is a sequence of messages from \\(M_{i,j}\\). The message sequence \\(c_{i,j}\\) represents the contents of the channel from process \\(i\\) to \\(j\\). (Note that \\(c_{i,j}\\) is empty when \\(i = j\\) since processes are presumed to not communicate with themselves.)</p> </li> </ul> <p>We detail each of these below or in subsequent pages:</p> <ul> <li>\\(N\\) below</li> <li>\\({ \\langle S_i \\rangle}^N_{i=1}\\), and \\({ \\langle o_i \\rangle }^N_{i=1}\\) in States,</li> <li>\\({ \\langle M_{i,j} \\rangle }^N_{i,j=1}\\) in Message Types,</li> <li>\\({ succ }\\) in Transition Functions</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/#number-of-processes","title":"Number of Processes","text":"<p>The processes we are concerned with represent the different Participants in their roles (Finder, Vendor, Coordinator, Deployer, and Other). Each Participant has their own process, but Participants might take on multiple roles in a given case.</p> <p>Number of Processes</p> <p>The total number of processes \\(N\\) is simply the count of unique Participants.</p> \\[N = |Participants| = | Reporters \\cup Vendors \\cup Coordinators \\cup Deployers \\cup Others |\\]"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/","title":"Formal Vultron Protocol Redux","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>We have formally defined an MPCVD protocol. Here we provide a summary of the result. See the linked sections for more details.</p> <p> </p> <p>Recapping the definitions given in the introduction, we have:</p> <p>Formal Protocol Definition</p> \\[ {protocol}_{MPCVD} =     \\Big \\langle         { \\langle S_i \\rangle }^N_{i=1},         { \\langle o_i \\rangle }^N_{i=1},         { \\langle M_{i,j} \\rangle}^N_{i,j=1},         { succ }     \\Big \\rangle \\] <p>where</p> Symbol Description Defined In \\(N\\) Number of MPCVD Participants Introduction \\(\\langle S_i \\rangle_{i=1}^N\\) \\(N\\) disjoint finite sets in which each \\(S_i\\) represents the set of states of a given Participant \\(i\\) States \\({ \\langle o_i \\rangle }^N_{i=1}\\) the set of starting states across all Participants in which each \\(o_i\\) is an element of \\(S_i\\) representing the initial state of each Participant \\(i\\) States \\(\\langle M_{ij} \\rangle_{i,j=1}^N\\) \\(N^2\\) disjoint finite sets with \\(M_{ii}\\) empty for all \\(i\\). \\(M_{ij}\\) represents the messages that can be sent from process \\(i\\) to process \\(j\\). Messages \\(succ\\) a partial function mapping for each \\(i\\) and \\(j\\), \\(S_i \\times M_{ij} \\rightarrow S_i \\textrm{ and } S_i \\times M_{ji} \\rightarrow S_i\\) indicating the state changes arising from the sending and receiving of messages between Participants. Transitions <p>Legend</p> Symbol Description \u21bc Message Received \u21c0 Message Sent \u21cc Message Sent or Received"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/#summary-diagrams","title":"Summary Diagrams","text":"<p>A summary of the MPCVD state model \\(S_i\\) for an individual Participant is shown in the diagrams below.</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/#report-management","title":"Report Management","text":"<p>The Report Management (RM) state model is shown below. Note that with the exception of receiving reports, the RM process primarily focuses on Participants communicating their status to other Participants, as it is primarily emitting messages as opposed to receiving them.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    S --&gt; R : r (#8636;RS)\n    R --&gt; I: i (#8640;RI)\n    R --&gt; V : v (#8640;RV)\n    I --&gt; V : v (#8640;RV)\n    V --&gt; A : a (#8640;RA)\n    V --&gt; D : d (#8640;RD)\n    A --&gt; D : d (#8640;RD)\n    D --&gt; A : a (#8640;RA)\n    A --&gt; C : c (#8640;RC)\n    D --&gt; C : c (#8640;RC)\n    I --&gt; C : c (#8640;RC)</code></pre>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/#embargo-management","title":"Embargo Management","text":"<p>The Embargo Management (EM) state model is shown below. The EM process is more interactive than the RM process, as it involves Participants negotiating with each other to determine the appropriate embargo period for a given vulnerability report.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    N --&gt; P : p ( #8652;EP)\n    P --&gt; P : p ( #8652;EP)\n    P --&gt; N : r ( #8652;ER)\n    P --&gt; A : a ( #8652;EA)\n    A --&gt; R : p ( #8652;EV)\n    R --&gt; A : a ( #8652;EC)\n    R --&gt; A : r ( #8652;EJ)\n    R --&gt; R : p ( #8652;EV)\n    R --&gt; X : t ( #8652;ET)\n    A --&gt; X : t ( #8652;ET)</code></pre>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/#case-state","title":"Case State","text":"<p>The Case State (CS) model is shown below. We have divided the CS model into two diagrams: one that is specific to a given Participant, and one that is agnostic to the Participant, reflecting changes experienced by all Participants. The CS model is the most complex of the three, as it involves all Participants communicating with each other about both their own state changes in the fix development and deployment process, as well as the state changes driven by outside events (e.g., public awareness, exploits in the wild, attacks observed, etc.).</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/#participant-specific","title":"Participant-Specific","text":"<p>The Vendor fix path is shown below, and is specific to individual Vendor Participants. It reflects each Participant's state changes in the fix development and deployment process.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Vendor Fix Path\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    vfd --&gt; Vfd : &lt;b&gt;V&lt;/b&gt; (#8640;CV) \n    Vfd --&gt; VFd : &lt;b&gt;F&lt;/b&gt; (#8640;CF)\n    VFd --&gt; VFD : &lt;b&gt;D&lt;/b&gt; (#8640;CD)</code></pre>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/conclusion/#participant-agnostic","title":"Participant-Agnostic","text":"<p>The CS pxa model is shown below, and is agnostic to individual Participants. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>In States, we identified four main roles in the MPCVD process:</p> <ul> <li>Finder/Reporter</li> <li>Vendor</li> <li>Coordinator</li> <li>Deployer</li> </ul> <p>Here we will examine the messages passed between them. Revisiting the definitions from the Formal Protocol Introduction:</p> <p>Formalism</p> <p>\\(\\langle M_{ij} \\rangle_{i,j=1}^N\\) are \\(N^2\\) disjoint finite sets with \\(M_{ii}\\) empty for all \\(i\\): \\(M_{ij}\\) represents the messages that can be sent from process \\(i\\) to process \\(j\\).</p> <p>The message types in the Vultron Protocol arise primarily from the following principle taken directly from the CVD Guide:</p> <p>Avoid Surprise</p> <p>As with most situations in which multiple parties are engaged in a potentially stressful and contentious negotiation, surprise tends to increase the risk of a negative outcome. The importance of clearly communicating expectations across all parties involved in a CVD process cannot be overemphasized. If we expect cooperation between all parties and stakeholders, we should do our best to match their expectations of being \"in the loop\" and minimize their surprise. Publicly disclosing a vulnerability without coordinating first can result in panic and an aversion to future cooperation from Vendors and Finders alike. CVD promotes continued cooperation and increases the likelihood that future vulnerabilities will also be addressed and remedied.</p> <p>Now we condense that principle into the following protocol recommendation:</p> <p>Participants whose state changes in the RM, EM, or CVD State Models SHOULD send a message to other Participants for each transition.</p> <p>If you are looking for a one-sentence summary of the entire Vultron Protocol, that was it.</p> <p>State Transitions</p> <ul> <li>RM state transitions \\(\\Sigma^{rm} = \\{ r,v,a,i,d,c\\}\\)</li> <li>EM state transitions \\(\\Sigma^{em} = \\{ p,a,r,t\\}\\)</li> <li>CVD state transitions \\(\\Sigma^{cs} = \\{\\mathbf{V},\\mathbf{F},\\mathbf{D},\\mathbf{P},\\mathbf{X},\\mathbf{A}\\}\\)</li> </ul> <p>As a reminder, those transitions are shown at right.</p> <p>We will address the specific circumstances when each message should be emitted in Transitions, but first we need to introduce the message types this recommendation implies. We cover messages associated with each state model, in turn, below, concluding the section with a few message types not directly connected to any particular state model.</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/messages/#rm-message-types","title":"RM Message Types","text":"<p>Finders have hidden states</p> <p>As we discuss in RM Interactions, the Finder's states \\(q^{rm} \\in \\{R,I,V\\}\\) are not observable to the CVD process because Finders start  coordination only when they have already reached \\(q^{rm} = A\\).</p> <p>With the exception of the Finder/Reporter, each Participant's involvement in a CVD case starts with the receipt of a report from another Participant who is already in the \\(Accepted\\) (\\(q^{rm} \\in A\\)) state. The remainder of a Participant's RM process is largely independent of the other Participants' RM processes. Therefore, the RM message types are primarily used to inform other Participants of the sender's state.</p> Message Type Name Description \\(RS\\) Report Submission A message from one Participant to a new Participant containing a vulnerability report. \\(RI\\) Report Invalid A message indicating the Participant has designated the report as invalid. \\(RV\\) Report Valid A message indicating the Participant has designated the report as valid. \\(RD\\) Report Deferred A message indicating the Participant is deferring further action on a report. \\(RA\\) Report Accepted A message indicating the Participant has accepted the report for further action. \\(RC\\) Report Closed A message indicating the Participant has closed the report. \\(RK\\) Report Acknowledgement A message acknowledging the receipt of any RM message listed above. \\(RE\\) Report Error A message indicating a Participant received an unexpected RM message. <p>A summary of the RM message types is shown below.</p> <p>RM Message Types</p> \\[M^{rm} = \\{RS,RI,RV,RD,RA,RC,RK,RE\\}\\] <p>All state changes are from the Participant's (sender's) perspective, not the recipient's perspective. We will see in Transitions that the receipt of a Report Submission is the only message whose receipt directly triggers an RM state change in the receiver. All other RM messages are used to convey the sender's status.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD act in accordance with their own policy and process in deciding when to transition states in the RM model.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD NOT mark duplicate reports as invalid.</p> <p>Instead, duplicate reports SHOULD pass through \\(Valid\\) (\\(q^{rm} \\in V\\)), although they MAY be subsequently (immediately or otherwise) deferred (\\(q^{rm} \\in V \\xrightarrow{d} D\\)) in favor of the original.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD track the RM states of the other Participants in the case.</p> <p>An example object model for such tracking is described in Case Object. Furthermore, while these messages are expected to inform the receiving Participant's choices in their own RM process, this protocol intentionally does not specify any other recipient RM state changes upon receipt of an RM message.</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/messages/#em-message-types","title":"EM Message Types","text":"<p>Whereas the RM process is unique to each Participant, the EM process is global to the case. Therefore, we begin with the list of message types a Participant SHOULD emit when their EM state changes.</p> Message Type Name Description \\(EP\\) Embargo Proposal A message containing proposed embargo terms (e.g., date/time of expiration). \\(ER\\) Embargo Proposal Rejection A message indicating the Participant has rejected an embargo proposal. \\(EA\\) Embargo Proposal Acceptance A message indicating the Participant has accepted an embargo proposal. \\(EV\\) Embargo Revision Proposal A message containing a proposed revision to embargo terms (e.g., date/time of expiration). \\(EJ\\) Embargo Revision Rejection A message indicating the Participant has rejected a proposed embargo revision. \\(EC\\) Embargo Revision Acceptance A message indicating the Participant has accepted a proposed embargo revision. \\(ET\\) Embargo Termination A message indicating the Participant has terminated an embargo (including the reason for termination). Note that an Embargo Termination message is intended to have immediate effect. \\(EK\\) Embargo Acknowledgement A message acknowledging receipt of any of the above EM message types. \\(EE\\) Embargo Error A message indicating a Participant received an unexpected EM message. <p>If an early termination is desired but the termination date/time is in the future, this SHOULD be achieved through an Embargo Revision Proposal and additional communication as necessary to convey the constraints on the proposal.</p> <p>A summary of the EM message types is shown below.</p> <p>EM Message Types</p> \\[M^{em} = \\{EP,ER,EA,EV,EJ,EC,ET,EK,EE\\}\\]"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/messages/#cs-message-types","title":"CS Message Types","text":"<p>From the CS process model, the following is the list of messages associated with CS state changes:</p> Message Type Name Description \\(CV\\) Vendor Awareness A message to other Participants indicating that a report has been delivered to a specific Vendor. Note that this is an announcement of a state change for a Vendor, not the actual report to the Vendor, which is covered in the Report Submission (\\(RS\\)) above. \\(CF\\) Fix Readiness A message from a Participant (usually a Vendor) indicating that a specific Vendor has a fix ready. \\(CD\\) Fix Deployed A message from a Participant (usually a Deployer) indicating that they have completed their fix deployment process. This message is expected to be rare in most MPCVD cases because Deployers are rarely included in the coordination effort. \\(CP\\) Public Awareness A message from a Participant indicating that they have evidence that the vulnerability is known to the public. This message might be sent after a Participant has published their own advisory or if they have observed public discussion of the vulnerability. \\(CX\\) Exploit Public A message from a Participant indicating that they have evidence that an exploit for the vulnerability is publicly available. This message might be sent after a Participant has published their own exploit code, or if they have observed exploit code available to the public. \\(CA\\) Attacks Observed A message from a Participant indicating that they have evidence that attackers are exploiting the vulnerability in attacks. \\(CK\\) CS Acknowledgement A message acknowledging receipt of any of the above CS message types. \\(CE\\) CS Error A message indicating a Participant received an unexpected CS message. <p>Vendor Awareness (\\(CV\\)) messages SHOULD be sent only by Participants with direct knowledge of the notification (i.e., either by the Participant who sent the report to the Vendor or by the Vendor upon receipt of the report).</p> <p>A summary of the CS message types is shown below.</p> <p>CS Message Types</p> \\[M^{cs} = \\{CV,CF,CD,CP,CX,CA,CK,CE \\}\\]"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/messages/#other-message-types","title":"Other Message Types","text":"<p>Finally, there are a few additional message types required to tie the coordination process together. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Each Participant in an MPCVD case has a corresponding RM state, an EM state, and an overall CS state. Therefore, we can represent a Participant's state as a triple comprising the state of each of these models.</p> <p>Participant State</p> <p>A Participant's state is a triple comprising the state of each of the RM, EM, and CS models.</p> \\[q_{Participant} = (q^{rm},q^{em},q^{cs})\\] <p>Good Participant situation awareness makes for good CVD decision making.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD track the state of other Participants in a case  to inform their own decision making as it pertains to the case.</p> <p>Elsewhere, we provide an example Case Object model to facilitate such tracking. However, the protocol we are developing is expected to function even when incomplete information is available to any given Participant.</p> <p>Adequate operation of the protocol MUST NOT depend on perfect information across all Participants.</p> <p>A generic state model for a CVD Participant can be composed from the Cartesian product of \\(\\mathcal{Q}^{rm}\\), \\(\\mathcal{Q}^{em}\\), and \\(\\mathcal{Q}^{cs}\\) as shown below.</p> <p>Participant State Space</p> <p>A Participant's state is a triple comprising the state of each of the RM, EM, and CS models. The set of all possible Participant states is the Cartesian product of the RM, EM, and CS state sets.</p> \\[  S_i      % = \\mathcal{Q}^{rm} \\times \\mathcal{Q}^{em} \\times \\mathcal{Q}^{cs}     =      \\underbrace{     \\begin{bmatrix}         S \\\\         R \\\\         I \\\\         V \\\\         D \\\\         A \\\\          C \\\\     \\end{bmatrix}     }_{\\mathcal{Q}^{rm}}     \\times      % embargo state     \\underbrace{     \\begin{bmatrix}         N \\\\         P \\\\         A \\\\         R \\\\         X \\\\     \\end{bmatrix}     }_{\\mathcal{Q}^{em}}     \\times      % case state     \\underbrace{     \\begin{bmatrix}         \\begin{bmatrix}             \\varnothing \\\\             vfd \\\\             Vfd \\\\             VFd \\\\             VFD \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             p \\\\             P         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             x \\\\             X         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             a \\\\             A         \\end{bmatrix}     \\end{bmatrix}     }_{\\mathcal{Q}^{cs}}\\] <p>Note that the above definition splits the case state (\\(\\mathcal{Q}^{cs}\\)) into chunks corresponding to the Vendor fix path (\\(vfd \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{V}} Vfd \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{F}} VFd \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{D}} VFD\\)) and the public-exploit-attack (\\(pxa \\xrightarrow{\\dots} PXA\\)) sub-models from the Case State Model. This is done for two reasons. First, it gives us a more compact notation to represent the 32 states of the CS model. Second, as described in Model Interactions, it highlights the fact that the Vendor fix path represents the state of an individual Participant, whereas the public-exploit-attack sub-model represents facts about the world at large. Because not all Participants are Vendors or Deployers, Participants might not have a corresponding state on the \\(vfd \\xrightarrow{} VFD\\) axis. Therefore, we add a null element \\(\\varnothing\\) to the set of states representing the Vendor fix path.</p> <p>Thus, one might conclude that a total of 1,400 states is possible for each Participant.</p> <p>Participant State Space Size</p> \\[  | S_i | =          % actor rm state         | \\mathcal{Q}^{rm} | \\cdot          % embargo state         | \\mathcal{Q}^{em} | \\cdot         % case state         | \\mathcal{Q}^{cs} |          % multiply         = 7 \\cdot 5 \\cdot (5 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2) = 1400\\] <p>However, this dramatically overstates the possibilities for individual CVD Participant Roles because many of these states will be unreachable to individual Participants. In the remainder of this section, we detail these differences.</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/states/#unreachable-states","title":"Unreachable States","text":"<p>For any Participant, the RM \\(Closed\\) state implies that the EM and CVD Case states do not matter. Similarly, for any Participant, the RM \\(Start\\) state represents a case that the Participant doesn't even know about yet. Therefore, the \\(Start\\) state also implies that the EM and CVD Case states do not matter. We use \\(*\\) to represent the \"don't care\" value.</p> Unreachable EM and CS States when RM is in  Closed or Start \\[q^{rm} \\in \\{S,C\\} \\implies (q^{em} \\in *) \\cup (q^{cs} \\in *)\\] <p>A public exploit implies the vulnerability is public as well. \\end{split}\\]"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/states/#vendors-fix-suppliers","title":"Vendors (Fix Suppliers)","text":"<p>Vendors are the sole providers of fixes. Therefore, they are the only Participants in a CVD case for which the \\(Vfd \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{F}} VFd \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{D}} VFD\\) path is possible. Furthermore, since they are Vendors by definition, they do not have access to the \\(vfd\\) state or the \\(\\varnothing\\) state that was just added. As a Vendor has a report in \\(Received\\), it is, by definition, at least in the \\(Vfd\\) case state.</p> <p>Vendors create fixes only when they are in the \\(Accepted\\) RM state. Because the \\(Received\\), \\(Invalid\\), and \\(Valid\\) states come strictly before the \\(Accepted\\) state in the RM DFA, there is no way for the Vendor to be in either \\(VFd\\) or \\(VFD\\) while in any of those states.</p> Vendor CS States When RM is in Received, Invalid, or Valid \\[q^{rm}_{Vendor} \\in \\{R,I,V\\} \\implies q^{cs}_{Vendor} \\in Vfd\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\] <p>Vendors with the ability to deploy fixes themselves have access to three states in the fix path: \\(\\{Vfd,~VFd,~VFD\\}\\). However, this is not always the case. Vendor Participants without a deployment capability can only create fixes, limiting them to the middle two states in the fix path: \\(\\{Vfd,~VFd\\}\\). Additional discussion of the distinction between Vendors with and without a deployment capability can be found in A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</p> <p>We <p>The \\(\\dagger\\) on \\(VFD\\) in the above indicates that the \\(VFD\\) state is accessible only to Vendors with a deployment capability. As tallied below, there are 128 possible states for a Vendor with deployment capability and 100 for those without.</p> <p>Vendor State Space Size</p> <p>With deployment capability:</p> \\[  \\begin{split}         |S_{i_{\\frac{Vendor}{Deployer}}}| = &amp; 1 + \\big(3 \\cdot 5 \\cdot (1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2)\\big) + \\big(2 \\cdot 5 \\cdot (3 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2)\\big) \\\\         &amp; + \\big(3 \\cdot 1 \\cdot (1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2)\\big) + \\big(2 \\cdot 1 \\cdot (3 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2)\\big) + 1 \\\\         % = &amp; 2 + 30 + 60 + 12 + 24 \\\\         = &amp; 128 \\\\         \\end{split}\\] <p>Without deployment capability:</p> \\[  \\begin{split}         |S_{i_{Vendor}}| = &amp; 1 + \\big(3 \\cdot 5 \\cdot (1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2)\\big) + \\big(2 \\cdot 5 \\cdot (2 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2)\\big) \\\\         &amp; + \\big(3 \\cdot 1 \\cdot (1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2)\\big) + \\big(2 \\cdot 1 \\cdot (2 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2)\\big) + 1 \\\\         % = &amp; 2 + 30 + 40 + 12 + 16 \\\\         = &amp; 100 \\\\         \\end{split}\\]"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/states/#non-vendor-deployers","title":"Non-Vendor Deployers","text":"<p>We just explained that not all Vendors are Deployers. Likewise, not all Deployers are Vendors. Most CVD cases leave Non-Vendor Deployers entirely out of the CVD process, so their appearance is expected to be rare in actual cases. However, there are scenarios when an MPCVD case may include Non-Vendor Deployers, such as when a vulnerability in some critical infrastructure component is being handled or when the Vultron Protocol is used in the context of a Vulnerability Disclosure Program (VDP). These Non-Vendor Deployers participate only in the \\(d \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{D}} D\\) transition on the fix path. Similar to the Vendor scenario above, it is expected that Deployers actually deploy fixes only when they are in the RM \\(Accepted\\) state (implying their intent to deploy).
