Therefore, for a CVD protocol, we only need to care about Finder states once they have reached RM \\(Accepted\\). Coincidentally, this is also a convenient way to mark the transition from Finder to Reporter.</p> <p>Finder-Reporter State Space</p> \\[  S_{i_{Reporter}} =      \\begin{cases}     (S,*,*) \\textrm{(hidden)}\\\\     (R,*,*) \\textrm{(hidden)}\\\\     (I,*,*) \\textrm{(hidden)}\\\\     (V,*,*) \\textrm{(hidden)}\\\\     {} \\\\     \\begin{bmatrix}         D \\\\         A \\\\      \\end{bmatrix}     \\times      % embargo state     \\begin{bmatrix}         N \\\\         P \\\\         A \\\\         R \\\\         X \\\\     \\end{bmatrix}     \\times      % case state     \\begin{bmatrix}         \\begin{bmatrix}         \\varnothing         \\end{bmatrix}          \\times         \\begin{bmatrix}             p \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             x \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             a \\\\             A \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}     \\end{bmatrix} \\textrm{maybe embargoed} \\\\     {}\\\\     \\begin{bmatrix}         D \\\\         A \\\\      \\end{bmatrix}     \\times      % embargo state     \\begin{bmatrix}         * \\\\     \\end{bmatrix}     \\times      % case state     \\begin{bmatrix}     \\begin{bmatrix}         \\varnothing     \\end{bmatrix}     \\times         \\begin{bmatrix}             P \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             x \\\\             X \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}         \\times          \\begin{bmatrix}             a \\\\             A \\\\         \\end{bmatrix}     \\end{bmatrix} \\textrm{embargo irrelevant} \\\\     {} \\\\     (C,*,*) \\\\     \\end{cases}\\] <p>Thus, for all practical purposes, we can ignore the hidden states in the above and conclude that Finders who go on to become Reporters have only 29 possible states during a CVD case.</p> <p>Finder-Reporter State Space Size</p> \\[  \\begin{split}         |S_{i_{Reporter}}| = &amp; \\big(2 \\cdot 5 \\cdot (1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2)\\big) + \\big(2 \\cdot 1 \\cdot (1 \\cdot 1 \\cdot 2 \\cdot 2)\\big) + 1 \\\\         % = &amp; 20 + 8 + 1 \\\\         = &amp; 29 \\\\     \\end{split}\\]"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/states/#a-lower-bounds-on-mpcvd-state-space","title":"A Lower Bounds on MPCVD State Space","text":"<p>Generic MPCVD State Space Size Formula</p> \\[|S_{total}| = \\prod_{i=1}^{N} |S_i|\\] <p>Now we can touch on the lower bounds of the state space of an MPCVD case. Generically, we would expect the state space for \\(N\\) Participants to take the form given at right.</p> <p>The upper bound on the MPCVD state space is simply \\(352^N \\approx 10^{2.55N}\\). However, because of the Role-specific limits just described, we already know that this overcounts the possible states significantly. We can do better still. If we ignore transient states while Participants converge on a consistent view of the global state of a case, we can drastically reduce the state space for an MPCVD case. Why? There are two reasons:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Because they represent facts about the outside world, the eight     \\(\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot pxa \\rightarrow \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot PXA\\) CS substates are global to the case, not     to individual Participants. This means all Participants should     rapidly converge to the same substate.</p> </li> <li> <p>Similarly, the five EM states are also global to the case and should converge rapidly.</p> </li> </ol> <p>Given these two observations, we can pull those Participant-agnostic terms out of the state calculations for individual Participants,</p> <p>MPCVD State Space With Participant-Agnostic Terms Factored Separately</p> \\[|S_{total}| = \\prod_{i=1}^{N} |S_i| = |S_{global}| \\times \\prod_{i=1}^{N} |S_{Participant}|\\] <p>where </p> \\[ |S_{global}| = 8 \\times 5 = 40 \\] <p>which leaves</p> <p>Participant-Specific State Spaces</p> \\[|S_{Participant}| =     \\begin{cases}         Reporter = 1 + 2 = 3 \\\\         Vendor =  2 + 3 + (2 \\cdot 2) + 3 + (2 \\cdot 2 ) = 16 \\\\         Vendor/Deployer =  2 + 3 + (2 \\cdot 3) + 3 + (2 \\cdot 3) = 20 \\\\         Coordinator = 2 + 5  = 7 \\\\         Deployer = 2 + 3 = 5\\\\         Others = 2 + 5  = 7 \\\\     \\end{cases}\\] <p>So our state space looks like</p> <p>MPCVD State Space Size Formula</p> \\[\\begin{split}     |S_{total}| = 40 &amp; \\times         3^{N_{Reporter}} \\times         16^{N_{Vendor}} \\times         20^{N_{\\frac{Vendor}{Deployer}}} \\\\         &amp; \\times         7^{N_{Coordinator}} \\times          5^{N_{Deployer}} \\times          7^{N_{Other}} \\\\     \\end{split}\\] <p>With these values in mind, we see that</p> <ul> <li> <p>A two-party (Finder-Vendor) case might have a lower bound state space of \\(40 \\times 3 \\times 16 = 1,920\\) states.</p> </li> <li> <p>A case like Meltdown/Spectre (with six Vendors and no Coordinators) might have \\(40 \\times 3 \\times 16^{6} \\approx 10^{9}\\) states.</p> </li> <li> <p>A large, but not atypical, 200-Vendor case handled by the CERT/CC might have     \\(40 \\times 3 \\times 16^{200} \\times 7 \\approx 10^{244}\\) possible configurations.</p> </li> <li> <p>In the case of the log4j vulnerability CVE-2021-44228 in December     2021, the CERT/CC notified around 1,600 Vendors after the vulnerability had been made public. Had this been an     embargoed disclosure, the case would have a total state space around \\(10^{2000}\\).</p> </li> </ul> <p>That said, while these are dramatic numbers, the reader is reminded that the whole point of the Vultron Protocol is to coordinate the process so that it is not just hundreds or thousands of Participants behaving randomly.</p>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/states/#starting-states","title":"Starting States","text":"<p>Each Participant begins a case in the state where the report management process is in the start state, there is no embargo in place, and the case has not made any progress.</p> <p>Participant Start State Formally Defined</p> \\[o_i = (o_i^{rm},~o_i^{em},~o_i^{cs}) = (S,N,vfdpxa)\\] <p>Following the discussion above, the starting states for Vendors, Deployers, and other Participants are shown below.</p> <p>Participant Start States</p> Participant Role Start State (\\(o_i\\)) Vendor \\((S,N,vfdpxa)\\) Deployer \\((S,N,dpxa)\\) Other \\((S,N,pxa)\\) Finder/Reporter \\((A,N,pxa)\\) <p>For a case to really begin, the Finder must at least reach the \\(A\\) state. Therefore, at the point when a second party finds out about the vulnerability from a Finder, the Finder/Reporter is presumed to be already at \\(q_{Finder}=(A, N, pxa)\\).</p> <p>We will show in Transitions how this plays out. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>In this section, we describe the transition functions for the RM, EM, and CVD Case processes, respectively. Note that while the RM process is largely independent of the other two process models, the EM and CVD process models have some noteworthy interactions, which we will cover in detail.</p> <p>Revisiting the formal protocol definition from the introduction:</p> <p>Transition Function Defined</p> <p>\\(succ\\) is a partial function mapping for each \\(i\\) and \\(j\\),</p> \\[S_i \\times M_{ij} \\rightarrow S_i \\textrm{ and } S_i \\times M_{ji} \\rightarrow S_i\\] <p>\\(succ(s,x)\\) is the state entered after a process transmits or receives message \\(x\\) in state \\(s\\). It is a transmission if \\(x\\) is from \\(M_{ij}\\) and a reception if \\(x\\) is from \\(M_{ji}\\).</p> <p>Notation Conventions on this Page</p> <ul> <li>By convention, CS states are labeled in the order \\(vfdpxa\\).</li> <li>Participant state is a tuple of the individual CS, RM, and EM states \\(S_i = (q^{cs}, q^{rm}, q^{em})\\).</li> <li>Dots (\\(\\cdot\\)) in states indicate single wildcards. For example, \\(Vfd \\cdot \\cdot \\cdot\\) includes \\(Vfdpxa, VfdPxA, VfdPXA, etc.\\)</li> <li>Asterisks (\\(*\\)) indicate arbitrary wildcards.</li> <li>Dashes (\\(\u2212\\)) indicate no state change.</li> <li>Left-harpoons (\\(\\leftharpoondown\\)) indicate a message received.</li> <li>Right-harpoons (\\(\\rightharpoonup\\)) indicate a message sent.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"reference/formal_protocol/transitions/#rm-transition-functions","title":"RM Transition Functions","text":"<p>Because it only reflects an individual Participant's report handling status, the RM process operates largely independent of both the EM and CS processes. Functions","text":"<p>Participant-Specific State Messages Promote Shared Situation Awareness</p> <p>Effective coordination is usually improved with Participants' mutual awareness of each other's state, of course.</p> <p>The Vendor-specific portions of the CS (Vendor Awareness, Fix Ready, and Fix Deployed) are per-Participant states. Therefore, the receiver of a message indicating another Participant has changed their \\(\\{v,V\\}\\), \\(\\{f,F\\}\\) or \\(\\{d,D\\}\\) status is not expected to change their own state as a result.</p> <p>However, this is not the case for the remainder of the CS substates. As above, the appropriate Participant response to receiving CS messages (namely, those surrounding Public Awareness, Exploit Public, or Attacks Observed) depends on the state of the EM process.</p> <p>Participants SHALL initiate embargo termination upon becoming aware of publicly available information about the vulnerability or its exploit code.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD initiate embargo                             owl:equivalentClass [ owl:intersectionOf ( as:Add                                                                        [ rdf:type owl:Restriction ;                                                                          owl:onProperty as:object ;                                                                          owl:someValuesFrom vultron_as:EmbargoEvent                                                                        ]                                                                        [ rdf:type owl:Restriction ;                                                                          owl:onProperty as:target ;                                                                          owl:someValuesFrom vultron_proto:VulnerabilityCase                                                                        ]                                                                      ) ;                                                   rdf:type owl:Class                                                 ] ;                             rdfs:subClassOf vultron_as:CaseOwnerActivity ,                                             as:Add ;                             rdfs:comment \"\"\"Add an EmbargoEvent to a VulnerabilityCase. This implies that the case owner is setting the embargo for the case, which would usually come after an embargo proposal has been accepted by the case owner as in ActivateEmbargo (which is otherwise identical to AddEmbargoToCase except that ActivateEmbargo is InReplyTo some embargo proposal.)</p> <p>For this reason, in most cases, ActivateEmbargo should be preferred when responding to a proposed embargo. normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>We have developed a non-normative ontology to describe the Vultron protocol. The ontology is available in the <code>ontology</code> directory.</p> <p> </p> <p> </p> <p> </p> <p> </p> Markdown DocumentationTurtle Ontology <p>Markdown Ontology Documentation is Incomplete</p> <p>While we have generated some markdown documentation based on the  raw ontology files, the files themselves contain considerably more detail  and structure than the markdown documentation.</p> <p></p> <p>Prot\u00e9g\u00e9</p> <p>We recommend using the Prot\u00e9g\u00e9 ontology editor to view and edit the ontology files.</p> <p>```turtle @base http://www.cert.org/ns/vultron_protocol# . @prefix : http://www.cert.org/ns/vultron_protocol# . @prefix dfa: http://www.cert.org/ns/deterministicfiniteautomata# . @prefix owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl# . @prefix rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns# . @prefix xml: http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace . @prefix xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema# . @prefix rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema# . @prefix rfc2119: http://www.cert.org/ns/rfc2119# . @prefix vultron_process: http://www.cert.org/ns/vultron_process# .</p>"},{"location":"reference/ontology/vultron_protocol/#classes","title":"Classes","text":""},{"location":"reference/ontology/vultron_protocol/#actor","title":"Actor","text":"Attribute Value Name Actor IRI http://www.cert.org/ns/vultron_protocol#Actor Description Superclasses owl.Thing Subclasses vultron_activitystreams.Actor"},{"location":"reference/ontology/vultron_protocol/#caseownerparticipant","title":"CaseOwnerParticipant","text":"Attribute Value Name CaseOwnerParticipant IRI http://www.cert.org/ns/vultron_protocol#CaseOwnerParticipant Description Equivalent To Nonevultron_protocol.CaseParticipant &amp; Understanding Vultron section assumes that you have:</p> <ul> <li>an interest in learning about the Vultron Protocol</li> <li>familiarity with the CVD process in general</li> </ul> <p>If you are already familiar with the Vultron Protocol, and are looking for implementation advice,  see Implementing Vultron. For technical reference, see Reference. If you're just trying to understand the CVD process, we recommend that you start with the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</p> <p>This section provides an overview of the Vultron Protocol, including:</p> <ul> <li> Background</li> <li> Process Models</li> <li> Formal Protocol overview</li> <li> Behavior Logic</li> <li> User Stories</li> <li> Measuring CVD</li> <li> Other Uses</li> <li> Future Work</li> </ul>"},{"location":"topics/background/","title":"Vultron Contextualized","text":"<p>Prerequisites</p> <p>The Understanding Vultron section assumes that you have:</p> <ul> <li>an interest in learning about the Vultron Protocol</li> <li>familiarity with the CVD process in general</li> </ul> <p>If you are already familiar with the Vultron Protocol, and are looking for implementation advice,  see Implementing Vultron. For technical reference, see Reference. If you're just trying to understand the CVD process, we recommend that you start with the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</p> <p>The CVD process addresses a human coordination problem that spans individuals and organizations. As we wrote in The CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure:</p> <p>Excerpt from The CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure</p> <p>Perhaps the simplest description of the CVD process is that it starts with at least one individual becoming aware of a vulnerability in a product. This discovery event immediately divides the world into two sets of people: those who know about the vulnerability, and those who don't. From that point on, those belonging to the set that knows about the vulnerability iterate on two questions:</p> <ol> <li>What actions should I take in response to this knowledge?</li> <li>Who else needs to know what, and when?</li> </ol> <p>The CVD process continues until the answers to these questions are \"nothing,\" and \"nobody.\"</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/#cvd-is-mpcvd-and-mpcvd-is-cvd","title":"CVD Is MPCVD, and MPCVD Is CVD","text":"<p>Any given CVD case is made up of many individual disclosure events, for example,</p> <ul> <li>from a Finder to one or more Vendors and/or Coordinators</li> <li>from Vendors and Coordinators to other Vendors and Coordinators</li> <li>from Finders, Vendors, and Coordinators to Deployers and the Public</li> </ul> <p>The following diagram illustrates the disclosure events between the various roles in the CVD process. Looping arrows indicate that an entity in one role might also disclose to another entity in the same role.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Potential Vulnerability Disclosure Events\n---\nflowchart TD\n    finder([Finder])\n    vendor([Vendor])\n    coordinator([Coordinator])\n    public([Public])\n    deployer([Deployer])\n    ovendor([Vendor])\n    ocoordinator([Coordinator])\n\n    finder --&gt;|discloses to| vendor\n    finder --&gt;|discloses to| coordinator\n    coordinator --&gt;|discloses to| public\n    vendor --&gt;|discloses to| public\n    finder --&gt;|discloses to| public\n\n    vendor --&gt;|discloses to| vendor\n    vendor --&gt;|discloses to| coordinator\n    vendor -.-&gt;|discloses to| deployer\n\n    coordinator --&gt;|discloses to| vendor\n    coordinator --&gt;|discloses to| coordinator\n    coordinator -.-&gt;|discloses to| deployer\n\n    public --&gt;|available to| deployer\n    public --&gt;|available to| ovendor\n    public --&gt;|available to| ocoordinator</code></pre> <p>However, we can simplify the diagram with the recognition that the C in CVD stands for Coordinated. The following diagram illustrates the coordination relationships between the various roles in the CVD process.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Coordination Relationships in CVD\n---\nflowchart TB\n    subgraph C[Coordination]\n        direction LR\n        subgraph A[Often&lt;br/&gt;Same&lt;br/&gt;Entity]\n            reporter([Reporter])\n            finder([Finder])\n        end\n        vendor([Vendor])\n        coordinator([Coordinator])\n        subgraph D[Sometimes&lt;br/&gt;Included]\n            deployer([Deployer])\n        end\n    end\n    public([Public])\n\n    finder &lt;--&gt; reporter\n    reporter &lt;--&gt; vendor\n    vendor &lt;--&gt; coordinator\n    reporter &lt;--&gt; coordinator\n    vendor &lt;-.-&gt; deployer\n    coordinator &lt;-.-&gt; deployer\n    reporter &lt;-.-&gt; deployer\n    C --&gt;|publish| public</code></pre> <p>In recent years, software supply chains have evolved such that software library and component vulnerabilities have become just as much a part of the everyday CVD process as vulnerabilities in Vendors' proprietary code. This means that many CVD cases we encounter require coordination across multiple vendors. As a result, we find it decreasingly useful to differentiate between \"traditional\" (i.e., two-party) CVD and MPCVD. In this documentation, we use both terms interchangeably.</p> \\[CVD \\iff MPCVD\\] <p>Practically speaking, this means that readers should not infer from our use of CVD in one place that we meant to exclude the multi-party scenario, nor that our use of MPCVD implies the exclusion of the single-vendor CVD scenario. Instead, our intent is to construct a protocol that adequately addresses the MPCVD scenario where \\(N_{vendors} \\geq 1\\) and for which the \"traditional\" CVD case is merely a special (and often simpler) case where \\(N_{vendors} = 1\\).</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/#context-of-our-recent-work","title":"Context of Our Recent Work","text":"<p>This documentation, in the context of recent CVD work at the CERT/CC, is one of four foundational documents aimed at increasing the professionalization of the CVD process. The following is the full set of foundational documents (thus far):</p> <ul> <li> <p>The CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (the     CVD Guide) in both its original     and updated forms, provides a \"field guide\" perspective to the     CVD process and its natural extension into MPCVD.</p> </li> <li> <p>The Stakeholder-Specific Vulnerability Categorization     provides decision support for prioritizing vulnerability response activities     closely associated with the CVD process.</p> </li> <li> <p>A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure     describes a model that encompasses all possible CVD case histories into a set of measures and metrics for the     efficacy of CVD processes. That report is an expanded version of Are We Skillful or Just Lucky? Interpreting the Possible     Histories of Vulnerability Disclosures, an article published in the ACM Journal Digital Threats: Research and Practice.</p> </li> <li> <p>Designing Vultron, the report on which this documentation was based, proposes an abstract formal protocol for     MPCVD, ties together various concepts from all three of the above.</p> </li> </ul> <p>Whereas the CVD Guide offers a narrative description of both the CVD process and the many scenarios one can expect to encounter as a Participant therein, in this documentation we provide an additional layer of formality in the form of a protocol for MPCVD.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/#what-we-mean-by-protocol","title":"What We Mean by Protocol","text":"<p>We first define what we mean by our use of the term protocol by providing a few common usages from the Oxford English Dictionary.</p> <p>Oxford English Dictionary on protocol</p> <p>(Computing and Telecommunications) A (usually standardized) set of rules governing the exchange of data between given devices, or the transmission of data via a given communications channel.</p> <p>(In extended use) the accepted or established code of behavior in any group, organization, or situation; an instance of this.</p> <p>Why Vultron?</p> <p>The working name for our protocol is Vultron, an homage to the fictional robot Voltron. In the Voltron animated series, a team of protectors joins forces to defend the universe from their adversaries. Their defensive mission requires a combination of independent defenders coordinating their efforts to achieve their goals. Like Voltron, our Vultron Protocol comprises a combination of humans and the technical processes and mechanisms that empower them. Together, those humans, processes, and mechanisms must function both individually and in coordination and cooperation with others to protect information systems and the people who depend on them from exploitation by adversaries.</p> <p>Both usages are relevant to this effort. First, insofar as we seek to scale the MPCVD process through the use of automation and software-augmented human processes, we wish to propose a formal technical protocol that can serve as the basis of such technical tools. The Formal Protocol section of this documentation addresses this first definition in specific detail after explicating its component parts and their interactions in Report Management, Embargo Management, Case State, and Model Interactions.</p> <p>Second, recognizing that MPCVD is primarily a coordination process among human Participants with the goal of remediating extant vulnerabilities in deployed information systems, a protocol must not only address the technical formalities of communicating code but also extend to the expected behaviors of the human Participants that rely on it. In this second sense, we address the term protocol in these ways:</p> <ul> <li> <p>The CVD Guide     offers a narrative protocol for practitioners to follow based on     decades of accumulated experience and observation of the     CVD process at the CERT/CC.</p> </li> <li> <p>The Case State model from A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure     offers a prescriptive protocol outlining the high-level goals of the CVD process, as derived from a first-principles approach to possible     CVD case histories.</p> </li> <li> <p>This documentation and the report it is based     on describes a normative protocol designed to structure and guide practitioners toward those goals.</p> </li> </ul> <p>To that end, we offer this documentation as a proposal for such an MPCVD protocol.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/","title":"What Does Success Mean in CVD?","text":"<p>Formalism development in Measuring CVD</p> <p>In the Measuring CVD section of this documentation (based on our 2021 report), we define a set of 12 ordering preferences over these 6 events.</p> <p>We take as a base set of criteria the ordering preferences given in the 2021 report A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (MPCVD) by Householder and Spring. While we incorporate this model fully later in this documentation, some notation is necessary to proceed here. The Case State model is built on the idea that there are six events of significance in the lifespan of every vulnerability, shown in the table below.</p> Event Notation Event Notation Vendor Awareness V Public Awareness P Fix Ready F Exploit Public X Fix Deployed D Attacks Observed A <p>For now, we simply present the 12 desired criteria in roughly descending order of desirability according to the partial order developed in that formalization. Items closer to the top of the list are indicators of CVD skill.</p> <p>Formalism</p> <p>The symbol \\(\\prec\\) is read as precedes.</p> Ordering Preference Notation Fix Deployed Before Public Awareness D \\(\\prec\\) P Fix Ready Before Public Awareness F \\(\\prec\\) P Fix Deployed Before Exploit Public D \\(\\prec\\) X Fix Deployed Before Attacks Observed D \\(\\prec\\) A Fix Ready Before Exploit Public F \\(\\prec\\) X Vendor Awareness Before Public Awareness V \\(\\prec\\) P Fix Ready Before Attacks Observed F \\(\\prec\\) A Public Awareness Before Exploit Public P \\(\\prec\\) X Exploit Public Before Attacks Observed X \\(\\prec\\) A Public Awareness Before Attacks Observed P \\(\\prec\\) A Vendor Awareness Before Exploit Public V \\(\\prec\\) X Vendor Awareness Before Attacks Observed V \\(\\prec\\) A"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#fix-deployed-before-public-awareness","title":"Fix Deployed Before Public Awareness","text":"<p>For a fix to be deployed prior to public awareness, a lot has to go right in the CVD process: The vendor has to know about the vulnerability, create a fix, and deploy it\u2014all without public knowledge\u2014and has to achieve all that prior to any exploits being published or attacks becoming known to the public. Furthermore, it requires that the Vendor has the capability to deploy fixes without intervention by the system owner or user, which is a rare engineering feat unattainable by many software supply chains.</p> <p>More often, fix deployment (D) requires users and/or system owners (Deployers) to take action. The need to inform Deployers implies a need for public awareness of the vulnerability, making this criteria impossible to achieve in those scenarios.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#fix-ready-before-public-awareness","title":"Fix Ready Before Public Awareness","text":"<p>Deployers (i.e., the public) can take no action until a fix is ready. Because public awareness also implies adversary awareness, the vendor-adversary race becomes even more critical when this condition is not met. Only Vendors who can receive and act on vulnerability reports---whether those reports originate from inside or outside of the organization---are able to achieve this goal.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#fix-deployed-before-exploit-public","title":"Fix Deployed Before Exploit Public","text":"<p>Deploying a fix before an exploit is made public helps reduce the net risk to end users.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#fix-deployed-before-attacks-observed","title":"Fix Deployed Before Attacks Observed","text":"<p>Attacks occurring before a fix has been deployed are when there's maximum risk to users; therefore, we wish to avoid that situation.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#fix-ready-before-exploit-public","title":"Fix Ready Before Exploit Public","text":"<p>Exploit publication prior to fix readiness represents a period of increased threat to users since it means that attackers can exploit the vulnerability even if they lack exploit development skills. When fixes are ready before exploits are made public, defenders are better positioned to protect their users.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#vendor-awareness-before-public-awareness","title":"Vendor Awareness Before Public Awareness","text":"<p>Public awareness prior to vendor awareness can cause increased support costs for vendors at the same time they are experiencing increased pressure to prepare a fix.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#fix-ready-before-attacks-observed","title":"Fix Ready Before Attacks Observed","text":"<p>As in the case with published exploits, when fixes exist before attacks are observed, defenders are in a substantially better position to protect their users.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#public-awareness-before-exploit-public","title":"Public Awareness Before Exploit Public","text":"<p>There is broad agreement that it is better for the public to find out about a vulnerability via a CVD process rather than because someone published an exploit for any adversary to use.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#exploit-public-before-attacks-observed","title":"Exploit Public Before Attacks Observed","text":"<p>This criterion is not about whether exploits should be published or not. X.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/cvd_success/#public-awareness-before-attacks-observed","title":"Public Awareness Before Attacks Observed","text":"<p>Similar to the exploit case above, public facilitate with the Vultron protocol.</p> \\(a \\prec b\\) \\(a \\prec b\\) \\(a \\prec b\\) \\(\\mathbf{V} \\prec \\mathbf{P}\\) \\(\\mathbf{F} \\prec \\mathbf{P}\\) \\(\\mathbf{D} \\prec Vulnerability Disclosure (MPCVD). Specifically, we revisit this list in Desirable Histories.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/interoperability/","title":"The Need for Interoperability in Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure","text":"<p>The overall goal of our Vultron Protocol effort is to achieve interoperability among CVD process implementations according to the broad definition of that term found in the 2004 report, Current Perspectives on Interoperability by Brownsword et al.:</p> <p>Brownsword et al. on Interoperability</p> <p>The ability of a collection of communicating entities to (a) share specified information and (b) operate on that information according to an agreed operational semantics</p> <p>This definition encompasses both (a) syntactic and (b) semantic interoperability. The goal of this documentation is to lay the foundation for the semantic interoperability of CVD processes across Participants. Syntactic interoperability, in the form of message formats and the like, is the focus of our ongoing effort.</p> <p>Addressing semantic interoperability first is a deliberate choice. If we were to go in the reverse order, we might wind up with systems that exchange data quickly and accurately yet still fail to accomplish the mutually beneficial outcomes that MPCVD sets out to achieve. Carney et al. illustrate the importance of semantic interoperability in their 2005 report Some Current Approaches to Interoperability:</p> <p>Carney et al. on semantic interoperability</p> <p>There is a limited number of ways that agreements on meaning can be achieved. In the context of design-time interoperability, semantic agreements are reached in the same manner as interface agreements between the constituent systems... However, in the context of run-time interoperability, the situation is more complex, since there is need for some manner of universal agreement, so that a new system can join, ad-hoc, some other group of systems. The new system must be able to usefully share data and meaning with those other systems, and those other systems must be able to share data and meaning from an unfamiliar newcomer.</p> <p>In this excerpt, replace the word \"system\" with the concept of a \"CVD Case Participant,\" and the need for semantic interoperability as a means of achieving coordination in MPCVD becomes clear:</p> <p>Paraphrasing Carney et al. in the context of CVD</p> <p>[...] However, in the context of run-time interoperability, the situation is more complex, since there is need for some manner of universal agreement, so that a new CVD Participant can join, ad-hoc, some other group of CVD Participants [in a CVD Case]. The new CVD Case Participant must be able to usefully share data and meaning with those other CVD Case Participants, and those other Participants must be able to share data and meaning from an unfamiliar newcomer.</p> <p>Elsewhere in the same report, Carney et al. write,</p> <p>Carney et al. on the necessity of trust in interoperability</p> <p>In the hoped-for context of unbounded systems of systems, trust in the actions and capabilities provided by interoperating parties is essential. Each party to an interaction must have, develop, or perceive a sense of whether the actions of interoperating parties can be trusted. This sense of trust is not Boolean (e.g., parties can be trusted to varying degrees), is context dependent (Party A can be trusted in a particular context but not in another), and is time sensitive (Party A can be trusted for a certain period). Further, the absence of trust-----distrust-----is less dangerous than misplaced trust: it is better to know that you cannot trust a particular party than to misplace trust in a party</p> <p>The protocol we propose is intended to promote trust between MPCVD Participants both within an individual case as well as over time and across cases.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/interoperability/#objectives","title":"Objectives","text":"<p>The objectives of this documentation are as follows:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Provide a set of common primitives to serve as an ontological     foundation for CVD process definitions across     organizations.</p> </li> <li> <p>Construct abstract workflows that support the inter-organizational     coordination and synchronization required for the     CVD process to     be successful.</p> </li> <li> <p>From those primitives and workflows, identify a set of message types     needed for the CVD process to function.</p> </li> <li> <p>Provide high-level requirements for the semantic content of those     message types.</p> </li> <li> <p>Explore options for the syntactic representation of those message     types.</p> </li> </ol>"},{"location":"topics/background/notation/","title":"Notation","text":"<p>This page is not normative</p> <p>This page is not considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol as proposed in the main documentation. We adopt the following notation:</p> <p>Set Theory Symbols</p> Symbol Meaning \\(\\{ \\dots \\}\\) depending on the context: (1) an ordered set in which the items occur in that sequence, or (2) a tuple of values |\\(x\\)| the count of the number of elements in a list, set, tuple, or vector \\(x\\) \\(\\subset,=,\\subseteq\\) the normal proper subset, equality, and subset relations between sets \\(\\in\\) the membership (is-in) relation between an element and the set it belongs to \\(\\prec\\) the precedes relation on members of an ordered set: \\(\\sigma_i \\prec \\sigma_j \\textrm{ if and only if } \\sigma_i,\\sigma_j \\in s \\textrm{ and } i &lt; CVD processes (and the DFAs representing them) can be decomposed hierarchically. We propose three main DFAs as the core of our Vultron Protocol:</p> <ol> <li>A Report Management DFA represents each CVD Participant's engagement with a particular report</li> <li>An Embargo Management DFA negotiates and establishes the timing of future disclosures and publications</li> <li>A Case State DFA tracks the events in CVD Substates,     as originally described in A State-Based Model for Multi-Party Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</li> </ol> <p>Model Interactions contains a discussion of the interactions among these three state machine models.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/overview/#formal-protocol","title":"Formal Protocol","text":"<p>However, a set of agents independently executing processes is not coordinated, and if they are not coordinated, then whatever they are doing does not deserve the name CVD. Hence, there is a need for a protocol to describe the interactions necessary to coordinate these processes. Communicating FSMs provide a formal way to define a communications protocol, which coordinates the activities of independent DFAs through the interchange (e.g., sending and receiving) of messages. We map our multiple DFA model onto a formal protocol definition in Formal Protocol.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/overview/#behavior-logic","title":"Behavior Logic","text":"<p>An MPCVD protocol needs to do more than just provide formally defined communication mechanisms. It also needs to normalize the expected behaviors and activities that the communication protocol enables. In this sense, our protocol expands upon ISO/IEC 29147:2018, ISO/IEC 30111:2019, and ISO/IEC TR 5895:2022, which, taken together, provide a high-level normative standard for CVD activities.</p> <p>Developed in response to the growing complexity of video game Non-Player Character (NPC) Artificial Intelligence (AI) Finite State Machines (FSMs), Behavior Trees offer a way to organize and describe agent behaviors in a straightforward, understandable way. Using Behavior Trees, agent processes can be modeled as sets of behaviors (e.g., pre-conditions, actions, and post-conditions) and the logic that joins them together. Today, Behavior Trees are used in video game software to develop realistic NPCs and in robotics to create reactive and adaptive behaviors from autonomous agents. Behavior Trees offer a high potential for automating complex tasks through a hierarchical decomposition of the logic and actions required to complete those tasks.</p> <p>The behaviors we are interested in modeling are the various CVD activities described in the CVD Guide (e.g., find contacts, send reports, validate reports, prioritize reports, create fixes, publish reports, publish fixes, deploy fixes). We use Behavior Trees to describe MPCVD Participant activities and their interactions with the formal protocol.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/overview/#implementation-notes","title":"Implementation Notes","text":"<p>Additional implementation notes, including a simplified case data model, are provided.</p> <p>Future work is discussed in Future Work.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/overview/#reference-material","title":"Reference Material","text":"<p>Reference material includes</p> Handling Processes, and ISO/IEC TR 5895:2022   Multi-party coordinated vulnerability disclosure and handling.</li> <li>an SSVC Crosswalk provides a mapping between the Vultron Protocol   and relevant portions of the Stakeholder Specific Vulnerability Categorization   (SSVC), a vulnerability response prioritization   model also developed by the CERT/CC</li> </ul>"},{"location":"topics/background/terms/","title":"Terms and Definitions","text":"<p>Throughout this documentation, we refer to CVD Roles from the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure:</p> <p>Finder</p> <p>The individual or organization that identifies the vulnerability</p> <p>Reporter</p> <p>The individual or organization that notifies the vendor of the vulnerability</p> <p>Vendor (Supplier)</p> <p>The individual or organization that created or maintains the vulnerable product</p> <p>The Vendor role is synonymous with the Supplier role as it appears in SSVC Version 2 and above.</p> <p>Deployer (User)</p> <p>The individual or organization that must deploy a patch or take other remediation action</p> <p>The Deployer role is synonymous with the User role in ISO/IEC 29147:2018 and ISO/IEC 30111:2019, while the other roles are named consistent with those standards.</p> <p>Coordinator</p> <p>An individual or organization that facilitates the coordinated response process</p> <p>We also add a new role in this documentation, which we expect to incorporate into a future version of the CVD Guide:</p> <p>Exploit Publisher</p> <p>An individual or organization that publishes exploits</p> <p>Exploit Publishers might be the same as Finders, Reporters, Coordinators, or Vendors, but this is not guaranteed. For example, Vendors that produce tools for Cybersecurity Red Teams might play a combination of roles: Finder, Reporter, Vendor, Coordinator, and/or Exploit Publisher.</p> <p>Finally, we have a few additional terms to define:</p> <p>CVD Case (Case)</p> <p>The unit of work for the overall CVD process for a specific vulnerability spanning the individual CVD Case Participants and their respective processes</p> <p>CVD Case Participant (Participant)</p> <p>Finder, Vendor, Coordinator, Deployer, etc., as defined above</p> <p>Vulnerability Report (Report)</p> <p>The unit of work for an individual Case Participant's Report Management (RM) process</p> <p>A diagram showing the relationship between CVD Cases, Participants, and Reports can be found in Case Object.</p>"},{"location":"topics/background/versioning/","title":"Vultron Protocol Version Numbering Scheme","text":"<p>Implementations may have their own versioning scheme</p> <p>The versioning scheme described here applies only to the Vultron Protocol itself. Implementations of the Vultron Protocol are expected to have their own versioning schemes.</p> <p>Current Version</p> <p>The current version of the Vultron Protocol is</p> <p>2024.4.4.dev1598+g2cfa08f9.d20260505</p> <p>Vultron Protocol versions follow Calendar Versioning (CalVer) using the format <code>YYYY.M.Patch</code>, where:</p> <ul> <li> <p><code>YYYY</code> is the four-digit year of the most recent non-patch release.</p> </li> <li> <p><code>M</code> is the month of the most recent non-patch release,     with no zero padding (e.g., <code>1</code>, <code>2</code>, <code>3</code>, ..., <code>12</code>).</p> </li> <li> <p><code>Patch</code> is the patch number for that release.     For the first (or only) release in a given month, the patch number     starts at <code>0</code> and is normally omitted, so <code>2024.4.0</code> and <code>2024.4</code>     denote the same version.</p> </li> </ul> <p>Version increments work as follows:</p> <ul> <li> <p>Significant releases use the current year and month, with the patch     number starting at <code>0</code> (normally omitted).     Example: the first significant release in April 2024 is <code>2024.4</code>.</p> </li> <li> <p>Patch releases increment the patch number from the most recent     non-patch release, even if the patch is published in a later month or year.     Example: the third small update to <code>2024.4</code> is <code>2024.4.3</code>, even if it     is released in May 2024 or later.</p> </li> </ul> <p>Because we are still in the early stages of the project, no backward compatibility commitments are made or implied at this time. We anticipate this will change as the protocol matures.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/","title":"Modeling an MPCVD AI Using Behavior Trees","text":"<p>With the formal definition of the Vultron Protocol behind us, we now turn our attention to reflect on one of many possible paths toward implementation. We find that Behavior Trees have a number of desirable properties when it comes to automating the kinds of complex behaviors our protocol demands.</p> <p>For more about Behavior Trees</p> <p>For more about Behavior Trees, see </p> <ul> <li>Behavior Trees in Robotics and AI: An Introduction</li> <li>A behavior language for story-based believable agents</li> <li>GDC 2005 Proceeding: Handling Complexity in the Halo 2 AI</li> <li>Increasing Modularity of UAV Control Systems using Computer Game Behavior Trees</li> <li>An integrated system for autonomous robotics manipulation</li> </ul> <p>Behavior Trees are a way of designing and programming hierarchical behaviors. They originated in the computer gaming industry to develop realistic AIs to control NPCs in games. More recently, Behavior Trees have been used in robotics to create adaptive behaviors using autonomous AI agents. Behavior Trees offer a high potential for automating complex tasks. Agent processes can be modeled as sets of behaviors (pre-conditions, actions, and post-conditions) and the logic that joins them. Behavior Trees offer a way to organize and describe agent behaviors in a straightforward, understandable way.</p> <p>In this section, we use Behavior Trees as a method for describing MPCVD Participant activities and their interactions with the formal protocol. These behaviors map approximately to the activities described in the CVD Guide (e.g., validate report, prioritize report, create fix, publish report, publish fix, deploy fix).</p> <p>If Behavior Trees were merely a notational convention, they would already have been useful enough to include here to structure the high-level business logic of the Vultron Protocol. But they also offer a way to prototype software agents that reflect the activities of CVD Participants. Because Behavior Trees are inherently hierarchical, they are composable. Both conditions and actions can be composed into small task-oriented behaviors, which can, in turn, be composed to represent more complex agent behaviors. As a result, independent agents using Behavior Trees can be composed into multi-agent behaviors that achieve goals.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/#a-brief-introduction-to-behavior-tree-notation","title":"A Brief Introduction to Behavior Tree Notation","text":"<p>Behavior Trees consist of a hierarchy of nodes represented as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). A Behavior Tree execution always begins at the root node, and execution is passed along the tree by ticking each child node according to the logic built into the tree. When ticked, each node does its job and returns one of three statuses: Success, Failure, or Running. A full introduction to Behavior Trees can be found in Colledanchise and \u00d6gren's book Behavior Trees in Robotics and AI: An Introduction.</p> <p>Behavior Tree Node Types</p> Node Type Shape or Symbol Description Root any control flow type has no parent nodes, has one or more child nodes, and can be of any of the control-flow types. Condition rounded oval does not change the state of the world, has no child nodes, and returns only Success or Failure. Task rectangle has no child nodes; performs a task, which might change the state of the world; and returns Success, Failure, or Running. Sequence \u2192 ticks each child node, returning the last Success or the first Failure, or Running if a child returns Running. Fallback ? ticks each child node, returning the first Success or the last Failure, or Running if a child returns Running. Loop \u21ba repeatedly ticks child nodes until an exit condition is met. Parallel \u21c9 ticks all child nodes simultaneously, and returns Success when \\(m\\) of \\(n\\) children have returned Success. <p>A basic Behavior Tree is shown below. When a tree is presented in the vertical orientation, each node's children should be read left to right. In the example below, we see two motifs that come up through the remainder of this section. On the left side is a Fallback node (\\(\\boxed{?}\\)), which short-circuits to Success when the \\(postcondition\\) is already met. Otherwise, some activity will occur in \\(task_a\\) and, assuming that it succeeds, the \\(postcondition\\) is set. As a result, the fallback node ensures that Success means that the \\(postcondition\\) is met.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: A Basic Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart TD\n    root[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    l[?]\n    root --&gt; l\n    r[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    root --&gt; r\n    l0([postcondition])\n    l --&gt; l0\n    l1[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    l --&gt; l1\n    l10[task_a]\n    l1 --&gt; l10\n    l11[set postcondition]\n    l1 --&gt; l11\n    r0([precondition])\n    r --&gt; r0\n    r1[task_b]\n    r --&gt; r1\n    r2[?]\n    r --&gt; r2\n    r20[task_c]\n    r2 --&gt; r20\n    r21[task_d]\n    r2 --&gt; r21</code></pre> <p>On the right side is a sequence (\\(\\boxed{\\rightarrow}\\)) that hinges on a \\(precondition\\) being met prior to some set of actions being taken. Assuming the \\(precondition\\) is met, \\(task_b\\) fires and, assuming it succeeds execution, proceeds to another fallback node. This fallback node represents a set of tasks in which one only needs to succeed for the fallback to return Success. If \\(task_c\\) succeeds, then \\(task_d\\) does not run.</p> <p>Behavior Trees can sometimes be wider than they are tall, so we sometimes display them in a horizontal orientation to save space. The semantics remain the same, but each node's children should be read top-to-bottom rather than left-to-right. The same tree as above is shown below in horizontal form.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: A Basic Behavior Tree (horizontal)\n---\nflowchart LR\n    root[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    l[?]\n    root --&gt; l\n    r[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    root --&gt; r\n    l0([postcondition])\n    l --&gt; l0\n    l1[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    l --&gt; l1\n    l10[task_a]\n    l1 --&gt; l10\n    l11[set postcondition]\n    l1 --&gt; l11\n    r0([precondition])\n    r --&gt; r0\n    r1[task_b]\n    r --&gt; r1\n    r2[?]\n    r --&gt; r2\n    r20[task_c]\n    r2 --&gt; r20\n    r21[task_d]\n    r2 --&gt; r21</code></pre> <p>Behavior Trees are composable\u2014that is, a task node in one tree can be replaced with a more refined Behavior Tree in another. We leverage this feature throughout the remainder of this chapter to describe an agent model for an MPCVD Participant as a set of nested Behavior Trees that reflect the protocol described in the previous chapters.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/acquire_exploit_bt/","title":"Acquire Exploit Behavior","text":"<p>Some Vendors or other CVD Participants might require a proof-of-concept exploit to accompany an incoming report for it to pass their validation checks. To that end, an Acquire Exploit Behavior Tree is shown below.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Acquire Exploit Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    fb[\"?\"]\n    have_exploit([\"have exploit?\"])\n    fb --&gt; have_exploit\n    seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt; seq\n    fb2[\"?\"]\n    seq --&gt; fb2\n    exp_priority([\"exploit priority&lt;br/&gt;set?\"])\n    fb2 --&gt; exp_priority\n    eval_priority[\"evaluate&lt;br/&gt;priority\"]\n    fb2 --&gt; eval_priority\n    exp_desired([\"exploit desired?\"])\n    seq --&gt; exp_desired\n    fb3[\"?\"]\n    seq --&gt; fb3\n    find_exploit[\"find exploit\"]\n    fb3 --&gt; find_exploit\n    dev_exploit[\"develop exploit\"]\n    fb3 --&gt; dev_exploit\n    pay_exploit[\"purchase exploit\"]\n    fb3 --&gt; pay_exploit</code></pre> <p>The goal of this behavior is for the Participant to be in possession of an exploit.</p> <p>If the Participant does not already have one, the main acquisition sequence is triggered. The sequence begins by ensuring that the exploit acquisition activity has sufficient priority to continue. If it does, the Participant has one of three options to choose from: they can find one somewhere else, develop it themselves, or pay someone for the privilege.</p> <p>The overall behavior returns Success when either an exploit is acquired or when one is not desired and is therefore deferred. It can fail in the scenario where an exploit is desired but not acquired.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/cvd_bt/","title":"CVD Behavior Tree","text":"<p>We begin at the root node of the CVD Behavior Tree shown in the figure below. The root node is a simple loop that continues until an interrupt condition is met, representing the idea that the CVD practice is meant to be continuous. In other words, we are intentionally not specifying the interrupt condition.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: CVD Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    root[\"#8634;\"]\n    seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    discover_vulnerability[\"Discover Vulnerability\"]\n    receive_messages[\"Receive Messages\"]\n    report_management[\"Report Management\"]\n    embargo_management[\"Embargo Management\"]\n    root --&gt; seq \n    seq --&gt;|A| discover_vulnerability \n    seq --&gt;|B| receive_messages\n    seq --&gt;|C| report_management\n    seq --&gt;|D| embargo_management</code></pre> <p>The main sequence is comprised of four main tasks:</p> <ul> <li> <p>(A) Discover Vulnerability. Although not all Participants have the     ability or motive to discover vulnerabilities, we include it as a     task here to call out its importance to the overall     CVD process. This task returns Success regardless of whether a vulnerability is found to allow execution to     pass to the next task.</p> </li> <li> <p>(B) Receive Messages. All coordination in CVD between Participants is done through     the exchange of messages, regardless of how those messages are     conveyed, stored, or presented. The receive messages task represents     the Participant's response to receiving the various messages defined     in the formal protocol. Due to the degree of detail     required to cover all the various message types, decomposition of     this task node is deferred until later so we can cover the next two items     first. Before we proceed, it is sufficient to know that a new report arriving in the receive messages behavior     sets \\(q^{rm} \\in S \\xrightarrow{r} R\\) and returns Success.     Each message is dispatched to one of four process-specific sub-trees:</p> </li> <li> <p>Process RM Messages \u2014 handles report-submission, error, and status messages       (<code>RS</code>, <code>RE</code>, <code>RV</code>, <code>RI</code>, <code>RA</code>, <code>RD</code>, <code>RC</code>, <code>RK</code>)</p> </li> <li>Process EM Messages \u2014 handles all embargo coordination messages       (<code>EP</code>, <code>EA</code>, <code>EV</code>, <code>EJ</code>, <code>EC</code>, <code>ET</code>, <code>ER</code>, <code>EE</code>, <code>EK</code>)</li> <li>Process CS Messages \u2014 handles case-state change notifications       (<code>CP</code>, <code>CX</code>, <code>CA</code>, <code>CV</code>, <code>CF</code>, <code>CD</code>, <code>CE</code>, <code>CK</code>)</li> <li> <p>Process Other Messages \u2014 handles general inquiry and acknowledgment messages       (<code>GI</code>, <code>GE</code>, <code>GK</code>)</p> </li> <li> <p>(C) Report Management. This task embodies the RM process     as integrated into the formal protocol.     It delegates to three sub-behaviors plus the main work loop:</p> </li> <li> <p>Report Validation \u2014 evaluates credibility and validity; transitions       \\(q^{rm} \\in R \\xrightarrow{v} V\\) or \\(q^{rm} \\xrightarrow{i} I\\)</p> </li> <li>Report Prioritization \u2014 applies triage (e.g., SSVC); transitions       \\(q^{rm} \\in V \\xrightarrow{a} A\\) or \\(q^{rm} \\xrightarrow{d} D\\)</li> <li>Report Closure \u2014 checks closure criteria and transitions       \\(q^{rm} \\xrightarrow{c} C\\)</li> <li> <p>Do Work \u2014 a parallel node covering the active work performed on       Accepted reports (\\(q^{rm} \\in A\\)), including:</p> <ul> <li>Deployment \u2014 prioritizes and executes fix or mitigation deployment;       transitions \\(q^{cs} \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{D}} D\\)</li> <li>Fix Development \u2014 Vendor-specific behavior to create a fix;       transitions \\(q^{cs} \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{F}} F\\)</li> <li>Reporting to Others \u2014 identifies relevant Participants and notifies them       (emits <code>RS</code>)</li> <li>Publication \u2014 manages preparation and release of advisories, fixes,       and exploits; transitions \\(q^{cs} \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{P}} P\\)</li> <li>Monitor Threats \u2014 watches for attacks, public exploits, and       public reports; may trigger embargo termination</li> <li>CVE ID Assignment \u2014 assigns or requests a CVE ID for the       vulnerability</li> <li>Acquire Exploit \u2014 finds, develops, or purchases a PoC exploit       when needed</li> </ul> </li> <li> <p>(D) Embargo Management. Similarly, this task represents the     EM process as integrated into the     formal protocol.     It uses three reusable sub-trees:</p> </li> <li> <p>Propose Embargo \u2014 selects terms and emits <code>EP</code> (new proposal) or       <code>EV</code> (revision), respecting embargo viability conditions</p> </li> <li>Evaluate Embargo \u2014 accepts a proposal (emits <code>EA</code>) or issues a       counterproposal via Propose Embargo</li> <li>Terminate Embargo \u2014 exits an active or proposed embargo when       termination criteria are met; transitions \\(q^{em} \\xrightarrow{t} X\\) and emits <code>ET</code> or <code>ER</code></li> </ul> <p>Behaviors and state changes resulting from changes to the CS model are scattered throughout the other Behavior Trees where relevant.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/cvd_bt/#section-overview","title":"Section Overview","text":"<p>The diagram below maps the full decomposition of Behavior Trees covered in this section. Each box corresponds to a page in this section.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: CVD Behavior Tree \u2014 Section Map\n---\nflowchart LR\n    cvd[\"CVD BT\\n(this page)\"]\n    vuldisco[\"Discover Vulnerability\"]\n    cvd --&gt;|A| vuldisco\n    recv[\"Receive Messages\"]\n    cvd --&gt;|B| recv\n    rm_bt[\"Report Management\"]\n    cvd --&gt;|C| rm_bt\n    em_bt[\"Embargo Management\"]\n    cvd --&gt;|D| em_bt\n\n    proc_rm[\"Process RM Messages\"]\n    recv --&gt; proc_rm\n    proc_em[\"Process EM Messages\"]\n    recv --&gt; proc_em\n    proc_cs[\"Process CS Messages\"]\n    recv --&gt; proc_cs\n    proc_other[\"Process Other Messages\"]\n    recv --&gt; proc_other\n\n    rm_val[\"Report Validation\"]\n    rm_bt --&gt; rm_val\n    rm_pri[\"Report Prioritization\"]\n    rm_bt --&gt; rm_pri\n    rm_clo[\"Report Closure\"]\n    rm_bt --&gt; rm_clo\n    do_work[\"Do Work\"]\n    rm_bt --&gt; do_work\n\n    deploy[\"Deployment\"]\n    do_work --&gt; deploy\n    fix_dev[\"Fix Development\"]\n    do_work --&gt; fix_dev\n    reporting[\"Reporting to Others\"]\n    do_work --&gt; reporting\n    pub[\"Publication\"]\n    do_work --&gt; pub\n    mon[\"Monitor Threats\"]\n    do_work --&gt; mon\n    id_assign[\"CVE ID Assignment\"]\n    do_work --&gt; id_assign\n    acq_exp[\"Acquire Exploit\"]\n    do_work --&gt; acq_exp\n\n    em_prop[\"Propose Embargo\"]\n    em_bt --&gt; em_prop\n    em_term[\"Terminate Embargo\"]\n    em_bt --&gt; em_term\n    em_eval[\"Evaluate Embargo\"]\n    em_bt --&gt; em_eval</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/deployment_bt/","title":"Deployment Behavior","text":"<p>The Deployment Behavior Tree is shown in the figure below. mon_req\n    mon[\"monitor deployment\"]\n    mon_seq --&gt; mon</code></pre> <p>Assuming neither of these conditions has been met, the main deployment sequence (C) falls to the Deployer role (C1). It consists of two subprocesses: prioritize deployment and deploy.</p> <p>The prioritize deployment behavior is shown in (C2) the fallback node in the center of the diagram. The subgoal is for the deployment priority to be established, as indicated by the Deployer's RM state \\(q^{rm} \\in \\{D,A\\}\\). For example, a Deployer might use the SSVC Deployer Tree to decide whether (and when) to deploy a fix or mitigation. If the deployment priority evaluation indicates further action is needed,</p> <ul> <li>the report management state is set to \\(q^{rm} \\in A\\)</li> <li>an \\(RA\\) message is emitted, and</li> <li>the overall prioritization behavior succeeds</li> </ul> <p>Otherwise, when the deployment is Deferred, it results in a transition to state \\(q^{rm} \\in D\\) and emission of an \\(RD\\) message.</p> <p>(C3) The deploy behavior is shown in the second fallback node of the center sequence (C). It short-circuits to Success if either the deployment is Deferred or has already occurred. The main sequence can fire in two cases:</p> <ol> <li>when the Deployer is also the Vendor and a fix is ready     (\\(q^{cs} \\in F\\))</li> <li>when the Deployer is not the Vendor but the fix is both ready and     public (\\(q^{cs} \\in P\\) and \\(q^{cs} \\in F\\))</li> </ol> <p>Assuming either of these conditions is met,</p> <ul> <li>the deploy fix task can run,</li> <li>the case status is updated to \\(q^{cs} \\in D\\), and</li> <li>\\(CD\\) emits on Success</li> </ul> <p>Should the deployment sequence fail for any reason, a fallback is possible if undeployed mitigations are available.</p> <p>(D) Finally, returning to the top part of the tree, Participants might choose to monitor the deployment process should they have the need to.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/do_work_bt/","title":"Do Work Behavior Tree","text":"<p>Although it is not directly addressed by the formal Vultron Protocol, the do work node of the RM Behavior Tree is where much of the CVD effort happens. As a result, it is worth spending some time reviewing what some of the underlying work actually entails.</p> <p>In this section, we will expand on the set of behaviors shown in the diagram below.</p> <p>Why is this a parallel node?</p> <p>The do work node is a parallel node because it is not necessary for a Participant to complete all of these tasks in order to complete their CVD work. We intentionally do not specify any Success criteria regarding what fraction of its children must succeed. Decisions about which (and how many) of the following tasks are necessary for a Participant to complete work on their \\(Accepted\\) CVD cases are left to the discretion of individual Participants.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Do Work Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    par[\"#8649;&lt;br/&gt;m/n\"]\n    deployment[\"deployment\"]\n    par --&gt; deployment\n    dev_fix[\"develop fix\"]\n    par --&gt; dev_fix\n    reporting[\"report to others\"]\n    par --&gt; reporting\n    publication[\"publication\"]\n    par --&gt; publication\n    monitor[\"monitor threats\"]\n    par --&gt; monitor\n    assign_cve[\"assign CVE ID\"]\n    par --&gt; assign_cve\n    exploit_acq[\"acquire exploit\"]\n    par --&gt; exploit_acq\n    other[\"other work\"]\n    par --&gt; other</code></pre> <p>Specifically, we will cover the following tasks, each in its own subsection.</p> <ul> <li>Deployment</li> <li>Developing a fix</li> <li>Reporting to others</li> <li>Publication</li> <li>Monitoring threats</li> <li>Assigning CVE IDs</li> <li>Acquiring exploits</li> </ul> <p>The other work task is included as a placeholder for any Participant-specific tasks not represented here.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/em_bt/","title":"Embargo Management Behavior Tree","text":"<p>The Embargo Management Behavior Tree is shown in the figure below. It follows the state transition function in the Embargo Management Process Model. Recall that the EM process begins in the \\(q^{em} \\in N\\) state and ends in one of two states:</p> <ul> <li>in the eXited (\\(q^{em} \\in X\\)) state after having established an     Active embargo, or</li> <li>in the None (\\(q^{em} \\in N\\)) state after having exhausted all     attempts to reach an agreement</li> </ul> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Embargo Management Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    fb[?]\n    rm_s_or_c([\"RM S or C?\"])\n    fb --&gt;|A| rm_s_or_c\n    em_x([\"EM X?\"])\n    fb --&gt;|B| em_x\n    seq1[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|C| seq1\n    cs_not_pxa([\"CS not in pxa?\"])\n    seq1 --&gt; cs_not_pxa\n    em_none([\"EM N?\"])\n    seq1 --&gt; em_none\n    n_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|D| n_seq\n    n_em_none([\"EM N?\"])\n    n_seq --&gt; n_em_none\n    n_fb[?]\n    n_seq --&gt; n_fb\n    n_stop_trying([\"stop trying?\"])\n    n_fb --&gt; n_stop_trying\n    n_propose([\"propose\"])\n    n_fb --&gt; n_propose\n    p_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|E| p_seq\n    p_em_prop([\"EM P?\"])\n    p_seq --&gt; p_em_prop\n    p_fb[?]\n    p_seq --&gt; p_fb\n    p_terminate[\"terminate\"]\n    p_fb --&gt; p_terminate\n    p_jump[\"evaluate\"]\n    p_fb --&gt; p_jump\n    p_fb_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    p_fb --&gt; p_fb_seq\n    p_reject[\"reject\"]\n    p_fb_seq --&gt; p_reject\n    p_em_to_n[\"EM P &amp;rarr; N&lt;br/&gt;(emit ER)\"]\n    p_fb_seq --&gt; p_em_to_n\n    a_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|F| a_seq\n    a_em_a([\"EM A?\"])\n    a_seq --&gt; a_em_a\n    a_fb[?]\n    a_seq --&gt; a_fb\n    a_terminate[\"terminate\"]\n    a_fb --&gt; a_terminate\n    a_current_terms([\"current terms ok?\"])\n    a_fb --&gt; a_current_terms\n    a_propose[\"propose\"]\n    a_fb --&gt; a_propose\n    r_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|G| r_seq\n    r_em_r([\"EM R?\"])\n    r_seq --&gt; r_em_r\n    r_fb[?]\n    r_seq --&gt; r_fb\n    r_terminate[\"terminate\"]\n    r_fb --&gt; r_terminate\n    r_jump[\"evaluate\"]\n    r_fb --&gt; r_jump\n    r_fb_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    r_fb --&gt; r_fb_seq\n    r_reject[\"reject\"]\n    r_fb_seq --&gt; r_reject\n    r_em_to_a[\"EM R &amp;rarr; A&lt;br/&gt;(emit EJ)\"]\n    r_fb_seq --&gt; r_em_to_a</code></pre> <p>(A) The tree starts with a check to see whether no report has arrived or whether the report has already Closed (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{S{,}C\\}\\)). If either of these conditions is met, no further effort is needed, and the tree succeeds.</p> <p>(B) Next, the tree checks whether the embargo has already eXited (\\(q^{em} \\in X\\)). If it has, that leads the tree to succeed.</p> <p>(C) Failing that, the treat checks to see if the case has moved outside the \"habitable zone\" for embargoes. The \\({q^{cs}\\not\\in\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot pxa}\\) condition is true when attacks have been observed, an exploit has been made public, or information about the vulnerability has been made public. If one of those conditions is met and the embargo state is None (\\(q^{em} \\in N\\)), the check returns Success, and the tree terminates, consistent with Negotiating Embargoes.</p> <p>See also</p> <ul> <li>Negotiating Embargoes</li> <li>Propose Embargo Behavior</li> <li>Terminate Embargo Behavior</li> <li>Evaluate Embargo Behavior</li> </ul> <p>Otherwise, we continue through each remaining EM state.</p> <p>(D) When there is no embargo and there are no outstanding proposals (\\(q^{em} \\in N\\)), the only options are to either stop trying or propose a new embargo. The decision to stop trying to achieve an embargo is left to individual Participants, although we did provide some relevant guidance in Negotiating Embargoes.</p> <p>(E) When there is an outstanding embargo proposal (\\(q^{em} \\in P\\)), we first attempt the terminate task. This task returns Success if there is a reason for \\({q^{em} \\in P \\xrightarrow{r} N}\\).</p> <p>At this point, if there is no reason to terminate, we proceed to the evaluate task described below. The evaluate task returns Success if either the proposal is accepted or a counterproposal is made.</p> <p>Assuming neither of these succeeds, we proceed to reject the proposal, returning to \\(q^{em} \\in N\\) and emitting a corresponding \\(ER\\) message.</p> <p>(F) The process within the Active (\\(q^{em} \\in A\\)) state is similarly straightforward. If there is reason to terminate the embargo, do so. Otherwise, either the current embargo terms are acceptable, or a new embargo should be proposed.</p> <p>(G) Finally, we handle the Revise EM state (\\(q^{em} \\in R\\)). The structure of this branch mirrors that of the Proposed state discussed above. Again, we check to see if there is cause to terminate doing so, if needed. If termination is not indicated, we proceed once again to evaluate the proposed revision, either accepting or countering the proposal. When neither of these succeed, the revision is rejected and the EM state returns to \\(q^{em} \\in A\\) with the original embargo terms intact. An \\(EJ\\) message conveys this information to the other Participants.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/em_eval_bt/","title":"Evaluate Proposed Embargo Behavior","text":"<p>The acceptance or counterproposal of an embargo is handled by the Evaluate Proposed Embargo Behavior Tree shown in the figure below.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Evaluate Proposed Embargo Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    fb[?]\n    seq1[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|A| seq1\n    eval[\"evaluate\"]\n    seq1 --&gt; eval\n    accept[\"accept\"]\n    seq1 --&gt; accept\n    em_to_a[\"EM &amp;rarr; A&lt;br/&gt;(emit EA)\"]\n    seq1 --&gt; em_to_a\n    seq2[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|B| seq2\n    will_counter([\"willing to counter?\"])\n    seq2 --&gt; will_counter\n    propose[\"propose\"]\n    seq2 --&gt; propose</code></pre> <p>(A) As noted above, the same process applies to both the Proposed and Revise EM states (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{P,R\\}\\)). An evaluation task is followed by an accept task. These tasks are placeholders for the actual decision-making process, which is left to individual Participants. In both cases, acceptance leads to an EM state transition to \\(q^{em} \\in A\\) and emission of an \\(EA\\) message.</p> <p>See also</p> <ul> <li>Propose Embargo Behavior</li> </ul> <p>(B) On the other hand, the proposed terms may not be acceptable. In this case, the Participant might be willing to offer a counterproposal. The counterproposal is covered by the propose behavior.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/em_propose_bt/","title":"Propose Embargo Behavior","text":"<p>The Propose Embargo Behavior Tree is shown in the figure below.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Propose Embargo Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    cs_pxa([\"CS in ...pxa?\"])\n    seq --&gt;|A| cs_pxa\n    fb1[?]\n    seq --&gt;|B| fb1\n    cs_d([\"CS ..d...?\"])\n    fb1 --&gt; cs_d\n    other([\"other reason\"])\n    fb1 --&gt; other\n    select[\"select terms\"]\n    seq --&gt;|C| select\n    fb2[?]\n    seq --&gt;|D| fb2\n    pr_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb2 --&gt;|D1| pr_seq\n    em_p_or_r([\"EM P or R?\"])\n    pr_seq --&gt; em_p_or_r\n    no_new_prop([\"no new proposal?\"])\n    pr_seq --&gt; no_new_prop\n    np_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb2 --&gt;|D2| np_seq\n    em_n_or_p([\"EM N or P?\"])\n    np_seq --&gt; em_n_or_p\n    em_to_p[\"EM &amp;rarr; P&lt;br/&gt;(emit EP)\"]\n    np_seq --&gt; em_to_p\n    ar_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb2 --&gt;|D3| ar_seq\n    em_a_or_r([\"EM A or R?\"])\n    ar_seq --&gt; em_a_or_r\n    em_to_r[\"EM &amp;rarr; R&lt;br/&gt;(emit EV)\"]\n    ar_seq --&gt; em_to_r</code></pre> <p>See also</p> <ul> <li>Negotiate Embargoes</li> <li>Default Embargoes</li> </ul> <p>It consists of a sequence that begins with (A,B) a check for embargo viability as outlined in Negotiating Embargoes.</p> <p>Once the checks succeed, it proceeds to (C) selecting embargo terms to propose. Implementations of this task might simply draw from a default policy, as in Default Embargoes, or it might be a case-specific decision made by a Participant.</p> <p>(D) Embargo terms can be proposed from any of the non-eXited states (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{N,P,A,R\\}\\)).</p> <p>(D1) If a new or revised embargo has already been proposed (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{P,R\\}\\), the tree then checks whether a counterproposal is desired. Assuming it is not, no proposal is made, and the behavior succeeds.</p> <p>Otherwise, (D2) proposals from state \\(q^{em} \\in N\\) emit \\(EP\\) and transition \\(q^{em} \\xrightarrow{p} P\\), whereas (D3) those from \\(q^{em} \\in A\\) emit \\(EV\\) and move to \\(q^{em} \\xrightarrow{p} R\\).</p> <p>Proposals from states \\(q^{em} \\in P\\) (D2) or \\(q^{em} \\in R\\) (D3) represent counterproposals and, therefore, do not change the EM state. They do, however, emit \\(EP\\) or \\(EV\\) messages as appropriate.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/em_terminate_bt/","title":"Terminate Embargo Behavior","text":"<p>The Terminate Embargo Behavior Tree is shown in the diagram below. It consists of two major behaviors depending on whether an embargo has been established or not.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Terminate Embargo Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    fb[?]\n    em_n_or_x([\"EM N or X?\"])\n    fb --&gt;|A| em_n_or_x\n    p_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|B| p_seq\n    em_p([\"EM P?\"])\n    p_seq --&gt; em_p\n    p_fb[?]\n    p_seq --&gt; p_fb\n    p_cs_not_pxa([\"CS not in ...pxa?\"])\n    p_fb --&gt; p_cs_not_pxa\n    other([\"other reason?\"])\n    p_fb --&gt; other\n    p_em_to_n[\"EM P &amp;rarr; N&lt;br/&gt;(emit ER)\"]\n    p_seq --&gt; p_em_to_n\n    ar_seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|C| ar_seq\n    em_a_or_r([\"EM A or R?\"])\n    ar_seq --&gt; em_a_or_r\n    ar_fb[?]\n    ar_seq --&gt; ar_fb\n    ar_cs_not_pxa([\"CS not in ...pxa?\"])\n    ar_fb --&gt; ar_cs_not_pxa\n    ar_timer_expired([\"timer expired?\"])\n    ar_fb --&gt; ar_timer_expired\n    ar_other([\"other reason?\"])\n    ar_fb --&gt; ar_other\n    ar_exit_embargo[\"exit embargo\"]\n    ar_seq --&gt; ar_exit_embargo\n    ar_em_to_x[\"EM &amp;rarr; X&lt;br/&gt;(emit ET)\"]\n    ar_seq --&gt; ar_em_to_x</code></pre> <p>(A) If the EM state is None or eXited, (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{N{,}X\\}\\)), the tree succeeds immediately.</p> <p>(B) The next node handles the scenario where no embargo has been established. The behavior descends into a sequence that checks whether we are in \\(Proposed\\) (\\(q^{em} \\in P\\)). If we are, we check to see if there is a reason to exit the embargo negotiation process. One such reason is that the case state is outside the embargo \"habitable zone,\" but there may be others that we leave unspecified. If any reason is found, then the proposal is rejected, the state returns to None, and an \\(ER\\) message is sent.</p> <p>(C) Should that branch fail, we still need to handle the situation where an embargo has already been established. Following a confirmation that we are in either Active or Revise, we again look for reasons to exit, this time adding the possibility of timer expiration to the conditions explicitly called out. Terminating an existing embargo might have some other teardown procedures to be completed, which we represent as the exit embargo task. Finally, the EM state is updated to eXited and an \\(ET\\) message is emitted.</p> <p>See also</p> <ul> <li>Early Termination</li> <li>Threat Monitoring Behavior</li> <li>Message Handling Behavior</li> </ul> <p>The Terminate Embargo Behavior Tree appears in multiple locations in the larger tree. We will encounter it again as a possible response to evidence collected via threat monitoring as well as in response to certain CS or EM messages in states when an embargo is no longer viable.</p>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/fix_dev_bt/","title":"Fix Development Behavior","text":"<p>The Fix Development Behavior Tree is shown below.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Fix Development Behavior Tree\n---\nflowchart LR\n    fb[\"?\"]\n    role_not_vendor([\"role is not vendor?\"])\n    fb --&gt;|A| role_not_vendor\n    cs_in_VF([\"CS in VF...?\"])\n    fb --&gt;|B| cs_in_VF\n    seq[\"&amp;rarr;\"]\n    fb --&gt;|C| seq\n    rm_in_a([\"RM in A?\"])\n    seq --&gt; rm_in_a\n    create_fix[\"create fix\"]\n    seq --&gt; create_fix\n    fix_ready([\"fix ready?\"])\n    seq --&gt; fix_ready\n    cs_to_VF[\"CS &amp;rarr; VFd...&lt;br/&gt;(emit CF)\"]\n    seq --&gt; cs_to_VF</code></pre> <p>(A) Fix development is relegated to the Vendor role, so Non-Vendors just return Success since they have nothing further to do.</p> <p>(B) For Vendors, if a fix is ready (i.e., the case is in \\(q^{cs} \\in VF\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\)), the tree returns Success.</p> <p>(C) Otherwise, engaged Vendors (\\(q^{rm} \\in A\\)) can</p> <ul> <li>create fixes</li> <li>set \\(q^{cs} \\in Vfd\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot \\xrightarrow{\\mathbf{F}} VFd\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\)</li> <li>emit \\(CF\\) upon completion</li> </ul>"},{"location":"topics/behavior_logic/id_assignment_bt/","title":"CVE ID Assignment Behavior","text":"<p>Many CVD practitioners want to assign identifiers to the vulnerabilities they coordinate.
