This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>As described in the EM process model, the EM process has the potential for unbounded propose-reject churn. To reduce the potential for this churn and increase the likelihood that some embargo is established rather than a stalemate of unaccepted proposals, we offer the following guidance.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#declaring-defaults","title":"Declaring Defaults","text":"<p>First, we note that all CVD Participants (including Reporters) are free to establish their own default embargo period in a published vulnerability disclosure policy. In particular, we recommend that CVD report recipients (typically Vendors and Coordinators) do so.</p> <p>Participants MAY include a default embargo period as part of a published Vulnerability Disclosure Policy.</p> <p>Report Recipients SHOULD post a default embargo period as part of their Vulnerability Disclosure Policy to set expectations with potential Reporters.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#using-defaults","title":"Using Defaults","text":"<p>Next, we work through the possible interactions of published policies with proposed embargoes. Each of the following scenarios assumes a starting state of \\(q^{em} \\in N\\), and a negotiation between two parties. We cover the extended situation (adding parties to an existing embargo) in Adding Participants. For now, we begin with the simplest case and proceed in an approximate order of ascending complexity.</p> <p>In each of the following, subscripts on transitions indicate the Participant whose proposal is being acted upon, not the Participant who is performing the action. For example, \\(a_{sender}\\) indicates acceptance of the Sender's proposal, even if it is the Receiver doing the accepting.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#no-defaults-no-proposals","title":"No Defaults, No Proposals","text":"Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in N\\] <p>We begin with the simplest case, in which neither party has a default and no embargo has been proposed.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; N</code></pre> <p>If neither Sender nor Receiver proposes an embargo, and no policy defaults apply, no embargo SHALL exist.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#sender-proposes-when-receiver-has-no-default-embargo","title":"Sender Proposes When Receiver Has No Default Embargo","text":"<p>Next, we consider the case where the Sender has a default embargo or otherwise proposes an embargo and the Receiver has no default embargo specified by policy.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; N\n    N --&gt; P : sender proposes\n    P --&gt; A : receiver accepts\n    A --&gt; R : receiver proposes revision</code></pre> Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in N \\xrightarrow{p_{sender}} P \\xrightarrow{a_{sender}} A\\] <p>If the Sender proposes an embargo and the Receiver has no default embargo specified by policy, the Receiver SHOULD accept the Sender's proposal.</p> Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in A \\xrightarrow{p_{receiver}} R\\] <p>The Receiver MAY then propose a revision.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#receiver-has-default-embargo-sender-implies-acceptance","title":"Receiver Has Default Embargo, Sender Implies Acceptance","text":"<p>The next scenario is where the Receiver has a default embargo specified by policy and the Sender does not propose an embargo.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; N\n    N --&gt; P : receiver default proposal\n    P --&gt; A : sender accepts</code></pre> Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in N \\xrightarrow{p_{receiver}} P\\] <p>A Receiver's default embargo specified in its vulnerability disclosure policy SHALL be treated as an initial embargo proposal.</p> Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in N \\xrightarrow{p_{receiver}} P \\xrightarrow{p_{sender}} P\\] <p>If the Receiver has declared a default embargo in its vulnerability disclosure policy and the Sender proposes nothing to the contrary, the Receiver's default embargo SHALL be considered as an accepted proposal.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#sender-proposes-an-embargo-longer-than-the-receiver-default","title":"Sender Proposes an Embargo Longer than the Receiver Default","text":"<p>Now we consider the case where the Sender proposes an embargo longer than the Receiver's default.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; N\n    N --&gt; P : receiver&lt;br/&gt;proposes&lt;br/&gt;default&lt;br/&gt;(shorter)\n    P --&gt; A : sender&lt;br/&gt;accepts&lt;br/&gt;(shorter)\n    A --&gt; R : sender&lt;br/&gt;proposes&lt;br/&gt;(longer)\n    R --&gt; A : receiver&lt;br/&gt;accepts&lt;br/&gt;(longer)\n    R --&gt; A : receiver rejects&lt;br/&gt;(shorter default persists)</code></pre> <p>If the Sender proposes an embargo longer than the Receiver's default embargo, the Receiver's default SHALL be taken as accepted and the Sender's proposal taken as a proposed revision.</p> Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in N \\xrightarrow{p_{receiver}} P \\xrightarrow{p_{sender}} P \\xrightarrow{a_{receiver}} A \\xrightarrow{r_{sender}} R\\] Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in \\begin{cases}     R \\xrightarrow{a_{sender}} A \\\\     R \\xrightarrow{r_{sender}} A     \\end{cases}\\] <p>The Receiver MAY then accept or reject the proposed extension.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#sender-proposes-an-embargo-shorter-than-the-receiver-default","title":"Sender Proposes an Embargo Shorter than the Receiver Default","text":"<p>Finally, we reach a common scenario in which the Sender proposes an embargo shorter than the Receiver's default.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; N\n    N --&gt; P : sender&lt;br/&gt;proposes&lt;br/&gt;(shorter)\n    P --&gt; A : receiver&lt;br/&gt;accepts&lt;br/&gt;(shorter)\n    A --&gt; R : receiver&lt;br/&gt;proposes&lt;br/&gt;(longer default)\n    R --&gt; A : sender&lt;br/&gt;accepts&lt;br/&gt;(longer default))\n    R --&gt; A : sender rejects&lt;br/&gt;(shorter persists)</code></pre> <p>If the Sender proposes an embargo shorter than the Receiver's default embargo, the Sender's proposal SHALL be taken as accepted and the Receiver's default taken as a proposed revision.</p> Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in N \\xrightarrow{p_{receiver}} P \\xrightarrow{p_{sender}} P \\xrightarrow{a_{sender}} A \\xrightarrow{r_{receiver}} R\\] Formalism \\[q^{em} \\in \\begin{cases}     R \\xrightarrow{a_{receiver}} A \\\\     R \\xrightarrow{r_{receiver}} A     \\end{cases}\\] <p>The Sender MAY then accept or reject the proposed extension.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#rationale-for-accepting-the-shortest-proposed-embargo","title":"Rationale for Accepting the Shortest Proposed Embargo","text":"<p>Here we provide two arguments for why a Participant should accept the shorter of two proposed embargoes, with the option to propose a revision to lengthen it.</p> <p>A Game Theory Argument for Accepting the Shortest Proposed Embargo</p> <p>Readers may notice that we have taken a shortest proposal wins approach to the above guidance. This is intentional, and it results directly from the asymmetry mentioned in Negotiating Embargoes The Receiver is faced with a choice to either accept the Reporter's proposal and attempt to extend it or to reject the proposal and end up with no embargo at all. Therefore, if we take the stance that for a vulnerability with no fix available, any embargo is better than no embargo, it should be obvious that it is in the Receiver's interest to accept even a short proposed embargo before immediately working to revise it.</p> <p>The alternative is impractical because the Reporter is not obligated to provide the report to the Receiver at all. In the scenario where a Reporter proposes a short embargo and the Receiver rejects it because it is not long enough, the Reporter might choose to exit the negotiation entirely and publish whenever they choose without ever providing the report to the Receiver. That is not to say that we recommend this sort of behavior from Reporters. In fact, we specifically recommend the opposite in Negotiating Embargoes. Rather, it once more acknowledges the time-dependent informational asymmetry inherent to the CVD process.</p> <p>A Logical Argument for Accepting the Shortest Proposed Embargo</p> <p>Perhaps the above reasoning comes across as too Machiavellian for some readers. Here is a different perspective: Say a Reporter proposes an embargo of n days, while the Vendor would prefer m days. If n and m are given in units of days, we can look at them as a series of individual agreements, each of 1 day in length. We will represent each Participant as a vector representing that Participant's willingness to perpetuate the embargo on each day. Embargo willingness will be represented as a 1 if the Participant is willing to commit to keeping the embargo on that day, and a 0 if they are not. For simplicity's sake, we assume that each Participant is willing to maintain the embargo up to a certain point, and then their willingness goes away. In other words, each vector will be a series of zero or more _1_s followed by zero or more _0_s. For example, \\([1,1,1,1,0,0,0]\\) represents a Participant's willingness to engage in a 4-day embargo.</p> <p>For our two Participants, let \\(\\mathbf{x}_ and _\\mathbf{y}\\) be zero-indexed vectors of length \\(max(n,m)\\).</p> \\[\\begin{aligned}     |\\mathbf{x}| &amp;= max(n,m) \\\\     |\\mathbf{y}| &amp;= max(n,m) \\end{aligned}\\] <p>The elements of each vector represent each respective Participant's willingness for the embargo to persist on each consecutive day.</p> \\[\\begin{aligned}     \\mathbf{x} =          \\begin{bmatrix} x_i :         x_i =          \\begin{cases}             1 &amp;\\text{if }i &lt; n \\\\             0 &amp;\\text{otherwise} \\\\         \\end{cases}         &amp; \\text{for } 0 \\leq i &lt; max(n,m)         \\end{bmatrix} \\\\     \\mathbf{y} =         \\begin{bmatrix} y_i :          y_i =          \\begin{cases}             1 &amp;\\text{if }i &lt; m \\\\             0 &amp;\\text{otherwise}         \\end{cases}         &amp; \\text{ for } 0 \\leq i &lt; max(n,m)     \\end{bmatrix} \\end{aligned}\\] <p>Note that we have constructed these vectors so that each vector's scalar sum is just the length of embargo they prefer.</p> \\[\\begin{aligned}     \\Sigma(\\mathbf{x}) &amp;= n \\\\     \\Sigma(\\mathbf{y}) &amp;= m  \\end{aligned}\\] <p>Now we can define an agreement vector \\(\\mathbf{z}\\) as the pairwise logical AND (\\(\\wedge\\)) of elements from \\(\\mathbf{x}_ and _\\mathbf{y}\\):</p> \\[\\begin{aligned}     \\mathbf{z} =      \\begin{bmatrix} z_i :          z_i = x_i \\land y_i         &amp; \\text{for }0 \\leq i &lt; max(n,m)     \\end{bmatrix} \\end{aligned}\\] <p>For example, if one party prefers an embargo of length \\(n=4\\) days while another prefers one of length \\(m=7\\) days, we can apply \\(\\mathbf{x}\\), \\(\\mathbf{y}\\), and \\(\\mathbf{z}\\) as follows:</p> \\[\\begin{split}     \\mathbf{x} &amp;= [1,1,1,1,0,0,0] \\\\     \\wedge~\\mathbf{y} &amp;= [1,1,1,1,1,1,1] \\\\     \\hline     \\mathbf{z} &amp;= [1,1,1,1,0,0,0]     \\end{split}\\] <p>From this, we can see that the scalar sum of the agreement vector---and therefore the longest embargo acceptable to both parties---is simply the lesser of n and m:</p> \\[\\Sigma ( \\mathbf{z} ) = min(n,m)\\] <p>As an example:</p> <p>The Shortest Proposed Embargo Wins</p> <p>If a Reporter proposes a 90-day embargo, but the Vendor prefers a 30-day embargo, we can think of this as a series of 1-day agreements in which both parties agree to the first 30 days of the embargo and disagree beyond that. By accepting the shorter 30-day embargo, the Reporter now has 30 days to continue negotiations with the Vendor to extend the embargo. Even if those continued negotiations fail, both parties get at least the 30-day embargo period they agreed on in the first place. This should be preferable to both parties versus the alternative of no embargo at all were they to simply reject the shorter proposal.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Embargo Agreement in a Nutshell\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    dots: ...\n    state Agreement {\n        direction LR\n        [*] --&gt; 1\n        1 --&gt; 2\n        2 --&gt; dots\n        dots --&gt; 30\n        negotiate: negotiate extension\n        [*] --&gt; negotiate\n        negotiate --&gt; 30\n    }\n    30 --&gt; 31\n    state Disagreement {\n        dots2: ...\n        31 --&gt; dots2\n        dots2 --&gt; 90\n    }</code></pre> <p>Typically it is the Reporter who desires a shorter embargo than the Vendor. We chose our example to demonstrate that this analysis works between any two parties, regardless of which party wants the shorter embargo.</p> <p>Resolving Multiple Proposals at Once</p> <p>On our way to making this principle explicit, we immediately came across a second scenario worth a brief diversion: What to do when multiple revisions are up for negotiation simultaneously? Based on the idea of extending the above to an efficient pairwise evaluation of multiple proposals, we suggest the following heuristic:</p> <ol> <li>Sort the proposals in order from earliest to latest according to their expiration date.</li> <li>Set the current candidate to the earliest proposal.</li> <li>Loop over each remaining (later) proposal, evaluating it against the current candidate.</li> <li>If the newly evaluated proposal is accepted, it becomes the current candidate and the loop repeats.</li> <li>Otherwise, the loop exits at the first rejected proposal.</li> <li>The current candidate (i.e., the latest accepted proposal) becomes the new Active embargo.</li> <li>If the earliest proposed revision is rejected---implying that none of the later ones would be acceptable either---then the existing Active embargo remains intact.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/defaults/#resolving-proposals-and-revisions","title":"Resolving Proposals and Revisions","text":"<p>Summarizing the principles just laid out as rules</p> <p>When two or more embargo proposals are open (i.e., none have yet been accepted) and \\(q^{em} \\in P\\), Participants SHOULD accept the shortest one and propose the remainder as revisions.</p> <p>When two or more embargo revisions are open (i.e., an embargo is active yet none of the proposals have been decided) and \\(q^{em} \\in R\\), Participants SHOULD accept or reject them individually, in earliest to latest expiration order.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/early_termination/","title":"Early Termination","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Embargoes sometimes terminate prior to the agreed date and time. This is an unavoidable, if inconvenient, fact arising from three main causes:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Vulnerability discovery capability is widely distributed across the     world, and not all Finders become cooperative Reporters.</p> </li> <li> <p>Even among otherwise cooperative CVD Participants, leaks sometimes happen.</p> </li> <li> <p>Adversaries are unconstrained by CVD in their vulnerability discovery,     exploit code development, and use of exploit code in attacks.</p> </li> </ol>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/early_termination/#be-prepared-for-embargo-termination","title":"Be Prepared for Embargo Termination","text":"<p>While many leaks are unintentional and due to miscommunication or errors in a Participant's CVD process, the effect is the same regardless of the cause. As a result,</p> <p>Participants SHOULD be prepared with contingency plans in the event of early embargo termination.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/early_termination/#reasons-to-terminate-an-embargo-early","title":"Reasons to Terminate an Embargo Early","text":"<p>Some reasons to terminate an embargo before the agreed date include the following:</p> Formalism <p>\\(q^{cs} \\in \\{ \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot P \\cdot\\cdot, \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot X \\cdot \\}\\)</p> <p>Embargoes SHALL terminate immediately when information about the vulnerability becomes public. Public information may include reports of the vulnerability or exploit code.</p> Formalism <p>\\(q^{cs} \\in \\{ \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot A \\}\\)</p> <p>Embargoes SHOULD terminate early when there is evidence that the vulnerability is being actively exploited by adversaries.</p> <p>Embargoes SHOULD terminate early when there is evidence that adversaries possess exploit code for the vulnerability.</p> <p>Embargoes MAY terminate early when there is evidence that adversaries are aware of the technical details of the vulnerability.</p> <p>The above is not a complete list of acceptable reasons to terminate an embargo early. Note that the distinction between the SHALL in the first item and the SHOULD in the second is derived from the reasoning given in the CS model , where we describe the CS model's transition function. Embargo termination is the set of transitions described in the EM model.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/early_termination/#waiting-for-all-vendors-to-reach-fix-ready-may-be-impractical","title":"Waiting for All Vendors to Reach Fix Ready May Be Impractical","text":"Fix Ready Definition \\[q^{cs} \\in VF\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\] <p>It is not necessary for all Vendor Participants to reach Fix Ready before publication or embargo termination. Especially in larger MPCVD cases, there comes a point where the net benefit of waiting for every Vendor to be ready is outweighed by the benefit of delivering a fix to the population that can deploy it. No solid formula for this exists, but factors to consider include</p> <ul> <li>the market share of the Vendors in Fix Ready (\\(q^{cs} \\in VF \\cdot \\cdot \\cdot \\cdot\\)) compared to those that are not (\\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot f \\cdot \\cdot \\cdot \\cdot\\))</li> <li>the software supply chain for fix delivery to Deployers</li> <li>the potential impact to critical infrastructure, public safety/health, or national security</li> </ul> <p>Embargoes MAY terminate early when a quorum of Vendor Participants is prepared to release fixes for the vulnerability (\\(q^{cs}  \\in VF\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\)), even if some Vendors remain unprepared (\\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot f \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\)).</p> <p>Participants SHOULD consider the software supply chain for the vulnerability in question when determining an appropriate quorum for release.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/em_dfa_diagram/","title":"Em dfa diagram","text":"<pre><code>---\ntitle: Embargo Management State Diagram\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; None\n    state Pre-Embargo {\n        None --&gt; Proposed : propose\n        Proposed --&gt; Proposed : propose\n        Proposed --&gt; None : reject\n        Proposed --&gt; Active : accept\n    }\n    state Active_Embargo {\n        Active --&gt; Revise : propose\n        Revise --&gt; Active : accept\n        Revise --&gt; Active : reject\n        Revise --&gt; Revise : propose\n        Revise --&gt; eXited : terminate\n        Active --&gt; eXited : terminate\n    }\n    state Post-Embargo {\n        eXited\n    }\n    eXited --&gt; [*]</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/","title":"Negotiating Embargoes","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Negotiating and entering into a new embargo for a case is only possible within an embargo \"habitable zone\" defined in terms of the Case State model as laid out below.</p> <p>The notation for CS model states is explained in Case State model, but the contextual explanation below should suffice for now.</p> <p>CVD Participants MUST NOT propose or accept a new embargo negotiation when any of the following conditions are true:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Information about the vulnerability is already known to the     public (\\({q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot P \\cdot\\cdot}\\)).</p> </li> <li> <p>An exploit for the vulnerability is publicly available     (\\({q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot X \\cdot}\\)).</p> </li> <li> <p>There is evidence that the vulnerability is being actively     exploited by adversaries (\\({q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot A}\\)).</p> </li> </ol> <p>CVD Participants SHOULD NOT propose or accept a new embargo negotiation when the fix for a vulnerability has already been deployed (\\(q^{cs} \\in VFDpxa\\)).</p> <p>Counterexamples include</p> <ul> <li>when an embargo is desired to allow for a downstream Vendor to synchronize their fix delivery or deployment</li> <li>when a Vendor has deployed a fix but wants to complete their root cause analysis prior to releasing information about the vulnerability.</li> </ul> <p>CVD Participants MAY propose or accept a new embargo when the fix for a vulnerability is ready but has neither been made public nor deployed (\\(q^{cs} \\in VFdpxa\\)). Such an embargo SHOULD be brief and used only to allow Participants to prepare for timely publication or deployment.</p> <p>CVD Participants MAY propose or accept an embargo in all other case states (\\({q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot pxa}\\)).</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/#asymmetry-in-embargo-negotiation","title":"Asymmetry in Embargo Negotiation","text":"<p>Asymmetry is inherent in the CVD process because those who currently have the vulnerability information get to decide with whom they will share it. This asymmetry puts Reporters at somewhat of an advantage when it comes to the initial report submission to another Participant. We discuss some ways to improve (but not fully remove) this asymmetry in Default Embargoes, but for now we just need to acknowledge that it exists.</p> <p>Participants MAY accept or reject any proposed embargo as they see fit.</p> <p>Receivers SHOULD accept any embargo proposed by Reporters.</p> <p>Receivers MAY propose embargo terms they find more favorable as they see fit.</p> <p>Participants MAY withdraw (reject) their own unaccepted Proposed embargo.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/#respond-promptly","title":"Respond Promptly","text":"<p>Timely response to embargo proposals is important. Explicit acceptance is expected.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD explicitly accept or reject embargo proposals in a timely manner. (For example, embargo agreement or rejection SHOULD NOT be tacit.)</p> <p>Participants MAY interpret another Participant's failure to respond to an embargo proposal in a timely manner as a rejection of that proposal.</p> <p>In the absence of an explicit accept or reject response from a Receiver in a timely manner, the Sender MAY proceed in a manner consistent with an EM state of None (\\(q^{em} \\in N\\)).</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/#no-embargo-means-no-embargo","title":"No Embargo means No Embargo","text":"<p>Once an embargo negotiation has failed the first time, Participants have no further obligations.</p> <p>In a case where the embargo state is None and for which an embargo has been proposed and either explicitly or tacitly rejected, Participants MAY take any action they choose with the report in question, including immediate publication.</p> <p>Incentives Matter</p> <p>We deliberately included the clauses \"explicitly or tacitly rejected\" and \"may take any action\" above to provide incentives for Participants to be declarative and negotiate in good faith. Were a rejected embargo proposal to carry any implied obligation to refrain from publication,  Participants might be motivated to use delayed or ambiguous responses to impose that obligation on others. Our goal is to avoid situations where a Participant is incentivized to simply ignore an embargo proposal while proceeding with an expectation that the other Participants are still bound by the proposed-but-inactive embargo terms. Therefore we have attempted to be very clear that only active embargoes impose obligations on Participants.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/#dont-give-up-too-soon","title":"Don't Give Up Too Soon","text":"<p>The above notwithstanding, Participants are encouraged to try again, especially when no explicit rejection has been communicated (i.e., in the tacitly rejected scenario described above).</p> <p>Participants SHOULD make reasonable attempts to retry embargo negotiations when prior proposals have been rejected or otherwise failed to achieve acceptance.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/#submitting-a-report-before-embargo-negotiations-conclude","title":"Submitting a Report Before Embargo Negotiations Conclude","text":"<p>Participants need not wait for embargo negotiations to conclude before submitting a report. However, by doing so, they might give up some of their leverage over the Receiver in the embargo negotiations.</p> <p>Participants MAY withhold a report from a Recipient until an initial embargo has been accepted.</p> <p>Submitting a report when an embargo proposal is pending (\\(q^{em} \\in P\\)) SHALL be construed as the Sender's acceptance (\\(q^{em} \\in P \\xrightarrow{a} A\\)) of the terms proposed regardless of whether the Sender or Receiver was the proposer.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/negotiating/#addressing-validation-uncertainty","title":"Addressing Validation Uncertainty","text":"<p>Participants might prefer to delay accepting or rejecting a proposed embargo until after they have had an opportunity to review the report through the validation and (possibly) prioritization processes. However, because other Participants are under no obligation to withhold publication of cases not covered by an active embargo, we recommend that a short embargo be used until the validation process concludes, at which point, it can be extended with a revision.</p> <p>Participants MAY use short embargo periods to cover their report validation process, and subsequently revise the embargo terms pending the outcome of their report validation and/or prioritization processes.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD remain flexible in adjusting embargo terms as the case evolves.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/","title":"Embargo Principles","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Embargoes are a means of inhibiting public disclosure of a vulnerability while defenses are prepared (e.g., until fix development has completed for a reasonable quorum of Vendors).</p> <p>The goal of the EM process is not to establish an exact publication schedule for every Participant to adhere to. Rather,</p> <p>Embargo Management Process Goal</p> <p>The goal of the Embargo Management process is to establish a window spanning from the present to some future time, during which Participants are expected to not publish or otherwise disclose information about the vulnerability to non-Participants outside of the CVD case.</p> <p>Hence our definition of an embargo:</p> <p>Embargo Definition</p> <p>An embargo is an informal agreement among peer CVD case Participants to refrain from publishing information about a vulnerability until some future point in time.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/#embargoes-are-a-social-agreement","title":"Embargoes Are a Social Agreement","text":"<p>An embargo is a social agreement between independent parties acting in the interest of providing vulnerability fixes to users in a timely manner while minimizing attacker advantage in the interim. However, embargoes are not always appropriate or useful within the context of any given CVD case.</p> <p>With that in mind, we offer the following principles as guidance. We begin with some behavioral norms that define what it means to cooperate with an embargo.</p> <p>Embargo Participants SHOULD NOT knowingly release information about an embargoed case until either</p> <ol> <li> <p>all proposed embargoes have been explicitly rejected</p> </li> <li> <p>no proposed embargo has been explicitly accepted in a timely     manner</p> </li> <li> <p>the expiration date/time of an accepted embargo has passed</p> </li> <li> <p>an accepted embargo has been terminated prior to the embargo     expiration date and time due to other reasons (e.g., those     outlined in Early Termination)</p> </li> </ol> <p>Additional Participants MAY be added to an existing embargo upon accepting the terms of that embargo.</p> <p>Adding Participants to an existing embargo SHALL NOT constitute \"release\" or \"publication\" so long as those Participants accept the terms of the embargo.</p> <p>See Adding Participants for more information.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/#embargoes-are-limited-short-term-agreements","title":"Embargoes are Limited Short-Term Agreements","text":"<p>Given all other facts about a vulnerability report being equal, there are two factors that contribute significantly to the success or failure of an embargo: scale and duration. The more people involved in an embargo, the more likely the embargo is to fail.</p> <p>Embargo participation SHOULD be limited to the smallest possible set of individuals and organizations needed to adequately address the vulnerability report.</p> <p>Similarly, the longer an embargo lasts, the more likely it is to fail.</p> <p>Embargo duration SHOULD be limited to the shortest duration possible to adequately address the vulnerability report.</p> <p>Furthermore, we need to establish a few norms related to embargo timing.</p> <p>An embargo SHALL specify an unambiguous date and time as its endpoint.</p> <p>An embargo SHALL NOT be used to indefinitely delay publication of vulnerability information, whether through repeated extension or by setting a long-range endpoint.</p> <p>An embargo SHALL begin at the moment it is accepted.</p> <p>Embargoes SHOULD be of short duration, from a few days to a few months.</p> <p></p> <p>CVD Embargoes Are Not NDAs</p> <p>CVD embargoes are not Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs). An NDA (also known as a Confidentiality agreement) is a legally binding contract between parties, often accompanied by a reward for compliance and/or some penalty in the event of unauthorized disclosure. NDAs do, on occasion, have a place in CVD processes, but the relatively rapid pace and scale of most MPCVD embargoes makes per-case NDAs prohibitively difficult. As a result, we are intentionally setting aside NDA negotiation as beyond the scope of this proposal.</p> <p>On the surface, many bug bounty programs may appear to fall outside our scope because they are often structured as  NDAs in which compliance is rewarded. For some bounty programs, the penalty for non-compliance or early disclosure is limited to the loss of the reward. For others, non-compliance can lead to the forfeiture of a promise of amnesty from the pursuit of civil or criminal charges that might otherwise apply because of security or acceptable-use policy violations. Nevertheless, we are optimistic that the bulk of this protocol (i.e., the parts that do not interfere with the contractual provisions of bounty-associated NDAs) will be found to be compatible with the full variety of  bounty-oriented CVD programs existing now and in the future.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/#embargo-participants-are-free-to-engage-or-disengage","title":"Embargo Participants Are Free to Engage or Disengage","text":"<p>As we just described, an embargo is not the same thing as an NDA, even if they have similar effects. Because it is a contract, an NDA can carry civil or even criminal penalties for breaking it. CVD embargoes have no such legal framework. Hence, CVD Participants are free to enter or exit an embargo at any time, for any reason. In fact, CVD Participants are not obliged to agree to any embargo at all. However, regardless of their choices, Participants should be clear about their status and intentions with other Participants. There are a few good reasons to exit an embargo early. (See Early Termination for more information.)</p> <p>Participants MAY propose a new embargo or revision to an existing embargo at any time within the constraints outlined in Negotiating Embargoes.</p> <p>Participants MAY reject proposed embargo terms for any reason.</p> <p>Participants in an embargo MAY exit the embargo at any time.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/#leaving-an-embargo-is-not-embargo-termination","title":"Leaving an Embargo is Not Embargo Termination","text":"<p>Note that a Participant leaving an embargo is not necessarily the same as the embargo itself terminating. Embargo termination corresponds to the \\(q^{em} \\in \\{A,R\\} \\xrightarrow{t} X\\) transition in the EM model and reflects a consensus among case Participants that the embargo no longer applies. A Participant leaving an Active embargo means that the embargo agreement between other Participants remains intact, but that the leaving Participant is no longer involved in the case.</p> <p>Participants stopping work on a case SHOULD notify remaining Participants of their intent to adhere to or disregard any existing embargo associated with the case.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD continue to comply with any active embargoes to which they have been a part, even if they stop work on the case.</p> <p>Participants who leave an Active embargo SHOULD be removed by the remaining Participants from further communication about the case.</p> <p>Embargo Engagement and Adherence</p> <p>We return to these concepts with the <code>case_engagement</code> and <code>embargo_adherence</code> attributes described in Case Object.</p> <p>These points imply a need for Participants to track the status of other Participants with respect to their adherence to the embargo and engagement with the case.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/#leaving-an-embargo-may-have-consequences","title":"Leaving an Embargo May Have Consequences","text":"<p>As we note in the CVD Guide, CVD is an iterated game, and actions have consequences. Leaving an embargo early in one case may have repercussions to Participants' willingness to cooperate in later cases.</p> <p>A Participant's refusal to accept embargo terms MAY result in that Participant being left out of the CVD case entirely.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD consider other Participants' history of cooperation when evaluating the terms of a proposed embargo.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/principles/#embargo-termination-is-not-the-same-as-publication","title":"Embargo Termination is Not the Same as Publication","text":"<p>Finally, embargo termination removes a constraint rather than adding an obligation.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD NOT publish information about the vulnerability when there is an active embargo.</p> <p>Participants MAY publish information about the vulnerability when there is no active embargo.</p> <p>Embargo termination SHALL NOT be construed as an obligation to publish.</p> <p>A discussion of how to decide who to invite to participate in a CVD case is addressed in Adding Participants.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/split_merge/","title":"Case Splitting and Merging","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Cases sometimes need to be split into multiple cases or merged into a single case. Case Splits can happen when a single vulnerability affects multiple products with different fix supply chains. Case Mergers can happen when two independent reports of vulnerabilities are found to be the same vulnerability. We discuss the impact of these events on embargoes below.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/split_merge/#impact-of-case-mergers-on-embargoes","title":"Impact of Case Mergers on Embargoes","text":"<p>While relatively rare, it is sometimes necessary for two independent CVD cases to be merged into a single case.</p> <p>Case Merge Example</p> <p>Two Finders independently discover vulnerabilities in separate products and report them to their respective (distinct) Vendors. Each Reporter-Vendor pair has already negotiated its own embargo. On further investigation, it is determined that both reported problems stem from a vulnerability in a library shared by both products. The decision is made to merge the cases into a single case. Once the cases merge, the best option is usually to renegotiate a new embargo for the new case.</p> <p>A new embargo SHOULD be proposed when any two or more CVD cases are to be merged into a single case and multiple parties have agreed to different embargo terms prior to the case merger.</p> <p>If no new embargo has been proposed, or if agreement has not been reached, the earliest of the previously accepted embargo dates SHALL be adopted for the merged case.</p> <p>Participants MAY propose revisions to the embargo on a merged case as usual.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/split_merge/#impact-of-case-splits-on-embargoes","title":"Impact of Case Splits on Embargoes","text":"<p>It is also possible that a single case needs to be split into multiple cases after an embargo has been agreed to.</p> <p>Case Split Example</p> <p>A vulnerability that affects two widely disparate fix supply chains, such as a library used in both Software-as-a-Service (SAAS) and  Operational Technology (OT) environments. The SAAS Vendors might be well positioned for a quick fix deployment while the OT Vendors might need considerably longer to work through the logistics of delivering deployable fixes to their customers. In such a case, the case Participants might choose to split the case into its respective supply chain cohorts to better coordinate within each group.</p> <p>Unlike the relatively simple situation of merging two cases, splitting a case into multiple cases is more complex. Case splits impose a dependency between the new case embargoes because the earlier embargo on one case might reveal the existence of a vulnerability in the products allocated to the later case. In these scenarios, it can be helpful to engage the services of a third party Coordinator (if one is not already involved in the case) to help navigate the case split.</p> <p>When a case is split into two or more parts, any existing embargo SHOULD transfer to the new cases.</p> <p>Participants approaching a case split MAY engage a Coordinator to act as a trusted third party to help resolve and coordinate embargo dependencies for the new cases.</p> <p>If any of the new cases need to renegotiate the embargo inherited from the parent case, any new embargo SHOULD be later than the inherited embargo.</p> <p>In the event that an earlier embargo date is needed for a child case, consideration SHALL be given to the impact that ending the embargo on that case will have on the other child cases retaining a later embargo date. In particular, Participants in each child case should assess whether earlier publication of one child case might reveal the existence of or details about other child cases.</p> <p>Participants in a child case SHALL communicate any subsequently agreed changes from the inherited embargo to the Participants of the other child cases.</p> <p>Note that it may not always be possible for the split cases to have different embargo dates without the earlier case revealing the existence of a vulnerability in the products allocated to the later case. For this reason, it is often preferable to avoid case splits entirely.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/","title":"Adding Participants to an Embargoed Case","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Here we describe the who and when of adding Participants to an embargoed case.</p> <p>As anyone who has tried to schedule a meeting with multiple attendees can attest, multi-party scheduling can be difficult. When that schedule must also accommodate work completion schedules for an MPCVD case, it becomes even harder. In Default Embargoes, we laid out a heuristic for resolving multiple embargo proposals, The Shortest Embargo Proposed Wins. More specifically, we recommended that Participants accept the earliest proposed end date and immediately propose and evaluate the rest as potential revisions. This principle applies to any MPCVD case, even at its outset.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#start-early-start-small","title":"Start Early, Start Small","text":"<p>Embargo negotiations can become contentious in larger cases. Many MPCVD cases grow over time, and it is usually easier to establish an embargo with a smaller group than a larger one. Conflict resolution via consensus agreement is fine if it works. In fact, in scenarios where Participants who have already agreed to an embargo get to choose who else to add to the embargo, the existing consensus can be a strong influence for the new Participant to consent to the existing agreement.</p> <p>In other words, it is usually preferable to present an already-accepted embargo to new Participants and get their agreement before potentially revising the embargo than it is to wait for a large multi-party negotiation to succeed before establishing an embargo in the first place.</p> <p>See also</p> <p>ISO/IEC 29147:2018</p> <p>When consensus fails, however, it may be helpful for the group to appoint a case lead to resolve any conflicts. Such scenarios are often an opportunity for a third-party Coordinator to be engaged.</p> <p>Therefore,</p> <p>Participants SHOULD attempt to establish an embargo as early in the process of handling the case as possible.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD follow consensus agreement to decide embargo terms.</p> <p>When consensus fails to reach agreement on embargo terms, Participants MAY appoint a case lead to resolve conflicts.</p> <p>Participants MAY engage a third-party Coordinator to act as a neutral third-party case lead to resolve conflicts between Participants during the course of handling a case.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#who-to-invite","title":"Who to Invite","text":"<p>The Finder/Reporter is, by definition, a Participant in any CVD case by virtue of their knowledge of the vulnerability in the first place. Additional Participants usually fall into one of three categories:</p> <p>All known Vendors of affected software SHOULD be included as Participants.</p> <p>Third-party Coordinators MAY be included as Participants.</p> <p>Other parties MAY be included as Participants when necessary and appropriate.</p> <p>Examples of Other Participants</p> <p>Examples of other Participants we have observed in past cases include</p> <ul> <li>Deployers</li> <li>Subject matter experts</li> <li>Government agencies with relevant regulatory oversight or critical infrastructure protection responsibilities.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#adding-participants-to-an-existing-embargo","title":"Adding Participants to an Existing Embargo","text":"<p>Adding new Participants to a case with an existing embargo might require the new Participant to accept the embargo prior to receiving the report.</p> <p>When inviting a new Participant to a case with an existing embargo, the inviting Participant SHALL propose the existing embargo to the invited Participant.</p> <p>A newly invited Participant to a case with an existing embargo SHOULD accept the existing embargo.</p> <p>The inviting Participant SHOULD NOT share the vulnerability report with the newly invited Participant unless the new Participant has accepted the existing embargo.</p> <p>The inviting Participant MAY interpret the potential Participant's default embargo contained in their published vulnerability disclosure policy in accordance with the default acceptance strategies listed in Default Embargoes.</p> <p>A newly invited Participant to a case with an existing embargo MAY propose a revision after accepting the existing embargo.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#when-to-invite-participants","title":"When to Invite Participants","text":"<p>In MPCVD there are practical considerations to be made regarding the timing of when to notify individual Participants. The primary factor in these decisions stems from the interaction of the Active embargo with the potential Participant's existing (explicit or implicit) disclosure policy.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#participants-with-disclosure-policies-shorter-than-an-existing-embargo","title":"Participants with Disclosure Policies Shorter Than an Existing Embargo","text":"<p>Adding a potential Participant with a known default disclosure policy shorter than an extant embargo leaves Participants with these options to choose from:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Shorten the existing embargo to match the potential Participant's     policy.</p> </li> <li> <p>Propose the existing embargo to the potential Participant, and, upon     acceptance, share the report with them.</p> </li> <li> <p>Delay notifying the potential Participant until their default policy     aligns with the existing embargo.</p> </li> <li> <p>Avoid including the potential Participant in the embargo entirely.</p> </li> </ol> <p>Example: A Vendor with a short default embargo</p> <p>Say a Vendor has a seven-day maximum public disclosure policy. Participants in a case with an existing embargo ending in three weeks might choose to notify that Vendor two weeks from now to ensure that even the default disclosure timeline remains compatible with the extant embargo.</p> <p>Participants with short default embargo policies SHOULD consider accepting longer embargoes in MPCVD cases.</p> <p>Participants in an MPCVD case MAY delay notifying potential Participants with short default embargo policies until their policy aligns with the agreed embargo.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#participants-with-disclosure-policies-longer-than-an-existing-embargo","title":"Participants with Disclosure Policies Longer Than an Existing Embargo","text":"<p>Similarly, adding a Participant with a known default disclosure policy longer than an extant embargo leaves Participants with the following options to choose from:</p> <ol> <li> <p>Lengthen the existing embargo to match the potential Participant's     policy.</p> </li> <li> <p>Propose the existing embargo to the potential Participant, and, upon     acceptance, share the report with them.</p> </li> <li> <p>Avoid including the potential Participant in the embargo entirely.</p> </li> </ol> <p>In the case of a Vendor with a longer default policy than the existing embargo, it is still preferable to give them as much lead time as possible even if it is not possible to extend the embargo to their preferred timing.</p> <p>In the interest of receiving the report in the first place, potential Participants with a longer default policy than an existing case SHOULD accept the embargo terms offered.</p> <p>After accepting an existing embargo, newly invited Participants with a longer default policy than an existing case MAY propose a revision to the existing embargo, if desired, to accommodate their preferences.</p> <p>Existing Participants MAY accept or reject such a proposed revision as they see fit.</p> <p>Participants in a case with an existing embargo SHOULD notify Vendors with a longer default embargo policy.</p> <p>Participants in a case with an existing embargo MAY choose to extend the embargo to accommodate a newly added Participant.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#untrustworthy-participants","title":"Untrustworthy Participants","text":"<p>Unfortunately, not all potential CVD Participants are equally trustworthy with vulnerability information.</p> <p>Examples of Untrustworthy Participants</p> <p>We can provide a few examples of potentially untrustworthy Participants:</p> <ul> <li>A Participant might have sub-par operational security or even business practices that result in adversaries often finding out about vulnerabilities in their products before the end of an embargo period.</li> <li>Participants might be subject to regulatory regimes in which they are required by law to share known vulnerabilities with government agencies having oversight responsibilities. Depending on the jurisdiction, these agencies might not be bound by the same embargoes as the other Participants in the case.</li> <li>The Participants in a case might consider a government agency to be an adversary itself and therefore not trustworthy with non-public vulnerability information.</li> </ul> <p>In these or similar scenarios, these concerns might lead to the exclusion of otherwise trustworthy Participants from an embargo.</p> <p>Participants that are known to leak or provide vulnerability information to adversaries either as a matter of policy or historical fact SHOULD be treated similar to Participants with brief disclosure policies.</p> <p>My Adversary Is Not Necessarily Your Adversary</p> <p>Trustworthiness has a strong subjective component, and individual perspectives on who is or is not trustworthy can vary widely. Thus, while acknowledging that adversary is not a universally agreed-upon category, the definition of adversary in the above is left to individual Participants.</p> <p>The maximal interpretation of the above is that untrustworthy Participants are left to be notified by the publication of the vulnerability report. This is the equivalent of treating them like a Participant with a default zero-day maximum embargo policy.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#coordinators","title":"Coordinators","text":"<p>Third-party Coordinators, as Participants who are neither Finders nor Vendors, often play an important role in MPCVD cases, especially those with broad impact across the software supply chain or with acute critical infrastructure or public safety impacts. Most Coordinators strive to be consistent in their MPCVD practices and have well-documented disclosure policies along with significant histories of handling previous cases. All of these factors make the argument for including third-party Coordinators in CVD cases of sufficient complexity, impact, or importance.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#other-parties","title":"Other Parties","text":"<p>Some Participants in CVD have their own policies that prohibit notification of any parties unable to directly contribute to the development of a fix for a particular vulnerability. Typically, these policies take the form of \"only Vendors of affected products\" or similar such restrictions.</p> <p>The CERT/CC's position as a third-party Coordinator in numerous cases is that this approach can be appropriate for straightforward scenarios, such as those in which a Vendor is in direct contact with their downstream Vendors and can coordinate the response within that community.</p> <p>However, it falls short in some cases, such as the following:</p> <ul> <li> <p>Vulnerabilities are found to affect a broad spectrum of Vendors and     products, especially when cases cross industry sectors or otherwise     include Participants having widely divergent operational tempos or     software delivery models.</p> </li> <li> <p>Vulnerabilities affect systems deployed in high-impact niches, such     as critical infrastructure, public safety, and national security.</p> </li> <li> <p>Outside expertise is needed to understand the implications or impact     of a vulnerability beyond the participating Vendors; sometimes the     most knowledgeable parties work for someone else.</p> </li> </ul>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/em/working_with_others/#consequences-of-non-compliance","title":"Consequences of Non-Compliance","text":"<p>Considering multiple cases over time, MPCVD can be thought of as an iterated game analogous to the Prisoner's Dilemma. One notable strategy for the Prisoner's Dilemma is tit for tat in which non-cooperation from one party in one round can be met with non-cooperation from the opposite party in the next. While MPCVD is usually much bigger than a toy two-player game, we feel it is necessary to encode the possibility that non-cooperation will have downstream consequences.</p> <p>Participants MAY decline to participate in future CVD cases involving parties with a history of violating previous embargoes.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/model_interactions/","title":"Model Interactions","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Here we reflect on the interactions between the RM, EM, and CS models within the overall Vultron process.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/model_interactions/#participant-agnostic-vs-participant-specific-aspects","title":"Participant-Agnostic vs Participant-Specific Aspects","text":"<p>This sounds like SSVC</p> <p>In SSVC, we distinguish between stakeholder-specific and stakeholder-agnostic decision points when describing vulnerability response decisions. Here, we carry a similar distinction into the CVD process. Some facts about a case are participant-specific, while others are global to the case, or participant-agnostic.</p> <p>Some aspects of the Vultron process are Participant-agnostic (i.e., they represent a global state of the case), while others are specific to a Participant. Specifically, the RM process is unique to each Participant, while the EM process is global to all Participants in a case. The CS process is a hybrid: some aspects are Participant-agnostic, while others are Participant-specific, which we will discuss in more detail below.</p> <p>Interactions between all these processes affect the overall MPCVD process for a case. The following diagram illustrates this distinction.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    PA: Participant-Agnostic\n    state PA {\n        EM\n        CS_pxa\n        EM --&gt; CS_pxa\n        CS_pxa --&gt; EM\n    }\n    PS: Participant-Specific\n    state PS {\n        RM\n        CS_vfd\n        RM --&gt; CS_vfd\n        CS_vfd --&gt; RM\n    }\n    PA --&gt; PS\n    PS --&gt; PA   </code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/model_interactions/#global-vs-participant-specific-aspects-of-the-cs-model","title":"Global vs. Participant-Specific Aspects of the CS Model","text":"<p>The CS model encompasses both Participant-specific and Participant-agnostic aspects of a CVD case. In particular, the Vendor fix path substates\u2014Vendor unaware (vfd), Vendor aware (Vfd), fix ready (VFd), and fix deployed (VFD)\u2014are specific to each Vendor Participant in a case. On the other hand, the remaining substates represent Participant-agnostic facts about the case status\u2014public awareness (p,P), exploit public (x,X), and attacks observed (a,A). This distinction in perspectives will become important in the Formal Protocol definition.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Case State Diagram showing Participant-Agnostic and Participant-Specific Aspects\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    CS: Case State Model\n    state CS {\n        ps: Participant-Specific\n        state ps {\n            [*] --&gt; We describe these interactions below.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD NOT close reports (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{I,D,A\\} \\xrightarrow{c} C\\)) while an embargo is active (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{ A,R \\}\\)).</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    state RM {\n        Invalid --&gt; Closed: close\n        Deferred --&gt; Closed: close\n        Accepted --&gt; Closed: close\n    }\n    state EM {\n        Active\n        Revise\n    }\n    EM --&gt; RM : avoid</code></pre> <p>Instead,</p> <p>Reports with no further tasks SHOULD be held in either Deferred or Invalid (\\({q^{rm} \\in \\{ D,I\\}}\\)) (depending on the report validity status) until the embargo has terminated (\\({q^{em} \\in X}\\)).</p> <p>This allows Participants to stop work on a report but still maintain their participation in an extant embargo. Notwithstanding the above,</p> <p>Participants who choose to close a report (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{I,D,A\\} \\xrightarrow{c} C\\)) while an embargo remains in force (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{A,R\\}\\)) SHOULD communicate their intent to either continue to adhere to the embargo or  terminate their compliance with it.</p> <p>Report closure or deferral alone does not terminate an embargo.</p> <p>A Participant's closure or deferral (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{C,D\\}\\)) of a report while an embargo remains active (\\(q^{em} \\in \\{A,R\\}\\)) and while other Participants remain engaged (\\(q^{rm} \\in \\{R,V,A\\}\\)) SHALL NOT automatically terminate the embargo.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\nstate RM {\n        Invalid --&gt; Closed: close\n        Deferred --&gt; Closed: close\n        Accepted --&gt; Closed: close\n    }\n    state EM {\n        Active --&gt; eXited: terminate\n        Revise --&gt; eXited: terminate\n    }\n    RM --&gt; EM : does not imply</code></pre> <p>It is expected that Participants will continue to adhere to the embargo until it is explicitly terminated. However,</p> <p>Participants MAY choose to terminate their compliance with an embargo at any time.</p> <p>While this is usually an undesirable development, it is important that it be clearly communicated to other Participants so that they can make informed decisions about the viability of the extant embargo.</p> <p>Any changes to a Participant's intention to adhere to an active embargo SHOULD be communicated clearly in addition to any necessary notifications regarding RM or EM state changes.</p> <p>Upon receipt of a Participant's notification of intent to end their compliance with an embargo, other Participants MAY choose to terminate the embargo.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/model_interactions/rm_em_cs/","title":"CVD Case State Interactions with the RM and EM Process Models","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol.
