However, we also acknowledged in CS Transitions that narrowly scoped attacks need not imply widespread adversary knowledge of the vulnerability. In such scenarios, it is possible that early embargo termination\u2014leading to publication\u2014might be of more assistance to other adversaries than it is to defenders. Thus, we need to allow room for Participant judgment based on their case-specific situation awareness. Formally,</p> <p>Once Attacks have been observed and the case state reaches \\(q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot A\\),</p> <ul> <li>New embargoes SHALL NOT be sought.</li> <li>Any existing embargo SHOULD terminate.</li> </ul> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Attacks Observed CS-EM Interactions\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    state CS {\n        a: No Attacks Observed\n        A: Attacks Observed\n\n        a --&gt; A : Attack Observation\n    }\n    state EM {\n        state PreEmbargo{ \n            Proposed --&gt; None: reject\n        }\n        state ActiveEmbargo {\n            Accepted --&gt; eXited: terminate\n            Revise --&gt; eXited: terminate\n        }\n    }\n    CS --&gt; EM: implies</code></pre> Embargo Effects on reaching Attacks Observed Formalized \\[q^{cs} \\in \\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot\\cdot A \\implies q^{em} \\in     \\begin{cases}         None \\\\         Proposed \\xrightarrow{reject} None \\\\         Accepted \\xrightarrow{terminate} eXited \\\\         Revise \\xrightarrow{terminate} eXited \\\\     \\end{cases}\\]"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/","title":"Report Management Process Model","text":"<p>This page is normative</p> <p>This page is considered a core part of the Vultron Protocol. This is a normative section of the documentation.</p> <p>Here we describe a high-level workflow for the CVD Report Management (RM) process.</p> <p>The RM process should be reasonably familiar to anyone familiar with IT Service Management (ITSM) workflows such as problem, change, incident or service request management. In particular, any workflow in which work items (e.g., incident reports, problem tickets, change requests) are received, validated, prioritized, and work is subsequently completed, should map onto the RM process outlined here.</p> <p>Vultron Does Not Dictate Internal Processes</p> <p>In the interest of maintaining the potential for interoperability among different organizations' internal processes, our protocol does not specify intra-organizational subprocesses within each state, although we give examples of such subprocesses in  Do Work Behavior.</p> <p>For further reference, ISO/IEC 30111:2019(E) provides recommendations for Vendors' internal processes that can be mapped into the RM process. We provide such a mapping in our ISO Crosswalk.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#rm-state-machine","title":"RM State Machine","text":"DFA Notation Defined <p>A Deterministic Finite Automaton is defined as a  5-tuple \\((\\mathcal{Q},q_0,\\mathcal{F},\\Sigma,\\delta)\\) where</p> <ul> <li>\\(\\mathcal{Q}\\) is a finite set of states.</li> <li>\\(q_0 \\in \\mathcal{Q}\\) is an initial state.</li> <li>\\(\\mathcal{F} \\subseteq \\mathcal{Q}\\) is a set of final (or accepting) states.</li> <li>\\(\\Sigma\\) is a finite set of input symbols.</li> <li>\\(\\delta\\) is a transition function of the form \\(\\delta: \\mathcal{Q} \\times \\Sigma \\xrightarrow{} \\mathcal{Q}\\).</li> </ul> <p>In this section, we first cover the states themselves before proceeding to a discussion of the transitions between them. Elsewhere , we provide a discussion of the Participant-specific semantics of the state transitions. We use Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) notation to describe our RM model (see inset).</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#rm-states","title":"RM States","text":"<p>Our proposed RM DFA models a report lifecycle containing seven states, defined below.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Report Management State Diagram\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    S: Start\n    R: Received\n    I: Invalid\n    V: Valid\n    D: Deferred\n    A: Accepted\n    C: Closed\n\n    [*] --&gt; S\n    S --&gt; R : receive\n    R --&gt; I: invalidate\n    R --&gt; V : validate\n    I --&gt; V : validate\n    V --&gt; A : accept\n    V --&gt; D : defer\n    A --&gt; D : defer\n    D --&gt; A : accept\n    A --&gt; C : close\n    D --&gt; C : close\n    I --&gt; C : close\n    C --&gt; [*]</code></pre> RM States \\(\\mathcal{Q}^{rm}\\) Defined <p>\\(\\begin{split}     \\mathcal{Q}^{rm} = \\{ &amp; \\underline{S}tart, \\\\                           &amp; \\underline{R}eceived,\\\\                           &amp; \\underline{I}nvalid, \\\\                           &amp; \\underline{V}alid, \\\\                           &amp; \\underline{A}ccepted, \\\\                           &amp; \\underline{D}eferred, \\\\                           &amp; \\underline{C}losed \\}     \\end{split}\\)</p> <p>By convention, we will use the underlined capital letters in the box at right as a shorthand for the state names. Each Participant in a CVD case will have their own RM state.</p> <p>RM States vs. CVD Case States</p> <p>RM states are not the same as CVD case states. Case states follow the Householder-Spring model summarized in Case State Model. Further discussion of the interactions of the RM and CS models is found in Model Interactions.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-start-s-state","title":"The Start (S) State","text":"<p>The Start state is a simple placeholder state for reports that have yet to be received. It is, in effect, a null state that no CVD Participant would be expected to reflect in their report tracking system. We include it here because it is useful when modeling coordination that spans multiple Participants in the formal protocol. Otherwise, the discussion until then will mostly ignore it.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-received-r-state","title":"The Received (R) State","text":"<p>Reports initially arrive in the Received state.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start\n    Start --&gt; Received</code></pre> <p>Vendors lacking the ability to receive reports will find it exceedingly difficult if not impossible to participate in the CVD process. Therefore,</p> <p>Vendors SHOULD have a clearly defined and publicly available  mechanism for receiving reports.</p> <p>Similarly, those who coordinate others' responses to vulnerability reports also need to have a report receiving capability; otherwise, they are not capable of coordinating vulnerability disclosures. Hence,</p> <p>Coordinators MUST have a clearly defined and publicly available mechanism for receiving reports.</p> <p>Exiting the Received state requires a Participant to assess the validity of a report. Note that validation is distinct from prioritization, as covered in our description of the Valid state. In other words, the Received state corresponds to the Validation phase of the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD subject each Received report to some sort of validation process, resulting in the report being designated as valid or invalid based on the Participant's particular criteria.</p> <p>Validity criteria need not be limited to technical analysis. For instance, a Coordinator might only accept reports within their specific scope of concern and consider reports outside their scope to be Invalid even if they believe the report accurately describes a real vulnerability. Alternatively, a Vendor might institute a policy designating reports unaccompanied by a working proof-of-concept exploit as Invalid by default.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD have a clearly defined process for validating reports in the Received state.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD have a clearly defined process for transitioning reports from the Received state to the Valid or Invalid states.</p> <p>Participants MAY perform a more technical report validation process before exiting the Received state.</p> <p>Regardless of the technical rigor applied in the validation process, Participants SHOULD proceed only after validating the reports they receive.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD transition all valid reports to the Valid state and all invalid reports to the Invalid state.</p> <p>Regardless of the content or quality of the initial report, once a Vendor confirms that a reported vulnerability affects one or more of their product(s) or service(s), the Vendor SHOULD designate the report as Valid.</p> <p>Participants MAY create a case object to track any report in the Received state.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-invalid-i-state","title":"The Invalid (I) State","text":"<p>Reports in the Invalid state have been evaluated and found lacking by the recipient. This state allows time for the Reporter to provide additional information and for the receiver to revisit the validation before moving the report to Closed.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start\n    Start --&gt; Received\n    Received --&gt; Invalid</code></pre> <p>The reasons for a report to be put in this state will vary based on each recipient's validation criteria, and their technical capability and available resources. The Invalid state is intended to be used as a temporary holding place to allow for additional evidence to be sought to contradict that conclusion.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD temporarily hold reports that they cannot validate pending additional information.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD provide Reporters an opportunity to update their report with additional information in support of its validity before closing the report entirely.</p> <p>Participants MAY set a timer to move reports from Invalid to Closed after a set period of inactivity.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-valid-v-state","title":"The Valid (V) State","text":"<p>Reports in the Valid state are ready to be prioritized for possible future work. The result of this prioritization process will be to either accept the report for follow-up or defer further effort. The Valid state is equivalent to the Prioritization (Triage) phase of the CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure. As an example, a Vendor might later choose to defer further response on a Valid report due to other priorities.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start\n    Start --&gt; Received\n    Received --&gt; Invalid\n    Received --&gt; Valid\n    Invalid --&gt; Valid</code></pre> <p>For Valid reports, the Participant SHOULD perform a prioritization evaluation to decide whether to accept or defer the report for further work.</p> <p>If one does not already exist, Participants SHOULD create a case from reports entering the Valid state to track the report's subsequent progress through the CVD process.</p> <p>Participants MAY choose to perform a shallow technical analysis on Valid reports to prioritize any further effort relative to other work.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD have a bias toward accepting rather than deferring Valid cases up to their work capacity limits.</p> <p>In other words, prioritization is only necessary if the workload represented by active valid reports exceeds the organization's capacity to process those reports.</p> <p>Prioritization schemes, such as SSVC or the CVSS, are commonly used to prioritize work within the CVD process; however, specific details are left to Participant-specific implementation. The SSVC model is illustrative here, although any prioritization scheme could be substituted as long as it emits a result that can be mapped onto the semantics of \"continue work\" or \"defer further action\". SSVC Crosswalk takes a closer look at how SSVC fits into the protocol we are defining.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-accepted-a-state","title":"The Accepted (A) State","text":"<p>The Accepted state is where the bulk of the work for a given CVD Participant occurs. Reports reach this state for a Participant only once the Participant has deemed the report to be both valid and of sufficient priority to warrant further action. The Accepted state has a different meaning for each different Participant.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start\n    Start --&gt; Received\n    Received --&gt; Invalid\n    Received --&gt; Valid\n    Invalid --&gt; Valid\n    Valid --&gt; Accepted</code></pre> <ul> <li> <p>For our purposes, Finders/Reporters enter the Accepted state only     for reports that they intend to put through the     CVD process. If     they have no intention of pursuing CVD, there is no need for them to track     their actions using this protocol. See the secret lives of finders for more.</p> </li> <li> <p>Vendors usually do root cause analysis, understand the problem, and     produce a fix or mitigation.</p> </li> <li> <p>Coordinators typically identify potentially affected Vendors, notify     them, and possibly negotiate embargoes.</p> </li> </ul> <p>We provide additional elaboration on the sorts of activities that might happen in the Accept state in Do Work Behavior.</p> <p>A report MAY enter and exit the Accepted state a number of times in its lifespan as a Participant resumes or pauses work (i.e., transitions to/from the Deferred state).</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-deferred-d-state","title":"The Deferred (D) State","text":"<p>The Deferred state is reserved for valid, unclosed reports that are otherwise not being actively worked on (i.e., those in Accepted). It parallels the Invalid state for reports that fail to meet the necessary validation criteria in that both states are awaiting closure once it is determined that no further action is necessary.</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start\n    Start --&gt; Received\n    Received --&gt; Invalid\n    Received --&gt; Valid\n    Invalid --&gt; Valid\n    Valid --&gt; Accepted\n    Valid --&gt; Deferred\n    Accepted --&gt; Deferred\n    Deferred --&gt; Accepted</code></pre> <p>For example, a Participant might use the Deferred state when a valid report fails to meet their prioritization criteria, or when a higher priority task takes precedence over an active case.</p> <p>A report MAY enter and exit the Deferred state a number of times in its lifespan as a Participant pauses or resumes work (i.e., transitions from/to the Accepted state).</p> <p>Reports SHOULD exit the Deferred state when work is resumed (to Accepted), or when the Participant has determined that no further action will be taken (to Closed).</p> <p>CVD Participants MAY set a policy timer on reports in the Deferred state to ensure they are moved to Closed after a set period of inactivity.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#the-closed-c-state","title":"The Closed (C) State","text":"<p>The Closed state implies no further work is to be done; therefore, any pre-closure review (e.g., for quality assurance purposes) should be performed before the case moves to the Closed state (i.e., while the report is in Invalid, Deferred, or Accepted).</p> <pre><code>stateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Start\n    Start --&gt; Received\n    Received --&gt; Invalid\n    Received --&gt; Valid\n    Invalid --&gt; Valid\n    Valid --&gt; Accepted\n    Valid --&gt; Deferred\n    Accepted --&gt; Deferred\n    Deferred --&gt; Accepted\n    Accepted --&gt; Closed\n    Deferred --&gt; Closed\n    Invalid --&gt; Closed\n    Closed --&gt; [*]</code></pre> <p>Reports SHOULD be moved to the Closed state once a Participant has completed all outstanding work tasks and is fairly sure that they will not be pursuing any further action on it. For example</p> <ul> <li>reports or cases that have been in Invalid or Deferred for some length of time,</li> <li>cases in Accepted where all necessary tasks are complete.</li> </ul> RM Start and End States (\\(q^{rm}_0, \\mathcal{F}^{rm}\\)) Defined <p>The RM process starts in the Start state.</p> \\[q^{rm}_0 = Start\\] <p>The RM process ends in the Closed state.</p> \\[\\mathcal{F}^{rm} = \\{Closed\\}\\]"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#rm-state-transitions","title":"RM State Transitions","text":"<p>A Participant's RM process begins when the Participant receives a report. The actions performed in the RM process represent the allowed state transitions in the corresponding DFA.</p> RM Symbols (\\(\\Sigma^{rm}\\)) Defined <p>These actions constitute the set of symbols for the RM DFA.</p> <p>\\(\\begin{align*}   \\Sigma^{rm} = \\{ &amp; \\underline{r}eceive, \\\\                    &amp; \\underline{v}alidate, \\\\                    &amp; \\underline{i}nvalidate, \\\\                    &amp; \\underline{a}ccept, \\\\                    &amp; \\underline{d}efer, \\\\                    &amp; \\underline{c}lose \\} \\end{align*}\\)</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#rm-transitions-defined","title":"RM Transitions Defined","text":"<p>In this section, we define the allowable transitions between states in the RM process model. The RM process, including its states and transitions, is depicted in the following diagram. State transitions represent messaging opportunities to communicate CVD case status among Participants. This point is the lynchpin that makes the RM model point toward a technical protocol. Every state transition implies a different message type.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Report Management State Diagram\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    S: Start\n    R: Received\n    I: Invalid\n    V: Valid\n    D: Deferred\n    A: Accepted\n    C: Closed\n\n    [*] --&gt; S\n    S --&gt; R : receive\n    R --&gt; I: invalidate\n    R --&gt; V : validate\n    I --&gt; V : validate\n    V --&gt; A : accept\n    V --&gt; D : defer\n    A --&gt; D : defer\n    D --&gt; A : accept\n    A --&gt; C : close\n    D --&gt; C : close\n    I --&gt; C : close\n    C --&gt; [*]</code></pre> <p>CVD Participants SHOULD announce their RM state transitions to the other Participants in a case.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#receive-report","title":"Receive Report","text":"<p>To begin, a Participant must receive a report. Recall that the Start state is a placeholder, so this action simply puts the receiving Participant into the Received state at the beginning of their involvement in the case.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Receive Report\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    [*] --&gt; Received: receive report</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#validate-report","title":"Validate Report","text":"<p>The Participant must validate the report to exit the Received state. Depending on the validation outcome, the report will be in either the Valid or Invalid state. Invalid reports are often waiting for additional information from the reporter, but they may also be reports that are not in scope for the Participant. Some Participants may choose to close Invalid reports immediately, while others may choose to periodically revalidate them to see if they have become Valid.</p> <p>Participants SHOULD create a case for all Valid reports. </p> <p>Paricipants MAY create a case for Invalid reports.</p> <p>Participants MAY periodically revalidate Invalid reports to determine if they have become Valid.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Validate Report\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    state Received {\n        state validate &lt;&lt;choice&gt;&gt;\n        evaluate: Valid?\n        [*] --&gt; evaluate\n        evaluate --&gt; validate\n    }\n    state Invalid {\n        state revalidate &lt;&lt;choice&gt;&gt;\n        await: More info?\n        [*] --&gt; await\n        await --&gt; revalidate\n        revalidate --&gt; await: no change\n    }\n    validate --&gt; Valid: validate (create case)\n    validate --&gt; Invalid: invalidate\n    revalidate --&gt; Valid: validate (create case)</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#prioritize-report","title":"Prioritize Report","text":"<p>Once a report has been validated (i.e., it is in the RM Valid state, \\(q^{rm} \\in V\\)), the Participant must prioritize it to determine what further effort, if any, is necessary.</p> <p>Participants MUST prioritize Valid cases.</p> <p>Our SSVC Crosswalk contains an example of how the SSVC model can be applied here, although any prioritization scheme could be substituted. Prioritization ends with the report in either the Accepted or Deferred state.</p> <p>A Participant might choose to pause work on a previously Accepted report after revisiting their prioritization decision. When this happens, the Participant moves the report to the Deferred state. Similarly, a Participant might resume work on a Deferred report, moving it to the Accepted state.</p> <p>Participants MAY re-prioritize Accepted or Deferred cases.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Prioritize Report\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    state Valid {\n        state prioritize &lt;&lt;choice&gt;&gt;\n        evalp: Priority?\n        [*] --&gt; evalp\n        evalp --&gt; prioritize\n    }\n    state Deferred {\n        state reprioritize &lt;&lt;choice&gt;&gt;\n        eval: Priority?\n        wait: Wait\n        [*] --&gt; wait\n        wait --&gt; eval\n        eval --&gt; reprioritize\n    }\n    state Accepted {\n        state reprioritize2 &lt;&lt;choice&gt;&gt;\n        do_work: Do work\n        evala: Priority?\n        [*] --&gt; do_work\n        do_work --&gt; evala\n        evala --&gt; reprioritize2\n    }\n\n    prioritize --&gt; Accepted: accept\n    prioritize --&gt; Deferred: defer\n    reprioritize --&gt; Accepted: accept\n    reprioritize --&gt; wait: defer\n    reprioritize2 --&gt; do_work: accept\n    reprioritize2 --&gt; Deferred: defer</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#participants-interact-from-the-accepted-state","title":"Participants Interact from the Accepted State","text":"<p>Some Participants (e.g., Finders and Coordinators) need to engage someone else (e.g., a Vendor) to resolve a case. To do this, the sender Participants must also be in the Accepted state; otherwise, why are they working on the case? In the following diagram, we show the interaction between two instances of the RM model: the left side represents the sender while the right side represents the recipient. Although the sender's state does not change, the recipient's state moves from Start to Received.</p> <p>Participants initiating CVD with others MUST do so from the Accepted state.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Participants Interact from the Accepted State\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    state Sender {\n        direction LR\n        other: ...\n        [*] --&gt; other\n        other --&gt; Accepted: accept\n    }\n    state Recipient {\n        direction LR\n        [*] --&gt; Received: receive\n    }\n    Accepted --&gt; Recipient: send</code></pre>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#case-closure","title":"Case Closure","text":"<p>Finally, a Participant can complete work on an Accepted report or abandon further work on an Invalid or Deferred report.</p> <p>Participants MAY close Accepted or Deferred cases or Invalid reports.</p> <pre><code>---\ntitle: Case Closure\n---\nstateDiagram-v2\n    direction LR\n    Accepted --&gt; Closed: close\n    Deferred --&gt; Closed: close\n    Invalid --&gt; Closed: close</code></pre> <p>Our model assumes that Valid reports cannot be closed directly without first passing through either Accepted or Deferred. It is reasonable to wonder why close is not a valid transition from the Valid state. The answer is that we wanted to allow prioritization and closure to be distinct activities; deferral is reversible, whereas closure is not. Often a Participant might initially defer a case only to resume work later, once more information has arrived. However, there is nothing stopping a Participant from instituting a process that goes from Valid to Deferred to Closed in rapid (even immediate) succession.</p> <p>Participants MUST NOT close cases or reports from the Valid state.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#possible-report-management-histories","title":"Possible Report Management Histories","text":"RM Transition Function (\\(\\delta^{rm}\\)) Defined <p>Following the state machine diagram above, we represent the RM process model as a right-linear grammar:</p> \\[\\delta^{rm} =  \\begin{cases} S &amp; \\to rR \\\\ R &amp; \\to vV~|~iI \\\\ I &amp; \\to vV~|~cC \\\\ V &amp; \\to aA~|~dD \\\\ A &amp; \\to dD~|~cC \\\\ D &amp; \\to aA~|~cC \\\\ C &amp; \\to \\epsilon \\\\ \\end{cases}\\] <p>The strings generated in the language defined by this grammar can be useful for exploring the possible sequences of states each report might encounter for each Participant. The 15 shortest paths are ric, rvac, rvdc, rivac, rivdc, rvadc, rvdac, rivadc, rvadac, rvdadc, rivdac, rivdadc, rvdadac, rivadac, and rvadadc. Due to the structure of the RM DFA, longer strings just add more defer-accept (da) or accept-defer (ad) cycles prior to closure (c). The usually limited duration of the RM process coupled with the tendency for CVD Participants to prefer to avoid frequent starts and stops means that we expect the vast majority of reports to follow one of the above paths, with the remainder falling into marginal extensions.</p> <p>See also</p> <p>Further discussion of a reward function to evaluate RM DFA strings is discussed as future work in Reward Functions.</p>"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/#rm-dfa-fully-defined","title":"RM DFA Fully Defined","text":"<p>The full definition of the RM DFA is given below.</p> <p>RM DFA Fully Defined</p> <p>Taken in combination, the full definition of the RM DFA is as follows:</p> \\[  RM =      \\begin{pmatrix}             \\begin{aligned}                 \\mathcal{Q}^{rm} = &amp; \\{ S,R,I,V,A,D,C \\} \\\\                 q^{rm}_0 = &amp; S  \\\\                 \\mathcal{F}^{rm} = &amp; \\{ C \\} \\\\                 \\Sigma^{rm} = &amp; \\{ r,i,v,a,d,c \\} \\\\                 \\delta^{rm} = &amp;                     \\begin{cases}                         S \\to &amp; rR \\\\                         R \\to &amp; vV~|~iI \\\\                         I \\to &amp; vV~|~cC \\\\                         V \\to &amp; aA~|~dD \\\\                         A \\to &amp; dD~|~cC \\\\                         D \\to &amp; aA~|~cC \\\\                         C \\to &amp; \\epsilon \\\\                     \\end{cases}             \\end{aligned}     \\end{pmatrix}\\] RM State Subsets Defined <p>Before proceeding, we pause to define a few useful subsets of RM states (\\(\\dots \\subset \\mathcal{Q}^{rm}\\)) for future use:</p> \\[  \\begin{align}         Open &amp;= \\{ R,I,V,D,A \\} \\\\         Valid~Yet~Unclosed &amp;= \\{ V,D,A \\} \\\\         Potentially~Valid~Yet~Unclosed &amp;= \\{ R,V,D,A\\} \\\\         Active &amp;= \\{ R,V,A \\} \\\\         Inactive &amp;= \\{ I,D,C \\}      \\end{align}\\]"},{"location":"topics/process_models/rm/rm_interactions/","title":"Report Management Interactions Between CVD Participants","text":"<p>Each Participant in a case has their own instance of the RM state model. Why? Because for anyone else to know about the vulnerability (and as a prerequisite to CVD happening at all), the Finder must have already validated the report and prioritized it as worthy of further effort to have any reason to attempt to coordinate its disclosure. In other words, CVD only starts after the Finder has already reached the Accepted state for any given vulnerability to be reported. Although within the page we might provide guidance in terms of SHOULD, MUST, etc., the content here is not normative.</p> <p>The Vultron Protocol is designed to support a variety of use cases. The following user stories are intended to capture the requirements of these use cases. While the protocol is designed to support these use cases, it is not required that all use cases be supported by the protocol.</p> <p>Where appropriate, we intend to provide a reference implementation for each applicable user story.</p> <p></p> <p>Original User Stories from 2022 CERT/CC Whitepaper</p> <p>Stories numbered from <code>2022_001</code> through <code>2022_102</code> originated in the Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure User Stories whitepaper.</p> <p>These user stories reflect internal discussions with the CERT/Coordination Center (CC) based on our own experiences in developing and using the VINCE platform as well as our ongoing CVD practices. The user stories are expected to be utilized by the CVD team to better understand, create, and implement a CVD Protocol. In addition, the CERT/CC believes that these user cases will be useful for any enterprise designing or implementing its own CVD policies, processes, and procedures.</p> <p>The remaining stories have been added since that whitepaper was published.</p>"},{"location":"topics/user_stories/#support-levels","title":"Support Levels","text":"<p>Each story page indicates a categorization according to the level of support provided by the originally published Vultron Protocol (version 0.4.0):</p> <ul> <li>Provided - Stories in this category are directly supported by the Vultron Protocol v0.4.0.</li> <li>Allowed - Stories in this category are indirectly supported by the Vultron Protocol v0.4.0.</li> <li>Unsupported - Stories in this category are not supported by the Vultron Protocol v0.4.0.</li> <li>Out-of-scope - Stories in this category are out of scope for the Vultron Protocol v0.4.0.</li> </ul> <p>In the future, we expect these categories will change toward simply Supported, Unsupported and Out-of-scope. (de-duplicate) 2022_014 As a Participant, I want to negotiate embargo/disclosure schedules, modify/renegotiate them, and know when others have published 2022_015 As a Participant, I want to notify others of my intent (date) to publish 2022_016 As a Participant, I want limited/ACK of vulnerability/and have full/proper advisory 2022_017 As a Participant, I want to share my draft publication with others 2022_018 As a Participant, I become aware of the existence of of public exploit (e.g. PoC) and want to tell others 2022_019 As a Participant, I become aware of exploitation in the wild and want to tell others 2022_020 As a Participant, I want to publish a vulnerability (external to protocol) 2022_021 As a Participant, I want to advertise the locale (language, location, geo-scope, area of authority), aspects of my policy 2022_022 As a Participant, I want to advertise the scope (e.g., products, version ranges, sites/domains) of my CVD capability 2022_023 As a Participant, I want to constrain whom I communicate with because I want to enforce an embargo and communicate only with those who have a need to know. 2022_024 As a Finder/Reporter, I want to constrain whom I communicate with because I want to maintain my anonymity. 2022_025 As a Vendor/Deployer, I want to constrain whom I communicate with until a patch or mitigation has been published and released. 2022_026 As a Coordinator, I want to constrain whom I communicate with to work within an embargo and communicate only with those who have a need to know. 2022_027 As a Participant, I want to Address Participants constraints, e.g., entity lists 2022_028 As a vendor or coordinator, I want others to find my information and reporting intake (duplicate of X) 2022_029 As a Vendor, Deployer, and Other, I want to assign my own ID to a case 2022_030 As a Participant , I want to discover and use/map to a global/shared case ID (Might just be a GUID assigned at first notification, CVE is a partial example, vxref) 2022_031 As a Finder, Reporter, Vendor, Coordinator, Other, I want to get from another Participant a list of cases I am involved in with them 2022_032 As a Reporter, Vendor, Coordinator, Other, I want to ask Participant A if Participant D is in a case. Operator may decide/policy, may be based on whether C and D are in the same case. \"A\" may or may not answer, that is their policy. Participant may decide/policy, may be based on whether C and D are in the same case. 2022_033 As a Finder, Reporter, Vendor, Coordinator, Other,  I want to request/state that I do not want others to know I am in a case. Participant still gets to decide their policy. Covers researcher asking for anonymity. Vendor can ask not to be listed and operator can disagree/still list. 2022_034 As a Participant I want to use my global/federated user ID to interact with other Participants 2022_035 As a Participant I want to have confidence in the identity and group membership of others (and be willing and able to use others' groups) 2022_036 As a non-vendor Participant I want to determine how integrated authentication/authorization is in the CVD protocol 2022_037 As a vendor I want to publish vulnerability advisories 2022_038 As a vendor or coordinator, I want to receive vulnerability reports 2022_039 As a Participant I want to ask questions/generally communicate with another case Participant 2022_040 As a Participant I want to ask questions/generally communicate with another case Participant unicast/point-to-point 2022_041 As a Participant I want to ask questions/generally communicate with all the Participants in a case (broadcast) 2022_042 As a Participant I want to ask questions/generally communicate with a subset of case Participants 2022_043 As a Participant, I want to communicate in a common case channel 2022_044 As a Participant I want to communicate with selected case Participants 2022_045 As a Participant, I want to produce a shared verified public record of case activity 2022_046 As a Participant, I want the case to have a leader (global case owner, CVD leader) 2022_047 As a Participant, I want to propose a case leader, possibly myself 2022_048 As a Participant, I want to vote/accept a proposed case leader 2022_049 As a Participant I want to announce the case leader to all Participants 2022_050 As a Participant I want to transfer case leadership to a different Participant 2022_051 As a Participant, I want to depose (or vote down?) a case leader, possibly myself (step down), possibly requiring a suggestion of a replacement 2022_052 As a Participant I want to add (declare and notify others) new Participants to a case 2022_053 As a Participant, I want to propose new Participants to a case 2022_054 As a Participant I want to vote/accept new Participants to a case 2022_055 As a Participant, I want to state that I paid or received a bounty 2022_056 As a Participant, I want to ask if another Participant paid a reporter 2022_057 As a Participant, I want to ask a reporter if they were paid 2022_058 As a Participant I want to share a draft advisory with others 2022_059 As a Participant I want to share a draft advisory with others and request feedback (including status) 2022_060 As a Participant I want to request advisory (draft) from a Participant 2022_061 As a Participant, I want to request someone else\u2019s (vendor) status so I can note changes in others status 2022_062 As a Participant, I want to state my status so others are aware of it. 2022_063 As a Participant, I want to include a non-vendor role Participant in a case 2022_064 As a Participant, I want to include the Government (some/any part, could include regulator) so that they may participate in the case. 2022_065 As a Participant, I want to include the Industry/trade group so that they may participate in the case. 2022_066 As a Participant, I want to stop participating in the case. 2022_067 As a Participant, I want to stop participating in the case and inform others that I am no longer participating. 2022_068 As a Participant, I want to stop want to convey what restricted information or degree of restriction I will accept so that I won't be accused of mishandling restricted information. 2022_073 As a Participant, I want to convey what TLP restricted information or degree of restriction I will accept so that I won't be accused of mishandling TLP restricted information. 2022_074 As a Participant, I want to keep track of events and timelines so that I have a complete report and don't miss a deadline. 2022_075 As a Participant, I want to see response times/states of other Participants so that I can be prepared for the next state in the CVD process. 2022_076 As a VDP operator, I want the CVD protocol to also support VDP 2022_077 As a Participant, I want to be able to ask further questions about a report, to ensure I fully understand the vulnerability and mitigation or remediation options. 2022_078 As a coordinator, I want to drive better (shorter?) embargo timelines, to ensure they are feasible. 2022_079 As a disclose sooner than others but minimize their (the others) exposure/risk 2022_081 As a Participant I want to communicate important public state change message/information with all Participants. want to be rewarded with a bounty. 2022_086 As a Participant I will prioritize my response to requests for information or action so that I contribute to a risk-minimizing CVD process and outcome Participant we need a mechanism which will ensure the appropriate level of authentication of all Participants 2022_091 As a Participant we need a mechanism which will ensure the confidential transport and storage of information. 2022_092 As a Participant, I want to know who else is participating in a case to ensure I don't void an embargo. 2022_093 As a Participant, in an effort to ensure the I can avoid delaying the delivery of a ready fix to a subset of my user base. 2022_106 As a Participant, I want the coordination process to be decentralized so that it is robust against individual Demos","text":"<p>In this tutorial, we will run the three multi-actor container demo scenarios end-to-end using Docker Compose. By the end of this tutorial, we will have:</p> <ul> <li>started a fleet of isolated Vultron API containers, each representing a   distinct CVD participant,</li> <li>watched those actors exchange vulnerability-disclosure messages   automatically across container boundaries, and</li> <li>observed how the Vultron Protocol handles two-party, three-party, and   multi-vendor coordination workflows.</li> </ul> <p>What we will learn</p> <p>These scenarios use trigger-based puppeteering \u2014 the demo runner calls trigger endpoints on each actor's own container so that the actor's behavior tree and outbox logic are exercised end-to-end. Each actor makes its own decisions; activities flow from sender outbox to receiver inbox over HTTP across the Docker network. No messages are injected manually. This is the full Vultron Protocol in action, not a simulation.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#prerequisites","title":"Prerequisites","text":"<p>We need the following tools installed before we begin:</p> <ul> <li>Docker (version   20.10 or later)</li> <li>Docker Compose   (version 2.x; included with Docker Desktop)</li> <li>Git (to clone the repository)</li> </ul> <p>We do not need Python installed locally; the containers include everything required.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#the-three-scenarios","title":"The three scenarios","text":"Scenario DEMO value Actors Two-actor <code>two-actor</code> Finder + Vendor + CaseActor Three-actor <code>three-actor</code> Finder + Vendor + Coordinator + CaseActor Multi-vendor <code>multi-vendor</code> Finder + Vendor + Coordinator + Vendor2 + CaseActor <p>Each scenario is self-contained: the demo runner resets all container state, seeds actor records and peer registrations, runs the workflow, verifies final state, and exits.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#step-1-clone-the-repository","title":"Step 1 \u2014 Clone the repository","text":"<p>First, let's get a local copy of the Vultron project:</p> <p><code>bash git clone https://github.com/CERTCC/Vultron.git cd Vultron</code></p> <p>Tip</p> <p>If you already have a local clone, <code>cd</code> into the repository root and run <code>git pull</code> to make sure you are up to date.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#step-2-run-the-two-actor-scenario","title":"Step 2 \u2014 Run the two-actor scenario","text":"<p>The two-actor scenario (D5-2) is the simplest: a Finder discovers a vulnerability and submits a report to a Vendor, who validates it, engages the case, adds the Finder as a participant, and exchanges notes with them. The CaseActor is co-located in the Vendor container.</p> <p>From the repository root, run:</p> <p><code>bash docker compose -f docker/docker-compose-multi-actor.yml \\     up --abort-on-container-exit demo-runner</code></p> <p>Docker builds the images on the first run (this takes a few minutes) and then:</p> <ol> <li>Starts <code>finder</code>, <code>vendor</code>, <code>coordinator</code>, <code>case-actor</code>, and <code>vendor2</code>    containers (all five are defined in the compose file; only <code>finder</code> and    <code>vendor</code> participate in this scenario).</li> <li>Waits until every container passes its <code>/health/ready</code> probe.</li> <li>Starts <code>demo-runner</code>, which resets state, seeds actors, runs the workflow,    verifies the result, and exits.</li> </ol> <p>A successful run ends with:</p> <p><code>text [multi-actor-integration] SUCCESS: scenario 'two-actor' passed.</code></p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#what-the-two-actor-demo-does","title":"What the two-actor demo does","text":"Step Actor Action 1 Finder Submits a <code>VulnerabilityReport</code> to Vendor's inbox 2 Vendor Validates the report (trigger: <code>validate-report</code>) 3 Vendor Case engagement cascades automatically (RM \u2192 ACCEPTED) 4 Vendor Adds Finder as a case participant 5 Finder Posts a question note to the case 6 Vendor Replies; case forwards reply to Finder's inbox \u2705 Vendor Case DataLayer holds 2 participants and 2 notes <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant F as Finder\n    box Vendor Container\n        participant V as Vendor\n        participant CA as CaseActor\n    end\n\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: RmSubmitReport (Offer)&lt;br/&gt;+ VulnerabilityReport\n    Note over V: \"validate-report trigger&lt;br/&gt;RM state = ACCEPTED\"\n    V-&gt;&gt;CA: CreateCase + AddReport\n    V-&gt;&gt;F: RmInviteToCase (Invite)\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    V-&gt;&gt;CA: AddParticipant (Finder)\n    F-&gt;&gt;CA: Add Note (question)\n    V-&gt;&gt;CA: Add Note (reply)\n    CA-&gt;&gt;F: Forward reply to Finder's inbox\n    Note over CA: 2 participants, 2 notes</code></pre>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#step-3-run-the-three-actor-scenario","title":"Step 3 \u2014 Run the three-actor scenario","text":"<p>The three-actor scenario (D5-3) adds a dedicated Coordinator and a separate CaseActor container. The Coordinator acts as the authoritative case manager, inviting participants and negotiating an embargo.</p> <p><code>bash DEMO=three-actor docker compose -f docker/docker-compose-multi-actor.yml \\     up --abort-on-container-exit demo-runner</code></p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#what-the-three-actor-demo-does","title":"What the three-actor demo does","text":"Step Actor Action 1 Finder Submits a report to Coordinator's inbox 2 Coordinator Creates the authoritative case on CaseActor 3 Coordinator Adds the report to the case 4 Coordinator Invites Finder; Finder accepts 5 Coordinator Proposes an embargo; Finder accepts 6 Coordinator Invites Vendor; Vendor accepts 7 Vendor Accepts the active embargo \u2705 CaseActor Case holds 3 participants; embargo EM state = ACTIVE <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant F as Finder\n    participant C as Coordinator\n    participant V as Vendor\n    participant CA as CaseActor\n\n    F-&gt;&gt;C: RmSubmitReport (Offer)&lt;br/&gt;+ VulnerabilityReport\n    C-&gt;&gt;CA: CreateCase + AddReport\n    C-&gt;&gt;F: RmInviteToCase (Invite)\n    F-&gt;&gt;C: RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    C-&gt;&gt;F: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer)\n    F-&gt;&gt;C: Accept\n    C-&gt;&gt;V: RmInviteToCase (Invite)\n    V-&gt;&gt;C: RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    V-&gt;&gt;C: Accept active embargo\n    Note over CA: 3 participants&lt;br/&gt;EM state = ACTIVE</code></pre>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#step-4-run-the-multi-vendor-scenario","title":"Step 4 \u2014 Run the multi-vendor scenario","text":"<p>The multi-vendor scenario (D5-4) extends D5-3 with case ownership transfer. Vendor initially holds the case; Coordinator takes over and then invites a second Vendor (Vendor2) to join.</p> <p><code>bash DEMO=multi-vendor docker compose -f docker/docker-compose-multi-actor.yml \\     up --abort-on-container-exit demo-runner</code></p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#what-the-multi-vendor-demo-does","title":"What the multi-vendor demo does","text":"Step Actor Action 1 Finder Submits a report to Vendor's inbox 2 Vendor Validates the report (trigger: <code>validate-report</code>) 3 Vendor Creates the case on CaseActor (<code>attributed_to</code> = Vendor) 4 Vendor Links the report to the case 5 Vendor Invites Finder; Finder accepts 6 Vendor Proposes an embargo; Finder and Vendor accept it 7 Vendor Offers case ownership to Coordinator; Coordinator accepts 8 Coordinator Invites Vendor2; Vendor2 accepts 9 Coordinator Delivers embargo proposal to Vendor2; Vendor2 accepts \u2705 CaseActor 3 participants; Coordinator is owner; embargo ACTIVE <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant F as Finder\n    participant V as Vendor\n    participant C as Coordinator\n    participant V2 as Vendor2\n    participant CA as CaseActor\n\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: RmSubmitReport (Offer)&lt;br/&gt;+ VulnerabilityReport\n    Note over V: validate-report trigger\n    V-&gt;&gt;CA: CreateCase (attributed_to = Vendor)\n    V-&gt;&gt;CA: AddReport\n    V-&gt;&gt;F: RmInviteToCase (Invite)\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    V-&gt;&gt;F: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer)\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: Accept\n    Note over CA: Embargo ACTIVE\n    V-&gt;&gt;C: CaseTransferOffer\n    C-&gt;&gt;V: Accept transfer\n    Note over CA: attributed_to = Coordinator\n    C-&gt;&gt;V2: RmInviteToCase (Invite)\n    V2-&gt;&gt;C: RmAcceptInviteToCase\n    C-&gt;&gt;V2: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer)\n    V2-&gt;&gt;C: Accept\n    Note over CA: 3 participants&lt;br/&gt;Coordinator is owner&lt;br/&gt;EM state = ACTIVE</code></pre>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#step-5-read-the-output","title":"Step 5 \u2014 Read the output","text":"<p>Each demo step is wrapped in a <code>demo_step</code> or <code>demo_check</code> context manager that prints structured lifecycle markers:</p> Symbol Meaning \ud83d\udea5 A workflow step has started \ud83d\udfe2 The step completed successfully \ud83d\udd34 The step raised an exception \ud83d\udccb A verification check has started \u2705 The verification check passed \u274c The verification check failed <p>Watch for <code>\ud83d\udd34</code> or <code>\u274c</code> markers to diagnose failures. The compose <code>--abort-on-container-exit</code> flag stops the entire stack when <code>demo-runner</code> exits, so a failed scenario terminates cleanly.</p> <p>To examine logs from a specific container after a run:</p> <p><code>bash docker compose -f docker/docker-compose-multi-actor.yml logs vendor</code></p>"},{"location":"tutorials/container_demos/#step-6-clean-up","title":"Step 6 \u2014 Clean up","text":"<p>Named Docker volumes persist the SQLite databases between runs. A second path shows the rejection outcome. Each step is logged so we can follow the state changes.</p> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant O as Case Owner\n    participant P as Participant\n\n    O-&gt;&gt;P: RmInviteToCase (Invite)\n    alt Participant accepts\n        P--&gt;&gt;O: RmAcceptInviteToCase\n        Note over O: CaseParticipant created&lt;br/&gt;added to case\n        Note over O: Participant status updated\n        O-&gt;&gt;P: Remove from case\n    else Participant rejects\n        P--&gt;&gt;O: RmRejectInviteToCase\n        Note over O: No participant created\n    end</code></pre> <p>See Manage Participants for details.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#transfer-ownership","title":"transfer-ownership","text":"<p><code>bash vultron-demo transfer-ownership</code></p> <p>This demo shows case ownership transfer: the current owner (vendor) offers the case to a coordinator, who either accepts (the case <code>attributed_to</code> field is updated to the coordinator) or rejects (ownership remains with the vendor).</p> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant V as Vendor\n    participant C as Coordinator\n\n    V-&gt;&gt;C: CaseTransferOffer (Offer)\n    alt Coordinator accepts\n        C--&gt;&gt;V: Accept\n        Note over V,C: case.attributed_to = Coordinator\n    else Coordinator rejects\n        C--&gt;&gt;V: Reject\n        Note over V: Ownership unchanged\n    end</code></pre> <p>See Transfer Case Ownership for the corresponding activity descriptions.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#step-4-embargo-management","title":"Step 4 \u2014 Embargo management","text":""},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#establish-embargo","title":"establish-embargo","text":"<p><code>bash vultron-demo establish-embargo</code></p> <p>This demo exercises embargo negotiation. A participant proposes an embargo period; the case owner either accepts (activating the embargo on the case, EM state = <code>ACTIVE</code>) or rejects (no change to EM state).</p> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant P as Participant\n    participant O as Case Owner\n\n    P-&gt;&gt;O: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer)\n    alt Case owner accepts\n        O--&gt;&gt;P: Accept\n        Note over P,O: EM state = ACTIVE\n    else Case owner rejects\n        O--&gt;&gt;P: Reject\n        Note over P,O: EM state unchanged\n    end</code></pre> <p>See Establish an Embargo for background.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#manage-embargo","title":"manage-embargo","text":"<p><code>bash vultron-demo manage-embargo</code></p> <p>This demo continues from an active embargo. Two paths are shown:</p> <ol> <li>Activate and terminate: coordinator proposes \u2192 vendor accepts \u2192    embargo activated \u2192 vendor terminates (removes) the embargo.</li> <li>Reject and repropose: coordinator proposes \u2192 vendor rejects \u2192    coordinator proposes a revised embargo \u2192 vendor accepts \u2192 activated.</li> </ol> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant C as Coordinator\n    participant V as Vendor\n\n    alt  Path 1 - Activate then terminate\n        C-&gt;&gt;V: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer)\n        V--&gt;&gt;C: Accept\n        Note over C,V: EM state = ACTIVE\n        V-&gt;&gt;C: EmTerminateEmbargo\n        Note over C,V: EM state = NONE\n    end\n\n    alt Path 2 - Reject then repropose\n        C-&gt;&gt;V: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer)\n        V--&gt;&gt;C: Reject\n        C-&gt;&gt;V: EmProposeEmbargo (Offer, revised)\n        V--&gt;&gt;C: Accept\n        Note over C,V: EM state = ACTIVE\n    end</code></pre> <p>See Manage an Embargo for details.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#step-5-acknowledgement-status-and-notes","title":"Step 5 \u2014 Acknowledgement, status, and notes","text":""},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#acknowledge","title":"acknowledge","text":"<p><code>bash vultron-demo acknowledge</code></p> <p>This demo shows how a vendor uses <code>RmReadReport</code> (as:Read) to acknowledge receipt of a report without committing to an outcome. Three paths are demonstrated:</p> <ol> <li>Acknowledge only \u2014 ack, then notify finder.</li> <li>Acknowledge then validate \u2014 ack \u2192 validate \u2192 notify finder.</li> <li>Acknowledge then invalidate \u2014 ack \u2192 invalidate \u2192 notify finder.</li> </ol> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant F as Finder\n    participant V as Vendor\n\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: RmSubmitReport (Offer)\n    V-&gt;&gt;V: RmReadReport (Read / ack)\n    alt Path 1 - Acknowledge only\n        V--&gt;&gt;F: Notify finder (ack)\n    else Path 2 - Acknowledge then validate\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmValidateReport (Accept)\n        V--&gt;&gt;F: Notify finder (validated)\n    else Path 3 - Acknowledge then invalidate\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmInvalidateReport (TentativeReject)\n        V--&gt;&gt;F: Notify finder (invalidated)\n    end</code></pre> <p>See Acknowledge a Report for the corresponding activity descriptions.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#status-updates","title":"status-updates","text":"<p><code>bash vultron-demo status-updates</code></p> <p>This demo shows three types of case record updates:</p> <ol> <li>Notes \u2014 vendor creates a <code>Note</code> and adds it to the case.</li> <li>Case status \u2014 vendor creates a <code>CaseStatus</code> and adds it to the case.</li> <li>Participant status \u2014 vendor creates a <code>ParticipantStatus</code> and adds    it to a case participant.</li> </ol> <p>See Status Updates for details.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#step-6-full-rm-case-lifecycle","title":"Step 6 \u2014 Full RM case lifecycle","text":""},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#manage-case","title":"manage-case","text":"<p><code>bash vultron-demo manage-case</code></p> <p>This demo exercises the Report Management (RM) state machine from submission through closure. Three paths are shown:</p> <ol> <li>Engage path \u2014 submit \u2192 validate \u2192 create case \u2192 engage \u2192 close.</li> <li>Defer and re-engage path \u2014 submit \u2192 validate \u2192 create case \u2192    defer \u2192 re-engage \u2192 close.</li> <li>Invalidate path \u2014 submit \u2192 invalidate \u2192 close report.</li> </ol> <p>Notice the RM state transitions logged at each step.</p> <pre><code>sequenceDiagram\n    participant F as Finder\n    participant V as Vendor\n\n    F-&gt;&gt;V: RmSubmitReport (Offer)\n    alt Path 1 - Engage\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmValidateReport (Accept)\n        Note over V: Case created\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: EngageCase\n        Note over V: RM state = ACCEPTED\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmCloseReport\n    else Path 2 - Defer then re-engage\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmValidateReport (Accept)\n        Note over V: Case created\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: DeferCase\n        Note over V: RM state = DEFERRED\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: EngageCase\n        Note over V: RM state = ACCEPTED\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmCloseReport\n    else Path 3 - Invalidate\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmInvalidateReport (TentativeReject)\n        V-&gt;&gt;V: RmCloseReport\n        Note over V: No case created\n    end</code></pre> <p>See Manage a Case for the corresponding activity descriptions and state machine reference.</p>"},{"location":"tutorials/other_demos/#step-7-run-all-demos-in-sequence","title":"Step 7 \u2014 Run all demos in sequence","text":"<p>To run every demo in the standard order, use the <code>all</code> sub-command:</p> <p><code>bash vultron-demo all</code></p> <p>The <code>all</code> sub-command stops and reports failure on the first demo that raises an exception.
